Rallied by Li Liejun's call to impeach Yuan Shikai, Liu Zhi saw service in the Second Revolution before being admitted to the Baoding Army Officer School. Successfully serving in the National Protection Movement within the Guangdong Army under Cen Chunxuan, Liu would continue to serve, being transferred by Chen Jiongming in 1921 to the General Headquarters. Joining the Kuomintang in that same year, Liu Zhi fought against the Old Guangxi Clique, against warlords in Jiangxi, and served under Xu Chongzhi.\n\nInvited to serve as a tactical instructor at the recently founded Whampoa by He Yingqin, Liu Zhi became a commander in the Whampoa First Teaching Regiment, where he formed a deep brotherhood with the likes of He Yingqin, Gu Zhutong, Qian Dajun and Jiang Dingwen. Gu Zhutong

Entering the French-styled Hubei Army Officer School, Gu Zhutong attempted a failed gambit at aiding the National Protection Revolutionaries in 1916, and ended fleeing home. Eventually, Gu was admitted to the Baoding Army Officer School, graduating with excellent results in 1919. He then found himself rather chaotically in the company of Dr. Sun Yat-sen in 1920, and would later meet Chiang Kai-shek in 1922, hitting it off and becoming compatriots. Zhang Lingfu

Having met Yu Youren by happenstance, Zhang would head south to Guangzhou on Yu's recommendation, enrolling in Whampoa Military Academy.\n\nExperiencing all of the standard facets of Whampoa ideology and education, Zhang would be incorporated into the 21st Division of the National Revolutionary Army as a platoon leader, and would see service in the Northern Expedition as a soldier of the 1st Division. Qian Dajun
§YBorn:§! §LJune 14th, 1893, in Wuxian County, Jiangsu Province, Great Qing§!\n§YAlignment:§! §PWhampoa Clique§!\n§YBackground:§! §LHeir to a merchant family, Qian Dajun would instead enter the military in 1909, enlisting in Jiangsu Army Primary School. Briefly serving in Xinhai, Qian would graduate in 1912, and almost immediately see service in the Second Revolution in 1913, joining the Dare-to-Die squad organised by Niu Yongjian. On the latter's sponsorship, Qian Dajun would travel to Japan to study at the Haoran Academy, meeting Dr. Sun Yat-sen and joining the Gemingdang (Later Kuomintang) in 1914. Returning to China in 1915, Qian would attend a military prep school before being forced to drop out due to Anti-Yuan Shikai activities, and upon Yuan's death, he returned to schooling, and would later enter the Artillery Section of Baoding Army Officer School.\n\nBefore official enrolment however, he would attend the Shinbu Gakko in Tokyo, and later the Shikkan Gakko. Graduating from his Japanese schooling and marrying Ouyang Zaoli, Qian would finally enrol in Baoding's Artillery Section, yet he would resin in 1921 to follow SUn Yat-sen to Guangdong, joining his army under Xu Chongzhi. One of the core figures in the preparation of Whampoa, he would serve as a weapons instructor. Entering into the Eastern Expeditions, Wang Boling would be replaced by Qian Dajun due to lacklustre performance in commanding the 2nd Teaching Regiment, and would later serve as He Yingqin's staff officer.\n\nBecoming director of the Guangzhou Garrison during the Northern Expedition, Qian Dajun's valiant defence against the German invasion could not prevent the KMT from losing the city, and following the rest of the NRA to Guizhou, Qian Dajun would find himself serving under He Yingqin as a division commander and staff officer.§!
Xu Tingyao

In 1920 he would briefly return to his home-town to become a primary school teacher, before returning to service as a company commander and battalion commander of the Guangdong Army in 1922. Seeing brave and fierce service in the Eastern Expeditions, he would be promoted to the rank of battalion commander. In August 1925, Xu would serve as a commander within the 1st Army under Xu Chongzhi, participating in the Northern Expedition. Seeing the collapse of the army unfold before him, Xu would follow the 1st Army to Guizhou, wherein he would be rapidly promoted to commander of the 2nd division, and later the 4th division.\n\nEntering a period of reflection, Xu Tingyao would become enamoured by the developing state of mechanised and motorised warfare, petitioning He Yingqin to allow him to establish a Transportation Corps School, in preparation for an armoured corps. Despite the dire state of the National Revolutionary Army following into the 1930s, Xu Tingyao hopes that one day his dreams of a Chinese Armoured Force descending upon warlords and imperialists alike will come to fruition. Jiang Dingwen
§YBorn:§! §LJanuary 25th, 1895, in Zhuji, Zhejiang Province, Great Qing§!\n§YAlignment:§! §PWhampoa Clique§!\n§YMilitary Career:§! §LAchieving a standard education, Jiang Dingwen would enlist in the Hangzhou Student Army following the outbreak of the Xinhai Revolution, and in the following year would enlist in Zhejiang Army Lecture Hall to finalise his military education. After graduation, Jiang would serve as a platoon and company commander, before travelling to Guangdong in 1917 to fight in Dr. Sun's Second Revolution. Appointed lieutenant colonel adjutant of the staff department, Jiang Dingwen would become a colonel staff officer in 1922, at the mere age of 27. Serving in various staff roles, Jiang Dingwen would willingly give up his rank and position to enter Whampoa Academy, becoming captain of the second student team, and a military instructor.\n\nHaving the odd habit of waking up early, he would often be met by principal Chiang Kai-shek. Reportedly, upon a field exercise being held at Whampoa, a foreign advisor was observing the exercises with Chiang and Jiang in tow. Asking impromptue questions, Jiang Dingwen would answer fluently, to the amazement of the advisor. Participating in the suppression of the Guangzhou Merchant Rebellion, Jiang would also see service in the First Eastern Expedition, gaining Chiang's personal respect. Entering the Northern Expedition with a flurry, Jiang would participate in the failed siege of Wuhan, before being beaten back all the way to Guizhou along with the rest of 1st Army.\n\nA youthful face in a clique of veterans, Jiang Dingwen is counted amongst the Whampoa inner circle, and serves his superior He Yingqin dutifully.§!\n\n§YNotes: Known for his quick thinking, swift action, and adept understanding of mobile warfare, Jiang Dingwen has gained the moniker of the \
Gui Yongqing
§YBorn:§! §LMarch 7th, 1901, in Guixi County, Jiangxi Province, Great Qing§!\n§YAlignment:§! §PWhampoa Clique§!\n§YBackground:§! §LSelected along with fellow Guixi people to study in Japan at a young age, Gui Yongqing would ultimately return to China upon hearing of the founding of Whampoa Military Academy, joining its first class. Participating in the Eastern Expeditions against Chen Jiongming, Gui is notable as one of the few Whampoa soldiers to have actively participated in looting and pillaging, which was noticed by Li Zhilong. Upon hearing of this, Chiang Kai-shek was furious, ordering that Gui be executed to serve as a lesson to the rest of Whampoa, requesting that He Zhonghan be the executioner. Albeit due to Gui Yongqing's connections to He Zhonghan, the latter intervened on behalf of the former, petitioning He Yingqin to ask Chiang to spare his life, and grant him a more lenient punishment.\n\nServing as commander of the special forces battalion of the 1st Army of the NRA, he would enter Fujian in 1926 during the Northern Expedition, yet in the failure of the Battle of Longtan, Gui would ultimately be sidelined following his performance. Miraculously surviving the collapse of the Northern Expedition, Gui Yongqing would find himself within Guizhou, and within an army far more honourable than he is.\n\nDesperate for employment, Gui would utilise his experience in amphibious warfare alongside connections within the Whampoa establishment, namely Teng Jie, to have himself receive an official commission as an admiral. Whilst ostensibly a useless position for there is no coastline to set sail from, Gui hopes that the NRA's fortune will result in him gaining proper employment. Until then, he is perfectly content reaping the benefits of a continued commission.§!\n\n§YNotes: Exceptionally craven, Gui Yongqing has been complicit in the torture of suspected syndicalists and the embezzlement of funds. With little to no actual naval experience, Gui survives purely on his connection to his Whampoa brethren.§!
Zhang Jingyu
§YBorn:§! §L1895, in Gaotang County, Shandong Province, Great Qing§!\n§YAlignment:§! §PWhampoa Clique§!\n§YMilitary Career:§! §LAdmitted to Tsinghua University's Preparatory School, Zhang Jingyu would ultimately prepare for studying in the United States. However with the sudden outbreak of the Weltkrieg, he instead chose to travel to Europe where he studied mechanical engineering in Liverpool. Joining the European Branch of the Chinese Kuomintang, he was appointed by Dr. Sun as the head of the Liverpool branch of the party in 1922. Returning to China in 1924, he was transferred to the Whampoa Military Academy, serving as secretary of the Central Party Committee. First serving as English Secretary of the Professorial Department under Wang Boling, he would later serve as English secretary directly for Chiang Kai-shek.\n\nAppointed as the party representative of the 2nd Teaching Regiment of Whampoa, he participated in the First Eastern Expedition, seeing a small amount of field service. After returning to Guangzhou, he served as party representative of Shijing Arsenal, and later director of the business department of Guangdong Aviation Bureau. Appointed director of the whole Aviation Bureau in 1926, he served as party representative of the Aviation Department for the NRA, and simultaneously for the Navy Department.\n\nFollowing the collapse of Kuomintang control in China, Zhang Jingyu continued to be the intermediary between party affairs and that of the air force, and whilst the Guizhou air force is currently paltry, he hopes to see it modernised, eventually to serve as the basis for a national Chinese one.§!
Teng Jie
Graduating from the Department of Sociology at Shanghai University, Teng Jie garnered a wide variety of experience in managing political affairs, and with radical, extreme ideology. Joining the Whampoa Military Academy, Teng Jie graduated from the 4th Infantry Department and saw service in the Northern Expedition as a common soldier. Serving as a clerk in the General Political Department of the General Headquarters of the National Revolutionary Army, he slowly became disgusted with the cooperation between the CSP and KMT.\n\nLeaving China following the collapse of the Northern Expedition to study at the Political Economy Department of the Meiji Univeristy in Japan, Teng Jie decided to focus on party politics and the study of party organisation. Deeply understanding the important role political parties play in modern politics, Teng Jie began to believe that in order to save China, it would first be needed to start with party building. Analysing the internal enemies of the Kuomintang as being local warlords and the Chinese Syndicalist Party, he deemed the former very fragile, and not an issue, whilst he thought the Chinese Syndicalist Party to be one of the greatest threats to the party. It had organisation, doctrine, strategy and support of the Internationale.\n\nTherefore in order to deal with the CSP, he desired the establishment of a new \

Li Shizhen
§YBorn:§! §L1896, in Ninghai County, Zhejiang Province, Great Qing§!\n§YAlignment:§! §nBlue Shirt Society§!\n§YBackground:§! §LIn his early years, Li Shizhen would be sent to Shanghai to study, graduating from Shanghai Public School, and later enrolling in Zhejiang University. Tang Jiyao
The much beguiled King of the Southwest, Tang Jiyao started his life on the path of a scholar. After passing the imperial exam, he travelled to Japan and studied at the Tokyo Jinbu School, joining the Tongmenghui soon after. Graduating from the Zhenwu School and serving in the Japanese Army as an intern, he was then admitted to the Japanese Army Academy in 1907, graduating the following year as a classmate of Sun Chuanfang, Liu Cunhou, Li Genyuan, Li Liejun, and Cheng Qian among others. Returning home, he served as director of the General Staff Office, instructor at the Kunming Military Academy, and commander of a regiment.\n\nOne of the leading plotters of the Double Ninth Uprising, Tang assisted Guizhou, and was elected as the province Governor in 1911. In 1913 he repressed the Second Revolution, and succeeded Cai E as Governor of Yunnan. In 1915, alongside Li Liejun and Cai E, he planned and initiated the National Protection War. Aligning with the Constitutional Protection Government, Tang led the Jingguojun until his deposition by Gu Pinzhen in 1921.\n\nReturning in 1922, he collaborated with Chen Jiongming and opposed the Northern Expedition. Attempting to take-over Liangguang after Sun Yat-sen's death, he was beaten back by Li Zongren. Around the same time, he accepted deputy leadership of the Public Interest Party in 1925, and founded the Minzhidang, or People's Government Party in 1926. Almost couped in 1927, he persevered, and managed to establish a delicate power balance in Yunnan for the years to come.
§YBorn:§! §LAugust 14th, 1883 in Huize County, Yunnan Province, Great Qing§!\n§YBackground:§! §L$YUN_tang_jiyao_bio$§!
§YBorn:§! §LAugust 14th, 1883 in Huize County, Yunnan Province, Great Qing§!\n§YAlignment:§! §aTang Jiyao's Clique§!\n§YBackground:§! §L$YUN_tang_jiyao_bio$§!\n\n§YNotes: Married by controversy and seeming hypocrisy, Tang is a man marred by the Warlord Era, despite his dedication to Republicanism.§!
Tang Xiaoying
§YBorn:§! §L1905, in Huili County, Yunnan Province, Great Qing§!\n§YBackground:§! §LOnly son of Tang Jiyao, few expected Tang Xiaoying to live up to his father's example in political notoriety, and yet here he is, President of the Chinese Republic. Picking up his father's mantle, Xiaoying himself has been a democrat of no small stature within Yunnan for years. Sent to Japan to study at the Department of Economics in the Tokyo Imperial University, Tang would return to a nation in turmoil. Serving his father dutifully, Xiaoying would organise important People's Government Party grassroots meetings within the Tang Family Garden, inviting various individuals such as Zhou Xinmin, Pan Dakui and Jiang Liangfu to help further PGP party organisation.\n\nCreating the Academic Constitutional Research Association, Tang would serve as its director and push his father to explore more democratic means of governance. Proposing that before the implementation of Constitutionalism and Federalism, the people must be granted freedom of speech and personal freedom, parties should be allowed to organise, and that the government should thus cooperate with the opposition, Xiaoying would develop a close relationship with the China Democratic League.\n\nHead of the Yunnan Provincial Party Committee of the People's Government Party, Xiaoying is unique in that he has cultivated close ties not only with Luo Longji and Wen Yiduo, but that of the Young China Party too, effectively bridging the political gap between opposition and incumbency, a role he hopes to continue fulfilling as president.§!
§YBorn:§! §L1905, in Huili County, Yunnan Province, Great Qing§!\n§YAlignment:§! §cPeople's Government Party§!\n§YBackground:§! §LOnly son of Tang Jiyao, few expected Tang Xiaoying to live up to his father's example in the military field, and alas he had been granted field command by his father upon his return to China, a much contested matter within the Yunnan Army. Sent to Japan to study at the Department of Economics in the Tokyo Imperial University, Tang ultimately returned to a nation in turmoil.\n\nWith next to no military education but a penchant for patriotic duty, Xiaoying has not allowed his father's opponents to dissuade him in military service. Ultimately untested, many eagerly look upon the relatively young Tang with latent curiosity to see if the economics major has what it takes.§!\n\n§YNotes: A political organiser for his father's People's Government Party, Tang holds meetings in the Tang family garden with Crescent Moon members, and even with Liao Xingchao on behalf of the Young China Party.§!
Tang Jiyu
§YBorn:§! §L1890, in Huize County, Yunnan Province, Great Qing§!\n§YAlignment:§! §aTang Jiyao's Clique§!\n§YBackground:§! §LBrother of Tang Jiyao, Tang Jiyu graduated from the Yunnan Army Martial Arts School, and in 1912 was appointed director of the Guizhou Military Police Bureau by his brother. Following Jiyao's appointment as governor of Yunnan, Jiyu would follow him back home, and assist his brother as director of the Yunnan Provincial Police Department and commander of the guard corps. During the National Protection War, Tang Jiyu travelled to Japan, and returned in 1916 to serve as dean of the Military Academy.\n\nIn 1923, on the orders of Tang Jiyao, he marched into Guizhou to rescue Liu Xianshi, and drive out Yuan Zuming. After his success, he became Governor of Guizhou, with Liu as director of the Military Reconstruction Committee. In 1925 Tang withdrew in light of Yuan Zuming's return, and instead marched into Guangxi on the orders of his brother.\n\nFighting catastrophically against the New Guangxi Clique, he returned in defeat and was appointed as the Yunnan Army Training Director. During the February 6th coup attempt, Tang remained steadfast besides his brother, and rallied his forces to fight against the plotters. With their success, Tang rose again in a right-hand position with Tang Jiyao, against all allegations of nepotism which may dissuade him.§!\n\n§YNotes: He is sworn brothers with Xi Ziqiang and Wang Jiexiu.§!
Sun Du
§YBorn:§! §LMay 5th, 1898, in Luliangzhou, Yunnan Province, Great Qing§!\n§YAlignment:§! §aTang Jiyao's Clique§!\n§YBackground:§! §LComing from a gentry family, Sun Du entered the infantry department of the Kunming Military Academy in 1915. Graduating in 1917, he then joined the Yunnan Army. In 1922, Sun was promoted to lead the independent regiment under direct control of Tang Jiyao, and would see service in Sichuan and Guizhou, returning to Yunnan in 1924. Thereafterwards he served as the director of the Yunnan Tobacco and Alcohol Monopoyl Bureau, and then as commander of the Yunnan Provincial Military Police.\n\nDuring the February 6th Coup attempt by Zhang Ruji, Hu Ruoyu, Li Xuanting and Long Yun, Sun adopted a wait-and-see attitude, which proved prudent when Tang Jiyao returned in power through the personal loyalty of his subordinates. Aiding Tang in putting down the plotters, Sun was in return promoted to director of the Yunnan Provincial Youth League General Administration, becoming Tang Jiyao's right-hand man, not counting Tang Jiyu.\n\nIn 1931, Sun was promoted to Chief of Staff of the Yunnan Army for his loyalty, and proposed to Tang a plan to radically downsize Yunnan's army. However, the idea was shelved due to pressure from the Yunnan Clique warlords.

Tang Jilin

In 1927 he served as the commander of the Second Division of the Yunnan Army, and in 1928 was appointed as military advisor to Tang Jiyao. In 1929 he was commander of Yunnan Martial Law, and later commander of the 7th Division. In the 1930s he was appointed as member of the Yunnan Provincial Government, and was promoted to Garrison Commander of Kunming.§!
Wang Jiexiu
§YBorn:§! §L1892, in Lijiang, Yunnan Province, Great Qing§!\n§YAlignment:§! §aTang Jiyao's Clique§!\n§YBackground:§! §LStudying hard in academics in his youth, in 1909 Wang Jiexiu was selected to attend the Kunming Army Primary School, and in 1911 he would take part in the Double Ninth Uprising. Soon incorporated into Tang Jiyao's army, this is where his long career of consistent servitude and loyalty would begin. Following Tang into Guizhou, he returned to Yunnan with Tang and was sent to the Kunming Military Academy once more to graduate in the artillery department. In 1915 he fought in the National Protection War under Cai E, and was promoted by Li Yuanhong to lieutenant colonel of artillery, on Tang's request.\n\nIn 1919 he was awarded various high ranking medals, and developed a close friendship with Xi Ziqiang and Tang Jiyu, becoming sworn brothers. Becoming one of Tang Jiyao's core subordinates, Wang would follow Tang Jiyu to his appointment in Guizhou, becoming commander of the First Army. Ordered to fight in Guangxi alongside Hu Ruoyu and Long Yun, his soldiers would be encircled and annihilated by Bai Chongxi.\n\nDuring the February 6th Coup, Wang wavered, almost attaching himself to Hu Ruoyu's portion, but with Tang's rebounding remained resolute with Tang Jiyao. A master at the art of artillery, Wang is de facto the head of the Jingguojun's cannons.§!\n\n§YNotes: He is sworn brothers with Tang Jiyu and Xi Ziqiang.§!
Wu Xuexian
§YBorn:§! §L1887, in Tonghai County, Yunnan Province, Great Qing§!\n§YAlignment:§! §aTang Jiyao's Clique§!\n§YBackground:§! §LA local Yunnan bandit chief, in the early years of the Republic Wu Xuexian formed a gang to compete with another bandit chief, Yu Ying, for water rights. In this fight, he amassed a large amount of men, guns, currency, and power in southern Yunnan. In order to squash Wu, Tang Jiyao sent Wang Jiexiu and Tan Shaoqing's soldiers to deal with him, but Wu managed to grow stronger, and even defeat the officers. In 1919 he would be recuited by Tang Jiyao, engaging in a triad-esque ceremony to cement their alliance.\n\nWith his newfound power, Wu killed Yu Ying, and was appointed a captain in the Yunnan Army. Fighting in the National Protection War, he would be wounded in the leg, and ultimately unsuccessful in much of the conflict. At the end of 1920, with Gu Pinzhen's successful coup, Wu began a guerrilla campaign against Gu, waiting for Tang to return.\n\nMaking the first contribution to Tang's return in 1922, and most importantly killed Gu Pinzhen later on. Following Tang Jiyu and Zhang RUji to attack Liangguang, he would be defeated. During the February 6th Coup, Wu would prove decisive in fighting against the coup plotters. For his consistent loyalty, Wu would be promoted, and continue tepid raids against the Mengzi Clique.§!
Xi Ziqiang
§YBorn:§! §L1894, in Lijiang, Yunnan Province, Great Qing§!\n§YAlignment:§! §aTang Jiyao's Clique§!\n§YBackground:§! §LA member of the Naxi ethnic group, Xi Ziqiang was selected to attend the Yunnan Army primary School due to excellent academic performance and strong physique. In 1909 he was admitted to the Yunnan Army Military Academy, and graduated, then participated in the Double Ninth Uprising. In 1912 he was retrained at the academy, and participated in the National Protection War, Constitutional Protection Movement, and other revolutionary movements. Serving with distinction, he would become very close with Wang Jiexiu.\n\nBelieving that Xi Ziqiang was loyal and resoruceful, and that Wang Jiexiu was serious about his work, Tang Jiyao would slowly grow to favour the duo as his most trusted subordinates. In 1919 he became head of the Personal Guard Colonel Regiment, and was sent to the Japanese Navy Fleet for training. After studying for one year, he returned to Yunnan. In 1922 he was transferred to the Southeast Tour Envoy Office, with Tang Jiyao as his Chief of Staff.\n\nIn 1923 he served as chief of staff of Tang Jiyao during his tenure as Guizhou Governor. In 1925 he was the engineer section chief of the Kunming Military Academy, and later governor of Tengyue. Circumnavigating the power struggle of the February 6th Coup in 1927, Xi would become the Chief of Staff of the Yunnan-Guizhou Pacification Office, and one of Tang's stalwarts.§!\n\n§YNotes: He is sworn brothers with Tang Jiyu and Wang Jiexiu.§!
Zhang Taiyan
§YBorn:§! §LJanuary 12th, 1869 in Yuhang County, Zhejiang Province, Great Qing§!\n§YAlignment:§! §cPeople's Government Party§!\n§YBackground:§! §LOne of China's greatest reformist politicians and the father of the Federalist movement, Zhang Taiyan, also known as Zhang Binglin, descended from a wealthy scholarly family. After a minor speedbump in his scholarly career, he pursued his studies under various intellectuals and philosophers. In 1894 he donated money to the reformist Qiangxue Society, and began correspondence with Kang Youwei and Liang Qichao. Serving as chief editor of Shiwu Bao on Liang's invitation, he was editor for only 4 months before engaging in a fistfight over Confucianism.\n\nEngaging with more conservative reformists there afterwards, he fled following the end of the 1898 Hundred Days' Reforms, and worked as a reporter in Japanese Taiwan. Sympathising with Kang and Liang, he met Sun Yat-sen through Liang in Tokyo, and began to focus intensely on Anti-Manchu writings. Engaged with the Tongmenghui, he advocated revolutionary morality, and splitted from Sun in 1909. After Xinhai, he briefly assisted Yuan Shikai before being imprisoned by him, and only left for Shanghai after Yuan died in 1916.\n\nIn 1917 he launched the Constitutional Protection Movement with Sun Yat-sen, and urged Tang Jiyao to take a leading role. Opposing peace talks between north and south, he began to advocate federal autonomy in 1920 alongside Xiong Xiling and Zhang Shizhao, and continually hoped that Beiyang would reform for the better, even in light of the Kuomintang. Helping to found the People's Government Party in 1926, he is one of Tang Jiyao's biggest intellectual inspirations.§!\n\n§YNotes: As one of the Founding Fathers of the Federalist Movement, and one of the last of the Old Reformers, his career has been exemplary.§!

In 1908 he studied philology under Zhang Taiyan, becoming one of Zhang's four disciples, and returned to China in 1910 to serve in the Xinhai Revolution. Wang Rongbao
§YBorn:§! §L1878, in Wu County, Jiangsu Province, Great Qing§!\n§YAlignment:§! §cPeople's Government Party§!\n§YBackground:§! §LAt the age of 15 Wang Rongbao was recommended to study at the Nanjing Academy, and later took the imperial examination, which led him to enter the Ministry of War. After the failure of the 100 Day Reform Movement of 1898, Wang asked for leave from his position and returned home. Becoming a teacher at the Nanyang Public School in Shanghai, he travelled to Japan to study at Waseda University and Keio University in 1901, majoring in Law and Politics.\n\nParticipating in the Youth Association's establishment, a proto-Tongmenghui organisation, he gradually transitioned to a reformist mindset, and returned to China to serve in the bureaucracy. A core figure of the constitutionalists in Beijing and a member of the Progressive Party, he assisted Yuan Shikai in the dissolution of the Qing. Serving as a member of the Provisional Senate and later the Assembly, he became Minister to Belgium, a member of the Constitution Drafting Committee, and a representative to the Spa Peace Conference.\n\nHe was then successively appointed Minister to Switzerland, and to Japan in 1922, before ultimately resigning from his position in 1932 in protest of the Shanghai Uprising's treatment. An associate of Liang Qichao, Wang is committed to reformist politics, and the advocacy of a constitutionalist government most represented by the People's Government Party.§!
Dong Ze
§YBorn:§! §L1888, in Yunlong County, Yunnan Province, Great Qing§!\n§YAlignment:§! §cPeople's Government Party§!\n§YBackground:§! §LIn 1907 Dong Ze entered the First-Class Agricultural School of the Yunnan Imperial Academy, and the following year was granted government funding to study in Japan, entering the Tokyo Tongwen College. Joining the Tongmenghui during this period, he returned to China during Xinhai to right in the revolution, directly taking part in the attack on the Nanjing Governor's Office. Returning to Yunnan he would serve as the secretary to Cai E, and was recommended by him to study in the United States.\n\nEntering Columbia University in 1912 to study politics, economics, and education, he would return to Yunnan to participate in the National Protection War, serving as secretary to the National Protection Military Governor's Office, and liaison to Hong Kong. After the NPA's victory, he returned to the United States to obtain his Master's Degree.\n\nReturning to China in 1920, he would become Director of the Prep Office of Donglu University in 1922, and Director of the Education Department. Hiring many well-known figures to work at the university, he advocated democratic school management, and various other progressive educational reforms. Dong would join the People's Government Pary in 1926 at its founding, and continue his role as Director of the Education Department.§!
Xu Yun
§YBorn:§! §L1864, in Jinjiang County, Fujian Province, Great Qing§!\n§YAlignment:§! §aTang Jiyao's Clique§!\n§YBackground:§! §LOriginally born as Xiao Guyan, Master Xu Yun would develop a deep affinity and interest in Buddhism from a young age. At the age of 17 he would be married to two wives by his father in order to prevent him from becoming a monk, however he nonetheless became one at Yongquan Temple in Fuzhou, alongside his wives. Touring much of the nation and Buddhist world in the early 1900s, Xu Yun would mediate confrontations between the Shanghai Buddhist Association and the Nanjing Datong Association in 1911.\n\nMeeting Sun Yat-sen, to discuss the revitalisation of the Buddhist Association's charter, he would travel to Beijing to meet Yuan Shikai, and have the Buddhist Association of China reorganised into the Chinese Buddhist Federation. A close associate and advisor to Cai E, he would negotiate a peaceful resolution to a Yunnanese-Tibetan War in 1913, and in 1920 he would rebuild the Huating Temple in Kunming.\n\nWell respected and admired by Tang Jiyao, Xu Yun has a token voice in Tang Jiyao's metaphorical court, with the latter always at least considering his wisdom.§!\n\n§YNotes: His actual birthdate is of poor documentation. Chronicles state as early as the 1840s, and as late as the 1870s.§!
Luo Longji
A famed scholar of the new era of intellectuals and a child prodigy, Luo Longji started his academic career in the Tsinghua Prep School for Studying in the United States in 1913. In 1919, he became a leading figure of the November Riot's Student Movement, and finally travelled to study in the United States in 1921. Entering the University of Wisconsin and later Columbia University to study political science, he graduated and entered the London School of Economics and Political Science due to his personal fascination over Laski, obtaining a doctorate in the field and becoming the latter's favourite student.\n\nReturning to China in 1928, he started to teach at Shanghai Guanghua University, and founded the Crescent Moon bookstore and magazine, serving as the editor-in-chief. Writing political publications in what was nominally intended to be a literature and prose analysis journal, he would draw much ire and strain relations with his more writing inclined fellows, such as Xu Zhimo, but nonetheless carried on his crusade against authoritarianism within the nation.\n\nIn 1933 Luo was almost assassinated due to his inherent controversy, and was dismissed from the Yi Shi Bao publication, fleeing into the interior. Promoting a humanistic, liberal, yet modern conservative vision for China inspired by Anglo-American writers and systems, Luo Longji serves as the informal face of the Crescent Moon Society, a gathering of his friends that transformed into a political outlet.
§YBorn:§! §LJuly 30th, 1896 in Anfu County, Jiangxi Province, Great Qing§!\n§YBackground:§! §L$YUN_luo_longji_bio$§!
§YBorn:§! §LJuly 30th, 1896 in Anfu County, Jiangxi Province, Great Qing§!\n§YAlignment:§! §lCrescent Moon Society§!\n§YBackground:§! §L$YUN_luo_longji_bio$§!\n\n§YNotes: He is another close friend of Hu Shih.§!
Cun Shusheng
Privately educated at a young age, Cun Shusheng was a diligent and active student, imaginative and praised by his Principal for his ambition. In 1918 he travelled to Japan to study at the East Asia Preparatory School, Tokyo Higher Normal School, and the Economics Department of the Faculty of Arts and Letters at Kyushu Imperial University.\n\nGraduating with a bachelor's in economics, he joined the Creation Society during his time in Japan, and in 1928 joined the Social Science Research Society, organised by Chinese students studying in Japan and Taiwan. Serving as their chief writer, he researched and promoted socialist theory, even stewarding one Ai Siqi. With encroaching foreign influence upon China, he finally left Japan in 1931 to teach at the Law and Business School in Beijing University.\n\nFollowing the League Uprising and intellectual crackdown, Cun retreated into the interior, later participating in Xing Xiping's World Forum, and joining the National Salvation Federation through Ma Xulun. Invited to Yunnan by Luo Longji, he became a rallying point for the left-wing of the Crescent Moon Society. Coveting an alliance with socialists, he has found himself at amicable odds with the Liberal Conservatives of the Society, but continues to insist on unity.
§YBorn:§! §LSeptember 25th, 1896 in Tengchong County, Yunnan Province, Great Qing§!\n§YBackground:§! §L$YUN_cun_shusheng_bio$§!
§YBorn:§! §LSeptember 25th, 1896 in Tengchong County, Yunnan Province, Great Qing§!\n§YAlignment:§! §lCrescent Moon Society§!\n§YBackground:§! §L$YUN_cun_shusheng_bio$§!\n\n§YNotes: As a teacher, he believes that strict Spartan training is necessary and beneficial for Chinese youth, who are burdened with thousands of years of tradition.§!
Su Honggang
§YBorn:§! §L1878, in Jinning, Yunnan Province, Great Qing§!\n§YAlignment:§! §lCrescent Moon Society§!\n§YBackground:§! §LStudying diligently as a child, Su Honggang was taught to always be kind in spite of any difficulties, and such a teaching would stay with him, profoundly impacting his life and mindset. Passing the imperial exam at the age of 16, he would teach at a local primary school, and be admitted to the Yunnan Provincial Higher School in 1903. In 1908 he participated in the Yunwu Alumni Association in Kunming, engaging in anti-Qing activities. During Xinhai, Su would become dedicated to the cause of people's enlightenment through education, and with the final approval of the Minister of Education in 1919k established a private school.\n\nRunning the school in a caring, thoughtful, and egalitarian manner, it would expand to encompass two middle school classes, and two primary school classes, with an additional junior high school department. Serving as principal, he would teach a whole generation of Yunnanese, without taking an additional salary.\n\nWith the retreat of various scholars into the interior, Su begun to associate with their reformist and progressive circles, becoming an informal member of the Crescent Moon Society in the Southwest. Hesitant to play politics however, he is far more keen to focus on education, social issues, and human rights.§!\n\n§YNotes: He is a close friend of Wen Yiduo and Li Gongpu.§!
Liang Shiqiu
§YBorn:§! §LJanuary 6th, 1903 in Daxing County, Zhili Province, Great Qing§!\n§YAlignment:§! §lCrescent Moon Society§!\n§YBackground:§! §LIn 1915 the young Liang Shiqiu would be admitted to Tsinghua University upon his father's arrangement. Enrolled in the prep class for studying in the United States, he would become close friends with Wen Yiduo, and forged a short friendship with the Creation Society. During November 4th, Liang was an active participant in the school movement. In 1923 he went to the United States to study at Colorado College, and in 1924 would graduate to study at Harvard. On the way he co-founded the nationalist writer's group the Dajiang Society with Wen Yiduo, Luo Longji, He Haoruo, Wu Jingchao and others.\n\nStudying for a master's degree at Harvard University, he would be influenced by Babbitt's New Humanism, and received a Ph.D. in philosophy. Returning to China in 1926, he would teach at National Southeast University, Qingdao University, and director of the Foreign Language Department alongside the library of Beijing Normal Unviersity. In 1927 he would found the Crescent Moon Bookstore with Xu Zhimo and Wen Yiduo, and would teach at Jinan University.\n\nBetween 1927 and 1936 Liang would engage in a series of intellectual debates with various left-wing writers, namely Lu Xun, due to his critical stance on class theory and promotion of modern conservatism. Following the Shanghai Uprising, Liang would enter the interior of his own volition.§!\n\n§YNotes: He is a close friend of Hu Shih, and the two exchange dialogue about their plans to translate Shakespeare.\nHe has been insulted by Lu Xun as merely \

In 1912 he was admitted to the Tsinghua School in Beijing, as second place amongst all Hubei students. Enamoured by ancient Chinese poetry, he began to publish a series of reading notes in Tsinghua Weekly. Crafting classical poetry, he actively participated in the November 4th Student Movement, and represented the School at the National Students' Federation Conference in Shanghai.\n\nIn 1920 he published his first vernacular Chinese Article, and his first new style poem, \
Ye Gongchao

Later he would travel to the United Kingdom and obtained a master's degree in literature from the University of Cambridge in 1924. After leaving the United Kingdom, he would travel to the Graduate Institute of the University of Paris for Research, and returned to China in 1926 to become a lecturer in the English Department of Beijing University.\n\nIn 1927 he participated in the establishment of the Crescent Moon Bookstore, and served as the director of the Department of Foreign Literature and director of the library of Jinan University, and concurrently as a professor of English literature at Wusong Chinese Public School. He Haoruo
§YBorn:§! §LMarch 16th, 1899 in Xiangtan County, Hunan Province, Great Qing§!\n§YAlignment:§! §lCrescent Moon Society§!\n§YBackground:§! §L Enlisting in the Tsinghua School in Beijing in 1913, He Haoruo would travel to the United States to study in 1920, and entered Stanford Universityy. In 1923 he entered the University of Wisconsin to study economics, obtaining a doctorate in philosophy. Later He would enter the Lowe Military Academy to study cavalry, and in 1926 returned to China to serve as a instructor at the Whampoa Military Academy. After the Northern Expedition began, he served as Chief of Staff of the 46th Army of the National Revolutionary Army.\n\nThrough clever defection via the intrigues of Federalists within Sun Chuanfang's camp, He would be hired in 1928 to serve as a professor at Jinling University, and in 1932 would be invited by Zhao Hengti to serve in the Hunan Provincial Government. Having been associated with the Dajiang Society during his time the United States, He would find himself reunited with his old colleagues in their new Crescent Moon Society, notably as one of the few members with any military experience.§!
Long Yun
A member of the Yi ethnic group and born into a landlord family with a slave-holder legacy, Long Yun could not learn to read at a young age, and so was sent to Zhaotong with his cousin, Lu Han, to study. Meeting Zuo Ruoheng, the three would become known as the \
§YBorn:§! §LNovember 19th, 1884 in Zhaotong, Yunnan Province, Great Qing§!\n§YBackground:§! §L$YUN_long_yun_bio$§!
§YBorn:§! §LNovember 19th, 1884 in Zhaotong, Yunnan Province, Great Qing§!\n§YAlignment:§! §gLong Yun's Clique§!\n§YBackground:§! §L$YUN_long_yun_bio$§!\n\n§YNotes: A moderniser first and foremost, Long has been known to flirt with Third Way democrats to bat against Tang Jiyao.§!
Lu Han
A cousin and childhood friend of Long Yun, Lu Han would join Wei Huanzhang's army in Sichuan with Long, participating in the Xinhai Revolution. Admitted to the fourth infantry class of the Kunming military academy in 1912 with Long, he would graduate in 1914 and be assigned to the Yunnan Army. Fighting in the National Protection War, he would see service in Sichuan under Cai E, and afterwards would serve as a platoon leader in the Yunnan Army stationed in Sichuan. Following the expulsion of the Yunnan army in 1920, he would serve as a major adjutant in Tang Jiyao's Governor's office, and would resolutely support him in light of Gu Pinzhen's coup.\n\nWith Tang's return Lu would become a commander of Tang's personal guard, and would fight in Guangxi under the command of Long Yun. Almost completely encircled, Lu Han would work with Hu Ruoyu, Zhang Ruji, Li Xuanting and Long Yun in an attempt to coup Tang in 1927, but due to its failure would be forced into Zhaotong. Sitting idly, he was considered to be Long Yun's most premier subordinate.
§YBorn:§! §LFebruary 6th, 1895 in Zhaotong, Yunnan Province, Great Qing§!\n§YBackground:§! §L$YUN_lu_han_bio$\n\nLu Han turned against Long Yun, in part because of Long's decision to appoint former Tang Jiyao loyalists to power but more directly due to Long's attempt at centralising power around himself. The daring general then betrayed Hu Ruoyu in turn, allowing him to seize control using his relative's former resources.§!
§YBorn:§! §LFebruary 6th, 1895 in Zhaotong, Yunnan Province, Great Qing§!\n§YAlignment:§! §gLong Yun's Clique§!\n§YBackground:§! §L$YUN_lu_han_bio$§!\n\n§YNotes: Whilst a close friend and relative of Long Yun, he is not necessarily loyal.§!
Zhang Chong
§YBorn:§! §LJanuary 25th, 1901, in Luxi County, Yunnan Province, Great Qing§!\n§YAlignment:§! §gLong Yun's Clique§!\n§YBackground:§! §LA member of the Yi ethnic group, the young Zhang Chong attended a private school, and later a primary school in his local county. Immersed in revolutionary sentiment from a young age, Zhang had developed a strong sympathy for the peasantry and working class, fighting in a local militia against bandits. Unfortunately due to his anti-landlord sentiments, he would be almost arrested in 1918, and forced into a life of banditry. More akin to a vigilante, fighting against local tyrants and evil gentry, he would become something of a local folk hero, and managed to trump provincial forces numerous times.\n\nInstead recruited by Tang Jiyao, he would serve as a regiment commander in the Yunnan army. Supported the coup attempt against Tang Jiyao by Long Yun, Hu Ruoyu, Zhang Ruji and Li Xuanting, Zhang was floated for the position of commander of the 5th division, but due to the failure of the coup was instead forced into Zhaotong with the rest of Long Yun's acolytes. Aiming to maintain his populist streak and aid the people, Zhang engaged in local reform efforts around Zhaotong, intending to turn it into somewhat of a model county.§!
Li Peiyan
§YBorn:§! §L1886, in Yunnan Province, Great Qing§!\n§YAlignment:§! §gLong Yun's Clique§!\n§YBackground:§! §LOne of the last students of Yunnan to receive a government scholarship in the late Qing, Li Peiyan ranked first in the provincial examination and served under Zhang Hangao. In 1915 he was the first principal of his local high school, and was well adoredb y the local people. In 1917 he was elected as the second provincial senator and concurrently served as the general manager of Luliang County, albeit he was later dismissed due to allegations of corruption and was imprisoned.\n\nIn 1922, after his sister Li Peilian married Long Yun, he was released from prison and resumed his duties. Appointed director of the Kaiguang Border Salt Bureau by Yuan Jiagu, Li would resolutely stand by Long Yun during the 1927 coup rumbling, and whilst floated for the position of President of the Yunnan Fudian New Bank, he would instead be shuffled to that of Yunnan Salt Transport Commissioner. Building a number of water conservation, education, health and industrial facilities in Binchuan, he has slowly amassed wealth and power within Yunnan.§!\n\n§YNotes: He is indirectly related to Long Yun as his brother-in-law.§!

After graduation he returned to China and taught at the Beijing University of Political Science and Law. In 1921 he returned to Kunming and served as the head of the Education Section of the Yunnan Provincial Government Supervision Office. Lu Chongren

After graduation he worked as a judge in Zhenxiong County, and was later elected as a member of the provincial senate of Qiaojia County. Introduced by his fellow villager Yuan Xihou to Long Yun, he was appointed military judge of the Jingguojun Fifth Army by Long Yun in 1922. Afterwards recommended by Long to serve as governor of Xundian county, he became something of a local hero and the local gentry built a memorial stone for him.\n\nTransferred to Kunming to serve as the director of the military law department of the Fifth Army, he would work closely with Long Yun to the point of being referred to as his \
Zhang Banghan
§YBorn:§! §L1885, in Zhenxiong County, Yunnan Province, Great Qing§!\n§YAlignment:§! §gLong Yun's Clique§!\n§YBackground:§! §LEntering the Yunnan Higher School in 1905, Zhang Banghan would graduate and then travel to the Nam Ba Vi School in Vietnam. In 1906 he joined the Tongmenghui in Hanoi, and followed Sun Yat-sen travelling within Southeast Asia. Serving as the Chief editor of Singapore's Zhongxing Daily and Hong Kong's China Press, he participated in the Zhennanguan Uprising and the Hekou Uprising, working with Hu Hanmin to shelter revolutionaries. In 1909 he travelled to Europe to study applied science, and returned to China on the orders of Sun Yat-sen.\n\nAppointed director of the department of foreign affairs of the Yunnan Military Government by Cai E, he would leave to obtain an electrochemical engineer certificate in Belgium, returning in 1920 to serve as an engineer at Yaolong Electric Light Factory, director of the Wireless Telegraph Bureau, Instructor at the Aviation School, and Professor of Science and Engineering at Yunnan University.\n\nSupporting Tang Jiyao and Dong Ze in establishing Yunnan University, he would be on the frontlines of negotiations with the Kuomintang as they marched into Guizhou in his capacity as special negotiator of the Department of Foreign Affairs. Recently he has been promoted to serve as Director of the Provincial Construction Department.§!\n\n§YNotes: Distinctly a member of the Yunnan clique with Pro-Kuomintang sympathies.§!
Hu Ruoyu
Heir to a landlord family, Hu Ruoyu was sent to private school when he was young, and later graduated from the local state Secondary School. In 1910 he was admitted to the fourth term of the Yunnan Army Primary School, and participated in the Double Ninth Uprising the following year. In 1912 he left Yunnan for Jiangxi to serve under Li Liejun, and returned to Yunnan on the eve of the Second Revolution. In 1914 he entered the officer class of Kunming Military Academy, and graduated to serve as a squad and battalion commander. During the National Protection War, Hu Ruoyu participated in the battle to retake Gejiu.\n\nOrdered to reinforce the Yunnan Army in Sichuan, he defeated Liu Cunhou, and became a brigade commander in 1918. In 1920 during the Gu Pinzhen coup attempt, Hu led his soldiers in support of Tang Jiyao, but diverted his force to Guangxi to rally with Sun Yat-sen, seizing Guilin from Shen Hongying, and contacting Tang who had fled to Hong Kong. Fighting on behalf of Tang to restore him to power, he led Long Yun and Zhang Ruji to victory, and was in return rewarded as Garrison Commander of Mengzi.\n\nFollowing several blunders from Tang, Hu insisted on attempting a coup against him in 1927. However, Long Yun outmanoeuvred him into instating Tang as puppet governor, with the latter then managing to force both of them back to the negotiating table. With the turn inwards in the 1930s, Hu has strengthened his position in Yunnan's south, relying on Mengzi's connections to German Indochina and extensive mineral wealth.
§YBorn:§! §L1894, in Luoping, Yunnan Province, Great Qing§!\n§YBackground:§! §L$YUN_hu_ruoyu_bio$§!
§YBorn:§! §L1894, in Luoping, Yunnan Province, Great Qing§!\n§YAlignment:§! §PMengzi Clique§!\n§YBackground:§! §L$YUN_hu_ruoyu_bio$§!
Li Xuanting
§YBorn:§! §L1881, in Maguan County, Yunnan Province, Great Qing§!\n§YAlignment:§! §PMengzi Clique§!\n§YBackground:§! §LOne of the four previously big garrison commanders under Tang Jiyao, Li Xuanting graduated from the Kunming Military Academy and subsequently participated in the Double Ninth Uprising. Sent to serve in the Yunnan expedition to Sichuan, he would rise to become a battalion commander. In 1914 he returned to Yunnan to serve as a magistrate of Guangnan County and Jianshui County. Fighting in the Huguojun, he would rise to regiment commander.\n\nIn 1918 he became ingratiated directly with the Yunnan Military Governor's Office, and in 1919 served as Chief of Staff of the Second Yunnan Garrison District Headquarters. In 1920 he engaged in skirmishes and conflicts with the Yunnan bandits in the South, and through Gu Pinzhen's coup attempt served as prefect of Mengzi and chief-of-staff of Mengzi regional bandit command. In 1922 he was appointed as garrison commander of Dali for his efforts in returning Tang Jiyao to power, and in 1925 effectively became the warlord of Western Yunnan.\n\nFollowing Tang Jiyao's continued blunders, he conspired with Long Yun, Hu Ruoyu, and Zhang Ruji to depose Tang, but they were outmanoeuvred by the Governor. He then lost his fiefdom of Dali to Sun Du, and was confined to Mengzi. Retained onwards, he serves as commander of the Yunnan Army Military Police Headquarters.§!
Zhang Ruji
§YBorn:§! §L1896, in Qujing County, Yunnan Province, Great Qing§!\n§YAlignment:§! §PMengzi Clique§!\n§YBackground:§! §LGraduating from the Yunnan Army Primary School and Guizhou Army Military Academy, Zhang Ruji served as an assistant in the faculty of the Kunming Military Academy. Participating in the Double Ninth Uprising, he served as the platoon leader of the Yunnan Military Governor's Office, and participated in 1912 in the Yunnan Army's expedition to Guizhou. Entering the Guizhou Army Academy to study, he graduated in 1914 and returned to Yunnan.\n\nIn 1915 he was company commander of Tang Jiyao's personal guard, and fought in the National Protection War against Long Jiguang. In 1921 he continued his service during Gu Pinzhen's tenureship, fighting Shen Hongying in Guangxi. During the Constitutional Protection Headquarters, Zhang served as Commander-in-Chief of the Northern Expedition Advance Army Headquarters, and in 1922 returned to Yunnan under Tang Jiyao.\n\nServing as Commander of the Fourth Army of the Jingguojun, he became commander of the Yunnan Army Military Police Headquarters, and Garrison Commander of Southern Yunnan. In 1923 he was deputy envoy of the Yunnan-Guizhou Allied Forces, and followed Tang Jiyu to occupy Guiyang, serving as Co-Director of the Guizhou Military Reconstruction Supervisory Office. Fighting against Guangxi under the auspices of Tang Jiyao, he then became Garrison Commander of Eastern Yunnan in 1926. The next year, he launched a coup with Hu Ruoyu, Long Yun, and Li Xuanting in an attempt to depose Tang. Since the failure of the coup, he has been confined to Mengzi alongside Li and Hu.§!
Wang Weiyu
§YBorn:§! §L1892, in Mengzi, Yunnan Province, Great Qing§!\n§YAlignment:§! §PMengzi Clique§!\n§YBackground:§! §LWang Weiyu's father ran a small curreny exchange business in Mengzi, and as a result of his entrepreneurial spirit, Wang himself would open a cake and snack stand at the age of 15. Managing to open a mine with a business partner, Li Pin, their enterprise would rapidly grow in size from its founding in 1912 and became moderately successful. However, due to the increase in banditry in the South of Yunnan at the time, their operations were consistently threatened. To this effect, the local mining corporations banded together to form a militia, with Wang selected as their commander.\n\nFounding a series of mining schools and engaged in charity work, Wang is connected to German mining interests in southern Yunnan, and is a local kingpin of the area, even if fairly benevolent. He serves as a minor military officer, and a financial backer, of the Mengzi Clique.§!
Wang Ruwei
§YBorn:§! §L1894, in Kunming, Yunnan Province, Great Qing§!\n§YAlignment:§! §PMengzi Clique§!\n§YBackground:§! §LGraduating from the Yunnan Military Academy, Wang Ruwei would participate in the Double Ninth Uprising, and afterwards would fight in the National Protection War in Sichuan, serving as a staff officer of Li Xuanting's Army. In 1916 he would be admitted to the 11th Infantry Department of the Kunming Military Academy. After graduation in 1918 he would steadily rise up the ranks of the Yunnan Army, including a stint in Guangdong where he was accused by the Kuomintang government of attempting to aid Chen Jiongming in 1924.\n\nReturning to Yunnan, he developed his own local fiefdom in Maguan in Southeastern Yunnan. By 1927 he was chief of staff of the local Reconstruction Commissioner's office. Accused of recruiting local bandits to help him secure Wenshan and Xichou counties, he would ultimately be unsuccessful in controlling said bandits. When his brother betrayed him, he allegedly murdered him in cold blood, all the while he was solicited by Long Yun and Zhang Ruji to participate in their anti-Tang coup, accepting both of their offers simultaneously. Fleeing upon Long Yun's reported success (albeit short-lived), Wang would find himself within Hu Ruoyu's Mengzi Clique, either out of concern for his safety, or for opportunity.§!\n\n§YNotes: He blurs the line between Military Officer and local bandit.§!
Miao Yuntai
Studying English at the Kunming Dialect School in the late Qing, Miao Yuntai enrolled in a prep class for studying abroad after 1911, and received his tuition to study in the United States in 1913. Studying mining and metallurgy at Southwestern College in Kansas, and later the University of Minnesota, he graduated in 1918 and worked for a steel company in New York. In 1919 he returned to Yunnan, and soon after was appointed by Tang Jiyao as President of he Gejiu Tin Company, which suffered financial losses at the time. Miao managed to clear the debts and adopt modern Western technology, greatly improving the company.\n\nIn 1924 he served as the co-manager of the Yunnan Fudian Bank, and in 1928 he was appoitned as Director of the Provincial Agriculture and Mining Department, alongside a member of the Provincial Assembly. In 1930 he served as Chairman of the Yunnan Provincial Financial Rectification Committee, and in 1931 was the General Manager of the Yunnan Industrial Bank. Continuing to serve as Director of the Industry Department, Miao became general manager of the Yunnan Tin Refining Company, greatly improving the standards of Yunnan tin.\n\nIn 1934 he was appointed president of Fudian New Bank. There, he transformed the provincial financial system from one focused on opium to one focused on tin, and improved the currency of the Yunnan Provincial Banknote. Contributing to the development of local industry, Miao is the chief representative of Yunnan's financial interests.
§YBorn:§! §LMarch 23rd, 1894, in Kunming, Yunnan Province, Great Qing§!\n§YBackground:§! §L$YUN_miao_yuntai_bio$§!
§YBorn:§! §LMarch 23rd, 1894, in Kunming, Yunnan Province, Great Qing§!\n§YAlignment:§! §mChamber of Commerce§!\n§YBackground:§! §L$YUN_miao_yuntai_bio$§!\n\n§YNotes: Notably non-partisan.§!
Xu Zhichen
§YBorn:§! §Lin Yunnan Province, Great Qing§!\n§YAlignment:§! §LNeutral§!\n§YBackground:§! §LA key aficionado of the Yunnan provincial adminsitration, Xu Zhichen was appointed in 1916 as Mengzi Supervisor under Cai E, and rising within the ranks under Tang Jiyao, would be appointed Director of the Foreign Affairs Department and of the Finance Department. During the time of the Guangzhou Military Government, Xu Zhichen was sent there to maintain tabs and gather intelligence for Tang Jiyao, and upon his return was retained within the ministry. He is notable for being detested by members of the Yunnan Clique who seem him as a beneficiary of nepotism, or as a core of Tang Jiyao's clique, albeit whether that is truly the case is up to interpretation.§!
Zhang Weihan
§YBorn:§! §LDecember 29th, 1886, in Daguan County, Yunnan Province, Great Qing§!\n§YAlignment:§! §LNeutral§!\n§YBackground:§! §LGraduating from the Yunnan Public Law and Politics School, in 1908 Zhang Weihan would join the 37th brigade of the 19th Army Regiment, serving as a teacher and secretary. During the Double Ninth Uprising, he participated on the side of the revolutionaries, and joined the Kuomintang in 1912. Serving as a subordinate of Tang Jiyao, he would become head of the General Affairs Section of the Yunnan Provincial Administrative Office, and the local governor of Yanxing and Gejiu counties. In 1915 he would join the Huguojun and fight in the National Protection War agaisnt Long Jiguang.\n\nEntering Sichuan in 1916, he was appointed as the Secretary-General of the Sichuan Military Governor's Office, and returned to Yunnan in 1917. The following year he was sent by Tang to represent Yunnan in the Guangzhou Military Government, and later travelled to Japan to study administration at the Imperial University. in 1922 he returned to Yunnan and was appointed the Governor of Mengzi, and later the first head of the Kunming Municipal Office.\n\nContinuing to serve into the late 1920s and early 1930s, Zhang Weihan would see positions as Director of the Civil Affairs Department and Director of the Foreign Affairs Department. Compliant, skilled, and undoubtedly useful, Weihan is viewed positively by the majority of the Yunnan Clique, except for chiefly Lu Han.§!
Zhou Zhongyue
§YBorn:§! §LOctober 17th, 1876 in Jianchuan County, Yunnan Province, Great Qing§!\n§YAlignment:§! §LNeutral§!\n§YBackground:§! §LOf the Bai nationality, Zhou Zhongyue was born into a scholar's family, and passed the provincial exams in Lijiang and Dali, ranking first in both. In 1900 he graduated from Xiyun Academy in Dali and became a clerk under Zhao Fan, following him to Hunan and Sichuan. Returning 1903 he would once again ace the Provincial Exams, and would travel to Japan to study at government expense, entering Waseda University for Law and Politics. Serving as a bureaucrat in Yunnan Province, he would protest the unequal treaties, and would return to Yunnan in 1911 to serve as Secretary-General to Cai E.\n\nServing in various roles, he would become indispensable to Cai E, the civilian yin to the militaristic yang. When Cai E left Beijing in 1915 to launch the National Protection War, Zhou worked to cover his tracks, and travelled to Japan for his own safety. Travelling to Sichuan in 1916, he would serve as Luo Peijin's Secretary-General, and then in the same capacity for Tang Jiyao. In 1919 he was acting governor of Yunnan Province, and later official governor. In 1922 he was Yunnan Salt Transport Commissioner under Tang Jiyao, Privy Councilor, and Chairman of the Provincial Legal Committee.\n\nIn 1926 he became director of the Yunnan Provincial Internal Affairs Department, and briefly mediating inner-Yunnan Clique politics, he would retire. In 1930 he became Director of the Yunnan General History Museum, and compiled provincial history. Recalled in 1934 by Tang Jiyao, he serves as the Yunnanese Civil Affairs Minister.§!
Li Wenyi
§YBorn:§! §L1903, in Wuhan, Hubei Province, Great Qing§!\n§YAlignment:§! §LNeutral§!\n§YBackground:§! §LGraduating from Hubei Provincial Women's Normal School in 1924, Li Wenyi would join the Chinese Syndicalist Party and the Kuomintang in March 1926, serving in the underground Hubei Provincial Committee of the Kuomintang. Especially engaged with the women's liberation movement, she organised and led the work of the Hubei Provincial Women's Association. Member of the Women's Committee of the Chinese Syndicalist Party, she would leave the country in 1928 to study at Sun Yat-sen University in France, and would return to serve as a member of the Shanghai Hudong District Committee of the Chinese Syndicalist Party in the 1930s.\n\nSlowly becoming enwrapped with Third Way politics following the Shanghai Uprising, she would informally associate herself with Zhang Junmai's National Renaissance Society, the Crescent Moon Society, Rural Reconstruction Movement, Political Science Clique, and all other Third Way parties or associations. A leading Feminist in the Chinese women's struggle for equality, she moved to Kunming to establish a provincial women's organisation there, based off of her work in Hubei.§!
Lu Daoyuan
§YBorn:§! §L1900, in Changning County, Yunnan Province, Great Qing§!\n§YAlignment:§! §LNeutral§!\n§YBackground:§! §LGraduating from the 13th Infantry Department of the Kunming Military Academy, Lu Daoyuan would serve in the Fourth Guard Regiment of the Jingguojun from 1922 to 1927. Promoted to Battalion commander, he would circumnavigate Yunnan Clique politics to position himself as a Pro-Long Yun Pro-Tang Jiyao member of the clique. Opposed to Zhang Ruji, and Hu Ruoyu's ambitions. Promoted to commander of the Fourth Guard Regiment in 1928, he would become a brigadier general that same year.\n\nEngaging in multiple skirmishes through the late 1920s, Lu has exhibited extraordinary wit and bravery in light of severe challenges in the Yunnan Army, and is noted for his severe propensity to steel-willed determination in light of overwhelming odds.§!
Liao Xingchao
Writing poems under his uncle's stewardship, Liao Xingchao graduated from the Kunming Junior Normal School in 1911. Enwrapped in the Xinhai revolution, he would abandon a career in literature and join the military, serving in a student volunteer dare-to-die squad. Admitted to the forth infantry class of the Yunnan Military Academy, he became classmates and subsequently close friends with Long Yun. Graduating in 1914, he was assigned to serve under Zhu De. Fighting in the National Protection War, he was promoted at its conclusion for his meritorious service.\n\nServing as chief of staff of Jin Handing in 1918, he stayed on the sidelines during Gu Pinzhen's coup in 1920. Under Yang Ruxuan, he then fought in the Yunnan Army in Guangdong against Chen Jiongming, in 1922. In 1924 he he became a sworn brother of Liao Zhongkai, but later that same year would leave China to study in France, entering the history department of Lyon University. Meeting there the future founders of the Young China Party, Zeng Qi and Li Huang, he became affiliated with their young ideology.\n\nReturning to China in 1925, he had to flee to Vietnam following oppression from Chiang Kai-shek, and then travelled to Japan to study in 1927. Returning to the country in 1935, he was appointed as Long Yun's Chief of Staff, although he is not particularly attached to his clique.
§YBorn:§! §LFebruary 12th, 1895 in Huaning County, Yunnan Province, Great Qing§!\n§YBackground:§! §L$YUN_liao_xingchao_bio$§!
§YBorn:§! §LFebruary 12th, 1895 in Huaning County, Yunnan Province, Great Qing§!\n§YAlignment:§! §nYoung China Party§!\n§YBackground:§! §L$YUN_liao_xingchao_bio$§!\n\n§YNotes: He is a close friend of Long Yun, and of Jin Handing.\nConsidered to be the leader of the Yunnan Provincial YCP branch.§!
Liu Xianqian
§YBorn:§! §LDecember 28th, 1864 in Xingyi County, Guizhou Province, Great Qing§!\n§YAlignment:§! §LIndependent§!\n§YBackground:§! §LCousin of Liu Xianshi, Liu Xianqian was admitted to a county school as the son of a bureaucratic family, but after a horrible string of academic failures, he instead idled at home. In 1902 he served in a local militia to repel Guangxi bandits, and was promoted to the rank of captain by the Qing. In 1911 he was appointed as commander of the Guangxi Frontier Defence Army by Governor Shen Bingkun, and fought the revolutionaries against the Qing, actively working with his cousin to do so.\n\nPromoted during Tang Jiyao's takeover of Guizhou, he became a local warlord, and rose through the ranks until 1915. With the National Protection War in full swing, Liu Xianqian would would reluctantly fight on the side of the NPA. Embroiled in the Guizhou factional struggle of the late 1910s, Xianqian followed his brother to Yunnan after the Minjiu Incident. Returning in 1922 alongside his cousin and Yuan Zuming, the Lius would be betrayed by Yuan, and so would side with Tang Jiyao for their reinstatement.\n\nOusting Yuan with Tang Jiyu in 1923, Xianqian would become acting governor in 1924. Aiding Tang Jiyao in invading Liangguang in 1925, his forces would be blunted by the New Guangxi Clique, and his homebase would be reinvaded by Yuan Zuming. Announcing his resignation, Liu retired to Xingyi, and following the chaos in the Southwest and the manouvring of the Kuomintang into Guizhou, was recalled into action by Tang Jiyao.§!
Liu Peiquan
§YBorn:§! §L1893, in Nanhai County, Guangdong Province, Great Qing§!\n§YAlignment:§! §LNeutral§!\n§YBackground:§! §LApplying to the Zengbu Vocational School and accepted in 1910, Liu Peiquan befriended there Chen Ce and Zhang Fakui - with future observations being made that the three individuals represented the Navy, Army, and Air Force of China respectively. In 1915, Liu worked as a reporter for Juehun Daily, reporting on the flying performances of Chinese-American pilot Tan Gen, and he subsequently became his secretary. In 1920, he was hired by Tang Jiyao as Director of the new Yunnan Aviation Department, and to serve as principal of the aviation school and senior councilor of the Yunnan Army Headquarters.\n\nIn 1922 he recruited a group of aviation personnel from Guangdong to Kunming, ordered six aircraft from Hong Kong, and set up the foundations for the establishment of the Yunnan Air Force. In 1926, he was briefly appointed as the Air Force Commander of the Eastern Route Army of the National Revolutionary Army, but would soon defect back to the Yunnan Clique following NRA failures on the field. In 1930, he served in the Preparatory Office of China Airlines, and then organised the Southwest Civil Aviation Company in 1933.§!
Oversized Military
While it has provided us with one of the best armies in Southern China, it is clear that the provincial government's heavy focus on the development of the military is unsustainable. If policy is to be continued, Yunnan's civilian populace and industry will continue to suffer.

Despite many improvements in handling provincial economics, the economy is still under heavy strain from previous heavy military spending. However, due to current circumstances the government can ill afford to decrease the size of the military any further. Until the political situation changes, the people of Yunnan will have to endure hardship.

With the provincial government's recent investments, the worst of Black Monday's damage has been mitigated. China itself is forever uniquely positioned to achieve a revolution to a class-led society without having to use class-warfare. If we are to restore the harmony between class and society, we must first restore mutual respect and self-love for neighbour and workplace.

National Health Administration
The brainchild of the Ministry of the Health, the National Health Administration is an ambitious attempt to institute an effective and proactive national health service across the country. With its ambitious Three Year Plan underway, the NHS plans to expand dramatically its medical facilities in the form of rural health stations and clinics in order to greatly improve the quality of life for the people in this country. Particular effort is drawn to enhancing the quality of life for women and young children, in order to provide much needed material and feminine medical care to the rural countryside.
The New Life Movement
As the Nationalist Government now controls the Chinese country and has completed the Northern Expedition, we must seek to regenerate and rejuvenate the energy by reviving the morality of Confucianism and by prescribing quaint rules of personal conduct. This is Confucianism for the masses, devoid of philosophical subtleties, stripped to its ethical essentials, a sloganised Confucianism that would ensure the consumption of the four virtues: property, justice, honesty, and self-respect. Through the 96 rules governing daily behavior, the Chinese people ought to be instructed on how to be prompt, button their clothes, stand straight, and not eat nosily. To restore our nation and gain revenge for our humiliations, then we need not talk about guns and cannon, but must first talk about washing our faces in cold water. In doing so, we will be able to transform the inner man and fabricate a new society and a stronger nation.

We should encourage women to be dutiful, to be the new matriarchs of innocent and revolutionary families, and to help participate in the war effort.

Autonomous Armies
For all of his posturing, rallying, and shaking of fists, Tang Jiyao's hold on Yunnan's ever ferocious militarism is fragile, and brittle. Deposed by Gu Pinzhen in the early 1920s, Tang Jiyao's influence never recovered, and control of his armies slowly slipped out of his grasp. Increasingly reliant on bandit armies, and increasingly dejected by his closest commanders such as Long Yun, or Hu Ruoyu, the state of the once proud Yunnan Army is that of a sorry one. Local garrison commanders and generals alike run their fractured units like fiefdoms based on personal loyalties, no longer swearing true fealty to the provincial governor. Run amok, the King of the Southwest is nothing more than the King of Kunming, and even that is debatable.

Legacy of the Huguojun
The Huguojun, or National Protection Army, were the great saviours of the Republic in its time of great struggle against Imperial Ambitions. The perfidious Yuan Shikai, sought to tear up the hard fought values of the Xinhai Revolution, trample over the constitution, and declare himself as the Hongxian Emperor. Yet the brave leaders of Yunnan stood defiant against his desire for autocracy. Liang Qichao, Cai E, Tang Jiyao, Li Liejun, Yang Sen, all of these brave men are heroes of modern China, having defied the odds against the Central Government's army, triumphing against the likes of Wu Peifu, Cao Kun and Feng Yuxiang.\n\nWithout a doubt, their brave victories could not have been so swift, so hard fought, if it was not for the superior training, vigour, and dedication of the graduates of Kunming Military Academy. Once again a time of great conflict is upon the nation, and the leadership of Yunnan must again march to save the nation. Recalling the glory days of the Huguojun, the Yunnan Army is focused on excellent drill, officers and command, rather than superior armaments. For if one thing is for certain, a devout militaristic warrior spirit can triumph even the most well equipped division.

Rejuvenation of National Struggle
With the restoration of the Kuomintang to power, the National Revolutionary Army has been granted a second lease on life. With the influence of both the party and Whampoa cliques, a new strain of military thought has been born from the failures of the Northern Expedition and realities of the situation. With their enemies far more well equipped, well supplied, and without a doubt more numerous, the Kuomintang have decided to use their greatest strength: the Chinese people themselves. By focusing on waging a people's war, the National Revolutionary Army will become a true conduit for the people's rage.\n\nFocused on agile irregular units, combined with guerilla harassment campaigns, and using every possible avenue to pitch battles and engagements in their favour, the National Revolutionary Army has focused on waging warfare in the most pragmatic fashion possible. Idolising not control of cities or villages, but instead total control for a total victory, the NRA hopes to elude their more centralised opponents, and out manoeuvre them with mobile warfare tactics.

Baring our Teeth
The Yunnan Army may be down, but it is not fully out. With limited capacity to still propel our influence out of our de facto borders, it is time for the beast to reawaken, to renew its arsenals, and show everyone why Yunnan has been the Kingmaker of the Southwest for the past decades.
To Be Rid of Personalism
Our institutions and armies are rife with decay, rot and corruption. A fate befallen by all warlord armies, the Yunnan Army bureaucracy thought itself immune to such encroachment by paltry forces at play, and yet the current state of the army is evidence enough of our fall from grace. To excise such rot, it is necessary to once again reimplement the frequent rotations of command, mandate continued education and study within Kunming, and consolidate our high command staff, removing those who were granted titles to alleviate their political ambitions.
Kunming Military Academy
Organised by Hu Jingyi on behalf of Governor Wang Wenshao, the Yunnan Military Academy was formally promulgated in August 1909, with the first general manager being Gao Erdeng, and the first principal being Li Genyuan. With Li consolidating his role by taking over as general manager, he would shape the course of the institution into that mirroring the Shikan Gakko, or Imperial Japanese Army Academy. Prioritising hard, extensive training, the academy has produced some of the best military minds of this era of warfare, and is considered one of the \

Ensuring Ideological Cohesion
To much of our dismay, the Yunnan Army is a hotbed of combative ideological adherents. Those self-serving, those beholden to the ideals of the Three Principles, to Republicanism, those apolitical. However first and foremost, the Yunnan Army is a military force, and should function on the basis that it is destined to achieve military goals to carry forth political ones. Undergoing a period of ideological pruning may be detrimental to our overall command, but in the long term it will ensure that the political prospects of our government are carried without a hitch.
Handling Over-glorification
With its tenure of activity, the Yunnan Army has become but a paper facade of its glory days. More focused on pompous displays of arrogance, parade marches, spiffy uniforms, and goose-step rather than field training and drills. If we are to return to the position of national champions, it is time to put away the military dress, the medals, and once again return to the down-to-earth reputation of our forebears.
Dare to Die Corps
Salvation does not come to those who wait but rather to those who are willing to give it all, their livelihoods, their families, and most of all their lives for the freedom and liberty of China. As the bugle sounds and the Yunnanese soldier rises to take his stand in this pivotal moment of Chinese history, bold and courageous individuals should seek to form dare to die corps in the mold of the martyrs of the Double Ninth Uprising, so that they too can offer their lives in sacrifice for a free China.
To Bear Sacrifices
If we are to truly march for the Chinese nation and its institutions, there is no greater strength for the military man to hold than the capacity to endure suffering. For if we can endure suffering, we cannot lose, and victory is but an assurances. Instilling this spirit not just in our military leaders, but in our populace too, we can bring forth China's own inherent Bushido, and become unconquerable.
Political Commissars
Beholden to an ever expanding army, and with increasing recruitment outside of Yunnan, the political outlook of our army grows increasingly muddied. Whilst we will not bend to the pure political indoctrination witnessed in party state armies such as the NRA, by enlisting and assigning a series of Political Commissars to units, we can ensure that the new Huguojun retains its course. Never shall we fall to self-serving corruption for as long as China believes in the might of its army, and if China is indeed to be saved by the army, then the education of the masses must begin with the armed forces - so that they will be truly an army dedicated to the cause of the militaristic nation-state.
Exalt the Military Victory
The Huguojun had one goal in mind when they declared their crusade against Yuan Shikai: military victory. For with military victory, their political goals can be instated upon the whole nation. Such unrelenting devotion to military success saw our men sacrifice everything to contribute towards it, and if we are to succeed in China's Darkest Hour, it will be up to our men to once again perish for the good of the nation.
Seizing our Rightful land
For too long have the Burmese held onto rightful Chinese land, granted due to the weakness of the decadent Qing. If we are to be the true protectors of China, we must unite all of it, no matter how small. The Yellow Book Affair, as it has become to be known, is perhaps scoffed at by foreign powers, but for us Chinese, it is merely setting things right.
Officer's Prerogative
Emphasising the initiative and command capacity of lower level commanders within the Yunnan Army, the military high command has granted them the ability to discern the best course of action to achieve their tactical military goals in line with the overall strategic goal. Our officers are our strongest asset, and it would be wise to trust their judgement.
People's Arms Manufacturing
Evidently, the Chinese people rush to support our armies every step of the way, however our men still lack guns and armaments. If we are to truly stand up as the defenders of the people and their rights, we must mobilise them not just in the fields but the factories also. For every bullet made, is another oppressor of the people's will vanquished.
Frontlines, Everywhere
It is not just enough for potential invaders to have to engage our military forces in direct combat, for they must also deal with the mobilisation of guerilla units in their backlines. The deeper they march into our territory, the increasingly fragile their supply lines become, and the bolder our guerillas become. Continuously, we will wage a complete and utter people's war against invaders.
Yunnanese Auftragstaktik
Auftragstaktik, or mission-type tactics, is a method of command practised by delegating junior military leaders a clearly-defined strategic objective, timeframe, and the necessary forces to complete said objective, whilst leaving the execution and best application of resources provided to their diligence. With our military theorists having studied the Weltkrieg, the superior performance of the German Imperial Army was in-part due to their delegation of command. To replicate their successes, we shall employ our own level of lower officer autonomy.
Hundred Regiments Offensive
A campaign masterminded by our greatest military theorists and strategists, the military high command has drafted an ambitious and extensive plan requiring only one hundred regiments. With one hundred regiments, our army can annihilate any invader or opponent, no matter their strength. Launching forward the great might and spirit of the Chinese nation, our enemies are sure to cower in utter terror.
