Returning to the Revolution's Root
The Kuomintang was first and foremost a movement borne from the desires and writings of intellectuals to transform and transition their beloved nation from a decadent dynasty towards a radiant future. Through the increasing trials and tribulations it would appear that the Party has lost its foundings, losing its lustre amongst intellectuals. Extending a hand to China's greatest thinkers, we can rebuild our previously close ties.
Calling on Shanghai Finance
Whilst Shanghai is a haven for crooks and gangsters, it remains one of the revolution's strongholds even in spite of the repression of the Shanghai Uprising. With stock brokers and revolutionary connections to the Green Gangs built upon the foundations of the Shanghai Kuomintang brothers of Chen Guofu and Chen Lifu, let us tap into their extensive networks to finance the National Revolution.
Constructing Kunming University
With the ever increasing demands upon the education sector of the government's administration, the Kunming Normal School has perhaps reached its capacity to foment Yunnan's new generation of thinkers and workers. By expanding its campus and constructing further departments and institutions we shall transform Kunming into an intellectual hub in the Southwest for generations.
Kicking up the Whirlwind
Perhaps many would consider us playing second fiddle to the greater ambitions of the Central Committee, however even [ROOT.GetLeader] and the provincial branch of the Kuomintang can pull on the right levers and pulleys within the Central Organs of the Party to ensure preferable influences arise. Tapping into mutual ties and connections, Yunnan shall bear its teeth even the broader political battleground.
Challenge the False Pretenders
For too long the Central Committee has abused the people and their trust, taking so much but granting so little in return. Neither stability nor a rice bowl, not even any of its revolutionary promises or protection from warlords and imperialists. All around us the dimming hope in China's National Revolution continues to progress. To prevent a complete abrogation of our revolutionary mission, we must complete the most grave sin...
Seizing the Revolution
Ultimately the banners of the Right Kuomintang have fallen to the wayside, be it through our own influence or another. Whilst noble in his efforts as Dr. Sun's most studious disciple, Hu Hanmin ultimately shackled himself to regionalists and warlords that would've seen the revolution torn asunder. Now with the demise of the old Central Committee, a new, proper Kuomintang must arise.
Bringing Sanmin to the Masses
The Chinese people have lamented under the warlords and imperialists for so long that very few interpret or understand the Kuomintang's own regime to be anything new; viewing the Party as simply the Beiyang system in a revolutionary coat of paint. If we are to be earnest and sincere, we must approach the people on common terms, not as despots lording over them but as the steadfast hand aiming for their liberation.
Enforcing Guiyang's Compliance
Enduring for decades now, the fate of Guizhou has long been chained to that of Yunnan. Whilst there remained Guizhou cliques in of themselves that would rather shirk the King of the Southwest, ultimately geography and proximity has bound them to Kunming one way or another. To ensure one united nation, the disbarment between Qian and Dian must end, for the nation unites us all.
Eject the Wayward Despots
Our battles are hard fought, our campaign is merciless, and our soldiers endeavour to fight even as their officers are honeycombed in a hail of bullets. Yet we must press on! Ever forward towards the total victory! For the nation cannot rest nor can it be united until the despots are deposed and responsible governance is restored!
Summoning the Spirit of the People
Truthfully we are not alone in the struggle against tyrants and revolutionary autocrats that would see the National Revolution swept up in its own nonsensical fury. All throughout the nation the people's spirit laments the injustices imposed upon them. We must merely endeavour to seize their hearts with our cause.
Heir to the National Construction Army
Our most primary force, the Third Army of the National Revolutionary Army, owes its true ancestry to the legacy of the National Protection Wars and the resulting heroes of the Yunnan Army in Guangdong. Morphing from the Second Huguojun into the Jianguo Dian Jun and then the Third Army, it has continued the spirit of its predecessors into the new era.
Heeding the Call of Revolution
This war is not merely one driven by the nostalgia of an era gone by, or due to hesitancy towards the violent nature of a nation's revolution. It is a people's war against a system that has spiralled out of their control, a war against the forces of entropy itself! All must rise and heed the call of the true White Sun, for the fate of the very nation is at stake.
Policy of Yunnan's Thermidor
The radicals within the Kuomintang have for too long dominated the captaincy of the National Revolution, and for all of their supposed triumphs the Chinese nation has wrought nothing but ruin. To restore stability to the Chinese nation, Yunnan must assume the role that Cai E bestowed upon it all those years ago.
Disseminating Our Message
Our brave act of defiance could not have been willed into existence without our ties to the business world, to the journalists, and to our brave compatriots on the other side that continue to champion our cause. Whilst we focus on the military action to back our claims, let us allocate resources to their endeavours to win the civilian hearts too.
Last Hurrah of the Model Erzhu
Without [ROOT.GetLeader], would the Party have reached the heights of the present? Without [ROOT.GetLeader], would the nation be this close to national unification? And yet it would appear that this Model Erzhu is doomed to meet his end. Whilst the Southwest still roars, let us carry on to the bitter end!
Awakening of the New Right
Besieged by all sides by inefficient bureaucrats, counter-revolutionaries, socialist traitors, and other corrupt and degenerative elements of party decay - the Chinese Kuomintang has long found its revolutionary fibres torn at the seams by those who seek to bring its destruction. It is under the leadership of the esteemed Dai Jitao, that the Party will seek a pure true reorganisation.
Rein in the Generals
Since the ascension of President Dai Jitao and his new reorganised government of the Kuomintang, unrest has spread throughout the ranks of the National Revolutionary Army as supporters of former Generalissmo He Yingqin, clamor for his return to national politics. In order to dispell rumours of government instability and a potential military coup, we shall seek to curb their power and influence within national politics.
Press on with Nationalisation
The turbulence following the defeat of the Whampoa Clique and the ascension of President Dai Jitao and Premier Chen Guofu has led to many foreign investors withdrawing their investments and a general economic downturn throughout the country. In accordance with the principles of Minsheng, the President has ordered executive action to continue the nationalisation process in order to create a self-sufficient economy.
The Singular Organ of Economic Society
Tending to the nation requires one to imagine the nation-state, as a body - with its brain and eyes leading the direction of the great Republic. In order to channel and control all economic activity in a means of which is productive and beneficial to the national reconstruction, we shall seek to merge unions and organisations under collective management in order to foster true class collaboration.
Dismantle Reactionary Degeneracy
So-called administrators and bureaucrats of the former corrupt Beiyang regime along with the liberal factions from and outside the Kuomintang must be dealt with as their presence throws a reactionary shadow over the nature and course of the revolution. If we continue to let this poison fester and grow, it will no doubt lead to the corruption and destruction of the revolution that we have fought so hard to achieve.
Purify the Party Organ
Impurities must be cleansed, society must draw itself closer to the ideal perfection that was envisioned by the great sages of old such as Confucius. In times of disorder, why must we continue to allow these imperfections to continue to delay and discourage the national revolution. It is only by purifying the party and cleansing it of counter-revolutionary degenerates, may we be able to bring a natural progression of the revolution.
The Scourge of Marxism
Since its arrival to Chinese shores, Marxism has brought nothing but counter-revolutionary and corrupt imperialist thoughts sent abroad by the social-imperialist nations of Britain and France. Marx's words do not resound with the traditional aesthetics of Chinese political ethos but rather they derive nothing more than struggle and disunity between the classes.
The Way for the Youth
Nowadays young men and women waste their lives, indulge themselves in pointless luxuries, and engage in unfruitful ambitions - completely deprived of any revolutionary sentiment. If the revolution is to continue to progress, it must be under the guide and vanguard of the Party to teach and to guide the youth of the nation, to prepare and train themselves to become true stewards of Dr. Sun's Chinese National Revolution.
Rehabilitate the General Staff
While we may have opposed He Yingqin's clique of military oligarchs and psuedo-warlords, we also do understand that the revolution could not have been won had it not been for the sacrifices and accomplishments of Whampoa's National Revolutionary Army. As the army will be put under the control of the National Government henceforth, we shall seek to pardon and grant amnesty to our comrades in the revolution.
Preliminary Plan for Reform of Education
In this country, far too many eager young students harken towards the unnecessary subjects of law and the humanities, while true modernisation begins with curriculum focusing on the engineering and technical fields. If we are to revitalise our country's youth into the new bulwark of the revolution, it is impertinent that we reform the schools and the universities to propagate a curriculum that will benefit the new modern Chinese state.
Restore Social Harmony
The success of the national revolution hedges not on the brilliant leadership of President Dai and his Premier Chen Guofu, but rather on the collective emplacement of harmonic values, expressed by the sages of Confucius. We must reject the hedonistic individualism of Taoism and embrace a collective society, such is the natural progression of mankind so that all under heaven will belong to all.
Create the Chinese Engineering Society
As part of ongoing National Reconstruction efforts, the American educated engineer Chen Lifu has created a sixteen point programme for Industrial Reconstruction which is to be led by the Chinese Engineering Society. Featuring experts from around the nation who have pledged their loyalty and service to the Kuomintang cause, the society seeks to help accelerate the Chinese industrialisation process under an enlightened leadership.
Expand the Central Banking System
Despite nominal national unification, the process of uniting the country under one currency under the new Republic still remains a graet challenge. At the direction of the Central Committee, the government is to expand the Central Banking System and to encourage state-capitalist principles in order to avoid foreign loans and to seek better trade and investment deals so that China may finally rise from the chains of imperialism.
Control the Means of Media
Man will always be curious but the people know best when they are shown only the truth. At the direction of the Central Committee, national broadcasting efforts under the China Central Broadcasting System are to increase - with a greater emphasis on national and patriotic music and news to rally the Chinese people and to strengthen the principles of Nationalism.
One Doctrine, One Party
The Kuomintang is the beloved creation of the dear late revolutionary and eternal premier of the party Dr. Sun Yat-sen, but since the collapse of the Northern Expedition - it has become a hideous shadow of itself. Infilitrated by counter-revolutionary liberals, socialist agitators, and warlords; we must continue to enforce that the doctrine of Dai Jitao is indeed the correct means to revive the National Revolution.
The Mother of Enterprises
In order to resurrect the Chinese motherland to her former glory, it is impertinent that we utilise the mother of enterprises; that is constructing new and vast networks of rail to connect the great nation together. With the rail networks finally under Chinese control, expanding the Chinese rail will improve trade, communications, and lead to growth amongst the cities after years of abject poverty.
Organise the Military Engineering Corps
As part of the Chinese Engineering Society's ambitious efforts to expand the nation's infrastructure and industries, the society's president, Chen Lifu, has ordered the creation of the Miltiary Engineering Corps. This organisation within the National Revolutionary Army will focus on constructing military infrastructure, forts, and coastal defences to strengthen the national state.
Tend to the National Beehive
President Dai Jitao has written often on the nation being a beehive analogy. In a beehive, the bees know their place, and do not question orders - rather they merely follow the queen. They delight in the sharing of their resources and within them, there is no struggle but unity. It is under this doctrine, that the Chinese people must understand, that the Party's leadership is to protect and guide them to a truly revolutionary future.

The Doctrine of National Servitude

We must embrace a concept of a productive society, a society in which obedience is understood, and that all work together hand in hand for the revitalisation of the modern Chinese nation.
Champion the National Dharma
While Buddhism has long had a turbulent history of both rise and decline throughout Chinese society, one cannot deny that Buddhism strengthens a nation as seen by the success of the Meiji Restoration and the anti-colonial rebellion of Anagarika Dharmapāla in Ceylon. China must also channel this concept of national Dharma and Buddhism to evolve and strengthen the moral character of the nation.
Curtail Internal Disunity
Scorned and treated as a radical idealogue among the Kuomintang's Old Right and seen as a reactionary from the Left, despite his great struggles - it was President Dai who has emerged successful in bringing Dr. Sun's revolution to its true path and orientation. The dissenting voices and vocal opponents of the regime must be dealt with and resolved so that true progress and reorganisation can begin.
Apostle of the Cultural Revolution
For years, Dai Jitao has written extensively and challenged the Kuomintang to return to her true roots and to embrace a revival for a new China, one that harkens to her traditional values and yet seeks a true anti-imperialist modern revolution. As a harbringer of reaction, the President now stands as the party's foremost idealogue - the true apostle of the late Dr. Sun Yat-sen's teachings.
Cardinal Principles and Eight Virtues
The four cardinal principles of properity, righteousness, integrity, and shame along with the eight virtues of fillal piety, loyalty, benevolence, harmony, love, justice, honesty, and peace have long been forgotten in this country as corrupt practices and beleifs have steered the people away. It is time for China to reawaken to her traditional past and to make right for the future generations to know.
Transfuse Western Sciences
While the spiritual sciences may be what Chinese civilisation has been an expert at it for the past centuries, there is merit if not outright benefit in adopting the sciences of the West without embracing their moral decay. Western modernisation and practices ought to be transfued into the very bloodstream of the Chinese nation, so that it may allow the revolutionary heart to beat ever faster.
Embrace the Spiritual Sciences
The moral decay of this nation lies in the corrupt practices of Western moral character which has destroyed and laid utter waste to morals and relationships. In these times of chaos and anarchy, it is impertinent that we look not to the future but rather the past for guidance, the nation from the peasant to the soldier must seek to follow the great moral principles of the days of Qin to revive Zhonghua.
Revitalise Filal Piety
It is the natural cycle of life that parents care for their children, and when they grow old - it is the children who care for their parents. Parents ought to disclipine their children in any means possible as a means of love and growth so that their children will learn to respect this order. Through this order, we may birth a society built on hierarchy and order - reviving societal harmony and ending class struggle.
Towards Revolutionary Datong
When an engineer wants the steamship to go, he turns the engine on ; when he wants the engine to stop, he turns the engine off. It is under great revolutionary leadership that the Chinese National Revolution will harken towards the utopia that is described by not just Sun Yat-sen but also by sages such as Confcius. For it was Sun, who truly understood and became the natural successor to the great Confucius in seeking the Great Harmony for all.
Whampoa's Legacy Lives on!

Reigning supreme without the perfidious civilians, the Academy's students have large shoes to fill.
The Militarisation of Education
Truly the Chinese people do not respect or appreciate the role of the military vanguard. In spite of all that we have won for them, they still view us as nothing more than warlords draped in a White Sun. Transforming high schools and universities into bootcamps, we shall instill respect for authority, critical thinking skills, foreign languages, science and maths into China's future generations.
Preserving Social Order
For far too long has chaos reigned in perpetuity. With previous Central Governments unable to exert much influence outwith their direct control, the nation has remained disunited. At least until now. Albeit the people are still restless, for they revolt against our very functions of governance. To revive China, we must enter the streets and restore order. By force, if necessary.
The Fight Against Inflation
Truly quite disastrous, the Chinese economy is a pitiful maze of misfortune and grievance. Despite marginal strides made in more prosperous coastal regions, none could withstand the impact that a war of national salvation could have on the Chinese nation. Despite being militarists, the Whampoa Clique are most certainly not economically blind. The people ail, and we must be their salvation.
Disciplined Men of Action
Above all else, the Kuomintang's mission is a modern, prosperous, and advanced China. One that is morally pure, free of vices, and practices self-dscipline to the utmost. A political revolution, no, a National Revolution, shall advance both the nation and its people. Yet its primary function will be that to benefit the modernisation and strength of China's military.
A Second Reorganisation
Under the rule of Generalissimo He Yingqin, the KMT was a hodgepodge of various groups and factions, and Party bureaucracy found itself bloated, with superfluous political councils working in tandem with Party subcommittees. If Chen Cheng is to lead the KMT and China to a new and better future, the work must begin with the abolishment of the Political Consultative Council in favour of the Central Reorganisation Committee.
Refining the Organic Economy
Tutelage seeks not only to educate the Chinese people on the values of the Three Principles, but also shepard the economy to a new age of prosperity and equity. Under the disastrous rule of He Yingqin, the Chinese nation suffered through rampant inflation and bourgeois intransigence. The levers of the economy must be put in the hands of capable revolutionaries, no longer the plaything of opportunists and counter revolutionaries.
Economic Centralisation
With a nation as large as China, and various natural barriers lying within her borders, it's no surprise that political and economic autonomy was rife in far flung regions. The National Government must control all aspects of the economy in the name of the National Revolution. With centralised control, the Nationalist government will be able to properly regulate as necessary, as well as defend from foreign economic imperialism.
Sanminzhuyi Youth League
If the doctrine of the KMT is to resonate among the populace, the youth of China must properly learn what the National Revolution truly stands for. The Sanminzhuyi Youth League will see to it that China's youngsters will learn, live, and breathe the Three Principles in Chen's eyes. The cacophony in the KMT will cease as the young rise to replace the old guard of the Party and assist the Generalissimo in the true work of the National Revolution.
Curtailing Gentry Oppression
In the late Qing Dynasty and early Republican Era, inequality was strife across all of China. The National Government shall adopt measures to curtail the gentry via rent control, tax reductions of produce, and various anti-inflation measures. The National Revolution cannot hope to succeed if the peasantry are not liberated, and Chen Cheng will see to it personally that every landlord and their CC handlers are brought to heel!
Reiterating Minsheng
When the Guofu first began his political career, he developed the revolutionary tenets of the Three Principles of the People, Minzu, Minquan, and Minsheng. Most important to the Generalissimo is Minsheng, people's livelihood. Generalissimo Chen was born into a struggling peasant family, and thus understands the plight of people most acutely. There can be no mistaking that Minsheng is of prime importance in our Revolution!
Institute of Revolutionary Practice
Under the old Party of He Yingqin, various counter-revolutionary groups such as the CC Clique and Political Science Clique were allowed to subvert the National Revolution without impunity. Generalissimo Chen Cheng's Kuomintang will not repeat old mistakes, and the Institute of Revolutionary Practice will make certain that newcomers to the Party never lose sight of the true goals of our National Revolution.
Rebuilding Party Cells
As the Party grows larger, the need for the revitalisation of the Party Cell system increases. The Party Cell network will root itself in the government, military, and military, and all members of the party will be mandated to join one and have their loyalty assessed. The cells will emphasise democratic centralism as the ideological guide to policy, and hierarchical authority, and Party authority will be reinstituted, in the name of the Generalissimo.
Bridging the Factional Divide
During the Northern Expedition, the KMT fractured into two halves. Wang and the Central Committee drifted towards socialism, while the forces loyal to Chiang combined with Hu Hanmin's supporters coalesced in opposition. This tragic division must be rectified, and Chen Cheng is uniquely fit for the task. As a student of both Deng Yanda and Chiang Kai-shek, the Generalissimo is capable of rallying the support of both wings.
Heir to the Red Napoleon
Although Deng Yanda ultimately failed to secure the nation, his legacy lives on. Chen Cheng will ensure that the KMT keeps its focus on the peasantry, and the dream of Datong is renewed. The Party must devote itself to the masses of China above all else, for the relationship of the KMT to the masses is that of a fish and water: the fish swims through the water and can't survive without it. In Deng's name, Chen Cheng will fight for the people!
All Forward with the Little Generalissimo!
The Chinese people were on the cusp of salvation when Generalissimo Chiang took charge of the Party. Tragically, German treachery halted the revolution and destroyed the KMT. Against all odds, the Party has not only returned, but triumphed thanks to the stewardship of Chiang's most faithful student, Chen Cheng. The Little Generalissimo will finish what Chiang started and deliver the nation to a bright and prosperous future!
Joint Commission on Rural Reconstruction
With the first stage of Revolution completed, the Nationalist Party must begin the process of reconstruction. The Chinese nation finds itself in tatters following the wars of unification and the war of survival against the Japanese aggressors. The countryside's devastation requires the Party to establish a rural commission, which will see the rural regions not only restoration of pre war conditions, but the liberating effects of capable governance.
Formalise the Government
For too long has the Party ruled rather irrationally. Through decree, rather than consensus. Through arbitrary mediation, and the decision of an elite few rather than the popular masses. Whilst democracy is far off, and with that a constitution, we can at least implement a semi-structured form of governance to end the inanity of the Party State. With it, China will find proper governance.
Broaden the Social Base
In order to properly alleviate the suffering of the people, the Party must understand society better. Party Work must see members investigate local economics and social conditions that may create socialist sympathy. The party too must open its doors further to the lower classes. Farmers, workers, and intellectuals must be enticed into the Party so that our National Revolution is truly a party for the workers and peasants.
A Revolutionary Democratic Future
After the Reorganisation of 1924, the Kuomintang has oftentimes found itself compared with other Leninist Parties, and were criticised for being autocratic vehicles of Revolution. These detractors fail to understand the goals of our Party, and the Chinese people must come to see the Party as ultimately a democratic one, which seeks to transition China into a free democratic society.
Re-energise the Cadres
To reinforce the Party's Revolutionary views, the Cadre System must be restored. These Cadres will be educated in the values of the Three Principles, have their loyalty put to the test, and be chosen through election, recommendation, training, or examination. Cadres will be divided into Party Work cadres, Political cadres, and Mass Movement cadres. Through the Cadre System, the Kuomintang will infiltrate every level of Chinese Society.
The People's Generalissimo
The Xinhai Revolution of 1911 promised to secure a free and democratic future for China. However, the Republic failed to achieve this, and Sun turned to more radical methods. It must be understood though that the KMT's goals never changed, and it was only the methods which became revolutionary. Generalissimo He Yingqin and the KMT only seek to deliver the abandoned promises of Xinhai and liberate the Chinese people.
Spiritual Authoritarianism
As Dr. Sun himself observed, the difference between the decaying China of old and the Kuomintang's reborn China is like night and day. While the monarchs treated our great nation as little more than their property, Generalissimo He has proven time and time again to be a self-sacrificing, magnanimous hero guiding China into a new era. For as long as his heart pumps blood, the Generalissimo will work tirelessly to steward the nation.
Engineering a Future for China
The Kuomintang has always been the party of the future, blasting ahead through the swamp of desolation that China once was. No party member exemplifies this perpetual grasping towards progress more than Chen Lifu. An engineer by trade, he has taken on a new role as the architect of China's future. Contrary to the socialists and reactionaries who seek to divide the nation into petty factionalism, we move not left, not right, but forward together as one nation.
Concord with the Republican Elite
Following Dr. Sun's example, the Kuomintang has always strived to work together with a multitude of movements for the sake of the Chinese nation. Now that China is unified, it would be wise to unify the political sphere as well, working with the best and brightest of like-minded republicans, who can bring their expertise to work in our reconstruction efforts. If you are pro-Kuomintang, you are pro-China!
Life as a Service

Our party's strength is dependent on the amount of the populace who have grown to believe in our ideals, and the fanaticism and drive of said members. To expand both of these factors, it is vital we display to the masses that the Kuomintang is the only party that cares for them, with the Generalissimo as the patriarch of all the people.
Restoring Spiritual Balance

The materialistic Western ideologies sought to rectify this imbalance by increasing production, and in turn merely increasing human greed to match. Now that we have conquered threats both domestic and abroad, our next target will be human nature itself.

Planned Free Economic Development
The Kuomintang did not fight for decades just to see China fall into the tyranny of empires before, nor to see it spiral into anarchy again. It is time to rebuild, and the PSC has proposed a unique economic system tailor made for recovery. With the party's best technocratic planners directing the heavy industry and the people's entrepreneurs revitalising the light industry, the engine of progress will be kickstarted under the guidance of China's most talented working in harmony.
Reshuffle the Executive Yuan
It's about time the Yuan received an overhaul. Hu Hanmin made a noble attempt at protecting the traditional, orthodox forms of Party governance. But the future is now. Representatives who have shown they care more about stirring up dissent than contributing to the party will be swiftly dismissed and replaced with truly loyal party cadres. China is built by good, honest, technocratic governance. Not petty idealism.
Political Consultative Conference
As part of our initiative to streamline the party's inner workings, we will formalise a Political Consultative Conference, consisting of Kuomintang bureaucrats, Dangwai representatives, and other technocratic thinkers aligned with the Kuomintang to debate about and direct coming policy. The party will evolve once more from a loose association of military factionalists to an efficient body of the most capable leaders in every political facet.
The Liberal Generalissimo

The people will no longer fall for the lies of demagogues, but rather be assured that the Kuomintang is the party of he who rules the greatest, rather than he who shouts the loudest.
National Military Education Movement
The intellectuals' disdain of physical, hard labour is a disease which cannot be allowed to fester. To this end, the National Military Education Movement shall seek to completely transform the students of China: Military indoctrination shall begin at a young age, schools shall be reorganised in a similar fashion to military regiments, and sciences oriented toward their wartime applications. With this, a new generation of warrios shall descend upon society.
Crackdown on Warlordism
Despite everything, China is not yet truly unified and integrated: Excessive taxation and inefficiencies remain. The cause of this problem does not come from class dynamics but from the nation: Split by warlords along provincial lines with their foolish, petty ambitions. No effort shall be spared in eradicating this disease, along with all those who perpetuate it. Soon, this period of disunity and inefficiency shall be nothing but a bad memory.
Promote Yellow Unionism
The Chinese workers are the backbone of the nation, and the time has come to alleviate their suffering resulting from decades of western imperialism: The National Voluntary Labour Movement shall be the ultimate salvation of labour, reducing unemployment and securing workers' rights while maintaining a patriotic, nationalist nature rather than encouraging senseless class conflict which only causes further setbacks, for the primary conflict is between nations, not classes.
The Realisation of Dictatorship
Despite accomplishing national reunification, the party has failed the Chinese people too many times to count, becoming remote from the masses, throwing away their trust and their faith, causing a lack of political and military solutions. There is, however, a solution to this problem: The Blueshirt Society, a truly patriotic force that seeks national revolution to the utmost. Loyaly to the Generalissimo, they shall serve as his much needed right hand from now on.
Restoring the Chinese National Character
Unfortunately, the Chinese national character currently occupies multiple extremes at the same time: Individualism, loyalty to family and clan, and cosmopolitanism, with them being cemented by Confucianism, feudalism, and western-exported materialism. It is the task of the state to diminish these influences and cultivate genuine nationalism, achieving national restoration through cultural control.

Reorganise the Fudian Bank
Whilst the tendrils of Yunnan's financial clans have almost penetrated into all local state-owned enterprises, Fudian bank has proven itself incredibly useful and sustainable in all manners of economic reform. By further ingratiating it within the provincial government's administration, Yunnan will bring all major local banks under one state-owned umbrella.
Commerce's Wartime Contribution
The Yunnan Chamber of Commerce under Miao Yuntai has long been the lifeline of Yunnan provincial economics since the 1920s. And yet with a surplus of currency, they have not contributed nearly enough to Yunnan's wartime goals. By allocating a special fund for the acquisition of guns and ammunition, Yunnan will have the latest in international weaponry at its disposal.
Militarisation to Save the Nation
Only through force of arms may the Chinese liberate themselves from foreign impression and national disunity. In the game of evolution, only the strong survive, and similarly so only nations that can impose their will upon others will be guaranteed latent security. For every rifle constructed, a bullet is loaded, and with that the nation takes one step forward.
Maximise Tax Revenues
Through the levying of a special consumption tax, and a special business tax, alongside forcibly implementing the stamp tax, Yunnan should be able to garner millions more yuan from its citizens to re-introduce into state enterprises. Furthermore, by monopolising the sale and transportation of opium, the government can both curb its influence, and rake in immense profits.
Cai E's Dream Realised
As advocated by Cai E, the spiritual successor of the present Central Government, the President should be strong, resilient, taking no stances on issues unless the principle of the central government was at stake, whislt the legislative branch should not be able to command the President or cabinet. The incumbent President certainly makes no qualms in following such a doctrine.
Obligations of the Military
The natural role of the military-man in the nation's politics is that to be a prominent participant in its nation-state building project; they are obliged to defend the nation against all threats, and thus are entitled, or warranted, to intervene in the political sphere should the nation's interest be at risk. Whilst the politicians squabble, the military must remain vigilant.
The Financial Regulation Committee
As the nation has undergone untold amounts of political and military strife, it too has been levied with severe economic collapse in multiple sectors, alongside a highly irregular state financial network. Through the establishment of a new economic regulation committee, the state shall begin its economic revival through abolishing all previous economic irregularities.
Building a Shared Nation State
It is in Cai E's ideal of a state, of a shared nation-state, that every individual assumes their respective responsibility for the well-being of the nation-state, thus ensuring its survival. To consider undermining any of the political institutions of the republic, be it through abrogation of elections, or strongarming the branches of government, would thus erode national unity.
Restoring Harmony to the Countryside
Whilst the gentry may balk at the notion, you cannot rule a nation in perpetual fear or contention with that of the masses. As Dr. Sun provided, it is necessary, in some retrospect, to see to it that Land to the Tiller be implemented throughout the nation. However, the government should refrain from politicising the masses in this programme, lest revolutionary fervour take hold.
All Under the King
As is his right, both as a war hero and the head of state, every individual minister of his cabinet should be absolutely responsible to President Long Yun; the parliament should exist to vote on the President's agenda, not the other way around. Partisan strife has no place in the new Beiyang Republic, and if any parties do exist, they should be entirely constitutionally based.
Duel of Twin Dragons
With the sundering of all other opponents, it would appear that the ensuing tide of Dian Clique backstabbing has succumbed even that of Hu Ruoyu and Lu Han. The incumbent herald of the remnants of Tang Jiyao's nation-building project, and the ambitious adventurous stalwart, once attached to Long Yun. Facing off despite their mutual victory, it would appear that the nation's strife is not over yet.
Restraining the Revolution
Soldiers should not take part in political parties, if they allow themselves to be distracted and disturbed by external matters, then the task of leading armies will fall bit by bit to civilian weaklings. Similarly, if every party had military backing, then it will become an untrustworthy institution. In principle the revolution was good, but you cannot politicise the army's men.
Three Trainings and Commandments
With the unification of the nation, there has been a grand increase in the extra-judicial rights of various middle to high ranking officers, who have become something of a new warlord class in of themselves. In order to restore strict discipline and reduce corruption, we must instill the virtues of honesty, diligence, and selflessness, and fight against all injustices.
From Flexibility to Dynamicism
Lacking in concrete ideological beliefs could be viewed as an innate sense of personalism, or lack of a state-building plan, for Hu Ruoyu and Lu Han it is perhaps their greatest strength. Staying true to the ideal of apolitical and decisive strongmen willing to make pragmatic choices, they are able to inevitably prioritise what is best for the nation, be it liberal, or conservative.
Cultivate the National Youth
Originally a pet project of the local Dian warlords, the Yunnan Provincial Youth League Affairs Bureau is the perfect model to extrapolate and export nation-wide. For the future generations need guidance, and a wise hand to induct them into steering the future of China. Thus, it is necessary to cultivate their spirit through youth leagues and other organisations.
China As a Melting Pot
Having synthesised a minority Yi armed forces with a majority Han provincial bureaucracy, Long Yun is no stranger to integrating China's various minority cultures into one broader national whole. Similarly, he has sponsored a great expanse of operas, libraries, and museums. Staying true to his origins, President Long has sought to embrace a new multicultural identity for the new cosmopolitan China.
Adherence to the New Literati
In dynasties past, the scholars were once upheld as virtuous civil servants who steered China into a direction of harmonious progress. With the Xinhai Revolution however, a new form of prestigious literati has emerged: the intellectual class. Exposed to Western ideas, or otherwise attempting to synthesise them with Chinese tradition, let us once more heed these men.
Exporting the Dian Economic Model
As opposed to Eastern China, Western China had to adopt its own form of economic development: it could not rely on foreigners to build factories and teach the workers, as they had little investments in the interior. As such, a major bureaucratic state apparatus, central planning, and government intervention within key industries ruled the day, and it shall rule the nation too.
On the Issue of Those of Merit
With the recent unification of the nation, the various leading warlords and otherwise commanding members of the high command have continued to complain to the new President about the \
Restoring Auspicious Governance
Whilst the Zhili core may have been decimated by the tide of history, their acolytes and layabouts remain within pockets of the country: the Assembly and Senate, even various informal provincial Guanxi networks remain in function. With a fitting career worthy of being the Marshal-President, let Hu Ruoyu emerge from the outskirts of the Zhili family tree, and into its core.
Revolutionary Constitutionalism
Ambivalent to the true revolutionary struggle of the Kuomintang, Lu Han nonetheless sees much merit in their ideals and practices. Reasoning with and understanding the plight of the common peasantry, and the desire for a strong, democratic, and nationalist state, Lu can call upon wise men such as Zhou Zhongyue to facilitate the re-admission of some revolutionaries into the mainstream.
The Steeled \
Close friends with many endearing democrats of all political stripes, Long Yun is at his heart a man born in the ostensibly democratic ideals of Xinhai. Whilst having ascended to the highest office at the helm of a military onslaught, President Long nonetheless prefers to rule by and through constitutionalist rule. Whilst democracy may not come soon, it can at least be an ideal to strive for.
Government By Efficiency
Democrats, demagogues, and idealogues suffer from the infallible nature of believing more in their ideological tenets than that of what is best for the nation. And it is only further admission of their faults and failures that President Long, pragmatic and erstwhile, ascended to the state office. Through the rules of efficiency and progress, China and its citizens will prosper.
Protecting China's Foundations
The Chinese nation-state is entirely beholden to its capacity to care for, control, and revitalise both its agricultural sector and the environment. Whilst the peasantry are actively seen to, it is necessary to maintain the fields they till too. Through regional conservation boards and land reclamation bureaus, China will never again suffer starvation.
Extending a Hand to the Gentry
Having been key backers of many individuals now close to the upper halls of the central government, the gentry have seen their position seemingly diminished in light of unification due to the nature of the peasant issue. However, it is still possible to strike a mutually beneficial deal. Protecting the gentry from instability, the government shall earn their compliance.

During his time in China, Hu Ruoyu drafted many books on modern military theory. Amongst them that of \
The Adventurous Turncoat
Ever willing to burn bridges in the pursuit of self-glorification and endless ambition, Lu Han has finally ascended to perhaps the highest office possible within the nation. With many lost along the way, Lu's almost nigh reckless nature, and intensely opinionated outlook, has caused much instability within the delicate balance of the nation. Yet, eternally a new adventure awaits.
Breaking the Dian-Qian Stalemate
By March 1927, the last stragglers of the battered National Revolutionary Army completed their grueling \
The §cGuizhou Kuomintang§! have [?YUN_introminigame_KMT_soldiers_difference_var|c0] more soldiers than the §fYunnan Clique§!

Initial War Preparations Are Completed
Though Yunnan and Guizhou are neighbours, laying the groundwork for an invasion will take some time. Supply depots need to be created, battlemaps need to be drawn out, and freshly arrived troops need to be positioned. On both sides of the border, generals are war-gaming their next moves, hoping to gain a critical advantage. The buildup along the frontlines continues across planting season, and by summer commanders believe their armies will be primed for an invasion.
Troops Flood to the Dian-Qian Border
The amassing of units on the Yunnan-Guizhou border has caused a cycle of continued escalation as both sides deploy forces to the front. With any hope of a surprise attack lost by this point, both sides race to try and outdo the other - ironically reinforcing the stalemate. It will only be through decisive action that one side gain a large enough advantage at the border needed to overrun the other.
Switch Sides
The Warlord Era is filled with tales of sordid betrayals, sometimes due to envy, sometimes due to greed, and sometimes for self preservation. For many of the lower ranking officers on both sides of the border, this choice has become more and more tempting as the tide begins to turn.
Commence the Assault
Thanks to our men's efforts, our troop buildup has outpaced that of our foes. Momentum is on our side. We must strike while the iron is still hot, and order our troops forward before our enemies can bring in more reinforcements.
Accelerate Our Buildup
This stalemate will only be broken if one side can take the initiative and outpace the other in assembling their troops. The wise general knows that the logistics game is not merely a military matter, but often political. The side that is able to marshal the resources of its people and overcome bureaucratic inertia will be the one with more guns, more supplies, and more troops.
Develop our Battleplan
They say the best laid plans of the generals never survive contact with the enemy, but that hardly diminishes the value of a good plan. We must work to piece together the information provided by our networks of spies, scouts, and soldiers on the ground, allowing our planners to create the best strategy possible for the coming war. Although the exact details are bound to change as the situation develops, having the right pieces in the right places before the battle will give us the flexibility to win.
Win the War of Wills
The supreme art of war is to subdue the enemy without fighting, and in an era of shifting loyalties the effective number of troops one side has on the ground may be far fewer or far greater than their actual soldiers gathered. With tailored propaganda on one hand and some generous bribes on the other, we may be able to convince our opponents to give up or at least not resist as hard. Similarly, adequate pay and good treatment of our men will go a long way for shoring up our own officers and mens' loyalties.
The Second Yunnan-Guangxi War
Yunnan has invaded Liangguang, reigniting conflict between the two southern powers. Success or failure in this war will likely hinge on their ability to take Nanning, the capital of Guangxi. Seizing it will open the door to march on Guangzhou. Failing to do so will cause many in Yunnan to lose faith in [YUN.GetLeader] and likely result in a diplomatic settlement.\n\n§Y[YUN_GXC_war_settlement_scripted_loc]§!

The war has gone horribly, just as expected. It might be time to covertly begin talks with our foes and end this war before it is too late!
Accept Yunnan's Surrender
Having received contact with the conspiring Yunnan Generals in Kunming and with battlefield developments going in our favor, it is time for us to press our terms. This will allow us to avoid a costly campaign in the mountains while securing our Western flank for the time being.
Grand Southern Reconstruction
Expand Gejiu Mining
With the commencement of the Grand Southern Reconstruction project, Gejiu's massive Tin deposits can finally be exploited thanks to our economy becoming more industrialised over the past few years.
Kunming Cement Factory
To reduce Yunnan's reliance on imported cement from its neighbouring provinces and abroad, Yunnan must develop its own cement industry to ensure the security and stability of the Grand Southern Reconstruction.
Modernised Smelting
If Yunnan's mineral deposits are to be optimally exploited and reduce our reliance on imported steel, there must be improvements made to Yunnan's current smelting industry. Outdated machinery must be replaced, missing equipment procured and new techniques must be learned.
Purchasing American Machinery
To further progress the development of our industry, and to replace our ineffective and rather outdated tools, the government must turn to the more experienced outside world. Being one of the world's more industrialised nations, it would be wise for our government to turn to the United States for assistance.
Yaolong Electric Station
With the Yunnannese economy becoming increasingly industrialised, it would be wise to construct a hydroelectric power station within Yunnan itself near Yaolong to supply Yunnan's future power needs.
Expand Kunming Charcoal Kilns
With much of Yunnan's population living in rural areas, charcoal has become a fuel source in high demand due due it often being much easier to cook with than fuel. By expanding Yunnan's domestic charcoal production, our government could see an increase in revenue as well as appeasing local charcoal dealers.
Arsenal Subsidies
Despite the Kunming arsenal being a historically stable supplier of arms for the Yunnan army, it may not be enough due to our government's increased demand for arms. With some hoping to expand the arsenals, it would be of the utmost importance to increase subsidies for the Kunming arsenal if our government hopes to maintain its supply of weapons.
Guizhou Brewing Factory
Despite Guizhou's underdevelopment and poverty, Maotai has remained one of Guizhou's most valuable, and most famous, exports throughout its entire history. Therefore, the expansion of current breweries and distilleries must be undertaken if we are to maintain, or even increase, Guizhou's supply of Maotai to the rest of China.
Guangzhou Cement Factory
Thanks to Guangdong's pre-existing relatively stable cement manufacturing industry, no additional investment of capital is necessary if we are to maintain it. However, if our government is to cut down on its use of foreign cement, Guangdong's cement industry must be further expanded to ensure the security of all future construction projects within the southwest.
Qingyuan Chemical and Fertiliser Factory
Although a primarily agricultural nation, China still lacks a modern fertilising industry. Economic planners have outlined an ambitious project to develop a chemical and fertilising plant in Guangzhou which will hopefully kickstart China's fertilising modernisation.
Wengjiang Hydroelectric Plant
With Guangzhou, and Guangdong in general, emerging as a new industrial centre for China, the region is projected to see a massive increase in power consumption in the following years. To supply Guangdong's new industry, the mighty Pearl River's flow can be exploited by constructing new hydroelectric power plants along it.
Qiongya Rubber
With Qiongya's warm climate and nutrient rich soil, it has become a plantation for cash crops in recent years. Profits from the island's cash crops can be further increased if a rubber plantation is started to supply both our government and any potential foreign buyers with rubber.
Guangxi Infrastructure Development Scheme
With Guangxi often lacking behind in development, its infrastructure is often not up to modern standards. Therefore, if our government hopes to develop Guangxi's industry, investment in new infrastrucutre projects is of the utmost imporance.
Guangxi Farmers Bank
Farmers live in a cycle of perpetual debt, taking on loans in the spring in hopes of reaping a fruitful harvest in the fall. To alleviate their plight, a farmer's bank will be created to lend money to rural granaries and farmers, accepting grain as collateral. Credit Offices for the Common People are to developed by shopkeepers under government regulation to facilitate rural business. We will not leave our farmers behind.
Arable Land Development
Due to Yunnan's mountainous terrain, large scale agricultural developments have always been a challenge in the region. Rice paddies and small scale farms make up most of Yunnan's current agriculture due to its adaptability to its difficult terrain. To aleviate this, the government must develop Yunnan's agricultural industry by further expanding its amount of arable land.
Yunnan Cotton Spinning and Weaving
With cotton production and spinning being one of Yunnan's more productive light industries, it would be wise in continuing the efforts of Tang's government in further developing Yunnan's cotton mills.
Yunnan Water Control
With Yunnan often experiencing large amounts of seasonal rainfall, flooding is quite a common occurrence for many Yunnanese people. With how destructive flooding tends to get during the rainy season, previous governments have often organised massive water control projects in an effort to limit the damage. Our government must continue to do the same.
Supporting the Indochinese Revolt
Forever sharing a form of cultural brotherhood with our Indochinese neighbours, we could not previously support their prior efforts to rid their lands of the German yoke. However, now that a new rebellion has sprung into action, fiercer and stronger than before, it is imperative that we seize the opportunity to both support a friendly regime, weaken German Influence in Asia, and remove German inroads into Yunnan. A win for everybody. Except the Germans.
Expand Mining Operations
The mining operations near Gejiu and other major mining cities in Yunnan are a major aspect of the Yunnanese economy, a vital one even. Expanding the mines, furnaces and smelters in our province as well as subsidising mining corporations will help us increase our output and quality.

Acknowledge Zhili Authority
Yunnan's autonomy should be preserved at all cost, but at times like these, we have to acknowledge the impossibility of achieving such a feat. With the Zhili at our doorstep, and their supporters continuing to pressure us into aligning with them, we have no choice but to send a telegraph to the Zhili leadership acknowledging our recognition of their national authority to secure what remaining autonomy we will have under their protection.
Building A Democratic Base
Yunnan was the first province to rebel against the tyranny of Yuan Shikai when he dismantled the republic by crowning himself Emperor of China, dragging the nation back towards the monarchical system many revolutionaries died to overthrow during Xinhai. To remember the sacrifices of the revolution, we must build upon this legacy by transforming Yunnan into a \
Entrust Our Finances to Commerce
Facilitators of economic growth in Yunnan through the development of local industry and the province's financial system, the Yunnan Chamber of Commerce has proven itself a worthy ally of the provincial government. With their previous cooperation with Governor Cai E and Governor Tang Jiyao, the Chamber of Commerce can surely be entrusted to handle the province's finances with care and responsibility. Together, this fruitful pact will bring prosperity to Yunnan for which will be enjoyed by all.
Continue Modernisation Efforts
Far away in the corners of the nation's mountainous frontiers, Yunnan has lagged in comparison to other provinces in the east. Without a coastline for the province to be reached, Yunnan has not felt the benefits of modernisation efforts that have already taken place across the nation, bar from attempts to do so by Governor Tang Jiyao. By continuing where Governor Tang left off, we shall ensure modernisation takes place in Yunnan socially, politically, and economically.
Repurpose the Bureaucracy
For all of Governor Tang's failings, it is thanks to him that we inherit a vast and functioning bureaucracy that has served him so \
Tolerate Mengzi's Monopoly
Much of the Yunnan's natural resources lay at the hands of Mengzi, which controls a majority of the province's mines in the southern reaches of the mountainous Yungui Plateau. Extracting minerals from the earth and exporting it towards Indochinese ports to be refined, this has come to be one of Yunnan's main sources of revenue, funding for its economic development, administration, and most importantly, its military strength. Predictably, due to their location, Mengzi holds a monopoly over the province's mining sector, of which we have to tolerate for now.
Pressure Yunnan into Alignment
Our efforts in Yunnan have begun to bear fruit as those loyal to our government are starting to wield enough power to contest the current authorities in the province. With General Long Yun's mandate on the verge of collapse, it is high time we apply pressure upon Yunnan to align with our government!
Allure of Money and Flattery
Distant from the reaches of the Central Government, it has led Yunnan to enjoy a certain level of political autonomy, free to carry out its administration independent of Beijing's wishes. However, this has also made Yunnan among the poorest provinces in the Southwest, neglected by central authorities as its economy suffers from underdevelopment. For us, this is but an opportunity for us to increase our influence in Yunnan by enticing them with financial aid.
Bribing the Disaffected
The Yunnan Army might be more professional than most warlord armies in China, but its loyalty is questionable. Despite Long Yun's position as Yunnan's hegemon is unquestionable, there remains those who remain loyal to Governor Tang and his clique as well as those more than willing to jump ship with a bit of cash injection. By sending waves of bribes to corrupt officers, and the apathetic rank and file, threats of desertion should help keep Long Yun in line.
Issue Threats to Yunnan
Although the Yunnan Clique has retreated into isolationism in light of its failure in the Second Yunnan-Guangxi War and subsequent coup against Governor Tang Jiyao, the mere idea of Yunnan maintaining its independence cannot stay any longer. With military defeat fresh in the minds of the Yunnan Army, being threatened with the possibility of another war should scare the Yunnanese leadership into aligning with us and strengthen our grip over the Southwest.
Deal with the Mengzi Junta
With Governor Tang out of the picture, a balance of power exists between Long Yun in Kunming and the three other garrison commanders in Mengzi: Hu Ruoyu, Zhang Ruji, and Li Xuanting. Unlike Long Yun who has shown himself in favour of isolationism and non-alignment, our agents in Yunnan have indicated the Mengzi junta to be more receptive towards aligning with our government. With that in mind, we could expand their influence in the province by directly making deals with Mengzi.
Send Out Peace Feelers
Aside from being an act of self-preservation by its conspirators, the coup in Kunming is a clear sign of Yunnan distancing itself from Export Militarism, and as a consequence, war itself. Isolated politically, we should send peace feelers to Yunnan, announcing our intention of achieving national unification without resorting to forceful methods. With the promotion of peace in our mutual interest, it is no doubt Kunming will accept and align with us in the name of uniting the nation peacefully.

He Yingqin's Autocoup
Mounting criticism from within the party, spearheaded by Premier of the Legislative Yuan Hu Hanmin, has created a brutal rift between Hu and President He Yingqin. As mutual suspicion grows, exacerbated by the drafting a Provisional Constitution, He Yingqin has resolved to eliminate his rival once and for all.\n\nTo do so he will need to not only purge the party of Hu Hanmin and his Moscow Committee Clique allies, but also disarm or threaten into submission the regional militarist cliques that dominate the party's southern heartland. This is no easy task, and to be successful he will need to demonstrate that he can preserve party stability and avoid allowing Nationalist Zeal from growing too high or too low.
Hu Hanmin's Outrage
Hu Hanmin is a fiery orator and one of Sun Yat-sen's closest disciples, allowing him to express an unusually large amount of dissent within the party-state. A devout democratic-centralist, he has lashed out against Kuomintang figures who have formed factions to advance private interests and government officials who hoard property and live off corruption, both understood by He Yingqin as a veiled attack on the Generalissimo.\n\nWhile his antics were once tolerable, or at least a necessary evil, the discussions over the Provisional Constitution have worsened relations between the two men. Hu, as Premier of the Legislative Yuan, argues that the Kuomintang can convene a National Assembly (also translated as the National People's Convention) but it cannot draft a provisional constitution. The National Assembly can only draft a full constitution at the end of the Political Tutelage. Moreover, Hu argues that a provisional constitution would not improve the life of the people, instead it would be a distraction from the national reconstruction, reduction of taxes, and development of communication infrastructure, agriculture, industry, and commerce.\n\nUnlike the empty words of the provisional constitution, Hu Hanmin is very much willing to back his views with his well armed supporters.

Promise a Constitutional Government
In a nation that has long been seen as backwards, violent, and effectively feudal, there has been a cry from inside and out for modernisation. The Political Science Clique is eager to demonstrate its commitment to modernity - modern industry, modern styles, modern business culture, modern technology, modern management. Chipping away at needless traditions and turning a new page is a difficult process, but a necessary one.\n\nAmid the chaos of the Hu Hanmin Crisis, there is a growing consensus that the arbitrary, capricious nature of military rule must end in favour of a civilian led government. In place of military dominance, a modern technocratic and responsible government must rise even if it might be too soon for a full democracy. Rule of law, not a party-state and its fanatics, must be the basis of our republic.
Release Political Prisoners
As Hu Hanmin was arrested, hundreds of his followers were detained with him and held without trial. Others were swept up over the years, sometimes on spurious or non-existent charges by an overzealous secret police or turned in by vindictive political rivals. A surefire way of winning over the bourgeoisie and intelligentsia that make up much of the recently elected National Assembly is to release political dissidents who are like them or even close to them. It will not be easy by any means, but it would be a nice starting point for further negotiations.
Bribe Military Officers
The National Revolutionary Army is a heavily politicised force by design, meant to be thoroughly loyal to the party-state and its revolutionary creed. Many of its core divisions and officers are ideological fanatics and thoroughly embedded into the Whampoa cult of personality. However, as the army expanded, they quickly became surrounded by many units freshly absorbed from defeated or integrated warlord armies or new recruits from less politicised training programs. While it might be too late to defang the army altogether, with some carefully placed bribes (both monetary and with regards to promotions) and leveraging our connections to prominent regional military men might be sufficient in deterring less zealous officers from endorsing a coup d'etat.

Dian-Qian Border Tensions
Yunnan (abbreviated as Dian) and Guizhou's (abbreviated as Qian) fates after the Xinhai Revolution were intertwined. While both overthrew their Qing Governors, Cai E of Yunnan proved more successful in consolidating control in the aftermath and deployed Tang Jiyao to Guizhou to aid the Constitutional Preparation Society in pushing out the more radical Self-Government Study Society.\n\nThis led to Liu Xianshi taking power in the province, though he was deposed by his nephew Wang Wenhua in a power struggle after Tang returned to Yunnan to serve as Governor. Wang, who was backed by the Kuomintang, orchestrated a coup known as the Minjiu incident with the help of Guizhou commanders He Yingqin, Gu Zhenglun, and Zhu Shaoliang in 1920. The bloodshed caused widespread condemnation and Wang Wenhua was assassinated in turn while in exile in Shanghai.\n\nGuizhou was thrown into chaos amid fears of a Yunnan invasion, but the Beiyang government struck first by sponsoring Yuan Zuming to invade Guizhou and depose the Kuomintang backed Lu Dao. By 1922, Yuan was master of the province but within a year he faced an invasion force led by Tang Jiyao's brother Tang Jiyu, who was tasked with restoring Liu Xianshi to power. Tang Jiyu then served as de facto co-Governor but was forced to retreat in 1925 after the disastrous Yunnan-Guangxi war. Yuan, who had briefly come to an arrangement with Tang, reluctantly sided with the Kuomintang during the Northern Expedition, only to betray them as the tide turned.\n\nYuan had hoped to recover Guizhou after the war, but fate had other plans. The National Revolutionary Army, defeated but not destroyed, undertook the \

One day, we shall leave Guizhou.

Anti-Concessionist Wave in the East
The League of Five Provinces started off as a Southern Zhili project in eastern China, but after the Northern Expedition Sun Chuanfang added Hunan, Guangdong, and Guangxi to his sphere of influence. The League formally expanded to Eight in 1930 during the aftermath of the controversial 1929 elections, which saw Zhang Zuolin walk out of peace talks in the North and triggered fears of a Zhang-Sun alliance against the Cao-Wu government's proposals for disarmament and centralisation.\n\nGuizhou very narrowly avoided being added to the League. The Zhili recognised Governor, Yuan Zuming, has been extremely dependent on Southern Zhili and later Hunan Clique patronage to maintain his rule over eastern Guizhou. But Sun, in his desperate manoeuvres against the North, sought to avoid antagonising the Sichuan and Yunnan warlords during the interwar and left Guizhou out of the League.\n\nUnfortunately, the League Marshal's doomed quest to challenge his mentors has proven to be his downfall. An oversized army, soaring costs, endemic corruption, extreme brutality and the undertaking of significant loans has weakened the foundations of his empire. His partnership with foreign concessionists, most notably the German AOG, has triggered massive unrest, which has reached a fevered pitch after the ZheGan Railway scandal.\n\nYunnan of course is no stranger to working with foreign merchants. The valuable Tin Trade has brought British, French, Japanese, American and now German traders into the province and Tang Jiyao has sought to balance potential riches with foreign exploitation. Overreach by the British and French have caused unrest in Gejiu in the past, which Tang has manipulated to his advantage. Still, German influence remains high particularly in Mengzi, where their patronage of Hu Ruoyu has allowed them to dominate Yunnanese trade.\n\nIn the east, the Wuhu Incident has given Chen Tiaoyuan of Anhui the pretext needed to secede from the League. Perhaps it is a sign the tide is turning?
Unlike the League, we shall pursue an equal partnerships with foreign traders.
Foreign exploitation will only end when China takes back what is rightfully theirs!
Black Monday Cripples the Tin Trade
Black Monday has triggered a global economic meltdown, a disaster that will cripple economies for years to come. For China, already struggling with diminished trade after the American Great Depression, this will likely worsen the political instability the year has wrought. For Yunnan, heavily dependent on the Tin Trade, the collapse of the German economy is devastating.\n\nVirtually all tin exports go through the Hanoi-Kunming railroad. Initially built and owned by the French, it was seized by the Germans after the Great War and has been folded into AOG subsidiaries. Likewise, mining rights in the Mengzi region which were once controlled by an Anglo-French syndicate, are effectively dominated by the Germans. Similar to the French, who exploited the lack of easy routes to Guangdong and also blocked efforts to create alternative railways, the Germans have charged extortionate rates to use the Hanoi-Kunming line.\n\nThe resentment against foreign concessions have been building for some time now, and recent events have created both a sense of national solidarity and xenophobia against outsiders. Nationalist and socialist agitators, usually affiliated with the Kuomintang, have exploited the moment to call for forceful seizures of foreign owned banks, plants, mines, and railways. Though often sparse on details beyond a retelling of Sun Yat-sen's vague Programme of National Reconstruction published decades ago, for many it captures the zeitgeist of the day.\n\nFor the technocrats working in the Yunnan government, there has been a greater urgency to modernise the economy. The province has long depended on tin and opium, and efforts by Minister of Finance Ma Cong in the 1920s to centralise the economy and promote currency reforms amid a severe economic crisis fell apart due to bureaucratic resistance. His successor, Miao Yuntai has been more successful in his efforts in the 1930s, particularly promoting industrialisation, but it often feels as if he has little time to work with.

Sun Chuanfang is Shot in Nanjing!
Sun Chunfang was gunned down by an assassin in Nanjing, bringing the League on the brink of war. While he is not dead yet, the sharks have been circling as his southern Zhili lieutenants angle to be his successor. Chief among them are Governor Qi Xieyuan of Jiangsu and the rebellious Governor Chen Tiaoyuan of Anhui, both of whom are positioning themselves for a coup.\n\nIn the south, Kuomintang uprisings have broken out almost simultaneously across the League as Nationalist leaders make their move. While most are in contact with Kuomintang hubs in MinGan or Guizhou, others are simply taking the initiative without waiting for higher authorisation.\n\nBeijing is in shambles, as northern Zhili leaders feud over how to respond to the crisis and warlords who accepted their authority for the past decade rapidly slip from their orbit. Unrest in the north and fears of another invasion by the Fengtian clique have paralysed the government, and for now they are left rudderless.\n\nFor the Kuomintang junta in Guizhou, the next steps are obvious. This might be their last chance to fulfill their revolutionary promises, and while the Beiyang establishment is distracted they must seize control of what provinces they can. For men like Tang Jiyao however, the present circumstances present a quandary.\n\nThe Federalist heyday was in the early 1920s, when China was seemingly irrevocably divided not only into North-South, but also into provincial warlord states. The idea of peacefully reuniting through negotiations was appealing to many reformist intellectuals, but even before the Zhili were able to reunite through force (thus proving them wrong), the Federalist movement lost steam as both the Northern and Southern governments ousted Federalist figures. Discredited as unpatriotic outside of certain southern circles, it seems using force will be the only route to Federalism in China. But for the regionalists in Tang's government, leaving Yunnan is unpopular after the last few failed military expeditions.

Refugees from Sichuan
A brutal combination of drought, flooding, and political instability have contributed to a major famine in the neighbouring province of Sichuan. As rice prices surge and the corrupt, ineffective government fails to provide significant relief, refugees from Sichuan have flooded into neighbouring provinces. Being a poorer, mountainous province relative to other destinations, Yunnan has received fewer refugees than others however news of the people's plight have made their way into Kunming.\n\nYunnan and Sichuan have had a complicated relationship throughout the Warlord Era. Sichuan has been divided into many competing warlord factions, in what has been described as a warlord era within a warlord era. During the National Protection War, Cai E invaded Sichuan in hopes of seizing its valuable salt fields, and ensuing conflicts between Yunnan, Guizhou, and Sichuan continued throughout the next decade or so.\n\nPerhaps the last remnant of these conflicts are the forces of Xiong Kewu and Lu Chao camped in the borderlands between Sichuan, Guizhou, and Yunnan. Former opponents of Tang Jiyao and not fully in the Kuomintang's sphere either, they act as a wild card of sorts in the looming conflict between Yunnan and Guizhou.
By aiding Sichuan's refugees, Tang hopes to heal past grudges.

Long Yun's Report
When the assault on Guizhou was first planned, Tang Jiyao was forced to entrust the task of preparing the groundwork for the invasion to Defence Commissioner of Zhaotong Long Yun. This week, Governor Tang has come with a delegation to the northern city to personally inspect the progress being made there.\n\nLong Yun has been hard at work constructing depots, organising supplies, and positioning incoming units for an offensive against Guizhou. After four months, his headquarters at Zhaotong has been brimming with activity. In his conversation with Tang, General Long estimated that the basic preparations necessary for offensive operations have been completed.\n\nBut he also warns that the Kuomintang have not been idle on the other side of the border. Noticing the troop buildup, the National Revolutionary Army junta has begun to mobilise troops of their own, constructing defence-works and conscripting locals. Any element of surprise has long since been lost.\n\nThe relationship between the Governor and his best general has been strained as well, even if neither man brings it up. The root of this tension comes from the 1925 Yunnan-Guangxi War, where Long Yun's forces led a successful capture of Guangxi on the way to Guangdong. However, the Kuomintang-aligned New Guangxi Clique launched a decisive counterattack, encircling Long's forces in the city and ultimately forcing them to surrender. Tang Jiyu's forces, responsible for guarding Long's flank, arrived late to relieve the siege, some accused the younger Tang of being too busy purchasing opium in Guizhou, and even when they finally arrived they were utterly defeated.\n\nTang Jiyao's decision to protect his brother in the aftermath of the fiasco and scapegoat Zhang Ruji played a major part in the coup a year later. Hunan's Stake in Guizhou
Yunnan's relationship with Guizhou can hardly be considered a particularly friendly one. Embroiled in the Xinhai Revolution, Guizhou would swiftly become a battleground between the revolutionaries, counterrevolutionaries, constitutionalists and local warlords. With Liu Xianshi and Ren Kecheng sending an envoy to Cai E, Tang Jiyao would subsequently invade Guizhou on his behalf. Aided by Liu, Tang would coup the government, becoming the provincial Governor in 1912. Consolidating his power, Tang would return to Yunnan in 1913 to become Governor there, turning power over to Liu.\n\nThrust into the National Protection War, Liu would form his portion of the Guizhou Clique; the Old Xingyi Clique. Due to familial appointments, Wang Wenhua, nephew of Liu, his brother Wang Boqun, and his brother-in-law He Yingqin, would similarly arise to power; and thus begin scheming against Liu Xianshi in what was known as the Minjiu Incident in 1920. With the subsequent resignation of Xianshi, and further infighting, Wang Wenhua would be assassinated by men on Yuan Zuming's payroll under orders of Xianshi.\n\nExpelling Lu Dao from Guizhou, Yuan would then also be expelled by Tang Jiyu in 1923, engage in the Sichuan warlord melee, and align with the Zhili. Making peace with Tang in 1924, Yuan would become Governor again in 1925, yet continue to fight in the Sichuan Civil War, leaving much of the administrating to his subordinates: Wang Tianpei, Peng Hanzhang, and Zhou Xicheng. Ejected by Liu Xiang and Yang Sen in 1926, Yuan would be hosted by the Old Hunan Clique, fighting in the Northern Expedition.\n\nBy 1927, Guizhou would be split between a Kuomintang portion, under the Yunnan Clique's umbrella, and a Beiyang portion, supported by Beijing. With the KMT's recent deposition of Zhao Hengti and vanquishing of the Old Guizhou Clique, they have refused to hand-over their portion of Guizhou to He Yingqin, citing concerns from Wang Tianpei and Lu Dao. Thus, in a rather curious term of events, border disputes have led to NRA infighting along the Guizhou spheres of influence, which could prove useful in Yunnan's own struggles.
Yunnan's relationship with Guizhou can hardly be considered a particularly friendly one. Embroiled in the Xinhai Revolution, Guizhou would swiftly become a battleground between the revolutionaries, counterrevolutionaries, constitutionalists and local warlords. With Liu Xianshi and Ren Kecheng sending an envoy to Cai E, Tang Jiyao would subsequently invade Guizhou on his behalf. Aided by Liu, Tang would coup the government, becoming the provincial Governor in 1912. Consolidating his power, Tang would return to Yunnan in 1913 to become Governor there, turning power over to Liu.\n\nThrust into the National Protection War, Liu would form his portion of the Guizhou Clique; the Old Xingyi Clique. Due to familial appointments, Wang Wenhua, nephew of Liu, his brother Wang Boqun, and his brother-in-law He Yingqin, would similarly arise to power; and thus begin scheming against Liu Xianshi in what was known as the Minjiu Incident in 1920. With the subsequent resignation of Xianshi, and further infighting, Wang Wenhua would be assassinated by men on Yuan Zuming's payroll under orders of Xianshi.\n\nExpelling Lu Dao from Guizhou, Yuan would then also be expelled by Tang Jiyu in 1923, engage in the Sichuan warlord melee, and align with the Zhili. Making peace with Tang in 1924, Yuan would become Governor again in 1925, yet continue to fight in the Sichuan Civil War, leaving much of the administrating to his subordinates: Wang Tianpei, Peng Hanzhang, and Zhou Xicheng. Ejected by Liu Xiang and Yang Sen in 1926, Yuan would be hosted by the Old Hunan Clique, fighting in the Northern Expedition.\n\nBy 1927, Guizhou would be split between a Kuomintang portion, under the Yunnan Clique's umbrella, and a Beiyang portion, supported by Beijing. Reigniting old tensions, Yuan and Zhou Xicheng have begun to press their claim to Guizhou in spite of Tang's backing of Liu Xianqian, albeit some ministers have suggested that Tang could cooperate with and then betray Yuan Zuming when the time is right.
