Winter Is Coming
Throughout history, even the mightiest of armies have been subject to the fickle whims of the weather. The climate of Southwestern China is relatively mild, but the mountains can grow extremely cold and difficult to navigate during the winter. Perhaps more importantly however, Tang Jiyao has given orders for a campaign to commence by the end of this year - the Governor has a very strict timetable for his plans for unification.\n\nAlong the frontlines, preparations continue. For the Kuomintang, the coming of winter offers a significant advantage in defending their position from invasion, but even if they were to hold out from the initial onslaught, their window for unifying the south may also be closing. The junta's leadership have ordered further mobilisations, but the October rice harvest has meant militiamen on both sides have returned home and are only slowly trickling back to the front.\n\nTensions remain high, there appear to be little respite for the nervous troops on the front line. The Guizhou Connection
Perhaps one of the greatest miracles, or disasters, of the fallout of the Northern Expedition was the National Revolutionary Army's Long March into Guizhou. Ordered by Chiang Kai-shek on the eve of his lamented assassination, it cost a significant amount of men, materiel, and supplies, but perhaps ensured the preservation of the Right Kuomintang as a whole. Setting up a 5-man Junta under Zhu Peide of the 3rd Army as acting Generalissimo, it included He Yingqin of the 1st Army, Li Zongren of the 7th Army, Tan Yankai of the 2nd Army, and Cheng Qian of the 6th Army.\n\nWith power struggles and poor relations beforehand, the consolidation of forces within the Guizhou KMT's sphere of influence in the 1930s would see Tan's 2nd Army incorporated into the 6th Army, with Tan himself departing to retire in Changsha. Similarly, the 3rd Army would absorb Fan Shisheng's portion of the Yunnan Army as well as Jin Handing's 16th Army.\n\nLi Zongren's decision to take his 7th Army on a foray into Liangguang and stake his claim on Guangxi province has moved most of his men out of Guizhou. Crucially however, a few key rearguard units remain to facilitate connections between the Junta and the adventurous Machiavellian. With resources limited and their initial foothold secure, Huang Shaohong and Huang Xuchu have raised the idea of pulling all remaining personnel from Guizhou into Guangxi to shore up his position in Liangguang.\n\nHowever doing so may doom the remaining Kuomintang forces in Guizhou in their battles with the hostile Yunnan Clique, and Guizhou junta leaders have pleaded Li to maintain the mutually beneficial supply lines between their forces. The Late-Autumn Offensive
The sand has run through the hourglass and the die is now cast. Tang Jiyao, impatient about the progress of his army's preparations against what should be a much weaker Guizhou, has ordered them forward. Fighting has broken out across the frontline as Yunnan Army probing attacks surge forward to meet National Revolutionary Army outposts.\n\nThe fog of war has confounded both high commands. The meticulously crafted war plans, inked onto maps with attention to the most minute details, have fallen apart as soldiers trade fire and schemes and counterschemes are unveiled. As battles rage on the frontline and behind it, individual bands of soldiers (loyal more to each other and their commanders than their ostensible masters) are forced to make business decisions for themselves: which side will be more likely to survive and pay us?\n\nThe fate of the southwest is being decided in these coming days, though for now the outcome remains unclear. What is obvious however is that the numerous delays and the unbreakable stalemate have led to a bloody battle: a devastating outcome for both sides who should be husbanding their elite divisions. Whoever the winner may be, the situation looks grim.
Come what may, we shall prevail.
The Yunnan Army Overruns Guizhou
After a hard fought offensive, Yunnanese preparation and Kunming Military Academy discipline paid off. The advantage began to turn, first slowly and then extremely quickly as the forward positions of the National Revolutionary Army were overcome and the Kuomintang's retreat quickly became a rout.\n\nBy the time the Yunnan Army reached the rear echelons, the battle for Guizhou was effectively over. Most Kuomintang militias either surrendered, defected, or melted away into the countryside - returning to their homes never to come back again. A fanatical last stand by Kuomintang regulars in Guiyang was planned but with their troops in complete disarray and quickly surrounded, the writing was on the wall.\n\nThe Kuomintang junta's leadership similarly either fled into exile or went underground, hopes of a Kuomintang resurgence in the southwest have been permanently dashed. Tang Jiyao is now the undisputed master of the region, but many issues remain unresolved.
Let us begin the next phase of unification!
The Zhaotong Incident
In a sudden twist of fate, the National Revolutionary Army gained initiative, something they used to their fullest advantage. With well-drilled precision, nationalist fervor and tenacity worthy of the party-state, Kuomintang units had lured the Yunnan Army into a false sense of security before launching a brutal counteroffensive.\n\nThe National Revolutionary Army's elite units punctured Yunnanese weak-points, taking advantage of reports from well positioned spies and courageous scouts. Sabotage by infiltrators and major defections across the line wreaked havoc in the Yunnan Army's rear, and soon enough their best units were encircled.\n\nAs panicked reports reached Kunming, Tang Jiyao personally rushed to Zhaotong to take command of the situation. But when he arrived at the Yunnan Army's headquarters, his bodyguard unit was ambushed by Long Yun's men and disarmed - a major betrayal. By the end of the day, a Kuomintang delegation was in Kunming to accept their surrender as Hu Ruoyu sent a telegram from Mengzi recognising the new government.
We will arrive in Kunming shortly.
Legacy of Export Militarism
The state of the Yunnan army is one of decay, reflective of the deteriorating quality of the men's morality and armaments. The seldom transports of archaic rifles from the Chengdu Arsenal that had come from Luo Peijin's Sichuan administration have long since ceased, along with the few that have come from Japan via China's Southern ports. The artillery likewise has worsened in quality with many Krupp mountain guns of the Jingguojun having been discarded or damaged, their crews chronically inexperienced due to the sheer lack of both live shells and blanks needed for training.\n\nAs such, parades and vain shows of strength have been preferred over actual field combat and drills, failing to impact the effectiveness of recruits to any meaningful degree. The cavalry detachments of the Yunnan army, an already neglected force owing to the mountainous terrain of the Yungui plateau have been inadequately trained and disciplined, with horses stirring and often routing at the slightest confrontation. This severe lack of either trained horses or vehicles also means that transportation units, often critical for combat cohesion in the Southwest, remain non-existent.\n\nDespite this, Kunming still maintains its reputation of strength and stern discipline - that of elite training, daring to face superior opponents and triumph despite the odds, no doubt fostered by the legacy of the Kunming military academy. This is, however, counteracted by the absence of this spirit in higher command, primarily among Tang Jiyao's clique and his brother, Tang Jiyu. The Jingguojun's rank and file are also mixed in quality, with experienced soldiers being easy to lead but often lacking intelligence and initiative while green recruits remain unruly, untrained and unmotivated. The time for reform of the once great Yunnan Army is of the utmost importance, torn to shreds by export militarism and infighting, it can only be hoped that its prestige can be returned to once more.
We have a long road ahead of us.
Reviving Whampoa
Expanding Kunming
Crucial to the formation of our new army is that of the position of the Kunming Military Academy. Integral to the successes of the Huguojun of the past was the dedication, brotherhood, and selflessness promoted here. With the likes of Cai E, Yang Jie, He Yingqin, Li Genyuan, Li Liejun, Tang Jiyao, and numerous other great revolutionary fighters having served in the faculty of the institution, it carries a deep legacy, having contributed greatly both to the Federalist and Kuomintang revolutionary movements.\n\nWith the intent on creating a warrior class as loyal to the institutions of China and Republicanism as the Japanese are loyal to their Emperor, the curriculum of Kunming is heavily inspired by the Shikan Gakko in Japan — in part due to a lot of the early faculty being graduates. Ran under the principle of unity in management and teaching, the academy is as bureaucratic as it is devoted. Total personnel are small but elite, yet staff and students alike are encouraged to directly inquire to the Principal about any issues. Alas however, the institution of today is not that of the past, and to bring new life to the famed academy, a new principal is required to steer the ship and restore the soul it once had.\n\n

Yunnan's Heart and Soul
In the core of it all, Kunming was founded under the principles of being the last bastion of true Chinese that would endeavour to save the spirit of China. With complete adherence to militarism as the only method in which they could do so, Kunming was one of the first military academies in the nation to integrate military skills into the broader educational curriculum. Through said education and the promotion of universal conscription, they had hoped that they could foster a battlefield spirit within the Yunnanese, one that would triumph even the greatest challenges facing the nation.\n\nThe curriculum in specific was different from the likes of Baoding, as it emphasised military preparation, physical fitness, and field experience. Most instructors were from Yunnan proper, with a token inclusion of Japanese instructors from the Shikan Gakko — where most of the Yunnan instructors graduated. The students are split into three classes, A for existing active-duty officers of the rank major or below, B for provisional defence force officers, and C for aspiring officers recruited from civilian or military schooling. Subjects include Chinese literature, ethics, mechanical drawing, infantry drill, battle discipline and others.\n\nWith the new era of the academy, a conversation has stirred amongst the military staff about the influence of the revolutionary Whampoa Academy in the new Kunming. For the party-state's curriculum saw an even further focus on the martial spirit of the soldier, and of their absolute duty to the revolution and nation. Yet critiques, proponents of the old ways, raise concerns that such integration of Whampoa's teachings would dilute the strength and character of Kunming, and that it is better to merely stay the course.
Amongst all academies, Whampoa is a glistening diamond.
Was this not the same curriculum that won National Protection?
Beloved Invaders
Over the years, armies of Yunnan have painstakingly built up an orderly impression among the provincial population, with high morale, good discipline and mannered interactions with civilians setting them apart from the brutish image of the Northern armies. This has been done through a code of conduct between the Yunnan Army and the populace, enforced by the army's officers, mainly surrounding the treatment of civilians, common mannerisms and hygiene.\n\nIn contrast to typical soldiers of the warlord era who are given a free hand off-duty, recruits of the Yunnan army are implored to act respectfully and with restraint, resulting in such values separating them from even regional rivals to the North and East. All ranks of military command condone moral vices that typically plague warlord armies, such as opium smoking, prostitution as well as excessive gambling and drinking.\n\nSuch a regimen in terms of behaviour throughout service has ensured an acceptable standard of moral discipline within the Yunnan Army's ranks, albeit one that has varied in regards to the degree of punishment should these rules not be followed as well as how harshly the policies are enforced.\n\nWith the need for reform of the Jingguojun back on the agenda, a debate has quickly risen within the military structure regarding how integral these regulations are, with many claiming that they detract from the already varied role of officers within the army. Contrary to this, advocates for military professionalism have continued to push for the legacy of the Yunnan Army as one that remains dedicated to the nation, the people, and their cause.
The Yunnan Army's reputation must be preserved!
These bureaucratic rules detract from the important role an officer plays.
Soul of the Rapid Offensive
In the formulation of an effective war-winning strategy, commanders have to integrate both the strategic theory they had been taught within the academies with the military, political and economic realities of China. From the first decade of the Republic, two patterns of warfare began to emerge: one for those wars fought in the interior, and another for those fought on the coastline. The two styles differed due to geography, logistics, modern armaments, and communications. Although in general, the common strategic objective of both strategies was the capture of key communication hubs, such as towns and cities that controlled key rivers, mountain passes, and railroad or road networks.\n\nIn contrast to the interior, Eastern China is blessed with more sufficient infrastructure yielding to better communication networks and railways. Such networks enabled efficient mobilisation and transport of troops and equipment. Thus developed the crucial status of those networks into contested military objectives, also determining the size and location of battlefields. Wars there became far more intense, and with the better acquisition of modern arms, turned them into slaughters.\n\nHowever the interior lacked utterly in both railroad and roads, with Yunnan possessing 267 miles of road compared to Guangdong's 2440. Therefore, a strategy for the interior's wars was the shared priority of communication hubs, however armies had to utilise rivers for transport, alongside the splitting of their armies into small detachments, little heavy equipment, restricted reconnaissance and difficulties in coordination.\n\nWith such restrictions, decisive, aggressive actions have been the preference for two decades, yet with the slow industrialisation of the interior, it has proved to become increasingly negligible in turning the tide of battle, with its risks potentially capsizing entire regimes, as what nearly happened to Tang Jiyao in 1926.
Decisive action wins Wars!
The Northern Expedition proved the folly of pure aggression.

To this end, the new Kunming has once again enacted strict discipline amongst all of its students, rebuking them for selfish and individualistic habits such as consorts, gambling, drugs, and jealousy of their commanders. Previous Generations of Yunnan Cadets
Promulgated under Tang Jiyao's initiatives on December 25th, 1922, the first erm of the Yunnan Aviation School officially opened with thirty students, who were all organised into an aviation recruit team, and trained together with the 17th class of recruits from the Kunming Military Academy. In April 1923, Tang Jiyao would later approve the admission of four Korean students to the class, Li Yingmao, Zhang Zhiri, Li Chun, and the first female pilot, Quan Jiyu, otherwise known as Kwon Ki-ok.\n\nMaintaining a two year curriculum, the Yunnan Aviation School featured six months of military training, following by one and a half years of aviation training. From 1922 to 1935, the Yunnan Aviation School opened four flight classes, and two mechanic classes, with 142 people graduating from the flight department (including 13 female pilots), and 100 from the mechanic department.\n\nWith 30 graduates from the Fourth class in 1935, a new class for a new era of the school has been drafted, seeing extensive recruitment from both inside and outside Yunnan. Alongside this, the staff of the revitalised school has sent out recruitment letters to old students and faculty alike, to moderate reception.

The Marked history of Wujiaba
Wujiaba, located four kilometres south-east of Kunming, is situated on the bank of the Dianchi Lake. Originally a wasteland, overgrown with weeds for thousands of years, the area did not even feature a place name. Later several families with the surname Wu would travel to the area, reclaiming it and settling there, with the village that developed gaining the moniker \

Yunnanese Affairs in the Colonial Administration
Reports have flooded in from border guards across the Yunnan-Indochina border, and those who travel the Kunming-Hanoi railway, of a series of mysterious shipments leaving Yunnan's provincial borders, and disappearing within the Indochinese miasma. Perhaps these reported shipments are being supplied by [YUN.GetLeader] to colonial resistance under the auspices of ejecting our administration from his immediate borders, ultimately losing our monopoly on the resources of the province.\n\nHowever there is also the possibility that such occurrences are merely the incompetence of Yunnanese officials to properly demarcate where their trade goods are ending up. Nonetheless, many officials have advised [ROOT.GetLeader] that it would perhaps be viable to completely shut off all trade between the colonies and Yunnan, as it would potentially sink the fragile Yunnanese economy, and cease the shipments.\n\nOthers however, advise that such action may inflame tensions even further, and that whether the Yunnanese support the INC or not does not matter, for the Indochinese National Congress will never be able to beat the Kaiserliche Marine on the battlefield.

Blood of Martyrs
The legacy of the revolutionary struggle in China is a long and arduous one, however it remains emblematic of the sheer determination of the nation's patriots to fight for its salvation. From the precursor revolts before Xinhai, to 1911 itself, to the Constitutional Protection Movement, to the National Protection War, to the Northern Expedition. China's revolutionary martyrs are innumerous, uncountable. The very cause is built upon a mountain of their corpses. Yet fully acknowledging this fact, our brave soldiers continue to sacrifice their hopes, their dreams, everything they thought had meaning, all for the revolutionary cause.\n\nThis is not any more evident than in the memory of the fallen, and in particular, the memory of the young Ca Guanzhu. Perishing in 1925, he had written a letter addressed to his brother, the day before giving his life to the National Revolution.\n\n\
Much more blood will be spilt.

Anniversary of Mianhu
One of the most important battles of the early National Revolutionary Army, the Battle of Mianhu is heralded as the foundational event of the Whampoa spirit within the army. Taking place in February of 1925, in the First Eastern Expedition to eject Chen Jiongming from Guangdong, the forces mustered by the Kuomintang was that of the Gui Army under Liu Zhenhuan, the Yunnan Army in Guangdong under Yang Ximin, the fresh teaching regiments of Whampoa under He Yingqin and Wang Boling, alongside the Guangdong Army under Xu Chongzhi.\n\nQuickly capturing swathes of territory, defeating Hong Zhaolin, the Yunnan Army and Gui Army ultimately held back their forces, causing the Whampoa regiments to face more than 20,000 elite troops with little more than 3000. Triumphing magnificently against Lin Hu's soldiers, the heavy casualties and tenacity seen here served to further bolster the morale of the new NRA, and the teaching regiments would form the basis of the NRA 1st Army.\n\nHe Yingqin has recently held a rally for veterans of the battle, stressing the importance of the victory but also the manner in which they achieved it. Viewing it as the key to the revolution, he reportedly rallied for the revival of the \

An Overall Strategic View of Chinese Armies
In terms of the military thought process for the Yunnan Army, three titans have emerged as progenitors of its strategy. The first, was Li Genyuan, founding principal of Kunming Military Academy, Li would be the driving force behind the Yunnan Army's bureaucratic selfless militarism. Second, was Cai E. A name that is titanic in of itself, Cai would lead Yunnan in its most pivotal moments, crushing the armies of Yuan Shikai, Sichuan, and any others who dared to face the Huguojun. Lastly, there was Tang Jiyao. Whilst most would decry him as an incompetent buffoon, there was merit in his best attempts to preserve the soul and merit of his Jingguojun, however its fate was already sealed.\n\nThe second of that trio, Cai E, was perhaps one of China's greatest military tacticians and strategists of the modern era. Equalisation of Command Posts
Imperative to the successes of the Yunnan Army and the National Revolutionary Army, the aspect of training and instruction is frequently overlooked in warlord armies. However, with Zhu De prescribing a systematic approach to his officer and soldiers, he has begun to advocate for the adoption of recently developed Western doctrines and systems. His methodical, detail-oriented approach to military training and teaching has been evident in the directives he has been issuing to the army. \

Strategy and Tactics of the New NRA
Based upon the strategic theory of the likes of Zhu De, Chen Yi and Tang Huaiyuan, the new National Revolutionary Army has seen an overwhelming influx of focus on speed and mobility as being the greatest assets to assure victory. That it should avoid combat with stronger enemy units until conditions were at their most favourable to launch an attack. Realising that the NRA units would be high priority targets for any opposing force, the commanders have advocated for the concentration of Red Army units in difficult terrain deep within Kuomintang territory, to entice enemy forces to advance.\n\nWith enemy forces usually advancing in an uncoordinated fashion, along separate routes, over difficult terrain, and in unfamiliar territory, they would be ripe targets for a surprise counter-attack against isolated battalions. At the tactical level, such a strategy invokes the need for local guerilla units around various villages, whilst the operational level necessitates NRA units to manoeuvre over large areas that could encompass several provinces. A novel idea, such strategies saw proto-use in the MinGan insurgent zone, proving effective there.\n\nHowever opposition to such strategies are multi-faceted, as it comes from both civilian and military sections of the party. Civilian cadres fear that such deep enemy incursions into KMT territory would see significant damage done to the economy, population and local institutions, whilst army hardliners fear that any strategic benefits would be declared null and void, if the NRA's own units end up encircled.
Interesting, but highly impractical.
It is the only way to beat a superior enemy!

Naturally starting with offensive objectives. Indicating that the army is harder to replace than [ROOT.Capital.GetCapitalVictoryPointName] itself. Defensive Objectives of the Party Army

Focusing on more defensive matters this time around, Zhu has derived there to be a new way of waging war for the National Revolutionary Army.\n\nOne should initially focus on defending either base areas or army headquarters from attempted hostile military invasion, with three critical objectives to maintain: preservation of the defensive area, preservation of the military defence force, and annihilation of all or part of the attacking force.\n\nWhilst retreating into the defensive zone, the NRA should manoeuvre themselves into a favourable position, and grant themselves time for the preparation of military assets and the concentration of manpower. The further an enemy pursued, the more frail his supply and communication lines, and the lower his morale. Berlin's Response
In the twilight decades of the Qing Dynasty, much of the country was carved up into economic spheres. Just as the Northeast was put under Russian and later Japanese influence, Yunnan was brought into the French sphere. When the Qing Dynasty finally fell and the warlord era began, many different warlords sought different foreign backers. Duan Qirui and the Anhui Clique developed a relationship with the Japanese, and was nearly entirely reliant on Tokyo's finances and military aid.\n\nThe Old Marshal in Shenyang also reached out to the Japanese, but his powerful economic base gave him the liberties to reach out to a multitude of benefactors. Yunnan under Cai E undertook a concerted effort of militarisation in the pursuit of unification by military means, but Yunnan's geographical position dictated that the province's military ambitions would be at the mercy of Paris, Kunming's only weapon supplier. When the French Republic fell to revolution and the Germans took over French Indochina, Berlin replaced Paris' economic dominance over Yunnan with their own as well as their position as Kunming's sole weapons supplier.\n\nEver since Berlin's China Policy brought Beijing closer to German interests, Tang's weapon supplies became less and less frequent in order to keep the Yunnanese Army in check. On top of Tang's ambitions, Yunnan's lack of Vietnamese bandit policing was also not to Berlin's favour. However, with the collapse of Zhili Hegemony, Germany's interests in China are greatly threatened and Governor Tang could always sooth Berlin's concerns by promising to use the gun shipments to combat banditry and maintain the German order in Southwestern China...
I can't believe that worked!
Tin and Rails
While Yunnan remains poor, backwards, and agricultural, the province is incredibly rich with natural resources. When the French colonised southwestern China, they immediately began opening up Yunnan to commercialisation. The cities of Mengzi, Manhao, and Longzhou were established in Yunnan and Guangxi. After the Sino-Japanese War, the French secured a 99-year lease on Guangzhouwan, as well as more railroad and mining rights. The French would build the Kunming - Haiphong Rail, otherwise known as the Dianyue Iron Road.\n\nThis rail whilst boosting Paris' modernist agenda massively boosted transportation between French Indochina and Kunming, and the Gejiu to Jijie section of the rail became the most profitable part of the rail and massively increased French exports. Before its construction, Gejiu's exports never exceeded four thousand tons, but from two years after the rail's construction all the way to the French Revolution, Gejiu exported over ten thousand tons annually. Regardless of export revenues, the rail was not particularly profitable but the benefits it provided made it a worthwhile venture for Paris.\n\nBefore the rail was built, the French relied on intermediaries and middle men in the tin trade, but the rail made them obsolete and delivered all commercial benefits directly to the Indochina Colonial Administration. While Germany's Colonial Indochinese Administration has been less successful at consolidating control, Berlin has maintained full control over the Dianyue Iron Road, and maintains the same level of interest in resource extraction as Paris did. While Yunnan's future direction is very much contested, there is full agreement that Germany's foreign economic dominance must be broken.
One day we'll be free...
A History of Financial Hardship
Yunnan even while never especially rich and prosperous was devastated without respite in the 19th century. In 1855, the Bubonic Plague which ravaged the world twice before struck Yunnan province for 15 years, killing millions. Shortly after, Yunnan province was engulfed in the Hui-led Du Wenxiu Rebellion, where a million on both sides died. The population drop caused mining to drop, which in turn caused the currency to devalue, which led to rampant banditry. Han and Hui tensions remained high after the Hui Rebellion, as well as a distrust for foreigners due to British support for Du Wenxiu. Anti-foreign sentiment led to the death of a British national possibly on the orders of the provincial government, which provoked the British into renegotiating the 1858 Tianjin Treaty.\n\nThe new treaty allowed London to increase trade from Burma into Yunnan and lease certain border regions, which further inflamed anti-foreigner sentiment. When the French began moving into Vietnam, Chinese mercenaries were hired by the Vietnamese government to fight off the French. 50 thousand Chinese soldiers would enter Vietnam to resist the French, but Paris secured control over the region regardless. The British and French developed a syndicate to control the mining rights of Mengzi, which continued until the French and British Revolutions, and fell into the hands of the AOG. Nearly a century has passed since the Bubonic Plague ravaged Yunnan, but the province remains unhealed.
The story of suffering is all too common in China.
Reclaiming Economic Agency
Ever since the Xinhai Revolution, economic reform has been unanimously hailed as the primary goal for the Yunnanese provincial government. The road to economic reform remains hazardous and rife with treachery however. The last Qing viceroy of the Yun-Gui region Li Jingxi attempted to revitalise Yunnanese mining and restore Yunnanese industry, but alienated the gentry in the process. The gentry forced Li to cut back on government spending and kept his reforms contained to Kunming and Mengzi.\n\nLi Jingxi's former subordinates, Cai E and Tang Jiyao, inherited Li's legacy and agenda in the wake of the Xinhai Revolution but faced many of the same challenges. Conservative elites and weak revenue streams continually hampered the Governorship, and Cai E's militarisation programme only exacerbated the problems. Regardless, the ambitions for economic development remain strong even to this day, and [ROOT.GetLeader]'s administration hopes that the Yunnan Economic Commission will succeed where earlier reforms failed.\n\nIt is widely understood that heavy economic intervention is required in the Southwest, for incentivising foreigners to build factories and then reverse-engineering western technologies as is done in Eastern China is infeasible. The immediate five undertakings of the Economic Commission are to develop cotton spinning and weaving, hydro-electric power, cement manufacturing, light engineering, and critical investments. While just ambitious goals at the moment, there is a current of hope that perhaps Yunnan's century of strife may finally be coming to an end.
Best of luck to the Economic Commission.
A Nationalist Junta in the Southwest
In the immediate aftermath of the anti-Tang Jiyao coup, the Kuomintang wasted no time in establishing themselves in Kunming. While Zhu Peide has taken up the position of Governor (translation note: the Kuomintang referred to Provincial Executives as Chairmen, but for simplicity they will henceforth be referred to as Governor), He Yingqin, Long Yun, and Hu Ruoyu exercise substantial authority in the administration as well. Although the Kuomintang is the dominant power in Yunnan, Zhu Peide recognised the need to align the local power players in the province, including the gentry. However, not all is well for the nascent Yunnanese Nationalist Junta. Student radical groups who were initially enthusiastic about the Kuomintang takeover found themselves severely disappointed by this moderating position.\n\nIn an ironic twist, student groups found themselves in cooperation with Tang Jiyao's loyalist remnants in opposing the new Kunming Administration. The anti-Kuomintang agitation was such that the Chamber of Commerce reported several instances of disruption. On top of marginalised groups outside of the government, Zhu Peide finds opposition from inside his government too. Long Yun, while the instigator of the anti-Tang coup, hopes that the Kuomintang rule in Yunnan is merely temporary, as the Kuomintang ambitions lie in Guangdong, the birthplace of the National Revolution.\n\nHu Ruoyu, while far more amenable to the Kuomintang takeover, has similar views and has ambitions to take the province for himself. However, the 3rd Army of the NRA, which Zhu Peide leads, displays substantial regionalist sentiment, preferring to consolidate Nationalist control of Yunnan. On the other hand, He Yingqin and the 1st Army of the NRA have pushed hard for a military campaign west for the restoration of a Nationalist presence in Guangzhou. To them, Yunnan is but the stepping stone to the true base of the KMT. If Zhu Peide is to govern Yunnan well, he will have to balance the competing interests of his subordinates, or fall as Tang Jiyao did.
The consolidation of Yunnan is the primary concern of the Kuomintang!
Yunnan alone cannot sustain the ambitions of the Kuomintang, Guangzhou must be secured!

The Kuomintang's Legacy in Yunnan
The roots of the KMT in Yunnan go as far back as the existence of the party itself. One of the leading organisers of the Tongmenghui, Li Genyuan, became the supervisor of the Kunming Military Academy in 1909. When the Wuchang Uprising occurred, Li Genyuan worked with Cai E and Tang Jiyao to launch a similar revolt in Yunnan and declare for the revolution. Li spent the 1910s opposing Yuan Shikai, and worked with Cen Chunxuan to form the Old Political Science Clique. The Old Political Science Clique, which would be dedicated towards mending relations between Beijing and Guangzhou, would find itself in opposition to Sun Yat-sen, Tang Jiyao, and Chen Jiongming.\n\nHowever, Tang had alienated Li Genyuan's protégé Gu Pinzhen by favouring his own brother Tang Jiyu, which prompted Gu to force Tang Jiyao to resign as Governor. When Tang prepared to make his return, his allies Li Liejun and Zhu Peide broke with him. While Tang and Gu were fighting, Sun schemed to have Jin Handing take Yunnan, but that ended in failure and Tang retook Yunnan. The Yunnanese KMT in the aftermath of Gu's death was exiled to Guangzhou and coalesced into the 3rd Army, with Jin Handing and Fan Shisheng's armies being integrated into the 3rd Army in the 30s.\n\nAll emerging from different roots, Zhu Peide, Fan Shisheng, Jin Handing, and the rising star Zhu De all demonstrate great political diversity as a result. Fan Shisheng and Jin Handing are considered to not be particularly ideological. Zhu De's contingent of the 3rd Army displaces socialist tendencies. Zhu Peide himself finds himself very much in line with the Old Right. Thus, Zhu's decision to bring back Li Genyuan to Yunnan at the behest of Fan, Jin, and Zhang Qun will in all likelihood be a hugely controversial decision, as the latter betrayed Sun all those years ago. He Yingqin and the Whampoa have already made their dissatisfaction clear to the Generalissimo.
Li has obviously atoned his mistakes, he is our ally!
His betrayal of the Guofu cannot be forgotten...
Malice in Victory
With the Kuomintang largely settled in its affairs in Kunming, the Generalissimo now turns his attention to the Yunnanese Bureaucracy. The various bureaus across Yunnan undoubtedly have some affinity for their old governor, a fact Zhu Peide would be a fool to ignore. To remove them would be even more troubling still. While Hu Ruoyu and Long Yun ushered the National Revolution into Kunming, they largely expect a continuation of the status quo. Yunnan's political history has always been somewhat more stable than its northern neighbours, and there has always been a contiguous line of reformism undertaken by its governors.\n\nAfter Viceroy Li Jingxi was ousted by the Xinhai Revolution, Cai E and Tang Jiyao maintained Li's political agenda, albeit with a more militaristic tilt. Various bureaus were established in the name of tax collection, economic development, and constitutionalism. The Kuomintang has historically been a force based in Guangdong, and is not descended from the political traditions of Yunnan. For Long Yun and Hu Ruoyu, maintaining the Yunnanese Bureaucracy is even more important due to its impacts on the military. The Yunnanese Army has long been a bureaucratic institution, with each army not just being bound by loyalty, but by the fact that commands came from higher up in the name of the entire institution.\n\nAuthority was not in the person but the position, and positions were a pyramid hierarchy. However, the Kuomintang's Revolutionary agenda is not necessarily compatible with Yunnan's bureaucratic tradition. To Kuomintang radicals, Yunnan's bureaucracy is a messy hodgepodge of Gentry interests and warlordism. Hu Ruoyu has taken it upon himself to appeal to Zhu Peide to find a compromise which will preserve Yunnan's bureaucratic machine, having already reached out to Zhu De, Fan Shisheng and Jin Handing, hoping that Zhu's respect for Cai E and the other two's more apolitical outlook.
Yunnan's Bureaucracy will now serve the National Revolution.
The National Revolution will not utilise the playthings of warlords!
Whampoa's Last Stand
While Sun found success in proliferating the cause of the Tongmenghui across Southern China during the Xinhai Revolution, the divergence of interests caused many to turn away from his call for a Second Revolution. Those that remained would form the core of Sun's inner circle. When Sun returned to Guangzhou, he spent years dealing with wayward allies and opportunistic warlords who would betray his cause on a dime. Cynical from the betrayals and lack of western support, Sun came to court the Syndicalist Powers for an alliance, and the Internationale Mission helped Sun reorganise the KMT into a disciplined, revolutionary party.\n\nOn the military end, Sun required a devoted military dedicated to his ideological goals and prioritised the party above all else, and so he created the Whampoa Academy. Sun chose his most loyal disciple, Chiang Kai-shek, to be the Headmaster and train the first generation of Whampoa officers. Bringing the teachings of the Baoding Academy and Imperial Japanese Army Academy, Chiang and his old classmate He Yingqin would instil every aspect of warfare and the core ideological goal of the National Revolution and the tenets of the Three Principles of the People to their Whampoa Cadets.\n\nThe Whampoa Clique would bring Chiang Kai-shek to prominence within the Kuomintang and achieve many victories in the Northern Expedition, but suffered greatly when German and Zhili forces collapsed on them from all sides and their beloved headmaster was slain. He Yingqin brought his portion of Whampoa remnants in the First Army to Guizhou, which houses some of the most ideological officers within the NRA. While merely a ragtag force at the moment, it would be a mistake to compare them to other rump warlord armies, a point the Whampoa Clique would be quick to point out.
The interests of the Whampoa Clique must be respected...
What a pretentious grouping of moralising cronies...
Zhejiang's Revolutionaries
By the 1900s, the Green Gang controlled all crime activity in Shanghai. The Green Gang became involved with the Kuomintang when Chen Qimei joined them in 1908. A few years prior, Chen had been working in Japan for Sun Yat-sen, and met fellow Zhejiangers Chiang Kai-shek and Dai Jitao. During the Xinhai Revolution, Dai, Chiang, and Chen all participated in the Shanghai Uprising. Chiang, Chen, and Dai operated in Shanghai and formed the Zhejiang Revolutionary Comrades Society. The trio fled to Japan after the failure of Sun's Second Revolution, and made an impression on Sun as one of the few revolutionaries who swore an oath of allegiance to him.\n\nIn 1916, Chen was assassinated by Zhang Zongchang. Chiang mentored Chen Qimei's nephew Chen Guofu thereafter to honour his death. The ZRCS resumed its Shanghai activities after Yuan Shikai's death, often collaborating with the Green Gang, including setting up illegal stock exchanges. Chiang spent much of his time with stocks until he lost a small fortune, while Dai would write fervently in newspapers. In 1924, Sun recognised Chiang's loyalty and made him the Headmaster of the Whampoa Military Academy. After Sun's death in 1925, Chiang became one of the leading figures in the Kuomintang, alongside Wang Jingwei.\n\nDai Jitao and the Chen brothers would also rise to high positions, and alongside Zhejianger Whampoa Alumni they formed the nucleus of Chiang's inner circle. The rise of the ZRCS in the Kuomintang was broken by the death of Chiang Kai-shek, and much of his vast array of associates and networks crumbled. Dai Jitao and the Chen brothers fled to Shanghai in the chaos of the NRA collapse, and continued their earlier Shanghai activities underground until the Kuomintang seized Yunnan. While Dai and the Chen brothers now work in Kunming, they maintain their connections to the Green Gang as well as their less savoury business operations, such as the illegal stock exchange.
The Green Gang remains a true ally of the National Revolution!
You'll never find a more wretched hive of scum and villainy than in Shanghai...

In a lavish Chongqing ceremony, he assumed command of the Allied Army of Seven Provinces, nominally also of Guangdong, Guangxi, Sichuan, Hunan and Shanxi. His attempt at a Northern Expedition was beset by infighting as power gradually shifted from Kunming to Guangzhou. Funding ran out, forcing them to rely on opium and banditry. Crushing defeats against both Beiyang and Guangzhou aligned governments forced them into isolation as they bid their time.\n\nNow, seeing favourable winds, Governor Tang Jiyao has led his forces into battle - eliminating the Kuomintang remnant holed up in Guizhou. Managing the Regionalists
The gentry and merchants are essential pillars of power in virtually all provinces throughout China, and Yunnan is no different. After rebellions of the mid-1800s, the gentry took a lead role in the reconstruction of the province, allowing them to take control over Yunnan's administration, finances, and mining/trade networks.\n\nThis power allowed them to dominate local assemblies, which they used to fight both the central government's bureaucracy and the exploitation of foreign corporations. Their control over local politics and economy made them a potent force of both reform and reaction - the 1911 Li Jingxi reforms were scuttled everywhere outside of Kunming and Mengzi due to gentry concerns over the deficit. Around the same time, their discontent over the British occupation of Pianma allowed them to organise the Baojiehui (Society for the Protection of Territory) massive boycotts against the British and call for revolution against the weak central government, leading to many Baojiehui members joining the revolutionary Tongmenghui.\n\nThis push and pull between reform and reaction as well as regionalism and nationalism continues today. The Yunnan gentry and merchants are reluctant to back significant reforms that would alter the balance of power in the countryside, yet insist on some form of constitutionalism and an elitist democracy in the form of the provincial assembly. They are hostile towards the prospect of war and the socio-economic disruption it entails, but also seek the unification of the country on patriotic grounds. Yet in this unification they desire considerable regional autonomy that no central government appears willing to give. It will take a lot of convincing to secure their support for a unification war.
It is our national duty to liberate our countrymen!
We fight in order to defend our right to self-rule!
Phases of the Crescent Moon
The waning Crescent Moon Society can be found teaching in Kunming's universities. Founded in 1923 by Xu Zhimo as a fashionable upper-middle class intellectual social club with a shared interest in dramatic theatre, the loose collection of friends attracted liberal scholars such as Hu Shih and Zhang Junmai, and quickly rivaled the realist-oriented Literary Association and the romantic-oriented Creation Society. They took a middle road amid New Culture iconoclasm, advocating for a re-assessment of Chinese tradition while evaluating Western culture in a rational manner.\n\nLuo Longji, Wen Yiduo, Liang Shiqiu and the Qinghua Literature Society soon joined the CMS orbit and were targeted together by their enemies in the socialist League of Left Wing Writers (successors of the Creation Society). The Second Dian-Gui War
[YUN.GetLeader] has made the fateful decision to go to war, ordering his troops to cross the border out of Yunnan and Guizhou. While his ultimate destination is the critical city of Guangzhou, which he needs to proclaim a rival government, his men's immediate target is Nanning - the Capital of Guangxi and a vital logistics hub along the way.\n\nDuring the first Yunnan-Guangxi War in 1925 (Dian is the abbreviation of Yunnan, Gui is the abbreviation of Guangxi), triggered by the death of Sun Yat-sen and the ensuing power struggle, the Yunnan Army under Long Yun was able to capture the city but were encircled during the Guangxi counter-attack led by Li Zongren. A failed relief effort by Tang Jiyao and the subsequent recapture of the city by the New Guangxi Clique ended the war as Yunnanese morale collapsed. This had fateful implications for Southern China as the disgraced Governor Tang narrowly avoided being overthrown by his disgruntled generals while the Guangzhou government was able to complete its reorganisation.\n\nThe coming war is expected to be similarly decisive. Yunnan, Guizhou, and Guangxi are all heavily militarised provinces but much poorer than Guangdong. If the Yunnan-Guizhou forces fail to move aggressively and knock out Guangxi quickly, they risk severe consequences down the line. On the other hand, should Guangxi and its rugged defensive lines fall, Guangdong will be vulnerable.
We must move quickly to achieve victory!

The city, though damaged from conflict, remains a potent symbol for revolutionary forces in China: it was home to several southern governments seeking to challenge Beijing's dominance in the 20th Century.\n\nWith multiple southern provinces under his thumb, Governor Tang has been empowered to call a conference where he is expected to become \

The Grand Republican Alliance
The Grand Republican Alliance was a joint project first founded by the sidelined Chen Jiongming and Duan Qirui in 1931 amid swelling anti-concessionism and disillusionment following the failure of Zhili-Fengtian reunification talks. Tang Jiyao and Zhang Taiyan's revitalised PGP joined the GRA along with Chu Fucheng's ZAM the following year as Zhili authority began to recede amid the 1932 Shanghai Uprisings. Others, wary of offending Zhili authorities, operated in parallel - including Zhao Hengti's PRS and Xiong Kewu's PFAPO.\n\nThe GRA's identity as a Federalist coalition took shape in the ensuing years, helping keep Federalism alive even as many began to see it as a disgraced, unpatriotic ideology. The Cao Kun Constitution's nominal provisions for provincial autonomy led many to question whether or not Federalism had run its course, and if it was merely an excuse for provincial warlords to continue to divide the country.\n\nThe failure of the Zhili not only to follow through with their constitutionalist promises, but even to keep the country together has allowed Federalism to get a second look by many in China. While not as radical as at the height of the 1920s movement, the revitalised GRA has attracted many disgruntled democrats who are former participants in the Federation of Chinese Democratic Parties and witnessed first-hand the failure of the FCDP to coax democratic change out of Beiyang.\n\nThe GRA vows to unite warlords, intellectuals, and other Chinese patriots to build a federal democracy. But before it can do that, it must resolve some simmering tensions within its ranks. Chen Jiongming is the incumbent chairman of the GRA as head of the PIP and had offered Tang the Vice-Chairmanship in the past, which he was ambivalent about accepting as to avoid subjugating himself to Chen. Now that Tang has emerged on top, it would only make sense for him to take on leadership of the GRA. But forcing Chen out would cause him to lose face, and alienate a potential ally.
Tang is our undisputed leader, and will easily win an election.
Leave Chen as nominal Chairman as an olive branch.
Embers of the FCDP
After the 1936 Seven Gentlemen Incident, many southern republicans disgusted by the Federation of Chinese Democratic Parties's muted response abandoned the coalition altogether. The largest party of the FCDP and the target of considerable ire was the Chinese Constitutionalist Party, the conservative successors of Liang Qichao and well integrated into the Beiyang establishment. Many left-Liangists, social democrats, and progressive liberals in the south gathered in Guangzhou to found the Democratic Socialist Party shortly afterwards.\n\nThe DSP is made up of two major factions. The first and more dominant one was the National Renaissance Society faction led by Zhang Junmai and Zhang Dongsun. Both were European educated reformist socialists who attempted to synthesise Eastern philosophy with Western political developments. The second was the Crescent Moon Society led by Luo Longji, Wen Yiduo and Liang Shiqiu, a more humanist-liberal oriented group educated in the United States. Both factions drew support from the intelligentsia and middle class professionals.\n\nThe party has vowed to \

Luo Longji Emerges
Zhang Junmai's downfall was predictable given the circumstances, but the DSP's rapid consolidation around Luo Longji's leadership was a surprise. Though Zhang initially vowed to fight for his position, under pressure he withdrew and fully endorsed Luo.\n\nLuo Longji grew up in Jiangxi after the Hundred Days Reforms were crushed by Empress Cixi. He attended Tsinghua University and became a student leader during the November 4th Protests. Brave and enthusiastic, he also had a pragmatic side - hiring a burly student-athlete to serve as his bodyguard. Later he enrolled in Columbia University, writing his PhD dissertation on \

Stewardship of Yunnan
With Tang Jiyao and much of his staff relocating to Guangzhou, the lingering question is who will manage day to day operations in Yunnan. The province, while poorer than Guangdong, remains Tang's core power-base and one he cannot afford to lose lest his house of cards come quickly crashing down. The Tang Clique's hold on the province also remains unsteady given Long Yun and Hu Ruoyu's questionable loyalties, though the situation is much improved from what it was before the expedition to Guangzhou.\n\nZhou Zhongyue would be the steady choice, a senior advisor in Tang's provincial government for many years. An ethnic Bai and a fellow Japanese-educated politician, Zhou was trained to be an educator but was recruited into Cai E's office as Secretary-General and Chief of Education, on top of several major economic roles. He was also briefly governor from 1919-1920. A well-cultured elder statesman, appointing him governor would be a natural continuation of Tang's rule.\n\nDong Ze is Tang Jiyao's brother-in-law. Another ethnic Bai, he served as secretary in the Governor's office before Cai E sent him to study abroad in Columbia University. He also served as a liaison to Hong Kong during the National Protection War. An educator in Yunnan University and later Director of the Education Department, he was known for hiring foreign educated scholars and promoting democratic school management. Despite the nepotism, he may be seen as a more liberal choice for the province.\n\nLastly there is Tang Xiaoying, Tang Jiyao's son. A Japanese educated economist, he has frequently been seen at his father's side during his political rallies and is reported as having sympathies to the YCP and FCDP. He has also had various minor roles in administration and the military. With Tang Jiyu now situated in Guizhou, he might be Tang Jiyao's closest relative and he is a fresh face in politics. This may lead to significant tensions however as some question his ability to control the unruly petty warlords.
Zhou Zhongyue is an establishment choice.
Dong Ze balances reform with stability.
Tang Xiaoying will follow in his father's footsteps.
A New Federalist Government
After several weeks of deliberations, the final outline of this latest Guangzhou Government has taken shape. It will be built along Federalist lines, vowing to respect provincial autonomy and unite the provinces - through diplomacy first, by force if necessary. Tang Jiyao naturally assumed the Presidency after a speedy vote, he also named Chen Jiongming as his Vice President to appease the Cantonese Federalists.\n\nA provisional legislature has been established, and Zhang Taiyan tasked with organising them as Prime Minister. Despite their similarities, there have already been some clear differences in their governing philosophies. Tang has advocated for a stronger central government, capable of fighting foreign imperialism and that can also play referee between the different provinces. Zhang has at times called for the near abolishment of the central government, arguing that China is too large to manage and prior central governments were prone to selling out to foreign powers - he has sought to empower the various provinces instead. Chen and most others have adopted a more moderate stance between them, and the new government is expected to operate as a compromise between the two extremes, held together by common fears of external attack.\n\nThere are many challenges ahead for the new government. The provinces across China are taking sides in the looming battle for reunification. Many question whether or not the Federalist regime will be able to resolve its internal contradictions, and whether they can do so peacefully.
Federalism rises again.
Yunnan Federalists Unite the South
Guangzhou has been home to multiple southern governments across the warlord era, each hoping to break Beijing's dominance. The latest leader to declare himself a contender is Tang Jiyao, who has rallied several southern provinces under his banner. Aligning with various warlords, intellectuals, gentrymen, and merchants, this new government claims to be fighting for a federal democracy in China - a reversal of fortunes for a movement many wrote off as doomed and unpatriotic a decade ago.\n\nStill, many question whether or not Tang's rule will be meaningfully different from those the tyranny he claims to fight. His authoritarian, adaptable, and sometimes progressive rule has won him both admirers and detractors throughout the years.

The Tang Jiyao Government
In the ever expanding Yunnanese bureaucracy that now encompasses Southern China, Tang Jiyao's bureaucracy and government has been ever growing. His inner circle are still made up of his family members such as his brother in law Dong Ze, his brother Tang Jiyu, and his son Tang Xiaoying. His secretaries maintain critical positions within the government such as Wang Jiuling, Li Yukan, and Bai Zhihan and are close confidents to the general's secrets and plans.\n\nThe PGP and Zhang Taiyan are championed as the political base of the Party, a mix of Federalist and liberal intellectuals and legislators. They also by extension include the now merged Grand Republican Alliance, an umbrella of pro-Federlaist groups. While the GRA is a prima facie democratic coalition beset on ending years of instability and autocracy; Tang is worried in regards to the factionalism within the groups. In particular, the Vice President, Chen Jiongming's Cantonese and Hakka origins holds sway in Guangdong while Zhao Hengti, the Federalist governor of Hunan has long ruled the province.\n\nThere is also the military, which is dominated by the Yunnan Army but has been forced to supplement themselves with absorbed military forces, militias, and even bandits. Even the original Yunnan Army was not fully loyal to Tang, with Long Yun and Hu Ruoyu proving to be less than trustworthy deputies. In their absence, Tang has staffed his military leadership with favorable candidates such as Zhang Weihan and Sun Du, who bring with them - both experience and loyalty to the ever evolving regime.

Luo Longji Organises the Crescent Moon
Since his publications on human rights, Luo Longji has acheieved a name for himself within the southern liberals and democratic bloc. As the now rising star of the liberal movement, he has been at work with slowly repairing the connections between the Crescent Moon Society (CMS) and the old Federation of Chinese Democratic Parties (the coalition of various independents, minor parties, and cliques - who are following the federalist movement but wary of the potential of authoritarianism). In addition to organising the day to day operations of the FCDP, Luo also has been hard at work in publishing articles and opinions in the Minzhu Zhoukan (Democratic Weekly) and Minzhu Ribao (Democratic Daily News); sometimes containing a tint of leftist admiration in his writings in support of the democratic principles behind the Second French Revolution.\n\nWith new found power and role, comes new found responsibility in preparation for national unification. A History of Federalism in China
The first stage of federalism (1895-1911) drew on from the observance of Chinese intelligentsia who looked to the United States and Switzerland. Liang Qichao led the initial charge by stating that federalism did not make a vast country like China weaker and the Tongmenghui echoed these calls by declaring that China deserved a republican government. Provincial assemblies operated de facto independent of Beijing's government and some provinces even flirted with the idea of autonomous provinces bound together in a federation.\n\nThe second stage of Federalism (1912-1920) drew upon the betrayal by Yuan Shikai towards the Republic of China. Federalism emerged as a weapon against the Yuan Shikai government, by increasing provincial autonomy and promoting true democratic ideals. After Yuan's death and the subsequent split between the Chinese Nationalist Party and Chinese progressives; federalist ideals largely fell on deaf ears. But in 1917 when reunification talks failed, federalism movements again became popular with politician Xiong Xiling pleading for the provinces to come together to draft a federalist constitution and convene an assembly.\n\nThe third and current stage of Chinese Federalism thus reflects the pinnacle of the federalist revolutionary movement. In 1920, Hunan took the first step and created a provincial constitution. Such ideas were also found to be in tandem with the success of Meiji Japan and the strength of the West. A true democracy is one where popular sovereignty belongs to the people and with powers unreserved given to the provinces. These sentiments were also reflected by the growing regionalism of Chinese politics with cliques and parties now being drawn from loyalty to their home provinces rather than Beijing. Federalist writers argue that the independence of the provinces and their unique strengths will ultimately strengthen the greater nation-state rather than weaken it, by allowing self government and development independent from the halls of Beijing.
Federalism is our way to restore harmony between nation and provinces.
Federalism is a means to the end of a constitutional, democratic republic.
Legacy of November 4th
Following the leaking of documents that exposed the Beiyang government's secret deals trading Shandong for predatory Entente loans in October of 1919, a vast array of national unrest especially in Beijing broke out in November 4th.\n\nThe anti-imperialist outlook of the protests triggered a new wave of intellectualism that flourished and developed contemporary Chinese political thought. Influenced by this evolutionary, teleological notion of time and history, many November 4th intellectuals viewed the modern \

America and the Federalist Movement
The Federalist Movement has historically had two inspirations; the United States of America and the German Empire. Many in the Crescent Moon Society had studied in the United States and early Federalist intellectuals found that the American experiment was the first true federalist democracy. With the collapse of the States into its second civil war, these ideas are of course challenged especially given China's precarious situation.\n\nOne of the predecessors of the Crescent Moon Society was the Great River Association. During the early 1920s, Liang Shiqiu, Wen Yiduo, and Luo Longji had met at the Drexel hotel (not far from where Wen and Liang were students at the University of Chicago). There the men drafted a manifesto and formed the Da Jiang Hui (Great River Association), which they believed symbolised the glorious past of China by likening it to China's historic Yellow River. Their manifesto argued that (1) Nationalism is needed to fight foreign imperialism, (2) Democracy and human rights must be implemented, and (3) a state controlled economy is needed to change the nation and prevent class struggle. This system would be driven by American federalist principles; seeking a strong federal government with checks and balances at each turn to ensure a vibrant democracy.\n\nOn the other hand, Zhang Taiyan rejected the American federalist model as incompatible with China. First, the structure of power of the federalist Chinese republic would be based in local communities. Instead of power centralised at the top in the hands of the premier and the president, the federalist republic would disperse power among the civilian and military leaders in the provinces. Second, the source of power of the federalist republic would be drawn from the local, treating the provinces as sovereign states and creating a confederation based on agreement.Third, there would be a central government implementing national policies and representing China in the \
A democracy just like the American states!
To emulate America is to be un-Chinese.
Red Scare in Southern China
As the federalist revolution continues to grow in southern China, it has naturally come into conflict with the hotbed of the Chinese socialist movement ranging from anarchists, communists, syndicalists, and of course the Kuomintang. From their guerrilla bases in Guangdong and Fujian to the dockyards of Shanghai and Hong Kong, the socialist movement brutally suppressed in 1932 continues to grow as the capitalist world recovers from the aftershocks of Black Monday. From pamphlets to open strikes, the socialists have denounced the Federalist movement as an unholy union between the warlords and the bourgeoisie, staining the glories of the social nationalist revolution with blood soaked hands and capitalist greed.\n\nSome liberals had found an ideological partner within more libertarian socialist groups such as the anarchists. Zhang Taiyan has long written on his idea of a utopian democracy that shares many of the similar views of Chinese anarchists in denouncing feudal Confucian world views in favor of a greater Datong. Originally, like many parties - it began with a group of friends with a vision and a dream; and now encompasses an electric ideology that blends social democracy and right. Federalist Nationalism
Nationalism dominates Chinese political thought in this era of the anti-imperialist struggle. However, for the Federalists this has been a difficult banner to fly under. While of course the Federalist movement hopes to destroy the foreign concessions and drive out China's imperialistic enemies like the German and Japanese Empire; the Federalists must also maintain a careful line with regionalist authorities.\n\nTang Jiyao himself believes in a powerful central state that could manage the province's autonomy but is ultimately devoted to the concept of a nation-state that is able to repel foreign influences. Indeed in Yunnan, nationalism varies from those who seek to be seen as commercial equals with the foreigners and the others who hope to drive the devils from Chinese shores at all costs. A unique nationalistic regionalism has thus emerged by building national autonomy through local development which in turns creates a unique identity.\n\nOn the other hand, Zhang Taiyan holds more...esoteric beliefs. Zhang's concept of nationalism was trapped between conflicting narratives of what a nation is - one that continually is moving forward together, but is somehow also tied to the past. National Essence therefore is the solution to true nationalism. National Essence is one where a nation does not have a state, and one whose presence/return needs institutional embodiment in the state. Local provincial governments may at times be more helpful to deal with foreign governments rather than relying on the bureaucratic nature of a centralised state.
The central government must be strong enough to fight off imperialists!
We can only rely on the collective provinces to fight off imperialism!
Education and Thought Control
Despite being the standard-bearer of the Federalist revolution, Tang is nonetheless still described by his opponents as nothing more than an opportunistic warlord. He has already begun to implement his influence in universities by reforming them and propagating his ideology. The recently constructed Donghu University staffed by Tang Jiyao's brother-in-law mandates a curriculum that is molded and approved by Tang's top officials. Denouncements from the Crescent Moon Society have arisen as a result of reported clashes between Donghu students and leftist students in the provinces.\n\nTang's mass education programme has extended not just to universities but also to popular education, building primary schools, vocational schools, night schools, and lecture schools. Radical students have taken it to organise vernacular and patriotic dramas and plays expressing socialist sentiments which has often led to clashes between the Donghu students. In some cases, military cadets have also reportedly stormed student headquarters and dorms; often to the muted responses from Tang's office.\n\nLiang Shiqiu of the CMS has denounced the suppression against free speech and thought within the provinces. He writes that the result of thought control would drive the people into three groups. In the first group would be those who had ideas but could not compromise their principles with the practice of thought control; consequently, they would either be forced to retire or compelled to join the revolution. The second group were the opportunists, those who had the education but had no principles and were without courage. The last group would consist of those who would not think except in blind obedience. Under such circumstances, the best brains and most courageous ones would be purged; only the opportunists and the ignorant masses remained. As such, Liang in his writings has pleaded for tolerance, freedom of thought, and liberal education.
A fist is needed in these dire times.
But perhaps we should loosen it.
Reflections on Human Rights
In an age of great political thinkers and nationalists eager to revive the Chinese nation; Luo Longji is one of the few prolific liberals within the Chinese nation. Admitted in 1913 to Tsinghua Preparatory School for Studying in the United States in Beijing, Luo became a student leader during the November 4th protests. In 1921, he went to study political science at the University of Wisconsin, Columbia University, and then the London School of Economics and Political Science. Following Sun Chuanfang's purge of the centre left and socialists in 1932, Luo and the Tsinghua University faction found a haven in Yunnan, where they continued to publish liberal critiques of the current government.\n\nIn his latest work \
We'll play our part to support the State!

Allies of the Crescent Moon
In the waning sunlight of democracy in China, a glow emits from the Crescent Moon Society. While the society is dominated nowadays by Luo Longji, Wen Yiduo, and Liang Shiqiu following the untimely death of Xu Zhimo, there are other Chinese intellectuals who have fled to the Southwest in the hopes of finding a safe haven for their ideas.\n\nPan Guangdan is a Tsinghua graduate who studied in the United States. A sociologist, eugenicist, and ethnologist, he is most interested in working with the ethnic minorities in Yunnan and the Chinese Southwest.\n\nZhu Guangqian left the mainland to study at the University of Hong Kong, the prestigious western-oriented university in the Legation Cities. During his studies, he became heavily influenced by the New Culture Movement by Liang Qichao and Hu Shi - tying their work to his own interest in aesthetics and psychology.\n\nWu Wenzao is a sociologist, anthropologist, and ethnologist - interested in the study of the Tibetan peoples. Influenced by writers such as Bertrand Russell, John Dewey, Sun Yat-sen, Hu Shi, and Liang Qichao - Wu is also a Tsinghua graduate who has studied for sometime in the United States.\n\nLi Gongpu is a former member of the Chinese Kuomintang but has since distanced himself from the Nationalist Party even before the collapse of the NRA during the Northern Expedition. He then moved to the United States before studying at Reed College. Following a brief stint in the States, he returned to Shanghai where he worked as a newspaper editor with socialists such as Liu Xi and Ai Shiqi. When Sun's brutal purge came to the Chinese eastern provinces following the 1932 uprising, Li like his other friends within the Crescent Moon Society fled to Yunnan.

The Direction of the PGP
After 1925, Tang Jiyao reached out to Duan Qirui in an attempt to lobby support from the north. When that failed, in July of 1926 - opposing the revolutionary factions that had sprung up in the Chinese south - Tang formed his own political party. The now Minzhidang (People's Government Party) claimed to have a unique ideology independent of foreign influence - compatible with Zhang Taiyan's Chinese essence. The party champions a democracy of a federalist nature devoid of autocratic vanguardist leadership but seeks to also syncretise with the concept of national essence. Several members from the Awaken Lion Society also joined Tang, finding an ideological compatibility.\n\nNational Essence derives itself from Japan and seeks to create China's own Kokutai, realising the nature of Zhonghua by studying the Chinese classics. Zhang and other nationalists look to the halcyonic days when China's cultural, territorial, and societal integrity were kept intact before the rapine nature of the barbaric northern hordes. National Essence is one where a nation does not have a state and one whose presence return needs institutional embodiment in the state. Once China had achieved her National Essence, she was to help the other oppressed countries find their own - such as India.\n\nWhile the two men may have come from different backgrounds, in the ever changing Chinese political landscape; both Tang and Zhang have found common ground. Zhang founded the Society for Promoting the Self Rule Movement in 1922 and sought to find military support. Given that his followers were composed mostly of intellectuals (such as the Society for National Learning) who would much rather focus on the Chinese heroic epics and classics; Zhang sought time and time again to find military support for his revolutionary movement. Following his entry into Yunnan, he has found an eager ear in Tang Jiyao's PGP - whose federalist ideology now seeks to syncretise with Zhang's National Essence nationalism.
Truly we strive to achieve federalism!

Tang Jiyao, Yunnan's National Champion
For the past years, Yunnan Province has been led by the dominating presence of Tang Jiyao. While denounced as a warlord by many, Tang is just one of many complicated characters in China's turbulent revolutionary history.\n\nBorn to a wealthy family, like many of his compatriots determined to have a strong education - Tang became a soldier in Tokyo and participated in the Lujuntuan (Army Society) and Wuxueshe (Military Study Society) during his stay. One of the authors of the Wuxue Zhazhi (Military Studies Magazine), the ever patriotic Tang joined the Tongmenghui as an early member. However, early doubts were raised regarding his loyalty when he apparently refused to go on strike against a Japanese crackdown and was accused of betraying his fellow students to the Yunnan Provincial Assembly. Banditry and Warlordism
Even before the collapse of central government authority following the Xinhai Revolution, banditry has long been aproblem in Yunnan with bandit gangs and warlords taking advantage of the chaotic unrest following revolution. Throughout the 1920s, discharged miners, discontented ex-soldiers and hungry villagers were amongst the bands of bandits that roamed the countryside and even frequented village and city centers. Of course to many, there is little distinctions between military generals, warlords, and lowly bandits and at times the lines are certainly blurred. Overmilitarisation has also played a part in the growth of banditry across the province. With warlords and generals hiring thousands of conscripts, militias, and paramilitaries - these overzealous hirers forget the limited nature of their treasury coffers and often have defaulted on paying their soldiers. As a result, many of these violent and battle-hardened veterans have turned to banditry, street gangs, and mercnaaries to local landowners.\n\nEven Tang Jiyao's personal secretary Li Yunkun, is accused of holding connections with prominent Yunnan bandits like Zhang Jieba and Wu Xuezian. These bandits , with their large forces of former soldiers, are notorious for having launched mutinies in the past and whom have terrorised the locals. Not only do they typically steal riches, crops, weapons, and silver but opium while officially generally banned in the province is a popular commodity. Especially amongst the rural peasantry in the countryside, these bandits are terrifying with horror stories and rumors of bandits like Zhang Jieba eating the hearts of his enemies.\n\nWith these bandit groups growing even larger and organised all over [ROOT.GetNameDef]'s territory, the situation has grown increasingly dire as popular support for the Federalist movement is wavering. While Federalism of course champions local democracy and self government, a lack of popular support will render any enthusiasm for the federalist revolution to be moot. On the other hand, dealing with the bandits while some may argue to be necessary; is also a serious strain on the government's resources and capabilities.
Bandits can be cowed, it just takes time.
Someone must DO something!

An Ancient Vice
Papaver somniferum is an innocent looking flower but one that has earned the scorn and hatred of Chinese nationalists across the country. Users of the plant, commonly known as opium, report a euphoric high devoid of any pain and one that allows them to escape into their wildest fantasies.\n\nIt's not secret, that opium is widely used across the country despite the historic ban on this illicit substance. Southwest China is of course no exception, with the climate and dry arid heat perfect for cultivation of the cursed plant. Members of society from both the high classes to the lowest beggar on the street consume the drug; typically by smoking with it's dazy and flowery scent flowing openly through city streets. Opium travels here from South and Southeast Asia and into the rest of the country. Despite the government's nominal ban on opium, regulations remain loose as magistrates and enforcers have typically turned a blind eye for favours and bribes.\n\nThroughout the territory of [ROOT.GetNameDef] the issue of corruption extends not only to opium control but also the control and monopolies of public resources such as copper. Prominent individuals in the government have connections to these gentry and new aristocrats which has earned the suspicion and scorn especially amongst activists and journalists who have denounced this outright corruption.
Nothing wrong with a little smoke break.
Corruption must be thoroughly purged!
Nationalism and the Federalist Movement
As the Federalist movement is gaining traction, there is concern amongst nationalists and patriotis that the Federalist movement's championing of local provincial autonomy will divide the country rather than sustain it. While nationalists are united in their desire to see their country unified from Shenyang to Kunming and for the territories lost to the imperialists to be returned; such sentiments come into conflict with more local identities. The past few decades of warlordism have shown that people place greater faith in their local governments, their towns, their mayors and perhaps governors but nothing more beyond that.\n\nIndeed regional identities may very well challenge the unity of the Chinese people. Of course, written Chinese is the same but linguistical dialects from Cantonese to Hokkien to Hakka, to Miao are greater than just stylistic differences in tones but can themselves be classified as unique languages.\n\nNationalists proclaim that the Federalist movement is one to protect national integrity and that the nation is in itself and always will be China. However, with the growing emphasis on de-centralisation and local autonomy; a greater number of voices especially within the Federalist cause have urged that nationalism will only breed centralism and that true democracy belongs to China's many peoples and tribes rather than as one nation.
Let's tone down the regionalist rhetoric.

The Southern Confederation
Huang Xing's Jing Flag now flies proudly in Kunming. Representatives from [YUN.GetLeader]'s government have arrived in Guangzhou today, officially declaring the creation of a Southern Confederation with Guangzhou. Indeed Federalists in the early 1920s had talked or perhaps even dreamed of forming a confederation to resist the whims of Beijing and the dictatorship of the Kuomintang. Truly, the dream of the National Protection Alliance and the Constitutional Protection Government, the true Republican dream for a unified China under a democratic government will be achieved.\n\nWith the Federalist movement now in full swing in Yunnan, hope and unification fever is rising throughout the ranks from the city streets to the highest political office. Tang Jiyao of course, has always ostensibly been a federalist but the Federalist movement faced true uncertainty and collapse with the Zhili victory and Xuantong Restoration. Now with the South unified under one Federalist confederation, the dreams of a national unification under a confederation of Chinese provinces is ever so near. Of course, with Federalism the main ideology of this democratic revolution; Zhang Taiyan's voice in government has emerged even louder. Of course Long Yun's service to Tang has long been admired but it's no secret that two men are contested in a bitter rivalry for control of the province. On the other hand Long Yun's influence and experience can still prove to be useful and keeping him around in command may serve the greater good.
