Democratic Education
With the success of the Federalist revolution in the South, China's democratic revolution of federalist principles has arrived in Yunnan. The revolutionary fever that had filled the streets during the National Protection War has once again found itself in the streets of Kunming and Dali. Luo Longji and the Crescent Moon Society are now frequently in Guangzhou negotiating and working with the Federalist headquarters. In their absence, local Yunnan democrat organisations and parties have sprung up and have taken the initiative in local politics.\n\nTang Jiyao's mass education programme intends to help spread popular education, increasing the number of primary schools, vocational schools, night schools, and teacher training schools. The actual implementation of these schools is delegated to Su Honggang and Cun Shusheng who have been in charge of creating these schools and staffing them. Addressing Military Regions
Under Tang's pre-existing model of implementing federalism - the provincial government was the highest level of authority. Here, as the provincial governor, Tang had the virtually unlimited and unchecked power to appoint or dismiss all other officials until a constitution is drafted. Backed largely by the Yunnan chamber of commerce, Tang's government also tried to separate the military and civilian organisations. Yunnan was thus divided into four military regions; Hu Ruoyu in Kunming, Long Yun in Zhaotong, Li Xuanting in Dali, and Zhang Ruji in Mengzi.\n\nDespite the appearance of a government infrastructure, the decentralised aspect of Tang's rule has naturally led to problems within military districts. Giving garrison commanders their own districts only made them more autonomous and troops frequently extorted cash or resources from local cities and districts for money to upkeep their soldiers.\n\nAfter the failure of Tang's invasion into Guangxi, there were fears of a Kuomintang invasion into the province. Having originally divided his provincial command structure into four regions, he reshuffled it and scapegoated Zhang Ruji. Thus Long Yun received Kunming, Hu Ruoyu received Mengzi, and Li Xuangting remained in Dali, but reshuffled it again after the 1927 counter-coup. The outcome is now Tang Jiyao rules Kunming, Long Yun is sent back north to Zhaotong, Hu Ruoyu, Li Xuanting, and Zhang Ruji are stuck in Mengzi. Sun Du, a former Hu Ruoyu subordinate and rival of Zhang Ruji, was rewarded with the fief of Dali.\n\nNow with Yunnan a key player in the democratic revolution, there does not seem to be a need to maintain these military regions. At the behest of democrats within the province, Tang could formally abolish these regions and redistribute powers to counties and local provincial assemblies. On the other hand, trusting these democrats with local rule may be ineffective and lead to anarchy - and perhaps appointing loyalists from Long Yun's old regions will ensure peace and stability.
It's time to trust the will of the people.
The military regions are fine as they are.
Regionalism and the Republic
As China's southwestern most province, despite being incorporated during China's early Han Dynasty - Yunnan has always enjoyed a history of independence and self-soverignty. Until the Mongols conquered Yunnan, the province was ruled separately of imperial China by the Kingdom of Nanzho and Dali. Furthermore, the province's cultural influences by Thervada Buddhism also reinforced the unique nature of Yunnanese cultural regionalism frmo the rest of China. Early attempts at independence during the Qing Dynasty failed under Wu Sangui (who attempted to found his own dynasty) and later an Islamic revolu under Du Wenxiu, who attempted to find \

Provinical Constitutionalism
During the early days of the Republic, when Republican and Federalist ideals were not diminished by the reality of the Warlord Era; there were many in Yunnan who wanted the province to have an autonomous constitution like Hunan. At the time Tang Jiyao refused to recognise a provincial constitution like Hunan. Tang has stressed time and time again the need for a strong central government which would thus eliminate the need for military activity and warlordism. And even when Cao Kun passed the 1923 Constitution, Tang refused to recognise Beijing's legislation and dug in on his beliefs.\n\nEven after the return of the Zhili Clique to national prominence in 1929, Tang Jiyao has still yet to accept the demands for a provincial constitution. The merchants and gentry have supported Tang's stance, likely enjoying their current lack of restraint of influence onto politics. However, since the establishment of a Federalist government in Guangzhou, democrats and democrat-aligned groups throughout the province have been demanding that a local provincial constitution be drafted at once in order to guarantee basic rights of assembly, property, speech, and right to pursue happiness.\n\nConfronted with the growing unrest and unhappiness of yet another year without provincial constitutional guarantees, Tang is once again confronted with the prospect of drafting and passing a Yunnanese constitution. His advisors have encouraged him to undertake significant caution in doing so and it is rumored that should Tang agree to a constitution; it will be a slow and arduous process with many drafts and revisions to accomplish. On the other hand, the success of the Federalist movement hinges on the unification of the country under the Jing flag. Until then, guarantees on individual liberties will have to wait.

A New Legislature?
In the 1920s, like many other provincial governors - Tang Jiyao attempted to install democratic reforms in his home province. In an ambitious legislature overhaul attempt, Tang attempted to redistribute delegate seats to allow poorer areas to vote for representatives through met pushback. These reforms would extend participation to all levels of society, but has been met by resistance by conservatives who refuse to accept a greater diluted democratic process. While not explicitly provided in any Yunnan law, gerrymandering effectively dominates the electoral process as those in the legislature are able to draw the borders between electoral districts.\n\nThe Political Science Clique and Gu Pingzhen continued to stall the strengthening of the legislature however and the assembly divided into rival factions that kept fighting and installing their candidates. Furthermore, these divisions have only sowed horrid tensions between sectors of society with the left-wing actively denouncing the local gentry and merchant class for classism and elitist attitudes in denying their less fortunate brothers and sisters the chance to participate in democracy. Now in [GetYear], the legislature is essentially ossified as it's controlled largely in the hands of Yunnan's gentry, merchants, and other local (warlord) elites. The Democrats of Yunnan have urged that the districts are to be redrawn and for gerrymandering to be outlawed in favour of more fair district borders. Of course, this has attracted the irate of conservatives and those who prefer the current status quo (such as Hu Ruoyu's camp); who have instead held that they will not hold new elections for the time being. The resulting backlash has led to an outcry of fury among the democrats with many finding allies within Yunnan's left such as the New Yunnan Society.
No new elections for the time being.

Campus Wars
Since 1902, Yunnan provincial schooling was drastically changed by the Guangxu Emperor's edict which closed traditional schools like Kunming Academy and reorganised them into modern schools. Two normal schools were founded, one for each gender, and also a School of Mines and a School of Law and Politics. By the 1920s, around 2,000 students had been enrolled in Kunming's 10 modern schools with students being naturally politically active. Those that went to study in Beijing or the major cities and who returned to Yunnan have been known to be increasingly radical.\n\nIt all started with a pamphlet denouncing the Tang Jiyao regime in the halls of Number One Provincial Middle School. The pamphlet declared boldly that Tang Jiyao and his warlord generals were going to draft all students and force them into the military. The pamphlet extended to a list of demands: the importance of anti-imperialism in the national revolution, the end of all military funding in favor of provincial development, pacifism to destroy the power of the warlords, and the resurrection of defunct groups such as the Dian Chao student publication and Da Tong Association. What started as a simple protest soon escalated to other schools with students demanding the Kunming Military Academy be shut down. Violence has extended past mere brawls and fighting words as well; the Director of the Military Academy has issued a direct order to forbid his students from carrying knives.\n\nTang has tried to move students with the carrot and not the stick by reasserting his influence in universities and reforming them and propagating his ideology. Supported by his brother-in-law Dong Ze, his Donglu University seeks to be a model institutional hall of benevolent education. Donglu's loyalist sentiment has attracted the hatred of radical students who view the school as nothing more than the establishment's cult of personality. Clashes between the radical students and Donglu students have become more frequent - school headmasters have pleaded for Tang to act quickly.

Yunnan's Local Self-Government
As one of his more immediate democratic reforms, Tang had previously sought to strengthen local self government in Yunnan. His programme to create committee meetings and departments amongst local villages staffed by both those from the upper and lower classes may have been hopeful - but has attracted nothing more than anger from local leadership.\n\nVillage heads and officials, citing a shortage of funds and lack of trained personnel to execute the self-government programme, have denounced Tang's plans as hopeless and stupidly idealistic. In areas where the reform measures were fully implemented, officials complained of basically endless committee meetings lasting from sundown to sunrise with effectively zero conclusive results as a result of whole nights of bickering and virtually no compromises. In his first iteration, Tang attempted to readapt the self government programme to only roughly eight relatively wealthy districts (Kunming, Ami, Mengzi, Yiliang, Geju, Simao, Huizi, and Tengzhong) as these wealthier districts may have more ltierate populaces. These attempts saw some local successes but still saw a widening gap between rich and poor districts.\n\nIn 1923, a power struggle had also emerged in Maguan District between the incumbent district official and newly elected head of the local self government committee, which saw the district official fired. The district official however simply stayed on and the resulting chaos led to a severe rice shortage. The district capital was then seized by a Yi bandit leader, Wang Shizen, who then drove out the district official and seized power for himself.\n\nWith the Federalist Movement now in full swing and the constant pleading for power to be delegated to the towns, villages, and small cities of China - Tang has once again reasserted his former local self-government plans in the hopes of improving support for the federalist revolution. Of course, the gentry have voiced their opposition; citing Tang's past failures and that such measures will be a lack of resource. They have insisted to maintain the current status quo and to allow things to be ruled as they are.

New Allies in a New Right

The Canton Compromise
After several weeks of the Conference seemingly going nowhere, General He Yingqin of the First Army and leading party theoretician Dai Jitao of the Zhejiang Society of Revolutionary Comrades has struck an agreement, outflanking the previously dominant Moscow Committee and Third Army Clique. Under their proposal, General He will assume the Presidency of the Revolutionary Government, Chairman of the Kuomintang, and Chairman of the Military Affairs Commission while Dai Jitao will become the first Premier of the (provisional) Executive Yuan and Deputy Chairman of the Party, controlling the executive branch of the government as effectively a Prime Minister.\n\nHe Yingqin, who has gotten along shockingly well with Huang Shaohong and to a lesser extent Li Zongren and Bai Chongxi of the New Guangxi Clique, has managed to secure their support for the proposal. Huang is expected to be named Minister of the Interior as part of the arrangement, with favourable appointments to Li and Bai who still de facto control Guangxi along with Huang Xuchu.\n\nDai Jitao in turn has been able to convince Hu Hanmin and his allies not to walk out of the conference, leaning on their past friendship and an offer to make Hu Hanmin Premier of the (provisional) Legislative Yuan and Ju Zheng Premier of the (provisional) Judicial Yuan. He has sworn to work to uphold civilian rule and that this arrangement will be a temporary matter until unification.\n\nThough far from a perfect setup, the fact that any consensus was found brought a relief to most delegates, who quickly fell in line with the He-Dai government. The herculean horsetrading that was required however bodes poorly for the long term prospects of this government, and it is clear that things are far from over.

The Guizhou KMT Return to Guangzhou
In a sudden turn of fortunes, the Kuomintang junta that retreated to Guizhou after losing the Northern Expedition was able to make their return to Guangzhou a decade later. Defeating warlords and rivals that stood in their way, the National Revolutionary Army has taken control over Liangguang, bringing the party-state back to the same position they were in 1925.\n\nForming a new Guangzhou Nationalist Government has not been as easy however. The party's right seemed hopelessly divided between various figures and negotiations dragged on until a compromise was finally struck between General He Yingqin and party theorist Dai Jitao. With most of the party falling in line afterwards, their attention now turns Northwards.

The Third Army's Backlash
While Zhu Peide always expected to have to share power, even when he was the head of the Guizhou Junta he always was merely first among equals, watching the Congress unfold the way it did was nothing short of an outrage. While Zhu is still a CEC member and Governor of Yunnan, the only position he has received in the new government is a token position on the Military Affairs Commission chaired by He Yingqin - an active attempt to sideline him.\n\nWith the situation rapidly escaping his control, Zhu has mused staging a coup with what forces he has before the junta is formally disbanded. However, the headwinds seem to be against him. He is still not on the best of terms with Hu Hanmin and the Moscow Committee, having seen multiple rounds of contentious negotiations fail. Hu Hanmin, despite his open disdain for General He, is a notorious stickler for party unity and commitment to democratic centralism, and has made his opinions well known. Meanwhile, the other regionalist generals needed to make a coup work like Li Zongren, Bai Chongxi, and Chen Jitang have largely been bought off. Faced with these warning signs, his fellow Third Army officers have informed General Zhu that they are simply too divided and that most of the rank and file simply would like to go along with the show and project unity.

General Zhu Humiliated
Zhu Peide's outburst against the He Yingqin-Dai Jitao compromise has achieved little traction. Zhu Peide Passes Away
Ever since the humiliating incident at Guangzhou, Zhu Peide has been working twice as hard as Governor of Yunnan to salvage his reputation as a patriot. Combined with the stress of managing his emboldened rivals, his health worsened. The questionable quality and access to medicine in Kunming finally struck when Governor Zhu took low quality anti-anemia drugs imported from Germany to avoid missing a meeting, causing him to grow extremely ill and pass away after a stay in the hospital.\n\nPrior to his death, he is quoted as telling [ROOT.GetLeader], who came to visit him in the hospital, that he has three remaining things to say. First, the war of resistance is imminent and national strength is limited, so he asked to be buried simply rather than expend money on a state funeral. Second, he asked that his family and children fend for themselves rather than receive preferential treatment. Lastly, he asked that his family nurse not be blamed for his death because ultimately China's medical science is underdeveloped.\n\nIf Zhu's goal was to rehabilitate his reputation, it seems like it has worked. Public praise from all sectors of the party, including from the left, has poured in. Major figures have noted his diligence, humility, and patriotism. Zhu Peide Aligns with Hu Hanmin
Before leaving for Kunming, Zhu Peide and his men sit down with Hu Hanmin and his allies for one last round of negotiations. In private talks, they discussed their mutual distrust for the new military dominated government, and have agreed to consult with each other further before making any moves.\n\nHu Hanmin is hard at work expanding his influence within the legislature and party apparatus, checking the influence of Dai Jitao and Zhang Qun. His connections with the Guangdong government and Chen Jitang in particular has given him a degree of hard power that most other civilian figures lack. They are hopeful that they will be able to secure the support of the New Guangxi Clique, who are still in the process of rebuilding, and potentially Guizhou and Hunan for their quest to reassert control over the party.

The South Consolidates Behind Hu Hanmin
While careful to avoid endangering the success of the revolution as a whole, Hu Hanmin has been the focal point of an intra-party resistance to He Yingqin's rule. He has worked diligently to peel off regional forces from He Yingqin, securing Guangdong, Yunnan, and most recently Guangxi. Working with warlords has always been a tough sell for the stubborn, centralist Hu Hanmin, but so is accepting military control over the party.\n\nWithin the national government, Hu has utilised his position as Premier of the Legislative Yuan and his prominence as a party leader to try and isolate He Yingqin. He has attempted on a few occasions to persuade Dai Jitao to abandon the Generalissimo, seeking to install Zhu Peide as the new Chairman of the Military Commission.\n\nHe knows that General He's position is precarious because unlike Chiang Kai-shek before him, he only really has military power and struggles to invoke genuine loyalty over the civilian apparatus. But control is more than just loyalty, and the mercenary Political Science Clique has a notorious tendency to go where the wind blows. Perhaps he would be better served securing their support. Either way the emergence of a unified southern bloc bears ominous tidings for the Whampoa government.
Through ideas and loyalty we save our party.
Through strength and control we bring down tyrants.
Two Titans on the Right
The two major ideological leaders of the right Kuomintang are Hu Hanmin and Dai Jitao, both close confidants of Sun Yat-sen who have published extensive works interpreting the party founder's theories. Having spent a lot of time editing each other's manuscripts late into the night, the two are close friends but their respective followers have since drifted apart from one another.\n\nThis split began with the divisions caused by the United Front, and the assembly of various rightist figures in the Western Hills suburbs of Beijing to resist the growing influence of the Chinese Syndicalist Party. Most of these men remained sympathetic to a conservative, bourgeois democracy more akin to the original Tongmenghui. Dai Jitao was violently ejected from the proceedings, accused of socialist sympathies.\n\nDai would ultimately rally members of the so-called New Right, a faction of rightists who initially sought to maintain the United Front but later backed Chiang Kai-shek in his quest to fight off the Chinese Syndicalist Party while not imperilling foreign aid from the Internationale. They distinguished themselves by claiming to purify Sun's ideology from foreign elements, mixing a Leninist vanguard political structure with traditionalism.\n\nWhat the right Kuomintang share is a common dedication to revolutionary nationalism and an attachment to Dr. Sun's theories but a rejection of the need for class warfare. While both Hu and Dai may have started their careers attracted to left wing ideas such as Marxism and Anarchism, ultimately it was with the intention of furthering a nationalist, anti-imperialist agenda and both would end up discarding those theories as ineffective or outright hostile to Chinese sovereignty. Hu Hanmin, in contrast to many of his followers, not only believes that the party-state will prevent the revolution from denigrating into class warfare, but also save it from \
We need to hold onto the ideals the party was founded on.
We need to embrace change and dynamism to win!
Whampoa's Cliques
The First Army prides itself on representing all of China. Unlike the regional biases of the other armies of the NRA (even if in practice Whampoa students and staff tended to be drawn from a select few loyalist provinces), the Whampoa brotherhood that emerged gave them a sense of interprovincial connectivity.\n\nThat has not prevented them from disagreeing on their plans for the future. The Old Guard, led by He Yingqin, takes inspiration from earlier conservative reformers in China as well as Japan's successful modernisation efforts. Holding many prominent positions in the establishment, their priority is maintaining order and balance, throughout the revolution.\n\nThe Civil Engineering Clique gets its name from Chen Cheng's command of the 18th Army (formed from defeated Guangdong Army units) and the 11th Division. The Chinese characters for 11 (Ten and One) when put together resemble the character for earth (Tu). The characters for 18 (Ten and Eight) when put together look like the character for wood (Mu), so put together one gets Tumu (Engineering). They are notorious for their absorption of leftist units and their arrogance - proudly walking in front of friendly forces and ignoring them, deriding their lessers as \

Rise of the CC Clique
Ever since Dai Jitao first took command of the Executive Yuan, he has remade it in his image. His followers, mainly Zhejiang provincials, dominate the top posts and are hard at work implementing Premier Dai's agenda. Given the exhaustive portfolio Dai received from his compromise with President He, there are few domains beyond his reach: the military remains controlled by He Yingqin and Whampoa, the Financial and Economic sectors is under the Political Science Clique, and the Legislature and Judicial Yuan under Moscow Committee Clique appointees.\n\nTo meet the Dai administration's growing demands, the Zhejiang Society of Revolutionary Comrades has evolved into a larger entity often referred by outsiders as the CC Clique (an abbreviation for either the Central Club/Committee Clique or a reference to the two Chen brothers). Its supposed existence however has been thoroughly denied by Chen Lifu as an attempt to sow party divisions and cast doubt on Dai's leadership. Whatever the case both Mr. Chen and his older brother Chen Guofu have emerged as Dai's leading lieutenants in the party-state's administration.\n\nWhat all three men prize is education, discipline, and the party's revolution. While Dai Jitao is the face of their clique and Chen Lifu is his understudy, Chen Guofu is their silent workhorse. He is said to enjoy a simple life according to Confucian terms - never drinking, smoking or going to movies. His only hobby apparently is reading books, spending money on them even if it was his last dollar. His greatest regret in life is said to be that he was denied a higher education due to joining the party at age 18, and thus glad to encourage young men to begin advanced studies. In poor health due to the lingering effects of tuberculosis, he nevertheless continues to work and lecture. He is quoted as saying \

The Theory of Political Discourse
Neither iterations of the Political Science Clique really fit in with the New or Old Right of the Kuomintang. The Old Political Science Clique, led by Cen Chunxuan and Li Genyuan, were notorious for sabotaging efforts by the Guangzhou government to write a new constitution and their growing dispute with Dr. Sun saw them collaborate with Guangxi warlord Lu Rongting to eject Sun from Guangzhou. Similarly, their attempt at securing Yunnan from Tang Jiyao ended in Gu Pinzhen's head being mounted on a pike.\n\nZhang Qun was a staffer under Cen, but similarly failed to fit in in Beiyang after the Kuomintang ousted the Political Science Clique, but found stable employment under Chiang Kai-shek and He Yingqin. The new Vice Premier of the Executive Yuan and concurrently War Minister (with a minor portfolio in the Foreign Ministry), he has built his clique around technocratic control over economic organs.\n\nYang Yongtai, a former affiliate of Huang Xing and a finance minister for the Constitutional Protection Government, aligned with Cen and Li against Sun. When their government fell, he was forced to flee to the Beiyang government but after a brief stint found himself in exile in Shanghai. Recruited after nearly a decade of retirement by Zhang Qun, he is eager to avoid finding himself out of power again, adopting a policy of political flexibility whilst remaining loyal to the Generalissimo.\n\nYang has proposed to solve the problems of the Beiyang Warlords (irreconcilably and actively against the Kuomintang) by military means, the problems of the Southern Warlords like Li Zongren (who possess military might but are fiscally poor) by economic means, and to solve the problems of Hu Hanmin and the Moscow Committee (militarily weak but with a strong political reputation) along with the foreign powers by diplomatic means.

The Sword and the Pen
The revolution is carried by the zeal of its acolytes, but they have been bitterly divided into competing factions. Though traditionally China's bureaucracy is said to be divided into the sword (men of action/the military) and the pen (men of knowledge/the bureaucracy), the largest Kuomintang camps can be compared to the colours of the Blue Sky, White Sun, and Wholly Red Earth Flag that adorns almost every building in [ROOT.Capital.GetCapitalVictoryPointName].\n\nThe Blueshirts (Blue) have grown tremendously in influence across the military, selling a radical vision for China governed in a similar lines to the fanatical, disciplined, and revolutionary order of their idealised memories of the old Whampoa academy. For the many young former cadets turned senior officers, they have known nothing but instability, revolution, and war since childhood, and are determined to save China at any cost - even if it means discarding a corrupt and bloated party apparatus and incorporating foreign ideas imported from Europe.\n\nThe CC Clique (White) dominated the party apparatus. Building a mass movement among civilian party cadres calling for a Neo-Confucian revival and strict adherence to Dai Jitao's interpretation of Sun Yat-sen's theories, they appeal to a more intellectual form of ultra-nationalism centred around the party's Orthodoxy. Their control over much of the establishment and education makes them potent, however many have a declining opinion of the military and their association with opportunists like the Yang Yongtai and the Political Science Clique.\n\nThe Civil Engineering Clique (Red) are another young guard branch, consisting of those disillusioned with the brutality of the BSS and the corruption of the CC Clique. Also harkening to the old days of Whampoa, they have cultivated a mass following for their discipline and care for the people while rejecting the radicalism of the socialists.\n\nThe three factions have wasted little time undermining one another, sometimes even more than party outsiders. As the party expands in territory, competition for influence has grown fierce especially as each maintains their own intelligence network, youth organisation, and patronage networks. All under the watch of the Generalissimo.

Faithful Guizhou
After the Long March, western Guizhou has been the centre of power for the remnants of the National Revolutionary Army. There were few regional state enterprises or really any industry prior to Kuomintang rule, something the junta had worked to partially address though not particularly successfully. Nevertheless, with the Kuomintang relocating its main bases to Guangzhou, a power vacuum has emerged as factions jockey for influence.\n\nWang Boqun is the brother-in-law to He Yingqin and the brother of Wang Wenhua, who led some of the original Kuomintang revolutionaries in the province against their uncle Liu Xianshi. A civilian with deep ties to the province, he is dogged by allegations of corruption. and He Yingqin is wary of the appearance of nepotism should he appoint him to the Governorship, especially since his younger brother He Jiwu is concurrently seeking an appointment to Mayor of Guiyang (the provincial capital).\n\nGiven Guizhou is his home province, it is quite valuable to the Generalissimo. Another potential member of his circle that is in consideration is his secretary and fellow native, Liu Jianqun. A propaganda officer responsible for keeping tabs on the BSS, he is said to have grown enamoured with their ideology.\n\nHe Yingqin's main rival in the province is Gu Zhenglun, a former ally before they had a falling out. His younger brothers are prominent members of Wang Jingwei's leftist RCA, which has cast a cloud of suspicion on him despite his anti-socialist tendencies. Another Kuomintang figure, Lu Dao, remains around but largely not in consideration - he has thrown his lot in with General Gu.\n\nGiven the extremely charged personal politics involved, there has been little room for Politicac Science or Central Club to make their own appointments; albeit, both cliques have solicited the support of Gu Zhenglun for their appointment to out-flank General He and Liu.

News and Newspapers of the Party-State
Despite prevalent censorship, the people's desire for gossip and news about their government can scarcely be quenched. To meet this need, a variety of party or quasi-government sponsored newspapers have sprouted up.\n\nCapital News is said to be the spiritual successor of Dai Jitao's Min Quan Li (People's Rights). Founded by the CC Clique, its stated mission was to publicise the Three Principles and their access to the confidential sectors to get first hand, exclusive stories while avoiding printing any state or military secrets. Publishing extremely late at night and including pictorial supplements about social satire as well as serialised novels, the money they made allowed them to create spin offs like the Current Affairs Monthly magazine, Political Review Monthly and Cheng-chung publishing house. Their pointed political commentary has often resulted in military and political figures complaining directly to President He to demand a retraction.\n\nThe Society Mercury is a militant paper associated with the BSS. Building a New Government
Building a new government is a formidable challenge. The Kuomintang seeks to build a party-state, but there is a dire shortage of qualified professionals, much less loyal party cadres across China. Only Yunnan, Guizhou, Guangxi, Guangdong, Hunan, Sichuan, Zhejiang, and Jiangsu actually have fully organised provincial party branches, making up 94% of the total number of city and county branches. Virtually every other province has temporary party affairs directory committees or a singular party agent sent by the Chen brothers directing party matters.\n\nThe bureaucracy meanwhile has been run almost directly from the Military Commission, with Military Councils having extensive power over regional administrations. Yang Yongtai is chief architect of this system, organising the creation of Bandit Suppression zones with three priorities: Administrative Efficiency, Political Education, and Political Mobilisation. Yang's reforms are directed around having separate provincial executive departments communicate horizontally rather than only with their Central Government and local equivalents, and also empower County Executives and sub-county forms of governments in local villages. This included creating the Baojia system, a rural militia supplemented by short term party education centres in the villages to fight bandits and socialist insurgents.\n\nTheir reforms have been stifled however by the slow speed in which they have been implemented and its heavy dependence on military solutions in the meanwhile. Only a few provinces, usually dominated by Political Science Clique officers, have implemented Yang's reforms and poor communication technology means a lot of these changes have only caused more confusion. The Baojia system has primarily appealed to the gentry, angering many peasants. Yang's attempts at centralisation has angered many regionalists and drawn envy from the CC Clique. Accusations of elitism and corruption have pushed many to call for reforms and the ejection of Political Science Clique leaders.
These reforms sow the seeds of a stable and free republic!
The party must embrace reform and change to survive!
Their failure causes cycles of resistance, violence, and brutality.
Kunming Intrigues
The Yunnan Kuomintang are generally seen as more regionalist and less anti-socialist than the rest of the right. Accusations of harbouring socialists have arisen, which weakened Zhu Peide's faction as he depended on men like Zhu De to aid him. Li Genyuan's return has similarly been controversial even after all these years, and his attempts at reasserting the Old Political Science Clique's influence has not been received well. Fan Shisheng and Jin Handing have begun to waver in their support thanks to these controversies, remaining loyal primarily due to their suspicion of Whampoa's influence.\n\nLong Yun and Hu Ruoyu, both who accepted the initial Kuomintang rule with the expectation of retaining local power and autonomy, have grown more bold in undermining the mainline Kuomintang apparatus, setting up their own Kuomintang party committees and appointing their loyalists to prominent positions - sometimes forcibly expelling Zhu's appointees.\n\nLiu Zhi, one of Whampoa's Five Tiger Generals and a former Yunnan Army officer, has been dispatched with a detachment to \

The Fallen Triumvir
Wang Jingwei and his Reorganisation Comrades Association (RCA) are the hated rivals of both the Old Right and the New Right, fierce competitors during Guangzhou's power games and seen as sellouts to the Third Internationale. Wang Jingwei is a proven political survivor however, flexible as he is arrogant, and is eager to worm his way back into the party's good graces - ostensibly in the name of party unity.\n\nIn reality, the RCA's relationship to [ROOT.GetLeader] is, in all regards, a highly complicated matter. With various individuals within the [ROOT.GetNameDef] continuously flirting between the Left and Right wings, few prove particularly stringently opposed to Wang on ideological grounds. Many, if not all, support the 1924 Reorganisation, albeit few support an alliance with the International or cooperation with the Chinese Syndicalist Party, but instead provoke the need for party unity in dire times. Whilst this perhaps falls directly into the RCA's desires, the most stringent opposition arriving from men such as Li Genyuan has, rather paradoxically, induced more within the ranks to support Wang's efforts to reconcile.\n\nUnlike the left, the right wing of the Kuomintang sees the end goal of the revolution as building a harmoniously cooperative national body, bound together by culture, acting in concert against mutual threat. Men like Dai Jitao, Hu Hanmin and Chiang Kai-shek saw themselves as truly revolutionaries despite their conservatism, and it is no surprise the left and right regularly branded each other as counterrevolutionary. In-between this resides men such as Zhu Peide, Chen Cheng, and Sun Fo. Men who, for one reason or another, advocate for and endorse the need for revolution, but are intent on reconciling both the left and right wing's outlooks.\n\nFor his part, Wang insists that he can still secure foreign support via backchannels if he is invited into government, and his presence will diminish support for the CSP. And on a global scale, Wang remains a leading face in the Kuomintang, it would be a strong message to refuse him.

In some ways, he served as Sun's shadowy alter-ego during their exile in Japan, which shaped the more conservative side of Sun. They believed the failure of Xinhai was due to a failure of national spirit, but Dai also argued that Xinhai lacked the popular dynamism needed for change. While Dai approved the vanguardism of the 1924 Reorganisation, he argued that a true revolution could only be enacted via the masses rather than a radical minority and disapproved of the alliance with the Internationale. Disapproving of the approval of dual KMT and CSP membership as allowing external control over the party, Dai went into de facto exile during the reorganisation era, and Sun's death destroyed any restraint he had against the left and pushed him into a nearly suicidal pessimism.\n\nUnlike the left, the right wing of the Kuomintang sees the end goal of the revolution as building a harmoniously cooperative national body, bound together by culture, acting in concert against mutual threat. The League of Chinese Syndicalists Collapses
The League of Chinese Syndicalists (LCS) was founded primarily by the Chinese Syndicalist Party (CSP) as a means of countering Kuomintang influence and expanding the limits of the United Front. The League almost immediately proved to be an unwieldy force as Marxists, Syndicalists, Anarchists, and various other independent leftists fought one another for influence. The rivals to the CSP, themselves divided into wings, are the anarchist (New) World Society led by the Four Elders of the Kuomintang.\n\nThe CSP, and the LCS by extension, is the raison d'etre of the right Kuomintang, which formed in opposition to the United Front. The right believes that China's unique socio-economic circumstances means class warfare and the dictatorship of the proletariat are unnecessary. While they reject the influence of the free market and note the social defects of the wage system, the right Kuomintang believe that Chinese capitalism is rudimentary enough that there is not yet a capitalist class consciousness.\n\nBelieving most stringently in the application of the Three Principles as opposed to any form of Marxism or Anarchism, Zhu Peide has nonetheless tentatively tabled discussion of incorporating the Chinese Syndicalists into the National Revolution.\n\nOne of the first members of the CSP, Wang Fusheng has been placed in a superior position within the party due to recent events, having been able to establish a provincial committee on behalf of the CSP's leadership. Operating with individuals such as Li Xin, the duo have entrenched themselves within the local student and worker movements.\n\nRather curiously, across the aisle, the Four Elders and their World Society have been viewed as far more palatable. Li Shizeng for example has called Marxist syndicalism regressive and Minsheng more compatible with the contemporary advances in human civilisation. The Kuomintang must lead a new third way against capitalism and syndicalism, two sides of western imperialism. To reconcile with the Four Elders would perhaps make it easier to keep the CSP in check.

The China Revival Society Clings On

The [GetYear] Proposal to Determine Educational Objectives and their Methods of Implementation
As part of his initial initiatives as the Minister of Education, Chen Lifu has drafted the [GetYear] Proposal to Determine Educational Objectives and their Methods of Implementation. Even despite the modernisation attempts following the Xinhai Revolution, education in China still remains one of the most chronically underfunded sectors of Chinese society. free schooling for all would cost hundreds of millions of yuan, and budget allocations have never scratched even 20% of that at its peak, and averages around 10% of what is needed. Even rudimentary schools are dispersed and difficult to attend in rural areas and in major cities less than 5% went to high school despite their greater access to resources.\n\nIn addition, to expanding funding for education, Minister Chen's proposal would incorporate the Three Principles of the People into the core curricula of all levels of schooling, replacing civic classes with party doctrine orthodoxy and an emphasis on Dr. Sun's life, teachings, lectures, and writings. Older students would be expected to learn Kuomintang policy and orthodox party histories as they prepare for a life of national servitude to their nation. Reviving the Revolutionary Spirit
After the original Xinhai Revolution, the Tongmenghui divided into those who believed it was possible to complete the revolution through parliamentary means and those who believed the party must remain a vigilant, revolutionary force. Although Huang Xing and Song Jiaoren won the majority of seats in the ensuing election, they were quickly quashed by Yuan Shikai's Beiyang army with the help of reactionaries in the legislature. Since then, Sun and many of his close followers have disavowed parliamentary means, denouncing Huang and Song as foolishly naive.\n\nFor Dai Jitao, this meant a transformation from believing in liberal individualism as the basis for an ideal state to one built on an ideological foundation backed by military power. Revolutionary Shock Troopers
In the tumultuous age of warlords, the average quality of a Chinese soldier varies significantly from village to province. While Yunnan has traditionally boasted a Republican martial heritage, thousands of soldiers still lack patriotic conviction and rather fight for a bowl of rice and a day's wages. Alerted of this problem, Dai has written extensively on the necessity of revolutionary shock troopers—soldiers driven by sheer moral fortitude and patriotic self-sacrifice who are armed with ideas, not just guns.\n\nThese soldiers would not just be driven by a sense of nationalism but rather they should feel called to their purpose; of which is to project and transmit the glories of the Three Principles of the People. As they march from town to town, they would instruct and guide the people on the teachings of Sun Yat-sen, and to proclaim the necessity of adopting the four rights so that the people may learn and understand democracy. Influenced by observations in regards to the Bolshivelks' monopolisation of propaganda during the Russian Civil War. Particular is even driven to the means of propaganda, such as the utilisation of trains and the halloficiation of figures like Trotsky. Despite being seen as part of the socialist menace that threatens the Kuomintang, Dai has spoken favorably of these tactics, arguing that China ought to emulate this model of revolution in the wars against imperialism.\n\nDai's proposed measures have been wholeheartedly supported by members of the CC Clique, including that of the journalist Chen Bulei. However, conservative elements within the military argue that the military ought to remain apolitical as possible and to trust that the institution of tutelege belongs to the civilian wing of the party.
The army is an instrument of the National Revolution.
The army must distance itself from politics at times.
Careerists and a Revolution
The late eternal premier was once quoted saying that once the revolutionary army had successfully risen up, the revolutionary party ought to be disbanded. Of course, context is greatly needed, at the time the Revolutionary Alliance (also known as the Tongmenghui) was disbanded and in its place the political party, the Chinese Nationalist Party took its place, depleted of revolutionary spirit.\n\nTimes change and so did Sun's outlook on the nature of the Chinese Revolution. With the failure of the Second Revolution, Sun's cynicism and pessimism drove him to more authoritarian heights, taking it upon himself to bear the burden of saving China. As a result of the oaths of loyalty, many of the liberal supporters throughout China and outside of his powerbase in Guangdong, simply abandoned his movement. Sun hated them in turn, seeing them as turncoats from the necessity of national salvation. Leading protégés of his including Hu Hanmin and Dai Jitao who wished to see a party entirely of Kuomintang loyalists, argued that formal institutions were not necessarily needed so long that a party is unified under a central orthodoxy.\n\nWith the Kuomintang seeking to make its ascension into Chinese national politics once more, members of the so-called Political Science Clique and those even within the CC Clique, identify themselves more closely with technocratic administration. While they certainly identify themselves with the nationalist cause, many of them carry weaker ties to the National Revolution, yet certainly their skills are necessary for proper national administration. Or are they merely just opportunists, willing to change sides as their earliest convenience?

Means of Public and Internal Security
Ever since the Qin Dynasty, Chinese rulers have long utilised legalistic means of oppression and suppression of internal dissent through dedicated police forces. As deep state survilance began its true origins in the Ming Dynasty, so too did policing truly began during the Hundred Days Reforms, and continued under Beiyang. The Kuomintang's model for policing began with the Canton Bureau of Public Safety, built along American lines. Indeed, all future Kuomintang police agencies have been called \

In recognition of this, March 29th has replaced May 4th as Youth Day in China (to avoid the obvious socialist connection), and large numbers of party-backed youth organisations have sprouted up. Recasting Confucius
Since the days the Qin Emperor united the various warring states following the Spring and Autumn Periods, Confucianism has been a staple bedrock of Chinese philosophy and culture. Associated with its tradition and an idealistic nostalgic lens into days long gone, Confucianism found itself rocked by the century of humiliation and the subsequent upheaval of the Xinhai Revolution.\n\nThe resurgent right-wing of the Kuomintang has long interpreted Dr. Sun's interpretation of Confucius as a necessary moral groundwork to lay a revolution in a world driven by concepts such as materialism and imperialism. While many acknowledge that concepts such as fillal piety should be encouraged and the great sages taught and learned (through public holidays and schools), others like Dai Jitao have explicitly argued that the Analects properly described how to run a country. To Dai, it is Sun, who properly understood Confucius as the moral and vital spirit that China must revitalise.\n\nAnd there are even others, who see Confucianism as beyond China's moral ideology. Those in the Blueshirts Society venerate China's historic authoritarian regime nearing despotism, a time when free thought was subjugated and pure obedience guided all.
A great figure but we must look towards the future.
Truly the blueprint for our civilisation going forwards.
Confucius is both the key to the past and a glorious future.
The Christian Crisis of Soul
The nature of Christianity has long been a subject of great controversy within China. First, Christianity was certainly seen as the foreigner's religion; the barbarians from around the world carried their crucifixes with their guns as they carved up the Chinese country. It was these very Christian nations that had sought to divide China, the same fierce struggle encapsulated by the Boxer Rebellion more than 30 years prior.\n\nWithin the Kuomintang, the relationship with Christianity is a rich tapestry of diverse perspectives. Party leaders such as Sun Yat-sen, Song Qingling, and Chiang Kai-shek identified as Christians, while others like Liao Zhongkai (founder of the Anti-Christian Federation) and Wu Zhihui denounced Christianity. Many viewed Christian schools in China as symbols of imperialism and a foreign ideology that had no place in the new China. This sentiment was echoed by Dai Jitao, one of Christianity's most vocal critics.\n\nAnd yet even within the Nationalist Government today, promiment figureheads like He Yingqin (a Catholic) and Zhang Qun (a convert thanks to his wife) defend Christianity as good principles that should be emulated in addition to Chinese morals. It has been rumoured that Chen Cheng's wife was said to have described that Jesus himself, was the leader of a social and religious revolution - a truly national revolution. Though Chen Cheng does seem to waver on this issue himself, as in a recent interview, he has mentioned that he does believe Christian missionaries have historically and still do, treat the native Chinese with a disrespectful and condescending attitutde.\n\nThis issue seemingly threatens party unity as the debates have grown more fierce in recent days, with Dai explicitly calling for the expulsion of Christians, the abandoning of protections to the Church, and the rejection of having Christian schools in China. Yet, the bitter irony is despite the Kuomintang's rejection of Christ, was it not a fact that the great eternal premier was a disciple of the Holy One above as well?
Christian values have always had some influence on the party.
Jesus was a good man, but Christians need to be better.

Like Chiang, they have leveraged the might of the National Revolutionary Army to achieve political dominance, while maintaining the front of political neutrality to play off the different ideological cliques within the party. A twelve foot tall effigy of General He Yingqin was raised on the 10/10 parade in [ROOT.Capital.GetCapitalVictoryPointName], a symbol of just how entrenched he has become. Yang Yongtai, the Generalissimo's Workhorse
Yang Yongtai is a consummate politician, for every important issue he always secretly brings two plans - one in favour and one against the present course of action. After sounding out the President's opinion, he would present the plan that aligns with the President and act as if they were in agreement all along. Thus, like a magician, he is able to create the illusion of being perfectly in sync with his leader.\n\nThe Political Science has no formal organisation (the fleeting New China Construction Society was quickly disbanded to avoid running afoul party rules on splitters, and hardly met anyway) and are fairly cliquey in nature. They have been compared to court personalities, remaining close to the centres of power while never courting the masses. The clique being built on connections between the elite have distanced themselves from the public, which has benefited in some ways (most notably, the military does not see them as a threat) but the lack of mass movement has made them easy targets for their growing ranks of enemies.\n\nTheir growing influence in the national bureaucracy, regional administration, and even segments of the army have raised the ire of rivals on all fronts. The CC Clique have conspired to undermine them from within the government while the Civil Engineering Clique have sought to raise the masses against them, and the Blueshirts have begun to target their membership for violence. The Lushan Conference
Since the early days of the morning bugle calls at the Whampoa barracks, certain cadets felt that the National Revolution might, in the future, be overtaken by something much more sinister - the spectre of syndicalism. It was in these very hallowed halls of martial revolutionary education and spirit, that groups coalesced, some pledging to follow the teachings of the Generalissimo and to combat the growing influence of the socialists within the academy. As cliques formed like the Whampoa Alumni Association, often in secret, there were some that believed in even more radical beliefs.\n\nAs radicalism and revolutionary zeal rise, He Yingqin has typically sent his secretary, Liu Jianqun, to monitor these meetings in the past. But on this day, he has decided to pay a visit himself. There, in the backdrop of the Lushan mountains, he declared to the hundreds of military and Blueshirt leaders present that now, more than ever, the revolutionary spirit must be rekindled.\n\nBlueshirt leaders like Teng Jie have declared that their doctrine seeks to reorganise a new Three Principles of the People: National Defence, Ultra-Nationalism, and a People's State. The Vokshod Programme in Russia, Japan's bushido spirit and successful modernisation, are seen as potential templates for a new Chinese state. As the meeting debated the nature and implementation of these concepts, some even brought up that it may indeed be time to reject the Three Principles of the People - abolish democracy and strive for National Populism.\n\nCertainly there are controversial elements within the ideologies of SZRS and the Romanian LAM, such as the fact that LAM is a movement that seeks to reconcile with the past and glorify the monarchy while SZRS strives towards the people's populism under authoritarian republican leadership. Many within Whampoa's military leadership favor a revolution on conservative lines, while more radical elements argue for an advance into a new future.

The Hubei Training Institute
They say that we never really forget where we came from and one can say that the same applies to Chen Cheng. Having grown up in abject poverty, Chen was unable to afford a proper education - instead having to opt for a career in the military. Chen never forgot where he came from and with the Nationalist Government controlling vast swathes of territory, he has taken it upon himself to transform and lift the provinces of China to escape the cycle of poverty.\n\nThe Civil Engineering Clique's first attempts at national reforms are in the province of Hubei which Chen has taken over as the local head of administration. They have began initial efforts to ban smoking, prostitution, dancing, and other frivilous activities of immoral decadence. Along with Yan Lisan and Zhu Huaibing, they plan to make greater sweeping reforms in the province; which would bring them into conflict with the layers of corruption underneath. Much of the wealth in the province is secretly controlled by the underworld linked by criminal organisations and the CC Clique.\n\nLeftists within the province have urged Chen to take greater measures as part of the National Revolution's commitment to minsheng, the principle of livelihood. However, members within the Whampoa Clique have expressed privately their concern at the tensions this may cause with the CC Clique in the government. Certainly now is not the time for greater factional disputes within the Party, but surely something must be done to help the people. Regardless, Chen finds himself now at a crossroads once more, pulled from all directions in an ever complex national revolution.

From First Protégé to Figurehead
It was said that during the days of the Guangzhou Nationalist Government following the death of Sun Yat-sen's, the three men that ruled the revolutionary junta were Wang Jingwei, Hu Hanmin, and Chiang Kai-shek. The Legislative Yuan
As the Kuomintang's right-wing-led revolution continues to press on, growing tensions have emerged within the Kuomintang parliamentary process. As the Head of the Legislative Yuan, Hu Hanmin had participated in an uneasy coalition with Dai Jitao, the President of the Executive Yuan. Despite being forced to share power with the rest of the Kuomintang's right-wing, Hu is still regarded as one of the most prolific and profound understanders of the Sanminzhuyi. Having been with Sun since the Tongmenghui days and one of his most beloved followers, Hu welds considerable power within the party apparatus.\n\nNow, with coalitions and party alliances being ever more apparent, Hu may seek to reach out to the other factions within the party to consolidate his position. Gu Zhongxiu and Huang Fu within the Political Science Clique might prove to be apt allies although the Political Science Clique has achieved a reputation of being more grounded in economic planning than any real revolutionary ideology which might prove to be uncomfortable for Hu, who is vested in the concept of adhering to an orthodox true revolution of the Three Principles of the People. The Zhejianger Chen Guofu might not be all too similar to Hu either, but he has in recent days, made meetings to Hu's office in secret. Chen's support would certainly reconcile Hu with Dai, and perhaps Hu could use both to curb the power of the Generalissimo. Alternatively, securing power with his own clique in Guangdong, especially the favour of the Crown Prince, Sun Fo, may prove the key to retaining Hu's prestige and influence in the party.

Life as a Warlord, Eternity as a Soldier
To mark down the true ideology of Zhu De would be that of a resolved case. A military man first and foremost, Zhu himself has always deigned it as his role to merely be the driving force of the revolution militarily, fighting on the battlefields and planning out the campaigns, as opposed to debating in symposiums and forums. To be expected, naturally, given his character as one of Cai E's protégés — a man infamous for his detesting of politics and politicians. That isn't to say however, that Zhu is without ideology, or socialist convictions, or that he even separates political thought from military affairs. Stipulating that his ideal composition of an army is that of workers and peasants, he believes that the military and political cadres should only come from urban proletarians.\n\nFurthermore, Zhu views militarism in a Social Darwinist mindset much akin to his fellow Yunnan soldiers; for the military can be the flag bearer of China's salvation, and soldiers should be consciously aware of exactly what they fight for. Lest they fall to influences such as corruption or greed. And Zhu himself knows that tendency of human nature more than anyone, as following Cai E's death, and admittedly Zhu laments this, he would become nothing better than a local warlord in Sichuan. Appointing his relatives to positions, seizing wealth, and even becoming addicted to opium. Yet the path to recovery is ever present, and following Zhu's departure to Europe, he would be instilled with new purpose.\n\nValuing the National Revolutionary Army as the most important defender of Socialist political power, Zhu upholds the ideals that it must be a class army, in mission and in spirit. That it must not be isolated from the masses, an instrument to oppress the workers and peasants such as the forces of imperialists. Instead the NRA should be the armed power of liberation for the masses of workers and peasants, an army with an international mission. For it is not technique, modern armaments, or extreme firepower that decides a revolutionary war, but that of politics, political systems and the inclination or disinclination of people's hearts. For in that ideal and hope, is why Zhu De serves and fights.
Hero of the left, shrouded in the right.

However, he knows when to be humble.
Yunnan's Socialist Stars
The Third Army, having secured the Kuomintang's new stronghold in the Chinese Southwest, has undoubtedly played a crucial role in the irreversible march towards modernity and advance of the National Revolution. Yet, a clear vision for the future remains unclear. General Zhu Peide is no stranger to compromise and is flexible on the outlook of a Nationalist China.\n\nMany in the 3rd Army have more radical views on China's future, seeing the Kuomintang National Revolution as a Socialist Revolution. Zhu De, as the head of the 3rd Army's Officer Training Corps, has worked to see the 3rd Army become a force for the peasants and workers of China, slowly shifting the Army towards a leftward direction, with two rising champions of the working class taking the spotlight.\n\nWang Desan found himself in socialist circles in the early 1920s, having joined the Marxism Research Society as a French translator. In 1922, he joined the CSP through Deng Zhongxia's introduction, and became associated with the Kuomintang when he later became a political instructor at the Whampoa Military Academy. Wang is one of the main faces of socialism in the Chinese Southwest.\n\nThe other rising star, Luo Binghui, was born to a family of destitute farmers. When he began his military career, he served under Tang Jiyao. He eventually came to oppose him, and joined Zhu Peide's Nationalist army. Luo's upbringing left him with strong hatred towards landlords and gentry and led him to secretly join the Chinese Syndicalist Party and drift towards Zhu De.\n\nWith the rise of the aforementioned stars, and of so many others in the leftist sphere, many have begun to refuse to recognise the authority of Zhu Peide, or of Li Genyuan. Even to the point that various members of the common soldiery have begun to only report to leftist aligned officers, such as Chen Yi, or Wang Jun. Such events have, rather obviously, caused friction between the bands of brothers known as the Third Army Rightists and Leftists, even in spite of the common comradery many on both sides still feel for each other.

The Party and the Qing Bang
Since the early revolutionary days of resisting the Manchu monarchy, the patriotic and heroic revolutionaries could be found in not just the Nationalist Party but also in the Chinese underworld. Many of the party's leaders, such as Sun Yat-sen, Chiang Kai-shek, and Chen Qimei, held links to the secret societies of triads and gangsters, bound together by the ethereal vice of greed but yet displaying a curious sense of heroic loyalty, one akin to that of the Japanese Yakuza or Italian Mafia. While these criminal ties helped the Nationalist Party in some of its weakest days, these unsavory connections have damaged their reputation since then.\n\nBoth the CC Clique and the Blueshirts (despite their mutual animosity and distrust for one another) hold extensive ties with the Green Gangs. Liu Shanchan for example, is a Blueshirts member but also holds close ties with Shanghai's underworld - boasting about 10,000 followers in the Big Sword and Small Swords societies. In recent times, the parties have even employed triads to enforce Kuomintang loyal unions and resolve labour conflicts in attempts at yellow unionism. While unions have generally been viewed with skepticism within the party due to the CSP and LCS, enforcing sole Kuomintang unions is a lot easier with the control of violent gangsters especially in the coastal cities.\n\nAs many of the party's right-wing sees the goal is to ultimately absorb the Triads, at the time being it does not seem possible given their pervasive influence. Scandals have already emerged when the gangster Shi Liangcai has discovered a secret silver deal between top Kuomintang officials like Kong Xiangxi, who has in turn through the Political Science Clique demanded that Shi be quietly dealt with. But with party cynicism at an all time high and corruption and brutality so normalized, one has to ask who the real criminals are?

The Cycle of Corruption
Guanxi, which does not have a direct translation (Guan means closed and caring, Xi means system), is heavily embedded in Chinese culture. It describes a deeply personal network, a connection stronger than a mere business relation or a personal contact, which has its origins in Confucianism and plays a role in politics, business, and society.\n\nTo many outsiders in the Republic however, this term has become synonymous with \

Corruption Scandals in Guizhou
As a party dedicated to the uplifting of China's masses, the issue of land is one that has torn apart the party at its ideological seams - with both the left and right wings of the Kuomintang unable to come to a compromise as to how to best handle the issue of land. Particular criticism has been leveled at Generalissmo He, who owns extensive land holdings in Guizhou despite the government promising fair and adequate land reforms. The Generalissmo's brother-in-law, Wang Boqun has also come under public scrutiny. An early member of the Tongmenghui who was associated with Dr. Sun Yat-sen during the attempted peace conference after the Wuchang Uprising, Wang had recently served as the Minister of Transportation and has been noted for his accomplishments in improving communications, railways, and even aviation. However, a public campaign in recent months has exposed Wang for participating in the opium trade, with many in the government, especially those in the Civil Engineering Clique, calling him to resign. Gu Zhenglun, the prominent Guizhou politician, has also called for an investigation into the Generalissmo's holdings (perhaps in an attempt to undermine He's control of the province) - a declaration supported by those in the Political Science Clique. To a lesser extent, He Yingqin's younger brother He Jiwu, who has worked under Wang Boqun as the Vice Chairman of China Airlines, a Provincial Government member, and a candidate for Mayor of Guiyang (the provincial Capital), has come under scrutiny.

The Zhejiang-isation of the Party
The Kuomintang, long based out of Guangzhou, is a primarily southern dominated party. Cantonese politicians and others hailing from Guangdong used to make up the bulk of the party apparatus. However, due to the influence of Dai Jitao, Chen Guofu and the late Chiang Kai-shek, the New Right draws much of its rank and file from Zhejiang and to a lesser extent Guizhou. This has caused resentment and a protracted battle for influence with the Cantonese old guard.\n\nShanghai, and the wealthy Jiangnan region has been a major hub of political activity among the Kuomintang right, even after the establishment of the Legation Cities. Guanxi networks that tie many leading party members to the city have allowed men like the Chen brothers to tap into family, social, banking, regional, and political connections to develop the CC Clique as a political force, and the memory of revolutionary leader Chen Qimei still carries weight even in a changed Shanghai society. Building a Party Line
Building a cohesive party-line has been the tireless mission of the Central Political School, dominated by the CC Clique. Dai Jitao and Chen Guofu personally had helped to pick out many of Whampoa's earliest classes, and Chen Lifu's involvement in the Organisational Department of the Party has helped to keep out unwanted fifth columnists like socialists.\n\nBut there is more to a Party Orthodoxy than simply excluding the disloyal. The Kuomintang must present a new vision for Chinese Society, one that can mobilise the masses to make the sacrifices needed to give them victory.\n\nThe New Culture Movement has influenced the party, both in opposition and in favour of its various ideals. Many of the civics textbooks printed by the Party School have emphasised the modern and egalitarian nature of the Three Principles of the People, mirroring New Culture demands for increased production, progressive development, women's rights, and social and economic welfare for the working classes.\n\nHowever, the party has also, under the influence of Dai Jitao, come down strongly against the anti-traditionalism and individualism of the movement. They have called for an organically integrated society, built and guided by the party which would simultaneously be integrated but also an independent, objective, and tutoring entity.\n\nThere are some internal contradictions within this concept of an ideal Kuomintang society. More traditionalist thinkers conceive it as reinvigorating the old family-based hierarchies of the idealised past - this Neo-Confucian style civilisation would be built on strings of hierarchical relationships that combine into a collective mass. More modernist philosophers have suggested instead that society ought to be effectively homogenised - one organically united party-state.

Zhu Peide's submission to the central government in exchange for their recognition of his governorship has strengthened his hand considerably. Having received secret authorisation from the Central Executive Committee, Zhu has moved to arrest Long Yun and many of his closest allies, bringing Zhaotong firmly under his control.\n\nKunming's submission to [ROOT.OVERLORD.Capital.GetCapitalVictoryPointName] has shifted the balance of power, not only in the broader Chinese political scene, but also in the southwest. Li Genyuan's Second Chance
In order to preserve his tenuous hold over Yunnan, Zhu Peide has been forced to lean on a host of different pillars. These include leftist radicals in the Third Army, his fellow rightists (of dubious loyalty) in the Third Army, the Yunnan Gentry, and now increasingly the resurgent Political Science Clique - carefully balancing all of these actors against one another.\n\nZhu's partnership with Li Genyuan is in some ways a natural one and in others a dangerous gamble. General Li remains an openly ambitious man, even if he nominally remains in the back seat. A former Cai E deputy and instructor at Kunming Military Academy, his has historically clashed with Tang Jiyao and the Yunnan Gentry over his revolutionary charisma but also broke with Sun Yat-sen and the mainline Kuomintang for his support of Cen Chunxuan and promotion of North-South unification talks in the 1920s. Many wonder if Governor Zhu will be much different from Gu Pinzhen (who's brief Governorship was marred by accusations of being a puppet of Li), though Zhu has always been capable of political independence of his own right - having remained neutral during the Gu-Tang conflict.\n\nThe Political Science Clique's influence extends to Guizhou too. He Yingqin's Fading Light
He Yingqin's relationship with his fellow National Revolutionary Army commanders has always been contentious, seen as too much of a Chiang lackey to be trusted but having betrayed Chiang at a few crucial moments that have undermined trust within the Whampoa circle. His chief advantage in maintaining his influence during the exile period was his extensive ties to Guizhou. But now that the Kuomintang has reasserted control over many other provinces, his leverage has weakened considerably. Not particularly intimidating as a person or exceptionally charismatic, he has been easy to isolate on a national scale.\n\nThanks to Zhu Peide and Li Genyuan's scheming, the Central Government has quietly pressured the Whampoa General to resign from his Governorship over Guizhou. They have noted his disloyalty and corruption, and accused him of insubordination to the national revolution. He has been replaced by Zhang Qun, an ascendant Political Science Clique alumni and a long-time Chiang Kai-shek staffer.\n\nZhang has promoted extensive reforms and close cooperation with Kunming, but has been unable to prevent a power vacuum from emerging. He Yingqin and his allies retain ties among prominent members of the local gentry. Zhang Qun's decision to recruit Yang Yongtai, a former Political Science Clique associate previously exiled, is expected to mend some ties, but they have proven obstinate with their leader's fall.\n\nMore concerning is the reaction of many members of the First Army, which is dominated by Whampoa Military Academy graduates. Leftists in the Southwest
The southwest has historically been a relatively conservative region, though revolutionary nationalism and leftist sympathies are hardly unheard of. There was a considerable revolutionary Tongmenghui presence during the 1911 Revolution, even as they competed with reformist factions for influence. Wang Fusheng and other Yunnan students studying under Li Dazhao in Beijing established an underground circle of syndicalists known as the New Dian Society, vowing to bring the revolution to their home province.\n\nThe arrival of the Kuomintang following the Long March brought another wave of leftism into the region. Wang made an attempt to establish a clandestine socialist circle in the College of Law and Politics, publishing the Minguo Ribao (Daily Republic) but was quickly evicted. The New Yunnan Society (sometimes translated as the New Dian Society, Dian being the Chinese abbreviation of Yunnan), broke from the more urban focused approach of the mainline CSP and fled to the countryside in hopes of organising the peasants, local minorities, and bandits against Tang Jiyao's rule.\n\nThe more serious contenders however are backed by the military. Chen Cheng is a protégé of both Deng Yanda and Chiang Kai-shek, embodying the revolutionary left-nationalism of the early Whampoa Military Academy. Nicknamed the Civil Engineering Clique, they have cannibalised other Whampoa factions in Guizhou.\n\nZhu De is another rising star. A close friend of Zhu Peide and with ties to Fan Shisheng and Jin Handing, he is prominent within the Yunnan military. In correspondence with Zhang Bojun, a prominent Provisional Action Committee member (a leading political force within the Kuomintang in the MinGan insurgency zone), his circle is a notable outpost of leftism in the province.
Wang Fusheng and the New Yunnan Society clash with the gentry!
Chen Cheng and the Civil Engineering Clique battle for Guizhou!
Zhu De and the Third Army Leftists struggle for Kunming!
The Party and the Tusi
The Kuomintang has had a complex relationship with minorities in China, mirroring the Han dominated Xinhai Revolution's own complicated attitudes towards Chinese nationalism. The Kuomintang claims to stand for all Chinese, regardless of ethnic heritage, but has frequently suffered accusations of Han Chauvinism. Their nationalism and social darwinism often clashes with their egalitarian ideals, which has been further complicated in practice by their webs of local alliances with warlords, the gentry, socialists, and minority groups.\n\nYunnan and Guizhou are both home to a large population of ethnic minorities. These include the Yi, Bai, Buyei, Dong, Tujia, Miao, Hani, Zhuang, Dai, and many others. Minorities have played a complex role in regional politics, historically the Qing Dynasty sought to enlist their leaders as allies, classifying native chiefs as Tuguan (for civilian roles) or Tusi (for military ones) and seeking to educate their children to create a reliable caste of vassals. This has played a role in border disputes with the British over Burma, where even after Qing authority receded local provincial leaders continued to recruit Tusi to stake Chinese claims over disputed territories and demanded tribute. Miao traders in Yunnan also played a role in commercial disputes against concessions.\n\nEthnic minorities in Yunnan have also played a major role during the Xinhai Revolution. Zhang Wenguang published a republican, populist manifesto and tried successfully to capture Dali with a Tongmenghui army that consisted of many minorities and Muslims. One of his chief deputies was Dao Anren, a minority chieftain who died in exile trying to make contact with Sun Yat-sen.\n\nWith the Kuomintang's victory in the region, many are watching to see how they treat local ethnic groups - a delicate matter when nearly a third of the population are non-Han. The role of the Tusi in particular can be contentious, with some arguing that they are critical allies to empowering local groups to oppose the gentry while others arguing that they are just as much backwards reactionaries as the gentry they oppose.
Let it be known that the Kuomintang seek to empower all people in China!
The Kuomintang is a force of progress and unity and will enlighten them!
Dueling Outlooks in Yunnan
Pre-Xinhai Revolution Yunnan had been dominated by two duelling political outlooks regarding the encroaching influence of foreign concessionists, one of reformist nationalism generally carried by the Yunnan gentry who sought to eliminate unfair foreign trade practices through domestic economic development and some cooperation with foreign traders and the other of revolutionary nationalism by commercial firms and ethnic minorities who sought to promote a freer domestic market and the nationalisation of foreign rail monopolies.\n\nThis divide between reformists and revolutionaries continued after the Xinhai Revolution, with Cai E's decision to side with the reformists being decisive in swinging provincial politics against the radical Tongmenghui. Attempts by the Tongmenghui and later the Kuomintang to instigate rebellions against the reformist status quo were harshly quashed, with Kuomintang generals like Gu Pinzhen, Li Genyuan, Zhu Peide, Fan Shisheng, and Jin Handing finding little success in their various endeavours to \

Maintaining the radical course is how real change can come about.
Zhang Qun's Reign in Guizhou
After an uneven start, Zhang Qun's administration in Guizhou has begun to make some progress. The leftward drift of the region has made it difficult to root out, with many local workers and peasants finding sympathy with the Civil Engineering Clique of the First Army. Zhang, under the counsel of Yang Yongtai, has quickly come to the conclusion that using military force would be ineffective against Chen Cheng's growing influence.\n\nTo strengthen his regime, ostensibly for the benefit of [ROOT.OVERLORD.Capital.GetCapitalVictoryPointName], Zhang has begun the reintroduction of the Baojia militia system by creating household registers, training local militias (often led by sympathetic gentrymen), building fortifications, searching and arresting socialist elements, and beginning a campaign of \
