Zhu Peide and the Yunnan Provincial Assembly
The Yunnan Provincial Assembly is dominated by the local elite, including gentrymen, merchants, and the intelligentsia. It has proven to be a conservative liberal force, insisting on government accountability but also resisting the revolutionary reform promised by the Kuomintang. This phenomenon is hardly unique to Yunnan, with many provincial assemblies being established in the waning years of the Great Qing as imperial authority fragmented.\n\nDuring the Xinhai Revolution, Cai E and his militaristic rule tended to ignore the provincial assembly, a trend Tang Jiyao continued somewhat, but in his drive to build a federal, democratic province he was also willing to offer some concessions to the assembly. While Tang has fallen from power, many of his followers remain, creating a liberal and conservative blend in the provincial assembly that has clashed with the influx of Kuomintang aligned legislators.\n\nThis reflects Zhu Peide and Li Genyuan's similarly complex history with the Provincial Assembly. Both have proven willing to work with the institution, with Li beginning his career as an ideological militarist like Cai E but swapping to a more civilian focused approach as the revolution developed. He has summarised his approach by saying that \
The Assembly has a place in our province's future.
It is the party that truly represents the wishes of the people.
The Bazhai Uprising
The gentry and officials appointed by Kunming are often at odds, a challenge that stalked prior administrations and reflects the continued difficult realities of Kuomintang rule. The landed elite often control local security militias that are usually operating independent of government directives.\n\nOne of these many battles has played out in the village of Bazhai, reflecting the complex nature of the Yunnan political situation. The Maguan district government attempted to confiscate weapons from local tribespeople after a local militia captain refused to submit and was accused of collecting taxes without approval. This triggered a revolt, which was quickly backed by agitators from the syndicalist New Yunnan Society, allowing it to gain momentum.\n\nTo many's surprise, the local landlords threw their weight behind the Bazhai rebels, sending their militias to support the rebellion and intimidating government forces into backing down. The alliance between minority tribesmen, socialists, and the gentry quickly proved to be a shaky one, as leftists and reactionaries battled for influence within the movement, allowing the government to send reinforcements as the revolt stalled.\n\nIn the face of the government advance, radical commanders have advised a surprise attack in hopes of turning the tide before the gentry switch sides and leave them to take the fall. Gentry militia captains have instead advised negotiations with the government, hoping to end the conflict with little bloodshed.
An aggressive surprise attack allows surviving socialist forces to get away!
The rebels negotiate to preserve their numbers for the future.
Guizhou's Rising Stars
Zhang Qun, in his quest to bring the First Army under his control in Guizhou, has elevated many younger and less politically connected generals into positions of dual military and civilian authority. Xiong Shihui has been the most promising of this young guard, long an accountant of the Administrative Affairs Committee in Guizhou and well known among Zhang Qun's circle.\n\nDespite his military background (or perhaps because of it), his focus has been on public works projects, streamlining taxation, and fighting banditry. Appointed by Zhang as Director of the General Office of the Guiyang Headquarters, he has received praise for building parks, bridges, guest houses, roads and also abolishing over a hundred exorbitant taxes.\n\nThis has naturally drawn comparisons to the reforms of the Civil Engineering Clique, who boast of rising stars of their own. [FROM.Capital.GetCapitalVictoryPointName] Recognises the New Yunnan Society
The Yunnan Special Bureau of the Chinese Syndicalist Party is a covert group of former students turned revolutionaries with military-political training from Whampoa. Led by brothers Wang Desan and Wang Fusheng, they have achieved some success infiltrating the province on behalf of the broader New Yunnan Society, a society of Yunnan born syndicalists within the CSP. Official recognition to these rabble rousers by the Central Government has allowed them to step out of the shadows, spreading their malicious lies in the open.\n\nThe long term beneficiaries of this political development remain in the air. The New Yunnan Society is expected to expand in size thanks to the proclamation, but not all Yunnan syndicalists are confident in the CSP's long term ability to hold onto the region. From Temple to Battlefield
The Victor of the Third Army
Old Chief Zhu
He Yingqin is no stranger to war and politics, having participated in the Xinhai revolution under Chen Qimei's group and in the struggle for Guizhou with Wang Wenhua before becoming Chiang Kai-shek's leading deputy. Always more of a follower than a leader, he largely stayed out of politics but changing circumstances has thrust him into the spotlight. While hardly the most adroit politician or the most charismatic leader, there is a certain stability associated with the Generalissimo. He has been able to parlay his recent victories on the battlefield to great political effect, and been dubbed the \
Chen Cheng has claimed multiple victories on the battlefield in recent days, elevating his profile on a national stage. The protégé of Chiang Kai-shek and Deng Yanda, he takes from the former a Confucian-inspired, spartan lifestyle and from the latter a leftist populist platform. His public image is one of a stern disciplinarian, leading by example by insisting on no more than four dishes on the table per meal and living ascetically. The areas under his jurisdiction have seen frivolous behaviour like smoking, liquor and dancing banned and his troops take great pride in their commitment to the revolution. He also has a lighter side, known to be polite, considerate and gentle to guests.
Teng Jie is the master of the illusive Blue Shirts Society, which has come under greater scrutiny in recent days as General Teng and his men demonstrated their fanaticism on the battlefield. Primarily a political officer, his appointment to field command came as a surprise to some, but he quickly silenced any doubt with his acumen in leading his divisions to victory. The extremism of his men, inspired by the global national populist movement, as well as their tendency to buck orders from the civilian wing of the party has drawn concern, but their effectiveness is hard to doubt. Whether or not they will be a liability in the future is another question.
Li Genyuan is an experienced military man, organising exiled revolutionaries in Japan in preparation for the Xinhai Revolution. One of his first acts of leadership was the Double 9 Rebellion, which solidified his reputation as a committed republican revolutionary. Li Genyuan remains in the press thanks to his recent accomplishments in service of the National Revolutionary Army, bringing the Political Science Clique and their advocacy for constitutionalism to the forefront. In an already deeply politicised military, moderation is a break from the norm and his successes are proof that fanatical extremism is not the only way to save the nation.
Wang Boling was a prominent revolutionary and co-founder of Whampoa, but retired to a Buddhist Temple shortly after the Northern Expedition. Studying under Master Yin Guang and receiving the Buddhist name Hui Chang, most assumed that his public career was finished. But like the rest of the Kuomintang, the revolution received a second wind in 1936 and Wang was summoned out of retirement. He has well lived up to expectations, winning significant engagements against the enemy and bringing honour to his allies in the CC Clique. Outside of his military responsibilities, he is known for championing Buddhist causes, growing close to Master Taixu and advocating for the reconstruction of the Jiuzu Mountain Dojo.
Zhu Peide is perhaps the most outstanding officer of the Yunnan Army, having spent several decades fighting for the national revolution. Known for his diligence, humility, and honesty, the Third Army continues to be a prominent force in Kuomintang affairs - bolstered by General Zhu's recent victories on the battlefield. Known for his advocacy of building up China's national defences and for material simplicity, his faction generally supports a more moderate interpretation of the Three Principles closer aligned to the \
Zhu De has lived a storied life, growing up in poverty before becoming a soldier for a variety of military men. His life changed when he became a revolutionary, dropping his opium habit and learning more about socialism and military theory in Europe. He returned home a different man, and has been instrumental in reorganising the Third Army into a potent revolutionary fighting force for the people. They proved their worth in recent days, driving back their foes in a gruelling campaign that has won Zhu the admiration of many leftists in Yunnan. With the support of the people and his innovative tactics, he vows to liberate the entire nation!

The Fall of Hunan
With Changsha firmly in our control, it's paramount we appoint a loyal Governor to the Province, and end its military occupation. To that end, we have a few options for Governor open to us\n\nFirst is the civilian candidate Qin Zhen, as a member of the Western Hills clique, he's naturally backed by the Moscow Committee. An old guard revolutionary, he would make a natural fit for Governor and could show a commitment to civilian rule from the KMT. The concern, of course, is that his ties to the Moscow Committee means he would serve as a potential future rival.\n\nThe second candidate, 6th Army Commander Cheng Qian, may be able to bridge that gap. Sympathetic to the Moscow Committee, Cheng was also a teacher at Whampoa, making him a solid pick to form a bridge between the Moscow Committee and He's Whampoa clique. However, Cheng is infamous for his Hunanese regionalism, to the point of leaving Guizhou and moving to take power in Hunan instead of marching on Kunming.\n\nThe third major candidate is Tan Yankai, while former commander of the 2nd Army, he was always more of a civilian figure, and has been Governor of Hunan three times in the past, still remaining popular in the Province. He left the Guizhou Junta for Hunan when the remnants of his Army were placed under Cheng Qian. Despite this, he kept contact with our forces, specifically with his son-in-law, Chen Cheng, and is attempting to leverage those connections in his bid for Governor.\n\nThere is a fourth candidate, while he may not hold the same clout as the other three, He Jian, a subordinate of the Left's Tang Shengzhi, has made a bid for Governor. A committed anti-socialist, he has tried to cultivate ties with the Whampoa Clique to gain the Governorship. An untested figure, he may be a controversial pick, but he would help shore up He Yingqin's position in Government.

The Northern Kuomintang
As the National Revolutionary Army crosses the Yellow River, the possibility of a Kuomintang victory in the Northern Expedition draws ever closer. Talk of the Kuomintang finally toppling Beiyang and installing their own vision for the Republic has created considerable chatter across the country, especially regarding what would happen next?\n\nThe Kuomintang, despite their pretensions of representing the entire country, is primarily a southern-based party. Sun Yat-sen, Hu Hanmin, and Wang Jingwei were all from Guangdong, and their successive governments were based out of Guangzhou. Chiang Kai-shek and Dai Jitao were from Zhejiang, and a smaller subset of the party comes from the southeast. Many of the party's rank and file come from the wider south, and the party has a short bench of candidates for leading northern branches of the party.\n\nAmong the party faithful, Ding Weifen and Yu Youren are the leading figures of the northern Kuomintang and thus have been rewarded with CEC seats. Ding Weifen has been described as reticent and a man of few but very impactful words. He had been a leader of secretive societies like the Grand Alliance, Revive China Society and Practice Society, active in provinces like Shandong and Hubei, which served as Kuomintang front organisations. Yu Youren has been described as a man of optimism and sincerity, with a powerful build and speech that epitomises the strength of the north, undeterred by age.\n\nDespite the hard work of our comrades Ding and Yu, there is a shortage of trustworthy party cadres. However, there is no shortage of people willing to be loyal to the party, as Zhang Qun and Yang Yongtai have pointed out. They believe that with their connections to Beiyang, they can secure the defection of many moderate republican figures like Huang Fu and unite the nation at last. This has raised the ire of the hardliners, who denounce these men as corrupt sellouts who dilute the party. They call for tried and true loyalists to be sent north to reinforce and “teach” a new generation of party members.
Let us lean on our northern comrades' connections to filter candidates.
The only way forward is to unite with our Beiyang brethren.
Let us stick with those we can trust...
The Kunming Coup
With most officers, including many loyalists of the old regime, understanding that the tide of the war had turned irrevocably against Yunnan, Generals Long Yun and Hu Ruoyu were able to stage a coup d'etat and bring the war to an unfavourable but accepted end. Yunnan Army units under Hu Ruoyu at the front line mutinied and arrested loyalist senior officers, Long Yun was able to seize Kunming with the help of sympathisers in the police and garrison. Other regions fell shortly afterwards as other generals defected to the conspirators.\n\nTang Jiyao is nominally still the governor, with no outsiders having recognised any change in leadership since his overthrow. However, he has not made any public appearances and it is an open secret that he has been detained with no access to outside communication - a lesson Long and Hu learned the hard way during his original overthrow in 1927. In the meantime, the two Generals are fighting over the spoils.\n\nThe disaster that was the Second Dian-Gui War likely marks the final end of Yunnan interventionism as Yunnan turns inward. As defeated troops march home with their tails between their legs, anger surges - some blaming their leaders for sending them on yet another doomed campaign, others accusing them of stabbing them in the back. In Kunming, there has been a similar upswing of anger and radicalism from both supporters and opponents of the war, a crisis that will have to be addressed soon. But leaders outside of Yunnan sigh in relief as the once belligerent province returns into isolation.
An ignominious failure.
Yunnan Generals Stage Coup in Kunming
Despite their bluster and their proud military heritage, the armies of Yunnan and Guizhou ultimately failed to make meaningful progress during the Second Yunnan-Guangxi War. With their advances either stalled or driven back, fears abounded that their opponents would invade Yunnan proper, propelling leading Yunnan Army officers to overthrow their own government and sign an armistice.\n\nLeadership in Yunnan is in a flux. Official documents continue to be signed by externally-recognised Governor Tang Jiyao, but he has not been seen in public in some time. The other names coming out of Kunming are Long Yun and Hu Ruoyu, who are said to be fighting for supremacy. Either way, this likely marks the end of Yunnan's military adventuring as the mountain province turns towards isolationism.
The Yunnan dragon sleeps.
An Uneasy Truce
The post-coup political situation is chaotic and uneasy as Yunnan polarises into different camps. Among the two ascendant strongmen, Long Yun generally represents the status quo, courting Tang Jiyao's old ministers and promoting a message of order and stability. He hopes to combine his extensive military ties with his former mentor's political patronage network to control the province and ensure a smooth transition.\n\nHu Ruoyu on the other hand has taken a reactionary stance, hoping to drastically shake up the government and eliminate the \
Long Yun represents an orderly transition to isolation.
Hu Ruoyu stands for peace and national unity!
The Death of Tang Jiyao
Tang Jiyao died in his home, ending weeks of speculation about his fate. Negotiations were tense between the Long and Hu camps, with the former preferring that he remain imprisoned to prevent him from raising another army outside the province and to project the image of stability to outsiders while the latter sought his exile or execution. Tang's followers, fearing for his declining health and his safety, asked for his safe passage out of the province.\n\nTang's death is officially due to illness, but he is widely believed by his supporters to have been poisoned. Whatever the case may have been, the fragile truce between the conspirators was broken. Hu Ruoyu struck first, surrounding Long Yun's home and disarming his bodyguard. Long was captured alive, albeit with an eye injury, and this may have been the end of it except most of his generals escaped and rallied forces in Zhaotong. A counterattack by General Lu Han, Long Yun's relative, shattered Zhang Ruji's attempt to seize the city. A siege is looming, and Long Yun's forces have the advantage - except Hu Ruoyu has been quick to point out that their leader remains imprisoned in a literal iron cage.\n\nMeanwhile, General Wang Jiexu has betrayed Hu Ruoyu and his Mengzi Clique. He and many Tang Jiyao loyalists have gathered in Dali in an effort to restore Tang Jiyu. The presence of a third force in the province and the prospects of a civil war forces the two sides to the negotiating table.
Long Yun's army helps him secure favourable terms for his release.
Hu Ruoyu's control of the railroads gives him bargaining power during talks.
Tang Jiyu's Rebellion
Long Yun has been released, ending the conflict between the Long camp and the Hu camp for now. But Tang Jiyu remains a serious concern, having been allowed to return from his brief exile for his brother's funeral. With the original 1927 coup explicitly targeting ten of Tang's closest loyalists, including Mayor Zhang Weihan of Kunming, Minister of Foreign Affairs and Finance Xu Zhichen, Manager of the Yunnan Tin Company Wu Kun, and many of Tang's top generals including Tang Jiyu and Chen Weigeng, this one is bound to be little different.\n\nRealising the gravity of the situation, they rallied under Tang's brother Tang Jiyu in Sun Du's fortress of Dali. General Tang, hoping to make a claim for his brother's old position, has sought to move quickly on Kunming in hopes of garnering the support of the gentry and uniting Tang's scattered loyalists across the province. Rumors have spread that they have even gotten the sympathy of General Lu Han, one of Long Yun's leading generals, for their plot - a great betrayal if true.\n\nHowever, the attempt was largely in vain. Many of Tang's forces are ex-bandits like Generals Wang Jiexiu and Wu Xuexian and melted away when facing their opponents. His brother's ex-guard units have mostly tried to defect to either Long Yun or Hu Ruoyu or are simply too isolated to do much. Within a few weeks they have been forced to surrender or go underground.\n\nWhile Long has resolved to absorb Tang's leading officials, the fate of his generals is another matter. Ironically, his position with Hu Ruoyu has been reversed, with Hu hoping to retain some of them like Meng Youwen in his desperation to gain an edge over Long and Long ordering the execution or exile of his armed opponents.
Make an example out of them!
Exile Tang Jiyu, absorb the rest.
A Dying Dream of Democracy
The budding democratic movement Tang Jiyao and Zhang Taiyan have cultivated in Yunnan across the late 1920s has suffered severe setbacks with Tang's fall. Tang was an authoritarian man to be sure, but the two men who have replaced him pay even less lip-service to democratic ideals, preferring backroom deals with known quantities in the provincial assembly than pursuing greater representation or reform.\n\nThe civil war after Hu Ruoyu's putsch, however brief, served to further demonstrate the powerlessness of Yunnan's intellectuals as they could do little but watch as the two strongmen traded blows. The elderly Zhang Taiyan has mostly retreated into retirement and Tang's People's Government Party has faded to basically nothing but another name in China's history books. His son Tang Xiaoying, who has been elected as his father's successor as Chairman, has mostly turned his attention to the youthful activism of the Young China Party while serving as a minor functionary for Long Yun.\n\nFor the remaining democrats, their options are limited. Some return to the traditional haughty aloofness intellectuals took during the high warlord era, either leaving the province for more prestigious academic jobs in wealthier provinces or limiting their activism in the province. Long Yun and Lu Han have made limited entreaties to groups like the Crescent Moon Society. Others, still hungry for political change and direct action, turn towards socialism, turning their rural schoolhouses and literature clubs into underground networks for radicalism.
The dream is not dead, it just needs adjustments.
We need to wake up and realise words alone are not enough.

The Situation in Guizhou
Despite the chaotic rout in the Second Yue-Gui War, Long Yun still has aspirations for maintaining Yunnanese influence over neighbouring Guizhou. The neighbouring provinces became even closer when Tang Jiyao intervened in the conflict between Guizhou's Constitutionalists and Self-Government factions. He continued to back the exiled Liu Xianshi for the Governorship until the latter's death in 1927, and his family continued to operate out of Yunnan while plotting their return.\n\nDespite recent reverses, no central government has been capable of extending their influence into the southwest, emboldening Long Yun to try to reassert Yunnan's dominance over Guizhou. Since the Yue-Gui War, Guizhou has been ruled by Liu's family, including his cousin Liu Xianqian and his nephew Liu Gongliang. Their feeble attempts at asserting Guizhou's independence from Kunming has been undermined by outside governments' indifference towards Dian-Qian affairs.\n\nHe has attempted to aid Zhang Ruji and the Mengzi Clique to check Long Yun's dominance, fearing the latter's ability to extend his power out of his base in nearby Zhaotong. Long has responded by sowing discord between the Liu clan and also funneling support towards local elite Yi Rongqian. Neither have much hope of truly escaping Kunming's orbit, but as the two sides circle one another with Yunnanese weapons, the proxy conflict has taken greater regional significance.
The more powerful Liu family fends off their new challenger.
With Long Yun's help, Yi Rongqian defeats the larger but divided Liu clan.
The Elder Statesmen Challenge Long
Tang Jiyao may be gone forever, but remnants of the old regime still remain. Despite his overtures, Long Yun's efforts at consolidating control has drawn resistance from Yunnan's old guard. The Provincial Assembly, those functionally subservient to the Governor, is a powerful symbol of legitimacy and a clique of elders led by Ding Zhoaguan, Wang Jiuling, and You Yunlong have stalled Long's agenda.\n\nDing Zhaoguan is a Japanese educated politician who served in the Sichuan Provincial Financial Department before returning to Yunnan to serve as principal of Yunnan Law and Politics College. A jurist by trade, he was appointed to multiple senior positions in the Department of Justice under Tang and was appointed Director of Civil Affairs by Long, but has since threatened to resign.\n\nWang Jiuling was also educated in Japan and participated in the Xinhai revolution before becoming Tang Jiyao's secretary. Tang later made him director of finance before sending him to negotiate with the Beiyang Government, where he was made Minister of Education under Duan Qirui in 1924, before returning to Yunnan to work in the salt commission and Fudian bank. An honorary president of Donglu University as well, he also holds roles in various social groups such as the Yunnan Provincial Buddhist Association, the Institute of Sinology, and the Southern Yashi Society.\n\nFinally, You Yunlong was the founder of the Yunnan Daily newspaper. He played a key role helping organise the National Protection War and served as acting governor of Yunnan while Tang was away. The former Director of Education as well as an industrialist, he was also the speaker of the Second Yunnan Provincial Congress and was recently elected Chairman of the Senate.\n\nThough mostly seen as antiquated and in prestigious but powerless positions, with Hu Ruoyu constantly searching for opportunities to undermine Long, handling these men quickly is essential for Long's survival.
Long outmanoeuvres the elders and consolidates his position.
The elders humble Long, weakening his grip.
The Role of the Modern Military Man
In traditional Chinese society, being a career military officer was generally looked down upon by respectable families. Of course, the apex of society constituted the scholar-officials who contended themselves with nothing more than the complexities of poetic discourse and bureaucratic administration. Of course from time to time they may have to lead the common rabble into battle, but that was merely a temporary sacrifice they had to make. Soldiers on the other hand, required no education and sophistication; they were merely expected to serve as sacrifices for the protection of their lords and superiors and to guarantee China's protection from barbarians.\n\nBut with the National Protection War, a curious intellectual passion for the military has arisen. The idea of a National Army dedicated to the support of Chinese constitutionalism and the Republic has resonated deeply with many especially in a province like Yunnan; which was the first province to secede and declare a war of protection against Beijing's autocracy. Military service was encouraged and conscription frequently practiced; as a necessary evil in the face of much larger superior military forces. By the time Tang Jiyao had reasserted control over the province however, civilian control was restored over the army and warlords - as military leaders like Tang asserted themselves as benevolent rulers rather than a perpetual sense of martial law.\n\nNow as Yunnan enters yet another turbulent period in its history, the question remains. How long should the military's rule over the people really last? On one hand, the military's leadership was undeniably needed in a time of crisis but crises abate and for now, there is time before the province must be fully mobilised. The military's presence however, provides a strong deterrent towards banditry and criminal behavior - that is surely well appreciated in these trying times.
We only use good iron to make nails in times of desperation.
The Generals keep us safe in these trying times.
Lu Han, the Discontented Kinsman
Lu Han has been at Long Yun's side since they were boys, studying martial arts together in Zhaotong. After graduating from Yunnan Military Academy, the two served together in the Yunnan Army. At one point they were so close to each other, that it was even rumoured that Lu Han was Long Yun's younger half brother. However, as of late, Lu Han has privately expressed his discontent with the direction of Long's regime. A myriad of potential issues illustrate the reason for Lu's break from Long but at its core, it appears that Governor Long's staffing decisions both in the ministry and in the military has apparently favoured many of the former Tang loyalists.\n\nThe crux of this issue is the recent appointment of Zhang Weihan. A politician under the Tang regime, Weihan was the former General Affairs Section of the Provincial Administrative office and the mayor of Kunming. After the coup, Long had merely retained Weihan in his position of power and both Weihan and Lu Han have never been able to agree on anything.\n\nWhile most of Tang's former camp that has been allowed to reside in the province sided with Long Yun, there are those who can be swayed with enough persuasion. Sun Du, is a graduate of the Yunnan Army Academy and served under Tang for a very long while. Appointed as the Director of Yunnan Tobacco and Alcohol Monopoly Bureau, he remained loyal to Tang until the very end. Known as the Confucian General and \
Long Yun finds ways to mollify his misguided relative.
Lu Han begins to undermine Long's clique from within.
Loyalty and Competence
Yunnan has been trying to stave off bankruptcy for some time. The treasury has been under strain for years due to expensive military adventures, chronic mismanagement of overambitious projects, an overreliance on opium, and endemic corruption. The coup may have saved Yunnan from being completely subjugated to the other southern provinces but for now, the economy is essentially dysfunctional.\n\nCharged with preventing total financial ruin is Miao Yuntai (also known as Miao Jianming), who is one of the few American-educated provincial businessmen. A successful tin industry executive, he was appointed by Long to co-manage Fudian bank and also oversee economic reconstruction in the province. His ambitious plans include shifting from a predominantly opium-based economy (the prices of which have collapsed due to virtually all warlords having the same idea, oversaturating the market) in favor of a tin-based economy and industrialisation.\n\nMiao has proven his worth time and time again, a true financial genius. But though Long values his services, unfortunately Miao and other successful technocrats' efforts have been hampered by many of Long's other appointments. Tang Hongtao, one of Long's former secretaries, in particular has attracted scandal due to his mismanagement of the Yunnan Tin Company. Despite his engineering background, he has proven horribly incompetent, neglectful and possibly outright lazy.\n\nThe clash between Long's patronage appointees, which are vital for bringing the economy under his control, and Long's technocratic appointees which are vital for bringing the economy under control is expected to continue to play out as investors weigh their options.
We must put our trust in Miao Yuntai and other experts!
Trust comes first, results will follow eventually.
Reflections on 1915
What was the National Protection War? Socialists like Guo Murou argue that it was a militarist conflict between the warlords, although even they disagree among themselves over the role of the masses and the Progressive Party and the Kuomintang. Other historians argue that Sun's revolutionary Kuomintang played a major role and that the masses themselves sought liberation during the war. In Yunnan, Tang Jiyao supported Liang Qichao and his Progressives during the war, but resisted Sun's demands for a revolution.\n\nIndeed the National Protection War reflected but also failed to resolve the on-going nationalist question in China. Among the Yunnanese gentry and traders by the Mengzi mining areas - nationalism meant becoming an equal partner to the foreigners and resolving trade imbalances. The so-called \

The National Protection Junta protected Yunnan's autonomy!
The National Protection Era was the building block of democracy.
Socialist Hopes Dim in Yunnan
In Yunnan, 75% of the peasants own most or all their land but they usually own less than 5 mu which rendered their ownership virtually useless in face of taxes and levies. Driven by the spirit of progress, democratic reformers like Cun Shusheng and Su Honggang have been active in the countryside providing rural education (with varying degrees of support from the provincial government) and seeking to improve the plight of the peasants.\n\nBut the limitations of these reforms have driven many others to a desperate radicalism. However, unlike in MinGan where the socialists were able to garner support from the peasantry and the bandits, their Yunnanese counterparts have failed to achieve meaningful class cooperation. As their movement began to fail, they sought shaky alliances with local magistrates.\n\nProvincial agitator Wang Desan and his New Yunnan Society tried to trigger an uprising in Liuliang country amid conflicts between the gentry and Kunming magistrates. Vowing to penetrate the \
Peasant uprisings convince the gentry that only Hu Ruoyu can protect them.

Yunnan and the Central Government
Protected by the mountains in China's distant southwest and home to a melting pot of cultures, Yunnan's isolation has created a complicated history with central authority. After the bloody 1800s rebellion, secessionism was crushed. But the locals feared European imperialism, and resented the central government's inability to police their borders - often taking matters into their own hands.\n\nDespite often being rebels themselves, Yunnanese leaders often spoke of the need for a strong central government. Virtually all of Cai E's stances revolved around that principle, insisting Beijing remain the capital to help maintain China's influence in Manchuria and Mongolia. He also stated that China was unsuited for federalism - instead proposing provinces be abolished as a governing unit. Yunnan itself could be ruled by four circuits with senior officials appointed directly from Beijing.\n\nCai resented the Tongmenghui legislature's obstinance towards President Yuan and believed corruption could be avoided if Presidents could remain impartial in their appointments rather than rely on partisan politics. Valuing national unity and strength over ideology, he prophetically warned that the parties would seek to each build an army around themselves and local despots would carve their own domains in the provinces. He stipulated that soldiers should not be part of any party, lest the army \
China needs a strong government, but it must be a responsible one too.
We must carefully safeguard our own interests until one arises.
Su Honggang, Educator of the Masses
While Yunnan has traditionally hosted a number of liberal elements following the Shanghai Uprising of 1932 and the subsequent brutal crackdown by Zhili forces, the Provincial Committee of the China Democratic League in Yunnan certainly brings its own weight to the table of anti-Beijing intellectuals. Su Honggang, one of the Provincial CDL's leading voices, was born in 1878 into a middling family in the village outskirts of Kunming.\n\nAlthough his family was not particularly wealthy, Su's father often aided poorer members of the village, a noble act that inspired Su Honggang as a child. After growing up, Su embarked on an educational career, staying in his home village to teach after his father's passing. Su became enthralled by the revolutionary politics of the Tongmenghui in 1903, and committed himself to utilising education to save the nation after the Republicans ousted the decrepit Qing monarchy. In the 20s, Su worked on establishing a private school that would eventually become one of the most famous middle schools in Yunnan.\n\nAfter the League crackdown in 1932, Su Honggang welcomed the fleeing intellectuals and together collaborated to establish the Southwest Associated University, which became a hotbed for progressive politics. Although not always particularly fond of newer idealistic progressive elements, the [ROOT.GetLeader] administration has seen fit to maintain a friendly relationship with Su and his colleagues at the Southwest University.
Su Honggang and the CDL are a credit to the people, indeed.
A bold dreamer with the administration's ear.
The Duel for Youth Support
It is often said that the intellectual class has preferred to stay in their ivory towers of learning and distrust the intellectual ability of the masses, but that certainly does not extend to the Chinese youth in academia. Viewed as a critical battleground in the war of ideas, various intellectual political groups are often found preaching to various student bodies in the hopes of attracting young minds to their political organisations. The China Democratic League's Yunnan branch is no different from the rest in this regard, and has launched an extensive educational campaign to draw supporters in various Universities with members serving as faculty.\n\nAt the centre of it all is of course Southwestern Associated University, which serves not only as the centre of the China Democratic League's Provincial Branch, but also the Crescent Moon Society. Recruiting from the student masses has become all the more important, as in the Northern plains, it is the nationalistic and militant Young China Party which has dominated student recruitment, becoming a mass party rivalling the Kuomintang through its impressive recruitment drive. Although differing substantially in political outlook, the progressive intellectual camps of the south have taken note of their approach to politics, and attempt to become mass intellectual movements themselves.
The CDL shall be the forefront of a Free China!
Allies in the fight for a Free China.
Miao Yuntai, the Miracle Man
Perhaps suffering greater than any province, Yunnan has gone through immense hardship throughout the Century of Humiliation. Perhaps these were the conditions which led to the rise of the Yunnan Chamber of Commerce headed by Miao Jiaming. Although Viceroy Li Jingxi's reforms were blocked by gentry forces, under the [ROOT.GetLeader] administration, the YCC seems to have spearheaded reform elements in Yunnan Province. Miao Jiaming was born into a prominent Yunnan gentry family in the 90s, and learned engineering in the United States. After a brief career in America, he returned home in October 1919.\n\nGovernor Tang Jiyao quickly took notice of the young engineer, and appointed him head of the Gejiu Tin Company. When he had taken over the company, it was suffering from financial losses, but Miao was able to pull Gejiu Tin out of its financial hole and modernised it with Western Technologies, restoring its status as a respected corporation. By 1924, Miao rose to head of the Fudian Bank, which was in practice the central bank of Yunnan. Tang Jiyao's severe borrowing from the bank led to high inflation, the dissolution of the Fudian Bank, and the collapse of the currency altogether.\n\nIn 1932, Miao Yuntai as President of the New Fudian Bank was able to use a system involving reserve half-krai dollars and New Yunnan Notes to remove the old Fudian Bank Notes from circulation and restore the financial state of Yunnan Province. After repairing Tang's finances, Miao was made the general manager of the Yunnan Tin Refining Company itself, and broke Yunnan's traditional economic reliance on Opium in favour of tin mining, and substantially curtailed German economic influence through the Kunming-Haiphong Rail. Today, Miao Jiaming retains significant control over Yunnan's economic organs as head of the Yunnan Chamber of Commerce, and the Yunnanese people are all thankful for his work.
His shrewd management has filled our coffers.
His capable hands have reinforced our regime.
The Chamber's Coffers
Beyond Miao Yuntai's impressive resume, the Yunnan Chamber of Commerce has played an integral role in the development of Yunnan's economic modernisation and reform movement. Before Miao Jiaming's time, it was Chen Jun who managed Yunnan's fragile economy. After studying politics in Japan, Chen took on the role of assistant manager of the Fudian Bank in 1912, a year after Li Jingxi's deposition in Kunming and the establishment of Republican rule. Cai E noticed his administrative talents and brought him on as director of internal affairs in Yunnan.\n\nFrom there, Tang Jiyao placed him in charge of developing the Gebishi Railway, which became China's first private railway. By the 20s, Chen had taken over Gejiu Mining Company and moved to Mengzi to oversee operations, and passed away in 1931. Yunnan's tin development was further aided by Wu Kun, who had come to head the Yunnan Mining Company under Tang's administration. The Yunnan Chamber of Commerce, having consolidated its control over the tin mining industry of Yunnan, has placed great emphasis on seeing Yunnan's economy transition away from its traditional opium poppy cultivation to exporting natural resources.\n\nTo that end, the Chamber has generally taken a supporting role in Tang's agenda, helping his attempt in separating civilian and military organisations. While students were typically leading protests against Japanese influence in China across the nation, in Yunnan it was the Chamber of Commerce who initiated such events, with students joining in after. Although uninterested in ruling themselves, the Chamber of Commerce continues to exert its influence on Yunnan's politics, urging the province to commit towards conservative gentry-led democratisation and anti-imperialism.
We praise their constitutional outlook.
Rare it is to meet such esteemed anti-imperialists.
[FROM.GetAdjective] Aid and Praise
Yunnan is among the poorest provinces in the Southwest, alongside the neighbouring province of Guizhou. Located furthest away from the capital and filled predominantly with mountains and hills, it is no surprise that Yunnan is largely neglected by central authorities when it comes to governance and economic development.\n\nWhile this is good for allowing Yunnan to remain political autonomous from the whims of Beijing, the underdevelopment on part of the Central Government has left Kunming to do most of the modernising work, especially so after the Xinhai Revolution and the Constitutional Protection War.\n\nRecently, however, [FROM.GetNameDef] has sought to do away with this economic injustice and offered to send financial aid to Yunnan, to further develop the local economy and bring the province up the par in comparison to the Eastern provinces. Reports of Bribery by [FROM.GetAdjective] Agents
Recruitment methods in China is different compared to other countries that have professional armies, owing the army's fractious nature, with many local warlords forming their own personal army. From street recruitment, forced conscription, and even recruitment through familial connections, local armies are often fueled by the lack of alternative employment and plagued with corruption.\n\nDesertion is a common occurrence among soldiers, whether it be out of self-preservation, disgruntlement due to personal issues, or bribery, making local provincial armies prone to disintegrate when cracks start to show. Thus, the high alert in Kunming as a reaction to reports of bribery within the army offered by [FROM.GetAdjective] agents is natural.\n\nPossibly part of a wider conspiracy to expand their influence within Yunnan and have Hu Ruoyu as their preferred candidate for Governor of Yunnan, this would spell disaster for General Long Yun should internal conflicts ever emerge between Kunming and Mengzi.\n\nTo counter this rot within the army, a comprehensive purge of the army has been suggested to remove elements that have been compromised. Although it might affect the fighting capabilities of the Yunnan Army in the short term, loyalty is the most important trait among others considering the chaos China has had to endure for the past decades.

[FROM.GetNameDefCap] Offers Peace Feelers
The national unification has been the goal of many ever since China fractured with the death of Yuan Shikai. Whether through negotiations or forceful means, all efforts failing to achieve such due to a multitude of reasons. Confucian Military Heroes
A nation long marred by eras of conflict followed by eras of peace, common Chinese thinking is that civil and military functions (wen and wu) were evidently meant to be complementary — unequal but not opposing. In the millennia of history that amounts in Chinese dynasties, peace and unity has been repeatedly interrupted by violent periods of disorder and division. In those latter periods, a premium would be placed upon military expertise. Therefore, for the young Tang Jiyao to have extolled the role of the military leader or hero was merely a modification, not a rejection of the prevailing traditional Confucian attitudes of society. Having written about such a mindset at the Shikan Gakko during his time as a student, Tang began:\n\n\

Success of the Yellow Book Affair
Emerging as an issue in Sino-British relations after the British Annexation of Upper Burma in 1886, the frontier between Yunnan Province and the territorial expanse of Burma has long been a point of contention for Yunnan authorities. British, and later Burmese concepts of the frontier and border lie within the information provided by officials of the old Burmese Kingdom, identifying states that had previously only paid nominal tribute to Burmese kings as rightful Burmese territory. In effect, the British authorities claimed all territories that had once belonged to the Burmese dynasties. Including but not limited to: the headwaters of the Irrawaddy River, the upper valleys of the Salween, Mekong and Yangtze rivers, however these regions were home to distinct yet sparsely populated indigenous communities of Shans, Kachins, Chins and Lashis. Pre-colonial Burmese influence did not likely even reach these areas.\n\nRepeated negotiations between the Qing and British failed to resolve the complete extent of the border, although by 1910 two distinct regions were unsettled. One in the north, and one in the south. Remaining unresolved due to the fundamentally different conceptions regarding the national boundary, with the dominance of the frontier by local subservient Tusi regimes, who were fluid and ambiguous in territorial extent. With the British seizure of Pianma in 1910, Cai E maintained a conciliatory policy towards the British, yet maintained in his heart's desire the goal of liberating Chinese land from British, and now Burmese, control.\n\nEven more pressing, was the location of a series of valuable silver mines within the southern region of dispute, Lufang, which were of particular note of the Yunnanese Provincial Government. Serving as a rallying point decrying foreign imperialism for two decades, the time to strike and retake lost territory had arrived at the zenith of Yunnan's military might. No longer kowtowing to any foreign powers, the Yunnanese launched a series of border raids and skirmishes, aimed at securing control of the small isolated forts manned by Burmese frontier guards.\n\n
$YUN.lategame.040.intro$Undoubtedly securing control of the local area in a matter of days, the Yunnan Provincial Authorities have managed to assert de facto control over the region. Parading it as the first step towards undoing the injustices of the Century of Humiliation.

New Strategies for the New Stage
With the national situation now somewhat stabilised, the reformers carrying on Zhu De's military plan have begun to propagate for the adoption of a more offensive strategy for the National Revolutionary Army, seeing as how the capacity for more aggressive action is available. Following through with their focus on encirclement and annihilation, and with their strength increasing in relation to that of their opponents, they have begun to extol the objective of enhancing their own territorial control through the destruction of large enemy forces as viable obstacles. With new offensives following the previous principles of offensive strategy, the roles of the opposition and of the NRA have actually been reversed. The burden of counter-encirclement now rested on the opposition, and with their lack of popular support and the other advantages with which the NRA possess, it is posited that their failure in such tactics is assured.\n\nWith a basic match-book in play, the reformers have espoused a basic pattern of success. The first phase of this strategic offensive is regarded as the phase of disconnection, wherein success relies upon the skilful coordination by NRA commanders of three types of operations: guerrilla disconnection, mobile disconnection, and positional disconnection.\nGuerilla disconnection involves disruption of opposition communications and coordination through harassment, ambush, or sabotage; the second entails manoeuvring of NRA regulars between separated opposition forces; the third, attacks at weak points in opposition defences.\n\nFollowing this phase would be the second phase of strategic encirclement, wherein the disconnection of enemy units is complete, albeit remaining strategic in nature. Afterwards, the third and final phase would come, that of annihilation. Employing a varied tactical arsenal to annihilate the encircled forces, be it short attacks, single and multi-point attacks, or shock attacks. With little to no opposition to the new successes of the new NRA, the reformer's plans have been put into action.
Heroes of the peasantry, advance!

Clash of the Old and New
Perhaps to be expected, numerous older National Revolutionary Army commanders continue to struggle in coming to terms with the new reformist doctrine espoused by Zhu De, Chen Yi and the young reformers in the army. Inspired by their initial successes, and intent on taking the army reforms to its natural conclusion, the reformers have begun to stipulate that in a war, mobile warfare should be primary, with varying stress on guerilla and positional action according to the stage of the conflict they are fighting.\n\nWith specific regard to the advance of any foreign imperialist power into Chinese territory, mainly concerning that of Japan, the reformers have stated that their intrusion would begin the first stage of mobile warfare operations, where the mobility of forces and flexibility of strategy is most important. Following this, the Japanese would have to consolidate their influence within the nation, ceasing to advance. Therefore in this phase it would be prudent to focus on guerilla harassment campaigns, and mobilising mass resistance in the enemy's rear. The third stage begins when the Japanese have been exhausted in their entirety, in which case a full offensive campaign would be launched forcing gradual Japanese retreat under heavy Chinese attack. At this point, mobile warfare is initially priority, with positional strategy slowly overtaking it to assume the chief position.\n\nOpposition to such theories has arisen quite strikingly from Liu Zhi, Wei Lihuang and Gu Zhutong. Hardcore proponents of traditional Whampoa strategy, they have instead fought back with their own concept of traditional NRA doctrine, in which they would rather pursue a policy of local encirclement of enemy forces through spearheads from the onset. With sheer force of will, and the stacking of decisive engagement after engagement, victory is assured with minimal suffering of the Chinese people. As opposed to what they view as waging a war of attrition.

Southern Reconstruction
Since the National Protection War, the Yunnanese military has achieved primacy in Yunnanese society and government. Cai E's militarisation reforms allocated 70% of the province's budget to the military, and provincial industrial programmes and agricultural reforms stagnated to a significant degree. Tang Jiyao's governorship oversaw limited attempts to demilitarise the economy and redirect funds towards economic modernisation, but militarisation remained the main drain on the provincial budget. Those in the government that propose rebalancing the economy along Guangdong lines argue that while militarisation served the government's needs while in Yunnan, continued militarisation will needlessly cause long term damage to the Chinese economy as Guangdong's relative wealth allows for both the military and economy to be financed properly. Those who push for continued militarist primacy argue that while militarist policies are an economic detriment, it is also a necessary evil to unify China by military means and end the Century of Humiliation. A controversial question, the choice lies with [ROOT.GetLeader].

Dian Military State
A teacher at the prestigious Kunming Military Academy, Cai E was a lifelong soldier. Many revolutionary heroes studied at the foot of Cai E, including Zhu De. Cai E found himself under house arrest in Beijing after Sun Yat-sen's second Revolution failed, and his colleague Tang Jiyao assumed the governorship in his stead. When Yuan made his imperial ambitions known, Cai escaped to Yunnan and coordinated with Tang to launch the National Protection War and establish the Huguojun.\n\nEven after Yuan's death, Cai E sustained the Yunnanese Army at great expense, which incentivised him to invade Sichuan to secure the local resources and lessen the economic strain of the army on Yunnan. Cai E would sadly succumb to disease, but his legacy remains with Yunnan to this day. The military-state that Cai E fostered remains in place, and stories of his bravery continue to inspire the students of the Kunming Military Academy.
We seek to emulate his example!

Fury of the Merchants
Like Yunnan, the gentry and merchants hold considerable power in Guangdong province. In order to pursue his political reforms whilst not offending the established regional power players, Chen Jiongming forged an alliance with Chen Lianbo and the Merchant Corps, which pushed the peasants into the arms of the Kuomintang. After the Guangzhou Nationalist Government collapsed from German intervention, Guangdong was split between various powers.\n\nThe Beijing-backed governor Shen Hongying controlled the northern regions of Guangdong, Chen Lianbo's Merchant Corps controlled the area surrounding Guangzhou including the city itself, and Deng Benyin ruled Qiongya. This state of affairs was brought to an end when the Yunnanese Army marched into Guangzhou bringing the four provinces of Southern China under a single administration.\n\n[ROOT.GetLeader] has deemed fit to reduce merchant privileges drastically and place the economy under the watchful eye of the provisional government. Unsurprisingly, the Merchant Corps protested severely in the face of reduced autonomy, and tensions were so high that the army had to be sent in to restore order. For the time being, a similar order to the one established by the Kuomintang in 1925 has taken place in Guangdong, and there is little the Merchant Corps can only hope that [ROOT.GetLeader] meets the same fate as Chiang Kai-shek.
We should tread carefully...

The Sister Province
Straddled between Yunnan and Guangdong, Guangxi shares many similar traits with its western neighbour as well as a rich history. Its mountainous terrain much like Yunnan's fostered many unique cultures. Its natural beauty, much like Yunnan's, came at the cost of the province's economic prosperity, with both Gui and Dian provinces finding themselves as China's poorest provinces.\n\nThis mountainous province filled with poverty proved to be a fertile breeding ground for political radicalism, which the Taiping Rebellion tapped into. Carrying over to the 20th century, Guangxi became one of China's leading republican provinces and joined in the Xinhai Revolution as earnestly as all other southern provinces. Lu Rongting's Old Guangxi Clique had similar ambitions to Cai E in Yunnan, and militarised the province to a similar level as Yunnan.\n\nThe New Guangxi Clique briefly controlled Guangxi after ousting Lu, and their ambitious reconstruction programme attempted to modernise and industrialise the province whilst also maintaining Lu's militarisation policy. Guangxi's leaders were ultimately unwilling and too conservative to endorse true reform, and it remains to be seen whether [ROOT.GetLeader] will reform Guangxi's existing economic situation or if the slate will be wiped clean and militarism be undone.
Guangxi will know prosperity!
Gui militarism still has its place.
The Unstable Balancing Act
While Yunnan was one of the poorest provinces of China, its governors were some of China's most ambitious men. When Yuan Shikai declared himself Emperor, Cai E and Tang Jiyao established Yunnan as the face of the republican movement, and it was the Yunnanese Huguojun which defeated the Beiyang Army in Sichuan and thwarted Yuan's imperial ambitions. Cai E's militarist aspirations led him to militarise the province further so that it could compete with Yunnan's surrounding provinces.\n\nAfter Cai E died, Tang Jiyao similarly held militarist aspirations for Chinese Unification, believing that a Federalist China must still maintain a strong military in order to defend the nation from foreign threats and imperialism. Aware of Yunnan's untenable economic situation, Tang pursued some demilitarisation efforts, but his militarist ambitions kept these reforms from taking full effect. This state of affairs stayed relatively unchanged until recently, when Governor [ROOT.GetLeader] took an unprecedented turn in favour of full economic rebalancing and demilitarisation of the administration.
No more half measures, Governor.
International Trade
Historically, China has long been the world's desire for exotic trading goods. In Roman times, the Han Dynasty produced silk and zealously guarded its production secrets. During the highs of international trade, the silk road would see times of prosperity, as Central Asian cities became popular destinations, acting as the middleman of international trade. China would retain this status until the 19th century, until the British opened up Chinese trade and broke the trade imbalance via opium.\n\nVarious regions of China were carved up among the Imperialist powers, and resources were extracted and traded away at their leisure. In many regions, Chinese businesses were locked out of international trade, as Western and Japanese companies ended up becoming the middleman that sent produce to the markets in the West and Japan for the consumers. This state of affairs led the Yunnanese Economic Commission to prioritise development of local companies and bring an end foreign economic dominance.\n\nWhile there is only so much that can be done at just the provincial level, Southern China's trade imbalance has indeed seen much less reliance on German business and mining operations, and much more of the wealth has gone into the hands of the Chinese state and local businesses. The increased state revenue from Yunnan combined with the Cantonese system's trade imbalance in China's favour has massively increased state revenue, and many in [ROOT.GetLeader]'s government have already laid out plans for its uses.
Support Chinese businesses, the time of plenty is upon us!
Resources must be marshalled for war.
Interregional Trade
While Chinese international trade can wax and wane like the tides of the ocean, it would not mean that trade itself stopped during times of Chinese isolationism. China's large size, geographical diversity, and wealth of natural resources led to provincial specialisation and the provincial barriers acted not too differently to the barriers between countries.\n\nWhile Northern China traditionally produced wheat, Southern China's different climate and geography made rice farming far more advantageous. When dividing Chinese regions even further, one of Yunnan's traditional exports was gemstones, which found a market further north in China, especially in the wealthier cities like Beijing. While the Imperialist powers sought to open China up for trade, interregional trade continued to take place as it had for millennia, and became the more popular method of trade as international trade was viewed as a den of exploitation.\n\nThe Yunnanese Economic Commission not only sought to balance trade with the Western powers, but also open up further economic integration between provinces. In the past Yunnanese tin was transported to Hanoi for transport to the west where the French were both middleman and consumer, Yunnanese resources now find themselves travelling east to Guangzhou, where the Cantonese System will ensure that wealth is retained by the Chinese people. While this is only on the provincial level, [ROOT.GetLeader] is confident that this is a model for the nation to move forward.
Chinese Solidarity!
Bah, the Cantonese and Germans both steal from us!
Bedrock of Chinese Society
Descendants of the Yellow and Yan Emperors, Chinese Society stretches back five thousand years. Chinese civilisation first emerged along the yellow river, and came to be primarily based in the Central Plains (Zhongyuan), a region stretching from the Yellow River in the north to the Yangtze to the south. Being situated along two major river systems, the ancient Chinese came to become a very agricultural people, a trait that has been carried down for millennia. Even in the modern age as industrial wonders emerge everyday from Europe and America, the Chinese people have by and large stayed agricultural, for better or for worse.\n\nIt is believed that even if China should modernise and industrialise along western lines, the middle kingdom will still root itself in agriculture, albeit while using more modern techniques and technologies. As such, Chinese agricultural and land reform has been the centre of Chinese modernisation. Chinese agriculture and society has always been unequal, and modern China is no different. Chinese peasants comprise most of the population, but a small class of landlords own most of the land.\n\nMore radical thinkers believe that an egalitarian and distributionist programme of land to the tiller ought to be adopted by the Chinese government in order to finally give the peasantry their just rewards. More moderate reformists believe that to truly modernise Chinese agriculture, the steady pace of reforms that started in the last years of the Qing Dynasty will bring genuine social mobility and modernisation without causing the chaos of forcefully rooting out landlordism and the risks of all-out confrontation with the Gentry. The choice lies with [ROOT.GetLeader].
Land to the Tiller is the goal of the National Revolution.
Reform by the Yunnanese method is our eternal aspiration.
Land to the Tiller
With roots in the Taiping Rebellion and founded as a Revolutionary Republican party, the Kuomintang has long presented itself as the reckoning of the Chinese peasantry against the tyranny of Beijing and decadence of the Qing court. When it became apparent that the legacy of Qing corruption was inherited by Yuan Shikai and his posse the Beiyang Clique, the Kuomintang became dedicated towards the overthrow of the first Republic and establishment of a truly modern republic led by Sun Yat-sen and his revolutionary comrades.\n\nThe north-south conflict culminated in the Northern Expedition, which saw the Kuomintang dream be crushed. Against all odds, the Kuomintang remnants in Guizhou brought order to Yunnan province and established a foothold for the National Revolution on the Chinese mainland. While the National Revolution has yet to succeed, Generalissimo [ROOT.GetLeader] stands ready to begin the liberation of the Chinese people on a provincial level as an experiment for policies to be adopted on the national stage.\n\nWith agriculture's centrality in Chinese society, it is no surprise that agricultural reform came up in the Generalissimo's agenda. To Chen Lifu of the CC Clique, the Kuomintang must form several key ministries including but not limited to the economic commission, ministry of interior affairs, and ministry of finance in order to accurately assess and develop recommendations in different provinces. While finding support from Dai Jitao, this bureaucratic approach is hotly contested by the Blue Shirts.\n\nTo Blue Shirt visionaries like Teng Jie and Zhou Fang, the reason why China's rural economy failed was due to burdensome land policies and special taxes, episodic reassessments, unpredictable surtaxes, and the undermining of the incentive and enterprises of the peasants. Chen Cheng, a rising star in the Whampoa Clique and leader of the minor Civil Engineering faction, proposes a combination of the two proposals, with a focus on the rural masses, combat corrupt wealth accumulation, and make use of public distribution of goods to combat the inflation plaguing China. The party in deadlock, only the Generalissimo can decisively declare the Kuomintang's land reform policy.
Peasant liberation comes with revolutionary bureaucracy!
Primacy to the rural masses!
Triangle Trade
In the aftermath of the Ming restoration of a Han-led Chinese Dynastic state, the government turned increasingly inwards, and isolationism once became the policy of the nation. When the Qing conquered the Ming Dynasty, isolationism was maintained, and foreign trade was limited to the port of Guangzhou, which operated under the Cantonese system which created a trade imbalance in China's favour. Desiring to break the imbalance, the British resolved to smuggle opium to compensate for the imports of Chinese tea to their Isles. Opium took the Chinese by storm to the extent that silver became a scarcity in the middle kingdom and addictions became commonplace.\n\nThe Chinese Emperor attempted to combat Opium smuggling, which resulted in the Opium Wars and humbled the long-proud Chinese. Opium smuggling and domestic production would intensify, and the Yunnanese economy would come to be dominated by opium exports. A triangle trade emerged as a result, with Yunnanese opium being sold in Wuhan, which in turn was used to buy cotton and yarn from the Western powers in Guangzhou and Tonkin. Cai E and Tang Jiyao's governorships saw attempts to curb opium, but lack of cash from opium production hurt the provincial administration.\n\nThe Kunming administration wasn't inclined to involve itself in rural reconstruction, but the ministry of finance attempted to sell grain stocks in the granaries of Kunming's surrounding towns, as by that point Yunnanese banditry was at a low, especially in comparison to Guangxi and Sichuan. With the KMT now in charge, poppy suppression has once again been enforced, but the issues that plagued Tang Jiyao and Cai E remain, and provincial revenues in Yunnan have plummeted since the enacting of poppy suppression. Many wonder whether it had been better to simply leave the Opium suppression for a future time and only implement opium taxation, but high ranking officials in the administration are adamant that opium has no future in a Revolutionary China, and the Yunnanese model will lead the way.
Opium is a disease that must be eradicated.
Maintaining the Course
In the last years of the Qing Dynasty following the Boxer Rebellion, the government undertook an unprecedented turn towards political and economic modernisation. In Yunnan, Viceroy Li Jingxi undertook an ambitious task to centralise the province, modernise the economy, and undo foreign dominance. Li found himself curbed by the resistance of the local gentry, and his reforms were ultimately cut short by the Xinhai Revolution. Li was succeeded by Cai E and Tang Jiyao, soldiers in the Yunnanese New Army that became sympathetic to the revolution.\n\nCai E and Tang Jiyao both ruled Yunnan with a reformist outlook, but militarism, poverty, opium, the gentry, and the warlord era prevented major reforms from being fully pushed through. Despite this, the attitude of reform always remained high in Kunming. With immediate military matters settled, the Kunming Administration has once again redirected its attention to the task of agricultural reform and modernisation. There is an undercurrent of belief that finally, what began with Li Jingxi will finally reach their natural conclusion with [ROOT.GetLeader].
The journey must be concluded.
Opium Question
In the aftermath of the Opium wars, opium smuggling and poppy production would intensify, and the Yunnanese economy would come to be dominated by opium. The triangle trade emerged, with Yunnanese opium being sold in Wuhan, which in turn was used to buy cotton and yarn from the Western powers in Guangzhou and Tonkin. Viceroy Li Jingxi attempted to centralise opium taxation, and Cai E and Tang Jiyao attempted to suppress the opium. Tang utilised public campaigns that included lectures, pamphlets, public demonstrations, and anti-opium songs. He also tried to levy a fine of three yuan for every mu of land planted with poppies.\n\nTang had hoped to free more arable land for food production, though government ability to enforce it outside of Kunming was so limited that the government officially decided the penalty would not be enforced south of Kunming to avoid unrest that could disrupt the Kunming - Haiphong railway. In practice, anti-opium penalties were ineffective at reducing cultivation or consumption and hurt rural regions which in turn increased banditry. Although a staple of Yunnanese economic reform, many are calling for poppy suppression measures to be dropped altogether due to poor results, and instead rely on opium revenue to modernise the economy in other areas.
Suppression will lead to long term prosperity.
The war on opium has not developed necessarily to our favour...
On Minsheng
One of the Kuomintang's leading political theorists, Dai Jitao's musings on China's economic situation is unrivalled in ambition and revolutionary fervour. Once a leftist firebrand, Dai Jitao shifted to the new right upon the realisation of the sycophantic nature of Parisian-worshipping parasites had on Chinese society and concocted a new understanding of Sun Yat-sen's principles in a pure, unfettered Chinese understanding. Unlike the leftists who would betray the Chinese revolution for the international socialist cause, Dai's vision for the Kuomintang owes loyalty only to the Chinese nation.\n\nUnlike the liberals who would betray the masses of China for useless good-for-nothing platitudes, Dai understands that liberation of the Chinese classes must be the primary undertaking. However, China's classes are not tools to be waged against one another; no, China's classes are a part of a greater social body. To achieve this great harmonic convergence, Dai reasons that revolutionary activists must be disciplined to push for the singular vision of the Kuomintang which encompasses all classes and has no higher calling than that of national revival.\n\nThe hedonistic and anarchistic chaos socialists promote have no place in the Chinese revolution, and the masses must not be led astray by such callings. No, the masses are a potent and yet unpredictable force which requires a disciplined revolutionary guard to shepard them to glory. That shepard is the Kuomintang, and the party will bring a new dawn to China.\n\nWestern concepts such as wage labour and surplus value are not relevant to the Chinese nation whatsoever, as labour is transhistorical in the middle kingdom. Thus, when the National Revolution unifies the classes as a unified machine of productivity that will bring soaring highs of development, cooperation, and prosperity, the Chinese people will have achieved that common livelihood via their own labour, the aspiration of all nations.
For our common livelihood!
Nationalist Decade
Under the Nationalist flag, much of China has been unified. No longer are peasants forced into a life of continual violence as war erupts across provinces every month. With each passing day, China's industries adapt to the modern age and inch that much closer towards parity with the Western powers. The China of today is leaps and bounds ahead of the China left behind by Yuan Shikai. Dr. Sun's dream of a strong and fair Chinese Republic is on the cusp of success. The free provinces of our republic enjoy the boons of economic integration, modernising transportation, and Chinese businesses flourish under the government's just watch. In the east, the western powers no longer enjoy all the privileges they had just a decade ago. The productive masses of China find themselves reinvigorated by the disciplined Chinese Revolution. Where labour leaders once fell to the spectre of socialism, they now flow against mob mentality and guide workers towards the enterprise of development. Of course, much work still remains to be done. Full comprehensive land reform remains elusive, as military unification takes priority and warlords still rule much of the hinterland. Regardless, the National Revolution has only just begun, and the day is coming that the White Sun will fly from Harbin to Dali, from Taipei to Dihua!
Zhonghua Minguo Wansui!

The last sounds of sporadic rifle and cannon fire were finally vanquished as the flag of the Blue Sky, White Sun, and Wholly Red Earth was raised over the Forbidden Palace and the National Assembly. Vestiges of the Beiyang Government, such as the Five-Coloured Flag and the official seal of the nation, were torn down, and soldiers from every city block were seen handing out propaganda leaflets to commemorate the completion of the late premier's vision.\n\nWas the fraternal bloodletting necessary? Our party has always advocated the use of peaceful means to build a unified government. On the one hand, the government of the Republic of China should be built by the Chinese people themselves; on the other hand, the people's livelihood is in a state of decline and cannot bear the disaster of internal strife again. Therefore, when the late Dr. Sun went to the north, he immediately urged the whole country to convene a National Conference to resolve the current situation. Who knew that the traitor Duan would openly agree to the National Conference but secretly refuse, and the imperialists incited the warlords to become more violent.\n\nOur party pleads for the people and eliminates traitors for the sake of national salvation. Soon the success of the national revolution will be closer.

Hu Hanmin Denounces the [GetYear] Constitution
Hu Hanmin, a stubbornly orthodox Sunnist, has been the most vocal opponent to the Provisional Constitution. According to Dr. Sun's three stage theory of national development, no constitution can be drafted until the end of the Political Tutelage when a national convention drawn from all parties and factions in China can participate in its drafting in preparation for the Constitutional Phase. A provisional constitution then, is not a compromise but a perversion of Dr. Sun's teachings.\n\nAs the specific contents of the Provisional Constitution has been unveiled, further critiques have emerged. For the most part, the Provisional Constitution simply reaffirms what already exists - the party-state and political tutelage will be officially codified into law, Dr. Sun's Three Principles of the People upheld, the Five Branches of the government will be established, and the government will respect the separation of power between the central government and regional governments except for in economic matters where the National Reconstruction Commission will directly implement Dr. Sun's agenda. However, the rushed nature of this constitution and its apparent lack of protection for human rights (outside of religious freedoms, personal freedoms are said to be determined by the law which can be changed at will), has attracted criticism from many moderates in the party and liberal outsiders.\n\nFor Hu Hanmin, this has been the moment he has been waiting for. A firm democratic centralist, he is unwilling to endorse any course of action that directly divides the party.
Hu Hanmin has begun an open campaign to stop the passage of the Provisional Constitution and demand He Yingqin's resignation. He has already quickly amassed a large following, including many leading politicians, warlords, and intellectuals who are said to have formed a secret Great Alliance for the Protection of Party Authority to seek He Yingqin's ouster.

He Yingqin Plans an Auto-Coup!

Seats are allocated not only to provinces and cities, but also professional groups, trade unions, farmers associations, chambers of commerce, educational associations, overseas Chinese communities, and officials from the party itself. Given the circumstances, such an arrangement is no easy task - in areas not currently occupied by Kuomintang authorities delegates are often de facto appointed by the party to represent them.\n\nWith opposition growing and Hu Hanmin leading the charge, there is a sincere concern that the Provisional Constitution will fail to achieve the 2/3rds majority required to pass it. Hu Hanmin, as Premier of the Legislative Yuan, has substantial sway over proceedings, and even if the CC Clique and Political Science Clique use every dirty trick in the book and the full weight of the party apparatus, there is no guarantee the vote will go their way. Dai Jitao, having received input from the Chen brothers and their party whips, has strongly advised against going forward with this proposal.\n\nIt is however too late, at least from Generalissimo He's perspective. Threatened, and perhaps insulted, by the growing rebelliousness of southern leaders, He Yingqin has made up his mind. Zhang Qun's Audacious Gambit
With both He Yingqin and Dai Jitao (at least temporarily) out of the picture, Minister of War Zhang Qun's appointment to the Presidency was quickly confirmed by the panicked members of the Central Executive Committee. The President-elect has turned out to be far more free-thinking than most gave him credit for however, and his first move in office has been to nominate Yang Yongtai as Premier of the Executive Yuan instead of Chen Guofu.\n\nThe stunned Central Executive Committee, which has spent the past few years effectively rubber stamping the will of the President, has awkwardly approved the appointment. The Zhang-Yang government has moved quickly to appoint sympathetic ministers from the Political Science Clique's braintrust to the cabinet, trying to sideline their rivals in the CC Clique. Similarly, Zhang Qun has assembled a small clique of aligned officers in the National Revolutionary Army, including Generals Li Genyuan, Lai Shihuang, and Xiong Shihui with orders to keep the army under control.\n\nZhang, in his hurried array of public addresses and speeches before the National Assembly, has attempted to consolidate his position. He has stressed the need for unity and negotiation with the South China Political Council and also reiterated the Political Science Clique's desire to pass the provisional constitution. How he intends to reconcile the two objectives he has not said, and given how much of a sticking point the matter has been it is unlikely to be easy. Perhaps he is gambling that the fear of He Yingqin's return (and his revenge) will motivate party moderates to action, but if that is the case he is making a very dangerous wager.
