Dai Jitao and the CC Clique Plot
Dai Jitao has been a faithful disciple of the National Revolution for decades now, and quite prescient as well. He correctly predicted that Chiang Kai-shek would lose his life fighting Wang Jingwei, and he correctly predicted that He Yingqin's ham-fisted attempt at resolving his dispute with Hu Hanmin would backfire. Now he believes that Zhang Qun, the latest military man to take the reins in China and probably the least principled, will fall.\n\nDai is determined to be the one who emerges from the ashes. Historically more of a background figure, the chronic mismanagement of the party and the intensity of the crisis has pushed Dai into a more active role. His well-timed resignation has allowed him to escape the fallout of Hu Hanmin's arrest less scratched than General He, and his long tenure in the party has given him more legitimacy than Zhang Qun. According to him, he is the only one who can truly bridge the divide between the rambunctious military and the unruly civilian party while still adhering to the principles set forth by Dr. Sun Yat-sen.\n\nHe is hardly alone in that assessment. Major party figures have historically eschewed if not outright denounced the formation of illegal internal factions, and true to form so has Mr. Dai. But it is indisputable that most of the followers coalescing around him are known as the CC Clique, spearheaded by the recently spurned Chen Guofu and his younger brother Chen Lifu.\n\nThe Chen brothers (who may give the CC Clique their name), have worked to engineer a plot in support of Dai Jitao, utilising their sway over many legislators and party functionaries to formally reject the Provisional Constitution at the National Assembly. This would deal a lethal blow to Zhang Qun and Yang Yongtai's claims of being effective stewards of the party, and the ensuing momentum would make a vote of no confidence inevitable. Dai would likely be sworn in as President in the aftermath and be empowered to find a resolution to the disputes with the army and followers of Hu Hanmin.

Our Efforts Foiled!
While authoritarianism in the Chinese political climate is well known, the Kuomintang party-state on principle operates on Democratic Centralist lines. And moreover, part of our arrangement with the central government implicitly grants us the right to express our opinions on national affairs. Despite this social contract, our efforts to participate in proceedings during the Provisional Constitution Crisis have been harshly suppressed by a barrage of intimidation, censorship, and sometimes outright violence. This is no way for a proper government to behave, and there is a growing sense of rebelliousness within our ranks.

Communications with the SCPC
The South China Political Council is not a monolithic entity, especially with Hu Hanmin still imprisoned. Nominally headed by Gu Yingfen, ironically his own nepotistic and autocratic style of leadership has spawned rebels within the rebel camp. Unified in their denunciation of He Yingqin, their demand for Hu Hanmin's release, and their general affiliation with Guangdong, the ensuing developments in [YUN.Capital.GetCapitalVictoryPointName] and their own slowing momentum has prevented them from playing a more proactive role in the crisis.\n\nThere is a growing consensus that a negotiated reunification between the central government and the SCPC is needed, predicated on Hu Hanmin's release. Sun Fo and his progressive liberal Reconstruction Faction have indicated their preference for opening talks with Zhang Qun and the technocratic Political Science Clique. Through trusted intermediaries like Wu Tiecheng, he has changed his mind on the Provisional Constitution Question, believing that a constitution (albeit one with significant changes from the one proposed) would be the best way to constrain military rule.\n\nHu Hanmin's vocal opposition to the Provisional Constitution has diminished support for the constitution however, and more conservative figures like Zhang Ji have suggested collaborating with Dai Jitao and the CC Clique to ensure an outcome closer to Hu Hanmin's vision for a party-state run by the party's civilian intelligentsia.\n\nAdding to the uncertainty is that many of the warlords backing the South China Political Council have begun to ignore the directives of the SCPC, weakening their hand. However, reconciliation between the two Kuomintang governments is a pressing concern for many National Assembly delegates, giving the council leverage if they act decisively. The bulk of the SCPC's membership is dominated by the so-called Western Hills Clique led by Lin Sen and Zou Lu, who will likely determine the final result.
The SCPC press for constitutional revisions and protections.
The SCPC demands the constitution be withdrawn altogether.
Negotiations ultimately fall through as the SCPC bicker.
Negotiations with the Left
The Kuomintang's left, never quite a unified or cohesive body, completely fractured after their destruction in battle. Some of them were successful in worming their way into the new government, usually by leveraging their personal connections and prestige, though they remain a largely fringe and distrusted body within the right-wing government. The fact that there are any backroom channels open is a testament to the weakness of the current government.\n\nWang Jingwei and his RCA has expressed interest in working with the Political Science Clique, even if he has been careful not to be around in person during the crisis. He has utilised his time in quasi-exile to demand a constitution be passed, and the recent drive for a Provisional Constitution has validated his views. No doubt he expects his allies to receive generous promotions should they win.\n\nSong Qingling and her PAC have been scheming to topple the right-wing government altogether, attempting to enlist Chen Cheng and his Civil Engineering Clique to aid them. This dialogue has gone both ways however, and Chen has tried to tap into his own connections within the WMARCA to potentially build a base for his own efforts to reorganise the government.\n\nOf course there are many smaller factions, searching for direction. The Four Elders and their World Society have anarchist roots but connections with the party old guard. They have tentatively offered to align with Dai Jitao, a fellow Zhejianger, though his history of denouncing socialism of all sorts (as opposed to just Marxism) has made a reconciliation difficult. For many independent socialists, former members of the LCS, and various progressive groups, there simply are no good options and they either seek opportunity within the government or to flee abroad.
The Kuomintang's left, still a major force in Chinese politics, have sought to exploit the divisions within our ranks by dangling the possibility of further partnership. While most realise that any lasting unity will simply never exist, with our movement as divided as ever there is a temptation among the lower ranks to seek a grand compromise.\n\nWang Jingwei and his RCA has expressed interest in working with the Political Science Clique, even if he has been careful not to be around in person during the crisis. He has demanded a constitution be passed in the past as a cudgel against the rightist government, and the recent drive for a Provisional Constitution has validated his views.\n\nSong Qingling and her PAC have been scheming to topple the right-wing government altogether, attempting to enlist Chen Cheng and his Civil Engineering Clique to aid them. This dialogue has gone both ways however, and Chen has tried to tap into his own connections within the WMARCA to potentially build a base for his own efforts to reorganise the government.\n\nOf course there are many smaller factions, searching for direction. The Four Elders and their World Society have anarchist roots but connections with the party old guard. They have tentatively offered to align with Dai Jitao, a fellow Zhejianger, though his history of denouncing socialism of all sorts (as opposed to just Marxism) has made a reconciliation difficult. For many independent socialists, former members of the LCS, and various progressive groups, there simply are no good options and they seek our final destruction.
Most of the left tentatively embraces the constitution.
The left remains militant and a new reorganisation.
The bickering groups on the left find no real direction.
The PSC Constitution Passes
After weeks of uncertainty, the National Assembly finally put up a revised version of the Provisional Constitution up for a vote. Though plenty of changes and compromises were made, many still agonised over whether or not it would be enough to ensure its passage. However, as delegates began to vote one by one, it became clear that Zhang Qun and the Political Science Clique had amassed enough support (enthusiastic or not) to claim triumph.\n\nWith their victory secured, slowly but surely the party has fallen back in line like it always has. The CC Clique and their functionaries have been sidelined, many of those who resigned in protest of the government have not been hired back, the others have by now cut ties with the CC Clique and changed sides. Chen Lifu remains Minister of Education, but Dai Jitao and Chen Guofu are now effectively powerless as ministers without a portfolio.\n\nThe South China Political Council, after a contentious vote of their own, elected to rejoin the central government after securing a tentative arrangement with the Political Science Clique. This has eliminated another threat to the regime, while also helping to counterbalance the threat from the military.\n\nWhampoa's influence over the National Revolutionary Army, though still significant, has similarly been humbled. In the aftermath, many leading officers have been reassigned from their posts. General He Yingqin's gambit has failed, with most officers abandoning the conspiracy to restore him. Political Science Clique aligned officers have quietly shuffled out the more outspoken threats, claiming choice leadership positions for themselves.\n\nThe next few months are expected to be an uneasy one as Zhang and Yang consolidate their hold over the Nationalist government. The worst of it, however, is over. As the delegates of the National Assembly go home, spreading the latest gossip of the goings of the National Government, few have any doubt that President Zhang is here to stay.
The party and military must be thoroughly purged.
Our internal rivals will find little peace.
We will expedite a return to stability.
But what will happen to the Revolutionary Spirit?
The CC Clique Collapses the Government!
After weeks of uncertainty and no successful compromises in sight, the National Assembly finally put the provisional constitution up to vote. By a resounding majority, the resolution failed, permanently sundering the Political Science Clique's chances of a much needed political victory. With a vote of no confidence inevitable, Zhang Qun and Yang Yongtai resigned rather than fight any further and risk annihilation.\n\nParty elders helped to broker an arrangement on the way out, ensuring the transition was smooth. By an easy majority, Dai Jitao was named the new President. As a reward for their faithful service, Chen Guofu of the CC Clique was nominated as the new Premier, demonstrating the enduring power of the Zhejiang faction. Zhang Qun and Yang Yongtai have controversially been able to retain their old portfolios in defence procurement and banking, though the various economic and production organs that previously fell in their domains have been reallocated to CC Clique stalwarts. Many of the business elite, like Kong Xiangxi, have recognised the changing winds and followed suit.\n\nThe South China Political Council, though not entirely satisfied with the outcome, have decided after a contentious vote and considerable work by intermediaries that this is the best they are going to get. With the Provisional Constitution having failed, they have mostly elected to recognise Dai as President and rejoin the national government, ending the crisis.\n\nDai, who holds a unique sway for a civilian over the military, has been able to significantly undercut He Yingqin's hold over Whampoa. A familiar face within the National Revolutionary Army's ranks and the author behind many even more famous writings, most officers have fallen in line with the new regime.\n\nDai has emerged as the new strongman of the party, able to wield both civilian and military power. Whether he has what it takes to retain that unquestioned power, not seen since Dr. Sun himself, is another question.
It is time we thoroughly clean out traitors.
Our worst rivals will be removed from office.
Dai Jitao's all encompassing power will keep them in line.
But can he really live up to Dr. Sun's legacy?
The Whampoa Coup d'Etat
After weeks of uncertainty, ceaseless debate, and hand-wringing over the Provisional Constitution, support for the collapsing Political Science government has bottomed out. Fear of civil war and foreign invasion has been on the rise, and there has been a growing nostalgia for He Yingqin's stable, if sometimes brutal and uncompromising, leadership.\n\nThe Zhang-Yang government had held out hope that splinters of the Whampoa faction, like the progressive Civil Engineering Clique, would remain loyal long enough for matters to settle down. However, when Chen Cheng's name was among the top three in a signed letter from the various chiefs of the National Revolutionary Army, time had clearly run out.\n\nIn a carefully arranged exit, Zhang Qun and Yang Yongtai resigned. The Central Executive Committee invited He Yingqin to return to form a new cabinet, and Chen Cheng was tapped for the Premiership as a reward for his loyalty. While Zhang Qun and Yang Yongtai nominally retain their old portfolios in the war ministry and banking, and Dai Jitao and the Chen brothers in education and party affairs, in practice Whampoa cadres have been appointed all across the government.\n\nThe provisional constitution was passed silently. The South China Political Council proved to be a paper tiger in practice - after a renewed, violent campaign the unwieldy alliance of warlords and party opposition collapsed under the weight of the New Right.\n\nTo an outsider, He Yingqin has made his point: he is indispensable to the good functioning of the government. However, within Whampoa's opaque ranks, there has been a significant shift. The need for a Generalissimo, and the National Revolutionary Army to guard the revolution, is now indisputable, but He Yingqin's suitability for that role has been tarnished by his association with months of political chaos. The race has begun to find someone younger and less cumbersome that can perhaps replace him one day.
Anyone who is not with us, is against us!
The internal opposition must be brought to heel!
Peace and order shall prevail.
Whampoa has its revenge.

It seems that most people have made peace with Generalissimo He Yingqin's leadership and the Provisional Constitution. The greatest beneficiary of course has been He Yingqin and the Political Science Clique, but the CC Clique is not too far behind.\n\nA more careful look at the situation reveals that the intricate dynamics that dominated the party since their return to Guangzhou have effectively solidified. An outside observer describes it:\n\n\

The End of the Moscow Committee
Almost a year after being imprisoned, Hu Hanmin and most other political prisoners have been quietly released into house arrest. Hu has retreated to a humble abode in Guangzhou, his health wracked by the poor living conditions while in prison. Stubborn as ever, he spends his days doing calligraphy and poetry while seeing few visitors. Efforts to get him to return to nominal office have been snubbed, the cantankerous old man continues to write damning statements against the government which are quickly censored.\n\nThe Moscow Committee factions have largely gone their own ways. The Elders clique have mostly dissolved without Hu Hanmin holding them together, their senior leadership coasting on their own prestige. The Western Hills Clique have similarly entrenched themselves in the Judicial Yuan, no longer willing to challenge He Yingqin outside of token party elections. Sun Fo and the Reconstruction Faction have flipped on the Constitutional issue, Sun has grown obsessed over drafting the \

The Vanguards of the Revolution
The Hu Hanmin Crisis has made the government ever more conscious of their image, fearing that Hu's barrage of attacks on their revolutionary integrity has gained traction among the population. While the regime is known to be more conservative (and sometimes reactionary), plenty of factions have been working overtime to prove them wrong and to show that the Kuomintang is indeed a revolutionary movement for the Chinese.\n\nPropaganda leaflets throughout the country's urban centers, reaching even Shanghai, are adorned with pamphlets proclaiming the revolutionary glory of the Blueshirts Society or those glorifying the works of Chen Lifu and Guofu. These works frequently invoke images of a sincere and dedicated solidarity between the soldiers, workers, and peasants - in a vein similar to that done by the Kuomintang's left wing.\n\nThe irony is that the Blueshirts have sought to popularise their ideas through mass media - given their own hatred of the liberal press. Instead of advocating for class struggle, however, the political tracts call for interclass harmony - an exploited national community and a seeming struggle for national survival in a Darwinist world full of hungry imperialist powers.\n\nChen Cheng and the Civil Engineering Clique have also turned to using mass media to project their ideas, although their tones are seemingly more socialist in nature. Pushing their ideas that the CEC stands for the workers and peasants of the country seeking true land equality, their work has been denounced by the Blueshirts and the CC Clique as nothing more than closet Marxism.\n\nThe Political Science Clique has chosen to emphasise its dedication towards modernising the Chinese economy, proclaiming itself the most well-suited towards planning for a new statist economy, termed the Planned Free Economic Development. All this talk of socialism and national survival, they argue, detracts from the true solution to Chinese weakness, rapid industrialisation and aggressive planning.
Under a true revolutionary vanguard, may we achieve the national revolution!
Follow the Blueshirt Principle, lest our people go extinct!
We seek a true realisation of Minsheng for all.
The best revolution is one that stays true to its course.

Whampoa Divided
Despite, or perhaps in spite, of our soaring heights, the intra-personal cliques and factions of Whampoa could never be more at eachother's throats. Scurrying and clawing for influence, Chen Cheng and his Civil Engineering Young Guard have ran a campaign of undermining Generalissimo He Yingqin's authority within military circles. It has reached the point wherein genuine blood has been spilt. One notable case is that of infamous He subordinate Cheng Zerun.\n\nVying for influence within Whampoa, Cheng Zerun was ordered by Generalissimo He to execute one of Chen Cheng's direct subordinates, ostensibly for corruption. Permanently placing a wedge between Chen and He, the Little Generalissmo managed to institute an elaborate scheme resulting in Chen Zerun's arrest and imprisonment for embezzlement, tossed into a military prison.\n\nWith the feud ever growing, General Guan Linzheng, a notable opponent of Chen Cheng, has thrown his hat into the ring. A blunt, stubborn donkey of a man, Guan's feud with Chen starts back in 1928, where he insulted Chen's field command ability directly towards Luo Zhuoying. Not one to miss an opportunity to berate Chen, reportedly the most recent incident became brutal, wherein their conflict over the choice of subordinates by He resulted in Guan accusing Chen of retaining a superiority complex, insinuating that he desires Civil Engineering's dominance over the army. With the confrontation fierce, many believed the men would come to blows. However with Chen Cheng's stomach ulcer resurfacing due to tension, the Little Generalissimo was brought to his knees, vomiting immense amounts of blood.\n\nDismissing the bleeding Chen, Guan would have his subordinate choice of Zhang Yaoming denied anyway, with Fang Tian of Civil Engineering placed in command. As reports of the incident surface in both civilian and military circles, there has been an outpouring of sympathy for Chen Cheng, viewing him as a man pushing his body physically to the limits for the nation. Others however, decry both He and Chen, with notable opposition arriving from Teng Jie. Accusing the duo of nepotism, corruption and allowing rot to fester within the party.
Chen Cheng is a national hero!
Generalissimo He is merely being impartial.

The Supreme Leader...and his Men
On paper, He Yingqin commands the loyalty of the NRA - battletested soldiers from the Wars of Unification, the Second Northern Expedition. Yet there is a palpable irony in the political situation as the Generalissmo's standing within Whampoa has utterly collapsed following the Hu Hanmin Crisis. While he maintains his standing in the Presidency, the dynamics of Whampoa remain drastically changed.\n\nRevolutionary radicalism has long permeated through the halls of the Academy and now the NRA's cliques are divided between Chen Cheng and Teng Jie with the Old Guard unable to stop their growing popularity. To appease the radical military, Chen Cheng has been appointed Premier of the Executive Yuan while Teng Jie is now the Chief of the General Staff.\n\nThat has not stopped them from ratcheting up their rhetoric about the need for a supreme leader. In a highly publicised speech, Blueshirt spokesperson Liu Jianqun has claimed that for centuries China's golden years were when she was led by a true autocrat, the fortunes of the nation have both risen and fallen due to the quality of its leadership. Why change this now, when a supreme Party and an infallible national revolutionary party is finally capable of bringing eternal glory to the Chinese nation?\n\nThe Blueshirts, not even Liu, never unambiguously referred to General He as the supreme leader of the party however. They have, however, been moving quickly to secure control of the army and its vast patronage networks. Chen Cheng, with his favourable marriage and natural ability to make friends, has staked his claim over the political realm. One can only wonder with the true Supreme Leader will be.

A Third Way for the Kuomintang
Liu Jianqun is not the only one who wants to reorganise the Kuomintang government. Amid the malaise, a growing demand for revivalism have pushed many Whampoa clique members to seek a return to the leftist revolutionary spirit of 1924, something fanned by Chen Cheng's rhetoric.\n\nThe head of the civilian government, Chen has proposed a second reorganisation, setting age limits and education requirements for all members of the government. He also has many other far reaching agenda items of both military and domestic matters:\n\nUnconventional troops are to accompany the main army in suppressing socialist traitors and reactionaries alike.\n\nThe army is to be totally reformed into a rigid, disclipined, an ideological force. No longer can the forces of the NRA be branded as merely warlord forces under the Nationalist flag.\n\nThe Sanmin Zhuyi Youth Corps are to be greatly strengthened with a secret police within it to purge anti-revolutionary activity. In reality, this is merely a ploy to keep He Yingqin and the CC Clique from encroaching on Chen's growing power.\n\nTo restore trust in the Kuomintang by working to mend the rift between the Moscow Committee and the Generalissmo. The ultimate goal for Chen is to bridge the party's differences with his own honesty and incorruptibility to restore faith in the Kuomintang system.

The Destruction of the Civilian Right
The civilian right has not been having a good time ever since the Hu Hanmin Crisis. While Hu Hanmin has been quietly released into house arrest, most of his followers are either keeping their heads down or have fled the country. Zhang Qun and Yang Yongtai have been sidelined, with the Political Science Clique all but dismantled. Dai Jitao has opted to accept a position as Premier of the Examination Yuan, effectively removing him from direct governance.\n\nIn the final accounting of the Hu Hanmin Crisis, presented before the Central Executive Committee, the crisis has been blamed on 1.) a lack of mass support 2.) the inability and refusal of the government to change 3.) bureaucratic mismanagement 4.) excessive factionalism 5.) disloyalty of regional governments. The need to reform after the crisis was clear as day and the ensuing purges have given a ready environment to change the party. Otherwise, the report warned, the long-term survival of the Kuomintang is in peril.\n\nWhile the CC Clique hoped to serve as a counter-balance between the CEC and BSS, their influence has been declining tremendously. Chen Cheng has utilised his position to push out CC Clique figures in short, decisive bursts rather than a gradual evolution. Still, with He Yingqin still nominally in power, this has only been a partial change. The latest target of his reorganisation of the government has been the position of Secretary-General of the Central Executive Committee, which manages the affairs of the secretariat itself. Chen Lifu, who is slated to be demoted to merely Minister of Education and whose brother has been effectively exiled to the Legislative Yuan, is no longer able to contest the title.\n\nNow with the CC sidelined, the CEC have pushed for Zhu Huaibing, a leftist military officer turned civilian administrator who is highly commended for his dutifuness. The BSS have pushed for Liu Jianqun, a pick whom they know would be approved by He Yingqin, and who is known for being a prolific writer yet infatuated with Narodism. Trying to preserve what is left of their legitimacy, CC and CEC members have also pushed for Zhang Lisheng, who is a CC Clique member but ultimately has close ties and interests with Chen Cheng's proposed rural land reform plans.

Three Phases of Development
The late eternal premier Dr. Sun Yat-sen attracted controversy with his three stage theory of revolution when it came to the Chinese Revolution. Accepted by the Kuomintang as true orthodox doctrine for practising revolution, he proposed a military stage of revolution (in which the warlords would be subjugated), the stage of political instruction or tutelage to prepare the nation for democracy, and the third which would be an era of constitutional rule with the end goal of achieving total freedom from imperialism and democracy to grow.\n\nThe Blueshirt Society however argues that China ought to remain in the first stage of military rule. The establishment of civilian tutelage was merely an illusion of progress that brought needless and inappropriate demands for constitutional rule. After all...did the people truly need democracy at this stage? Federalists and other democratic idealists might opine so, but when illtieracy has not yet been eradicated and the masses uneducated, for China to install democracy today would be like asking a girl with bound feet to dance on high heels.\n\nPerhaps, therefore the Blueshirts opine - democracy is not necessarily needed in the liberal sense but a true democracy, faith in the Lingxiu's guidance and trust in the Party ought to be the third phase of revolution.\n\nChen Cheng himself vaguely agrees with the concept of a military-led tutelage but he himself is committed to an idea of an eventual democracy. The party must introduce new and energetic members to rectify the errors of political tutelage, and to prepare China for a gradual liberalisation and march towards democracy.
Democracy is a total and utter SHAM to the people!
The new guard will fix the errors left by those before.
The Party-State Reorganised
Peace has settled within the Party's apparatus with the Hu Hanmin Crisis finally resolved. Now, Dai Jitao finds himself the undisputed leader of the Chinese Kuomintang. An idealistic conservative, Dai believes in reviving the nature and notion of Datong that had existed in centuries past, harkening back to a time when classes lived in harmony prior to the advent of Marxism and the syndicalist revolution. A true nationalist revolution needs not class warfare but rather a united consciousness of nationalism and class; this is the true goal of Dr. Sun Yat-sen.\n\nTrue to his doctrine, Dai refuses to side with any particular party faction, but he is associated with the CC Clique. The two brothers, Chen Lifu and Chen Guofu, lead a faction of supporters that seek to model China with a combination of Confucianism and authoritarianism akin to that of the Russian National Solidarist movement.\n\nNow as the new President takes over, the first phase in his planned reorganisation of the Kuomintang is to take place. Certainly empowering his own cult of personality and power would strengthen Dai's own image, but seeking the strength and support of the powerful CC Clique could also increase the nature of the revolutionary spirit within the party. Alternatively, the new President can instead bridge divides in the country to focus on harmonising the nation under one pure ideology.
Dai is truly China's man of the hour!
Towards a true harmony, shall the people strive for.
Empower the loyalists in the CC Clique.

The Army and the Party
The army has long been a voltaile force in Chinese politics and the National Revolutionary Army is unquestionably one of the main factors due to the National Revolution's success. With the army having been divieded following the Northern Expedition, the true National Revolution Army certainly acknowledges Dai Jitao as the de facto leader of the party; given Dai's own personal political clout in the sphere of the right-wing.\n\nWith He Yingqin effectively disgraced from politics, it would appeared that the rumoured plot of the Generalissmo working with the thuggish and radical elements of the army who pledge loyalty to the Blushirt Society, has also been swept away. Reconcilling with the Old Right
With the chaotic Hu Hanmin Crisis behind the National Government, Hu Hanmin has formally resigned all his posts within the Kuomintang and has instead returned home to Guangzhou, in a declaration of self-exile. His friendship with Dai Jitao is heavily strained these days with all that has transpired, but Dai has still consistently made overtures to rebuild his friendship with the esteemed Kuomintang elder.\n\nThe South China Political Council remains an issue to Dai's legitimacy as the party's head, with Hu having isolated himself. The Elders Clique of Cantonese party leaders have largely resigned themselves to their estates in the South, enjoying afternoon teas and discussing the later developments within China and the world. Their power, long consolidated in the cradle of Yue civilisation, continues to remain locally dominant with Dai and the CC Clique disinterested in destroying them. The Western Hills Clique continues to hold on to their positions in the Judicial Yuan while the Reconstruction Faction continue to clamor in the Legislative Yuan and amongst the diaspora community of Chinese expatriates.\n\nNonetheless, while Hu Hanmin may have escaped from the crisis without being labeled a traitor, the same cannot be said for other southern elements. The Political Science Government
The passing of the Provisional Constitution was met with widespread acclaim within the Legislative Yuan of the Kuomintang. With the dominance of the Political Science Clique within the ranks of the representative government of the people, the cabinet has stabilised the restored government. Fears of an overt military coup have largely subsided as the NRA's rank and file have proven to be loyal. The CC Clique's attempt to shoot down the Zhang government has ultimately failed leading to a rare sense of peace within the Party.\n\nTheir next steps on their agenda are to heal the divisions with the former Beiyang Republic. While the Beiyang Government ended up a corrupted dictatorship under the Zhili Clique, politicians such as Huang Fu, Wu Dingchang, and Wu Tiecheng are willing to pledge loyalty to the new Nationalist Government in China, bringing their wealth of experience with them. Huang specifically toured Germany decades ago, becoming inspired by the economic reforms of Walther Rathenau.\n\nWith the Political Science Government ascendant, technocrats and other political moderates have thrown their support behind Zhang Qun. Weng Wenhao and Ding Wenjiang, noted former Beiyang technocrats, have proposed new technocratic corpuses such as the National Resources Commission and Central Planning Board. Staffed with the most capable experts from all across China, the CPB will manage the central economic planning of the nation, while the NRC will be responsible for industrial development.\n\nThese bodies would thus become the springboards for Chinese industrialisation through an ambitious series of Five-Year Plans and place the economy firmly under the grasp of the Kuomintang. More liberal business elements rallying behind Kong Xiangxi's Clique of financials have criticised such measures as socialist nonsense, but these statist proposals have thus far earned the backing of Yang Yongtai, who has established a number state-run farms in the Southwest.

Negotiations with the Southern China Political Council
After his arrest and brief imprisonment, former triumvirate leader of the Kuomintang, Hu Hanmin was finally released. But the esteemed politician and writer refused to acknowledge the new government. Denouncing the new government as nothing more than compradors and warlords in disguise, Hu went south to a self-imposed exile of solitude and reflection in Guangzhou. Angered and stubborn, his visitors are few and his patience thin.\n\nHis following have since dispersed with the Elders Clique having also denounced the new government in [ROOT.Capital.GetCapitalVictoryPointName]. However, the Western Hills Clique and the Reconstruction Faction are amicable to resolving broken ties and to building a true new constitutional government. Militarists within the South China Political Council are fine with the ongoing crisis, seeing it as an opportunity for them to sideline influence of the national government.\n\nMost in the Political Science Clique are open to the idea of reviving discourse with the southern Republicans. Some have suggested opening up a Political Consultative Council to Sun Fo, Ju Zheng, and other scholars from the Reconstruction Faction and Western Hills Clique to start working on a full constitution. Of course, Sun himself has changed his view on the Provisional Constitutional issue, once having seen it as heretical to thinking that a full constitution is now needed to prevent future military coups.\n\nPoliticians have always been open to taking bribes and so many could certainly be persuaded to join the new national government with strong financial incentives, promises of lucrative stability in an unstable era of Chinese politics. Perhaps it would be best to grant the militarists their own autonomy as a means to watch and curb their influence, lest a coup end this new genuine attempt at creating a government.

Nationalist Women at War
With China in a desperate struggle for survival, the country has called upon not just its sons but also its daughters to the battlefield. Girls from around the nation are trained to administer first aid, expected to perform tasks around the home, and in some cases, even to learn how to handle machine guns and ammunition, lest the situation grow ever so dire.\n\nAmong the women in China who support the Kuomintang government, there is none other than Song Meiling, the wife of the former Generalissimo Chiang Kai-shek. A minor party celeberity, given her brief connection with the former headmaster of Whampoa, Song has urged women to mobilise into the factories in a manner similar to the British, French, and German women who had done so during the First World War. This mobilisation will help advance the status of women in society, helping women be seen as an equal to man especially in a society dominated by patriarchial norms like China. In an act of philanthropy and utilisng her familial connections, Song has also organised sophisticated and modern orphanages, complete with recreational facilities like pools and playgrounds, and with modern classrooms to fit China's growing orphans.\n\nShe and other prominent women, including He Yingqin's wife, have also called for women to participate in military units. Whether or not they will be deployed on the frontlines as actual combatants is yet to be observed, but thousands have answered the call to serve as medics and nurses in the ongoing struggle for national salvation.
The children must be our priority!
Mobilising the women will ensure their liberation!

Nationalist Local Government Reform
A very common thread among 20th Century Chinese reformers is that they desire to revive the local village and district. The Nationalists are no different, though unlike their Federalist rivals they believe that the Central Government ought to play the leading role in encouraging this grassroot revival, and in turn local administration would help give strength to the central government. The Expanding Central Committee
The Kuomintang operates as a party-state, but the actual relationship between the government and the party itself has always been ambiguous. This quandary has been worsened by the fact that the actual governance of the party has grown haphazard and unwieldy. The Central Executive Committee is supposed to rule the party on behalf of the Kuomintang National Congress. Originally 24 members in 1924, it has since grown to 36, then 71, and by this time is at 119. With so many members, it has grown unwieldy and effectively makes the Legislative Yuan and National Assembly redundant, especially since many Central Committee members hold seats in both as well!\n\nReducing membership has attracted too much pushback since CEC membership has been effectively a reward for submission, so an alternative plan to create a standing committee of 3-15 members has been tried. Originally it was supposed to be President He Yingqin, and Premiers Dai Jitao and Hu Hanmin, then it included all five branches, and soon enough it became as rudderless as the CEC.\n\nA third attempt to reform the system is underway, with calls to create a Political Council that includes 9 standing committee members and 6 others elected by a general session of the CEC, with non voting membership drawn from the cabinet. Sun Fo and Hu Hanmin have drafted a description of what it ought to look like: \
What a disaster...but we will make it work I suppose.
The Political Council will have 200, but only a standing committee of 15.
This entire system needs to be overhauled from scratch!
Audits and Inquiry in Nationalist China
The formation of a provisional Examination and Control Yuan was a moment to celebrate, but like most things in China, it has been mired in the same issues of corruption and incompetence that they were designed to stop. The Examination Yuan, based on the pre-existing structure of imperial examination, is designed to weed out incomptent political hopefuls and to encourage a government based on meritocracy and prowess.\n\nThe Examination Yuan is supposed to have supreme power over civil service appointments both nationally and provinically. The Examination Commission is to set the time, place, and forms for examinees - including a temporary board of examinations. The Ministry of Personnel has been keeping records of office holders in accordance with the Kuomintang's ranking system of bureaucrats. However, this elongated bureaucracy favours those at the top, with those at the bottom ranks often unable to create a sustainable income.\n\nThe Control Yuan, designed to vet out corruption, has been overwhelmed with the expansion of the examination Yuan. With such a large government to administer, the COntrol Yuan is the supervisory and auditory branch of the government of the Republic of China. Theortically, it has the power to impeach government officials (not the President and Vice-President), censure officials, corrective measures, and it has the capacity to audit the annual budget. However, with the problem of corruption seemingly endemic in Chinese political society, many are just simply weighing towards moving the annual budget towards other measures while others are voicing for increased funding towards the Control Yuan.
More funding for the Examination Yuan.
More funding for the Control Yuan.
We need that money elsewhere.
4 Elements and 9 Principles of Education
Chen Lifu, despite the ups and downs of his career, remains prominent as an educator. He has presented a bold, comprehensive proposal for adapting the educational system to wartime needs before an Extraordinary National Congress of the Kuomintang, offering 4 Elements and 9 Principles for guiding our educational reforms, as well as 17 ways of implementing it.\n\nThese elements are as follows: 1.) Revise education systems and teaching materials and pursue the wartime curricula 2.) Train personnel with different skills and assign them to suit wartime needs 3.) Emphasise the cultivation of national ethics, intensify scientific research, and expand scientific facilities 4.) Train the youth to serve society and further strengthen our capacity to fight the war of resistance\n\nThey are to be supported by nine principles 1.) Advance education by mental, physical and disciplinary means 2.) Combine the civil with the military 3.) Place similar emphasis on agrarian and industrial demands 4.) Have consistency in educational and political objectives 5.) Link education of family to school 6.) Use scientific methods to reorganise and teach literature, history, philosophy, and art to build national character based on Chinese tradition 7.) Increase natural sciences training to meet the pressing needs of national defence and production 8.) Revamp the social sciences to learn, suit our own national conditions, and encourage innovation 9.) Make clear our education goals, work to equalise education and training in different localities, make compulsory education universal for all, implement our social and family education programme.\n\nThe situation is grim, but the silver haired gentleman is undaunted in his efforts to reshape Chinese minds. The future does not wait for the youth to grow up, and if China is to survive these years of troubles, it must teach its youth how to navigate and overcome the challenges ahead.
The youth must learn fighting and discipline to survive.
Through science and production, students can build our future!
Our future comrades must learn the Principles and public service!

The BIS At Work
The Kuomintang Central Organisation Department's Bureau of Investigation and Statistics has been hard at work under the auspices of Chen Lifu and his ever growing portfolio. In addition to their namesake investigation and clandestine intelligence gathering roles, the BIS also takes their auxiliary communication and counterintelligence functions seriously as well.\n\nThey pride themselves on operating several shortwave radio stations between cities surrounding the capital, pioneered by one American trained engineer named Li Fanyi, who specialises in shortwave electricity. This system of radio signalling is said to enhance government communications and may also have battlefield applications as well for communicating between military headquarters and the Presidential palace.\n\nIn support of their communications role, they have become quite proficient in code-breaking and operation security. Chen Lifu, who keeps a code book on him at all times, has personally developed a 5 stroke indexing system for Chinese characters that he has attempted to popularise both within government offices and the general public.\n\nThe BIS, and the CC Clique by extension, is known to operate various front organisations. The largest of which is the Zhengyuan Industrial Company (roughly translated, means Origin of Basic Industrial Company), a shell corporation that allows the CC Clique to move money around and conduct economic espionage. There are rumours that Chen Lifu's prized Chinese Engineering Association and their headquarters is also a front for CC Clique safehouses.\n\nThe BIS's activities have not gone completely unchecked however. Ding Mocun, head of the General Affairs Section, has recently been accused of corruption - his BSS rivals have demanded an investigation into expenditures related to securing prominent leftist defectors. While Ding maintains the accusations are politically motivated, his status as a former socialist who switched sides during the 1924 reorganisation has added to suspicion as does his reputation as an impulsive man.
Their work in communications unites the nation.
Codes and espionage help protect the party.
I think its time we examine their funding records.
A Model Police force for a Model China
The Public Security Bureau has been making strides under Zhang Qun and Political Science Clique aligned lawmen. At the forefront of their public activities is the [Root.Capital.GetCapitalVictoryPointName] Police Academy, praised by [ROOT.GetLeader] as the model for all other Police Academies to follow. Over 350 young cadets of both genders are trained at the facility and receive both free housing and a generous monthly stipend.\n\nParamilitary in nature, their days open with bugle calls and flag ceremonies before a lengthy curriculum that includes swimming, jujitsu, military science, and political education under military officers and political commissars. Second year students are allowed to major in one of four areas: administration, criminal investigation, foreign services, and policewomen training and administration. Cadets are allowed to experience a tour of duty with the local police force during training, and upon graduation they are equipped with guns and modern automobiles.\n\nThough America's star may have waned, its influence can be felt in the Chinese police system. Nationalist police forces are said to be copying principles established by August Vollmer in California, cutting crime rates in half. Leading investigators have sought FBI expertise, imitating their fingerprinting and federal police methods.\n\nPerhaps the most elite police force however is the Chinese Military Police. Established as a separate branch of the National Revolutionary Army, it has achieved a reputation as being a Praetorian Guard for the President. Headed by Gu Zhenglun, dubbed \

The Conscription Debate
China has an enormous population, but the sheer scale of the war and the declining number of volunteers has triggered major debates over conscription. The Military Affairs Commission has presented their case for a widespread draft, first proposed by He Yingqin presciently in 1928. According to his plan, all houses will be surveyed across a year, then the next year a list of all eligible men would be created. Men would be drawn according to the Baojia system, with recruits annually sent to training camps where they serve as militia-men for three years full-time and then six years as \

Predictions of the Dharma Assembly
When the Northern Expedition began so many years ago, the arch-imperialists in Berlin saw fit to put down our flame. Once again, with the revolution finally underway once again, the Japanese bandits have seen fit to inherit Germany's mantle for destruction and international tyranny and march against us. If China is to finally throw off the shackles of imperialism and repudiate the Century of Humiliation, then the war must not be fought through simply the rifle, but with the spiritual prowess of the Chinese people.\n\nNone more aware of this is Dai Jitao, who has organised the National Protection and Disaster Relief Dharma Assembly (hu guo xi zai fa hui) to bolster the Republic against the Japanese onslaught. On the advice of Dai, Generalissimo He Yingqin has invited prominent Chinese and Tibetan monks to this Dharma Assembly, organised to be in session for many weeks. Among the attending monks is the Venerable Master Xuyun, secretly invited by the Nationalist Government to preside over the rituals and was welcomed personally by Chen Lifu upon his arrival. On the first day of the Assembly, Dai set up a vegetarian feast at a local temple in the capital which saw both He Yingqin and Xuyun attend.\n\nDuring the festivities, the Generalissimo and Dai engaged in a lengthy discussion over the War of Resistance. When pressed about his opinions on the war, Master Xuyun asked a waiter for a pair of scissors and took out a piece of paper from his pocket. After cutting the paper into a square, folding it three times diagonally, and then cutting it horizontally three times, Xuyun unfolded the paper to reveal two characters: a cross and sun. After a moment of contemplation, Xuyun declared that this was a sign that Germany and Japan, the two imperialist titans who plundered China, would fall in the War of Resistance. Generalissimo He Yingqin said nothing in response but merely smiled.
We march to see Master Xuyun's predictions through!

A War on Two Fronts
With the situation swiftly deteriorating both within the south and northeast, Zhu Peide's personal amassing of loyalists and allies have effectively become boxed-in. Having allocated the majority of the Third Army to forward positions against Hu Ruoyu in the south, there remains very little potential defenders available to blunt Chen Cheng's advance.\n\nUltimately even the defenders that persist are but a paltry shade of the pride of the NRA. Mostly composed of demoralised PSC units under Zhang Qun and Xiong Shihui, many have only freshly retreated from Guizhou. Focused first and foremost on their own force preservation, their hold on Zhaotong is far from assured.\n\nConvening a war council of his senior staff, Zhu Peide has consulted with Li Genyuan, Zhu De, Jin Handing and Fan Shisheng as to the best approach to solve their predicament. Alas they have become loosely split upon three camps:\n\nZhu Peide for his part desires to press against Hu Ruoyu in Mengzi, eliminating the threat in the south before wheeling back to fight Chen Cheng's advance in a rapid campaign, even at the expense of PSC forces. On the opposite end, Li has proposed to redeploy units to Zhaotong from the backlines in Mengzi, blunting the advance of the First Army with enough force to launch a counterattack. Clearly such a move most benefits Li politically as he preserves Zhang Qun's forces, strengthening the PSC.\n\nPlaying spoiler before an argument could brew between Li and Zhu, General Zhu De has courteously proposed to his senior commanders of his own plan. Instead of launching any offensive, the Third Army pulls back all forces, including Li Mi in the south and Zhang Qun in the north, to defensible positions and request the Central Government to demand Chen Cheng to stand down.\n\nWith Jin and Fan remaining on the fence, their opinions would undoubtedly sway the overall outcome of the meeting. With all plans having their merits, faults, and political machinations behind their creation, neither are a surefire way to success. However it has become evidently clear that Jin is in crisis, torn between his friendship, his mentor, and his superior.

Mengzi's Dilemma
Elsewhere in the Yunnan cascade of chaos, things are similarly unfolding rapidly into a deteriorating hurricane of politics and bloodshed. Loosely brought together by their opposition against the Kuomintang, but otherwise at each other's throats, Hu Ruoyu's southern gentry coalition has begun to expand his operatons. Aiming to influence western Yunnan, a traditionally radical hotbed willing to buck the influence of Kunming, Hu has begun to engage in negotiations with the majority of the more conservative and reactionary gentry that reside there.\n\nWithin Hu's direct staff, many are advising the general to approach the crisis diplomatically, scooping up support of Yunnan's establishment to return the province to the isle of stability it once was. Others have suggested that Hu should pursue an offensive policy of open confrontation with the Kuomintang radicals, aiming to quickly destroy the Kuomintang regime whilst their infighting is at its worst.\n\nTo strengthen Hu's resolve and position further, many if his aides have begun to tap into contacts across Yunnan, such as the Chamber of Commerce, and outside the province to the outside world. Establishing covert ties with potential allies in Siam, Burma and Indochina, Hu's position has in turn been strengthened with material and aid, if only for promises down the road.\n\nNonetheless, Yunnan's civil war remains anyone's game, with Hu Ruoyu, Zhu Peide and Chen Cheng all engaging in nigh self-destructive last-ditch gambits to secure their control over the province. Zhu De's Coup

Hu Ruoyu Marches on Kunming
The revolutionaries in the Kuomintang have evidently worn out their welcome in Yunnan as Hu Ruoyu's forces surged from Mengzi to Kunming with little resistance. The overstretched Third Army garrisons were rapidly overrun, a process accelerated by an uprising in the city organised by General Lu Han, a staff officer under Long Yun, who avenged his relative and avoided the need for a long siege.\n\nThe vaunted Whampoa Spirit proved to be little more than talk as Chen Cheng's forces were destroyed assaulting Old Dian Clique positions. After regrouping their forces, Hu Ruoyu and Lu Han were able to capture Guizhou after routing their opponents. While the two nominally retain their Kuomintang membership and profess their absolute loyalty to the revolution, the party's true faithful have been driven out of the Southwest.\n\nThe ensuing political situation has been tense. While the central government has been too preoccupied to be concerned with Southwestern Affairs and have refrained from commenting on the situation, ironically the lack of an external threat has destabilised the province. Tang Jiyao is an absolute persona non-grata to the KMT, but Long Yun's is more divisive. The Kunming garrison and provincial elders briefly installed him as Governor, but clashes between him and his long-time rival Hu Ruoyu (who now has the upper hand) caused them to trade accusations of counterrevolutionary behavior.\n\nLong hoped to shore up his failing position by counting on the support of Lu Han, whose forces are slowly trickling back from Guizhou. But General Lu decided to make a fateful decision - coming to an agreement with Hu Ruoyu behind his back and agreeing to accept Hu as Governor to avoid further conflict. Long Yun, invited to participate in the planned junta, has left Kunming for Zhaotong - unwilling to be a figurehead and hoping for more favorable winds in the future.

Chen Cheng Seizes Kunming
In a daring manoeuvre Chen Cheng and the First Army have proven the value of their Whampoa training. Ensuring their principal's lessons were not lost or forgotten, their daring descent from Guizhou onwards towards the southwest's capital has been that of legend. Reminiscent of their Long March in light of the Northern Expedition's collapse, General Chen's own escapade was not one driven by fear or sorrow, but that of duty, spirit and honour.\n\nOverthrowing the Political Science Clique's rule in Guizhou through an elegant but expertly enacted coup, the First Army successfully followed Zhang Qun and Xiong Shihui's soldiers to Zhaotong and crushed the PSC armies.\n\nWith momentum on their side, the demoralised garrison in Kunming put only a token defence against Chen's triumphant advance into the fortified city. Spared from a prolonged siege, the majority of Kunming remained in-tact. However during Chen's advance outside the walls, a palace coup occurred led by the leftist contingent within the Third Army, notably Zhu De, Tang Huaiyuan and Zeng Wanzhong. Joined by Zhu De's best friend Jin Handing and General Fan Shisheng, the Third Army dissenters successfully detained the discredited Zhu Peide, Li Genyuan and Zhang Qun.\n\nCaught in the chaos, Hu Ruoyu attempted to launch his own advance from Mengzi to capitalise on the confrontation within Kunming. Notably low on intelligence however, Hu's forces were all but annihilated despite their superior arms due to the combined offensive of the Third Army and First Army.\n\nAs the dust settles, tensions still remain. With the leftists within Yunnan and Guizhou split on policy in the aftermath, numerous issues have arisen. Zhu De and Jin Handing for their part desire Zhu Peide's release from prison, insisting that he simply didn't do anything wrong, and that the counter-revolutionary actions of the old regime were at the behest of Li Genyuan. Zhu and Li Hold Out in Kunming
Once again thrust into the nightmare of bloodshed, the city of Zhaotong has recently been the crux of defence for the Kunming administration. Much akin to the previous Yunnan-Guizhou war, and already devastated and in recovery from that conflict, Zhaotong was placed under siege by an invading force descended from Guizhou, aiming for the heart of the southwest. However that is where the events cease to reflect eachother, as this time Yunnan proved valiantly victorious. With the First Army's forces beaten back following a blunder by Jiang Dingwen, Chen Cheng has been pushed to order a retreat, nominally in disgrace.\n\nEmboldened by their successes, Zhu Peide's forces under Li Mi launched a determined, courageous, and somewhat suicidal assault against Hu Ruoyu's mustering gentry forces within the south. Characteristic of Li Mi's harsh, chaotic, and rowdy command of the battlefield, Hu Ruoyu's forces were caught blindsided by Li's rapid assault. Reportedly Li's artillery destroyed Hu's fortifications at almost point blank range, and purposefully led a few of his division to be encircled. Believing that he had won, Hu's forces rushed in to capture or kill Li and his men, and yet they were met with a tenacity unbeknownst to encircled soldiers. Beaten back, they would be encircled themselves by the outer ring of Li's forces, and promptly captured.\n\nHaving kept the castle from being besieged by invaders, Zhu Peide and Li Genyuan remain the masters of China's southwest. In a similar vein, Wang Desan's rebellion has all been but crushed in the meantime.\n\nA most curious matter has presented itself however. An All Too Familiar Face
Despite their triumphant successes in the Yunnan Civil War, the actions of both Zhu Peide and Li Genyuan in the crisis have brought to light the increasingly fraught nature of the Zhu-Li diarchy. Naturally, bad blood of the past was sure to arise given their now nigh total dominance as Kings of the Southwest. Earlier, during the times of the Yunnan Army in Guangdong, Li and Zhu were once superior and subordinate. Serving alongside the likes of Li Liejun, Yang Ximin, Liu Zhenhuan, Fan Shisheng, and Zhang Kairu. With Zhu having long been the most loyal to Dr. Sun out of the whole Yunnan Army in Guangdong, he would ultimately side with Li Liejun during the Liejun-Genyuan power struggle. With Genyuan taking an anti-Sun stance, and insisting upon cooperation with Old Guangxi as opposed to Tang Jiyao, he would be driven out by his subordinates.\n\nAnd it would appear once more that such an atmosphere has risen, with Li's continued attempts to increase his power be it directly, and through the utilisation of the gentry and Political Science Clique. With their heroes in the form of Zhang Qun and Hu Ruoyu driven from power, Li has accepted their support with open arms. Certainly not helping matters in any form, considering Li's already despised nature by the mainstream Party.\n\nTheir most recent spat however is that of the duo's different approaches to ruling Yunnan without the need to parley with local power players. Zhu Peide, ever a loyal servant to the Party, insists upon Yunnan being a cog in the National Revolution. A stepping stone and a model for the rest of the Kuomintang's journey. Li himself instead advocates for Yunnan to focus their efforts on local reconstruction, seeing to the provincial people's needs, working with the establishment, and building a stronger province for everyone.

Socialist Flowers Bloom in the Southwest
Perhaps a most fortuitous but unexpected outcome of the recent Yunnan Civil War, and the hoisting of the Red Banner within Kunming after decades of struggle, is that of the blooming literature scene. Whilst always home to various philosophers of all political creeds, the Southwest has nonetheless been a traditionally conservative area of much hesitancy to the concepts of socialism. Yet with the recent leftist takeover, Yunnan and Guizhou have become something of a socialist writing utopia. Attracting not only philosophers, but artists, musicians, and theorists.\n\nPerhaps most related to the field of politics would be that of Ai Siqi, a Yunnan-Mongol native, and devoted socialist philosopher and theorist. Studying in Japan, teaching himself German, and ultimately working for Shen Bao newspaper, Ai would join the CSP in 1935. A devoted, young, and collected writer, he has already won some repute for his publishing of Popular Philosophy in 1935. Similarly, Ai himself is friends with the likes of Shen Junru, and other CDL figures.\n\nOther notable individuals that have risen to prominence are the likes of Nier Er, a famous musician and composer. Having contributed to many recent films, national music, and considered a pioneer of China's new music movement. There is also Wu Deng, famed local student leader within the Yunnan United Student Organisation. A local feminist and radical socialist leader, she was once the schoolmate of the late Long Yun's wife, Li Peilian, and even managed to maintain some sort of rapport with Tang Jiyao's lieutenant.\n\nFinally, there is Zhang Bojian. A member of the Bai minority, he travelled to France on a Work-Study agreement. Friends with the likes of Zhou Enlai, Zhang endeavoured to spread the word of socialism to Chinese students, attaining a high theoretical and practical understanding of socialism following the French Revolution.\n\nTruly, Yunnan has transformed itself from the black spot of the nation, full of conservatism with scant reflections of reformist attitudes to that of socialism's cornucopia within China, a flourishing intellectual battleground full of debate, and the furthering of the nation. Of course, Yunnan could always be so much more...
Forevermore shall Yunnan harbour Socialism!
We shall fulfill our revolutionary duty!
The Guangming Daily
For as long as anyone can remember, Zhang Bojun has always been a figure much admired and detested. Hated by his own party for being a leftist by the right wing, and for being a rightist by the left wing, Zhang has found limited solace in his own comrades; scant a few strong friendships, such as his personal one with Chen Mingshu, and Zhu De. However a certain sect of the Chinese political sphere has always welcomed Zhang and his efforts with at least admiration, if not support. Personal friends with the likes of Zhang Junmai, Shen Junru and others, Zhang has surprisingly managed to win token support from figures of the new generation of Third Force Democrats.\n\nSimilarly, on Zhang's end, he has recently been noted to have attended multiple meetings with various China Democratic League figures, keen to parley with them for a common goal. Offering a provincial political alliance, Zhang and his CDL friends would appear to be building a broad bloc of democratic reformist socialism within the southwest, at least as espoused by their Guangming Daily articles.\n\nOf course such efforts are not merely homely ones, intended for pure compromise. They serve a dual function, the stabilisation of Zhang's administration, and the fervent display that Zhang is not like his contemporaries within the Kuomintang, treating the Dangwai with suspicion. Instead, Zhang sees it fit to utilise his informal alliance as a weapon and tool, to advance a common progressive cause in the southwest. However some have begun to suggest to the Governor that he should be looking far beyond his idealistic purview of Yunnan-Guizhou, and instead aim for the national level.

Kunming Seizes the Reins
Kunming has long been within the periphery and outside rung of the Kuomintang's revolutionary movement. Whilst never directly controlled by the party itself, the city, and by extension the province, has commonly found itself in similar circles and even in lockstep. Ranging from Cai E and Li Genyuan's Double Ninth Uprising (in which a plethora of future Yunnanese Kuomintang memebrs would participate), or the legendary National Protection War in which Yunnan rallied the cause of numerous neighbouring provinces to vanquish Yuan Shikai's proliferation of a new Hongxian Era. However, Yunnan has also commonly found itself in odds with the Party more often than most. With Cai E's refusal to participate in the Second Revolution of 1913, or Tang Jiyao's consistent machinations over control of the Revolutionary Movement, and of Guangdong and Guangxi.\n\nTherefore it is rather unsurprising to find that the regionalist kingpin of the Southwest managed to subvert the ostenible rule of the mainstream revolutionary hubs, and march ever onwards to vanquishing the Kuomintang's Central Committee, sweeping away the old order in utter triumph. Those within the personal inner circle of [YUN.GetLeader] and the [YUN.GetRulingParty] understood that they never truly had any intention of utterly eradicating their once benevolent benefactors, and yet history has thrust them into the role of National Protector once more.\n\nWith the old government gone, few can feasibly oppose the titanic rise of the Kuomintang's Periphery, but their leadership still remains contested. Not by the Party on the open field, but by the people, by the demobilised, and by the economy. Warlords on the outside nonwithstanding. Whether or not [YUN.GetLeader] has a blissfully quick path to national unification or if there will be further conflicts over the fulfillment of the National Revolution will shape China for years to come.

The Second Federalist Conference
Beijing has fallen into the hands of Tang Jiyao, and with it the old order has been swept away. Some Beiyang remnants continue to fight on or have fled into the arms of our rivals, but at least for now resistance near the city has subsided. A new order emerges, this time along Guangzhou's designs.\n\nTang Jiyao is the man of the hour. Once merely the governor of an isolated province, repeatedly humiliated in wars outside of Yunnan, and a name people would know at best as a pale imitation of Cai E, he has become the presumptive future President of the united republic. Indeed, it seems that fate has given China into the hands of the southerners - a rarity in Chinese history where most unifications have been north to south.\n\nBut how will he rule? It was one thing to lead as President for a quasi-recognised regional government. It is another to be the central government in China, especially at the helm of a movement ostensibly dedicated to empowering the provinces. The Federalists have always been an odd quantity in Chinese politics - familiar to the old system and often intertwined with it, and yet in other ways proclaiming to be a new, reformist movement.\n\nWith momentum shifting in their favor, the Federalists are at an advantage in the coming proceedings. Many defeated Beiyang assemblymen have sought to retain their seats in the new government, pleading for entry into the victorious Guangzhou government. At least some of them will have to be integrated for the sake of stability, but in these precious moments at the start of a new order Tang Jiyao has so much power to set the tone for the future. Democracy means so many different things to so many different people. Now the republic waits to learn what it means to their new President.
Democracy means the end to the endemic corruption of Beiyang.

Beiyang Reorganises
Beijing has a new master and the old Beiyang parties have scrambled to adapt to a new order where they are no longer dominant. Many of their leaders, sensing the instability of the new regime and wanting to hedge their bets, have decided to sit out of proceedings for now - allowing their underlings to do the dirty work in representing their interests in the Federalist legislature.\n\nThe old Beiyang system was dominated by independents, at least nominally. Organised political parties were viewed with suspicion after the Yuan Shikai's conflict with the Tongmenghui, and many Beiyang politicians prized their independence - banding together in informal cliques and associations. Many of these independents continue to operate, hoping to remain relevant in the new regime by leveraging their personal followings, patronage networks, and/or political machines to hold onto power.\n\nMany of the northern left have joined the budding opposition, particularly those associated with the National Salvation Federation. Hu Shih and other old CMS members have begun intense dialogue with Luo's faction, though have not ruled out working with the President either. Revolutionary socialist parties such as the KMT and CSP have faced heavy suppression and their central committees have prohibited their members from participating in the Federalist government.\n\nIt is the Liangists who have seen the biggest shift. The Chinese Constitutionalist Party, once the largest formal party in the assembly behind the Harmony Association, has disintegrated after its leadership moved to join the GRA. A young guard splinter has worked with former members of Zhang Junmai's DSP to form the Democratic Constitutionalist Party. The Young China Party has sought to benefit from the exodus of CCP members, though at the same time has been cozying up with the Tang Clique.
Tang's endorsement of Liangism gains him allies in the north.
Luo Longji and the CMS are easily able to reconnect with old friends.
Beijing's independents fiercely protect what is theirs.

Democracy in Wartime
With increasingly militaristic attitudes and a tenuous state of nigh continuous war throughout the nation, the attitudes of democracy and waves of freedoms have seen much comparable restrictions and debate throughout the halls of the resilient statesmen and lofty intellectuals. Should rights and freedoms be restricted? Is it practical to maintain democracy during wartime? What of insurgents, spies, or domestic stability?\n\nLuo Longji, writing in Ziyou Juntan (Liberty Forum), believes he has found the solution. In response to suggestions that rights and freedoms be significantly restricted during the war, he retorted by quoting Harold Laski. Positing that liberty should be enjoyed in times of war as well as normal times, otherwise the people would not want to fight the war or make any contributions to it.\n\nCiting evidence for his claims, he procures that of military defeats in the British Revolution, or in the American Civil War; significant restrictions on rights deprive the governments of their own citizen's support directly due to the deprivation of their rights.\n\nInstead of an autocratic or oppressive government, Luo supports the notion of a \

Federalism On the March
The North has fallen and the South ruminates as China turns a new page in its history. The Chinese Federalist movement has traditionally taken a more cautious approach towards unification, preferring diplomacy over the pursuit of a northern expedition - either due to a genuine belief in negotiations or a desire to conserve resources and pessimistic outlook. Nevertheless, [UPC.GetNameDef] has emerged victorious in its wars, capturing the internationally recognised capital of Beijing in a brutal fight.\n\nThe costs have been heavy, even on the victors. Yunnan may not be the centre of the Federalist government, but it has been forced to levy heavy taxes on its people and endure the trials of modern warfare. For all the sacrifices the people have made, there is a growing expectation that the Federalists deliver on their promises for democracy and economic reform at home.\n\nFor Tang Jiyao, his debts might be called sooner than he would hope. Unrest has been simmering for some time now, with Tang's attempts at centralising power in the province alienating many of his garrison commanders. The intelligentsia he has allied with have similarly grown discontented with the slow pace of reforms - constitution drafting has stalled and elections have been limited. As Guangzhou considers its next steps, Kunming faces a reckoning of its own.

Agitation in Kunming
Tang Jiyao has always adopted a cautious approach towards leftist radicals infiltrating his province. Cognisant of his need to attract intellectuals from prestigious schools in order to build his democratic credentials and staff his government, he has at times looked the other way but his stringent anti-socialism has also led to the formation of the Yunnan Innovation Society by his opponents, which later evolved into the Marxist New Yunnan Society.\n\nTang has kept careful track of these dissidents, recruiting informers within student bodies and the faculty. Surveillance and censorship are commonplace in China, but what is not as easy to address is the growing disenchantment of the centre-left, who chafe under the restrictions imposed on them.\n\nWord of a revival of the Federation of Chinese Democratic Parties in Beijing has been spreading. This latest iteration, the China Democratic League, is being organised by many leading reform socialists, progressives, and liberals nation-wide. Among its co-founders is Luo Longji and the Crescent Moon Society who have built extensive ties in Yunnan across the last decade or so.\n\nBut in Kunming, it will be up to the natives to build their party. The Defence Commissioners Plot
Tang Jiyao has worked to sideline his senior military officers after their failed coup in 1927, taking advantage of his alliance with the central government and his success securing Yunnan's flanks to undermine and subjugate them. He has slowly restricted their funding, pitted them against one another, and redistributed their authority and privileges to his own loyalists to great success over the years.\n\nHis schemes have not gone unnoticed by his rivals however. Tang's removal of General Long Yun from Zhaotong permanently altered the balance of power in Yunnan in his favor: Long was the strongest of Tang's deputies, at one point stronger than all of them combined. Long remains in service under careful watch, his power mostly broken, but the real threat to Tang's rule now is General Hu Ruoyu.\n\nIn some ways, Hu was always the man to beat. The more aggressive schemer of the two, he continues to attempt to build a coalition capable of toppling Tang. Despite his mutual animosity with General Zhang Ruji, Hu has tried to peel off General Sun Du from Tang's sphere. Similarly, he has reached out to the ambitious General Lu Han, a cousin of Long Yun, in hopes of swaying him and his relative to his side. Hu has ties to the local CDL and even some of the KMT, on top of commercial interests and even some bandits. In the same way Tang Jiyao has been preparing for a showdown, Hu Ruoyu has been waiting for this moment for years.

The Luliang Uprising
While Yunnan's leaders scheme against one another in the cities, the conflict has already broken out into open fighting in the countryside. Tensions have been brewing for some time as bandits, gentry, socialists, and officials all fought for supremacy. For the most part, Yunnan's socialists are the weakest faction - unlike their MinGan peers the fragmented Yunnan rebels have failed to consolidate into an army capable of beating out the much larger gentry militias and instead are forced to rely on local, less ideological bandits for support.\n\nThe sole exception, it seems, is in Liuliang county where the local gentry rose up against local officials trying to disarm the region's tribesmen. Socialists led by Wang Desan attempted to exploit the moment, playing off both sides before making a move with the help of local students, bandits, farmers, and others with a grudge against the local landlords. Though quickly driven back as both the gentry and Tang government officials regrouped, the incident has thrown the province into anarchy.\n\nHu Ruoyu has attempted to march on Kunming, accusing Tang of harbouring socialists and allying with the rebels against the gentry. Tang has ordered the Provincial Assembly to declare martial law in response and arrest socialist leaders in the capital. CDL leaders, fearing that they are next, huddle in their homes as they desperately try to chart their next course of action before the sun rises...

Hu Ruoyu Seizes Kunming
The coup was swift, faster than anyone could have imagined. Tang Jiyao's rule proved far more fragile than it seemed - his advanced forces melting away as the Mengzi Army advanced. Zhang Ruiji led the left flank and quickly captured Dali as local gentry militias joined him along the way. Hu Ruoyu and Li Xuanting meanwhile made a rapid march to the capital.\n\nKunming fell almost without a shot, preventing the need for a long siege. Telegram From Kunming
The President has received an urgent telegram from Kunming, signed by leading members of the Yunnan CDL. Concerning reports of a sudden spike in political violence in the semi-isolated and heavily autonomous province have been flowing into [UPC.Capital.GetCapitalVictoryPointName] for a few days now, but they have been sporadic and unreliable. The provincial government, led for decades by the Federalist Governor Tang Jiyao, has repeatedly stated that he has the situation under control - that rural bandits (an endemic, but stabilised problem) and socialist insurgents have exploited the uncertainty following the fall of Berlin to stir up trouble.\n\nThe recent telegram paints a different picture. It castigates the Tang government as corrupt, authoritarian, and brutal - arresting dissenters and suppressing civil rights. He has refused to adopt a provincial constitution or hold free elections as he grasps onto power. A civil war apparently is brewing between Tang Jiyao and his lieutenants, with local democratic intellectuals caught in the crossfire. The message pleads with the central government to intervene and order Tang's removal - further charging him with planning treason against [UPC.GetLeader]'s government.\n\nThe writers are clearly being hyperbolic but it does underscore their desperation. The 1920s Federalist concept for central government-provincial relations has generally been favourable towards local autonomy and non-intervention in their electoral affairs. Tang Jiyao especially has been a long-time and prominent member of the Federalist movement, though the message has touched a nerve when it comes to questioning his loyalty, a concern that has persisted throughout his tenure. Given the extreme situation, Presidential intervention to broker a compromise might be the only way to resolve the situation peacefully in a timely manner, but will carry significant political risks. Then again, so does non-intervention.
