The Four Heavenly Kings
In Chinese Buddhist mythology, the four directions of the world (north, south, east, and west) are governed by the four devas (gods). They are Vaisravana, Virduhaka, Dhrtrarastra, and Virupaksa and they all serve Sakra, the lord of the devas. On the 8th, 14th, and 15th day of each Lunar month; the four kings either send out emissaries or go inspect the state of virtue and morality of men. On the orders of Sakra, the Kings also protect the Traystrama (the mythical celestial realm of the devas) from attacks from the Asuras , demons representing cardinal sins such as gluttony and lust.\n\nWith Tang having consolidated power in the province, a greater focus is on the rise of his so-called \

The Crescent Moon Rises
After their resistance and unrest against the Tang Jiyao government, the CDL was suppressed with many of their members forced into hiding. Reports have it that CDL members can be found as far as Shanghai and Hong Kong from China's most southwestern province. The moderate members of the CDL have tried to reconcile with the government and have sought to join the Crescent Moon Society (CMS) under Luo Longji, Wen Yiduo, and Liang Shiyi.\n\nThe CMS' ties to the CDL are historic. Luo Longji in his early days was involved in setting up the National Socialist Party and worked as the head of propaganda for the CDL. Identifying early on as a social democrat - Luo turned to liberalism as he admired the concept and framework of a separation of power and rejected that socialism would be a means to the end. As a Professor on Political Science and specialising in human rights, Luo has been teaching in Kunming's Southwest United University since 1932. Given his proximity to the governor and his long standing ties to the democratic movement; Luo holds close ties with Tang Jiyao.\n\nOf course, naturally the Crescent Moon Society poses a threat. It is an idealitsic blend of both Federalist principles, conservative democratic ideals, and a constitutional party. The Death of Tang Jiyao
The soldiers came at midnight. In the dead of the night, a squad of infantrymen stormed into Tang's home with an offer reading aloud from a warrant that Tang Jiyao was to be placed under close supervision for an undisclosed period of time. The orders came directly from Tang's former subordinates Hu Ruoyu and Long Yun and also Cun Shusheng, leader of Yunnan's CDL. In a sudden palace coup, Tang's decades long aspirations for power came crashing down several months ago.\n\nThere have been disagreements amongst the new triumvirate on the fate of Tang. Cun has vehemently stated that Tang should be sent far away from Yunnan to Guangzhou, while Long Yun has argued that Tang's presence in the province would serve them best given Tang's influence amongst his supporters. Hu Ruoyu on the other hand wants Tang to face trial for his crimes and hopefully sentenced. The discussions however proved to be moot. A telegram from the central government had arrived last week trying to secure Tang passage to Guangzhou under armed guard but he was found dead in his bed this morning. While Tang himself has been in declining health lately, rumours are quickly flying that he was posiioned and neutralised completely.\n\nTang Xiaoying has been in Guangzhou, attempting to lobby the central government authorities on his father's behalf. This has ingratiated him to the prominent figures of the Grand Republican Alliance particularly PIP leaders such as the towering figure of Chen Jiongming, Chen Qiyou, and Ma Yuhang. Following his father's death, many especially within Tang's People's Government Party have seen this as an opportunity to curry favour with the central government and to rally against opponents of the former regime. Believing Tang to have been assassinated, they have urged Xiaoying to take the role of the party's chairman as the natural successor to his father. Xiaoying has also received a powerful endorsement from none other than Zhang Taiyan who has also pledged to support the young Tang in a political career as head of the PGP. The triumverate of Hu Ruoyu, Long Yun, and Cun Shusheng have been viewing Xiaoying's political fortunes with a mix of both scruitny and suspicion but perhaps a compromise can be made with the younger Tang to steer him in acorrect direction.

The Second Sino-Japanese War
The Second Sino-Japanese War has broken out, pitting Japan and their Chinese allies against those seeking to resist Japanese dominance. Across the land, nationalist sentiment has spiked as the normally apathetic people begin to demand that their bickering leaders set aside their petty differences and contribute to the fight between civilisations. Regionalism, at least in its previous form, has become taboo and all provinces are expected to join the war on one side or another.\n\nLong Yun has vowed to commit troops to the conflict but he is also caught in a bind. Either way his position is threatened - if he does send troops to the front lines that means fewer troops inside the province helping protect his rule. If he fails to send his promised troops, then he will be branded as a disloyal and cowardly warlord. With emotions so high, popular anger is not a threat to be ignored.\n\nIn order to pre-emptively diffuse criticisms, Governor Long has ordered a provincial conference between legislators, military officers, and members of the bureaucracy. For Hu Ruoyu, this is his best chance - all know that he will use this moment to strike. Speaking to his officers, he stressed the importance of thought, morality, and belief in patriotic principles, casting himself as a patriotic hero in uncertain times:\n\nThe spirit of the nation leaves a deep impression on the mind of a soldier. Nor, when an army with principles meets an army without principles, the army with principles is victorious. When an army which practices principles suited to national conditions meets one not suited, the one suited to national conditions is victorious.

The [GetYear] Political Conference

If successfully implemented, this plan would mark the final destruction of Tang Jiyao's military system which divided power among the defence commissioners - bringing the province probably permanently under Long and his bureaucrat's dominion.\n\nThe proposal has angered many in the army high command, including his chief lieutenants like Lu Han, Zhu Xu, Zhang Chong, and Zhang Fengchun. The military still believes they can propose and dictate policy as they will, but power has been shifting for some time now towards Long, who controls the province's civilian administration and retains considerable public and gentry support.\n\nHu Ruoyu and Lu Han have decided to call his bluff, staging a mutiny in the capital. Long Yun's Ambition
Long Yun is now the effectively uncontested leader of Yunnan, having dispatched of his rivals and brought the military, administration, and economy under his thumb. His family and close friends now sit in powerful positions of government, the gentry-dominated legislature obeys his whims, and there is no one able to question his will. Except for one man.\n\nLong Yun may have started his life as a military commander, but he has proven extremely shrewd and flexible in his political alignments. But this hardly means he is uneducated or unaware of world-wide political developments, having personally encountered and interacted with the revolving door of ideologies that have come and gone in Kunming - the Qing monarchy, Xinhai Republicanism, the Kuomintang's Revolutionary Nationalism, Cai E's Stratocratic Militarism, Tang Jiyao's Conservative Federalism, and many more.\n\nHow he rules now, with no one but himself in his way, will determine his long term legacy. He could be like Cai E, an aloof military strongman who sagely selects ministers without the need to consult legislature and deal with partisan bickering. Or he can model himself after Tang and his attempts at courting local democrats while never letting them too far off their leash. And it is Whampoa that will decide the fate of the nation for future generations.\n\n\n\nOf course the battle is not over yet, there are many challenges that lie on the path of the National Revolution. Whilst the nation may be united against external threats and independent warlords, the Party is but merely the opposite. A Torrential Storm of Hyper-Inflation
Considering the Party's cruel circumstances, and the history of the nation, it is perhaps inevitable that the economy transformed into the malignant tumour of the present. With the Nationalist Central Government tasked with the prerogative to maintain a large army to stave off Japan's hounds, alongside similarly to bring the hammer down on warlords, and most importantly provide jobs to defeated generals to prevent revolts, the army has become the largest drain on the national treasury. Contributing significantly to the dteriorating quality of life in China, this is not necessarily a new phenomenon, for practically every government before us has suffered a similar fate, and even our own government has had such bouts.\n\nThis latest round of hyperinflation knocks the rest out of the park however, as our unification of the nation has resulted in the absorption of both explicit and implicit debts, many of which are due. Even worse, we have exhausted many options for raising revenue due to years of war and economic devastation, which leaves us with two much detested choices. Raise taxes, or continue printing currency. An attempt to check inflation by offering to exchange currency for our gold and silver reserves has caused a run on the banks, and the worsening crisis has resulted in major knock down effects across the board.\n\nAs the economy ever so gracefully collapses, unrest has risen in tandem. Traditional economisits like Kong Xiangxi have sought to clamp down on inflation by eliminating the deficit through heavy taxation and slashing the budget. Whilst most likely the most effective in the long run, the Kuomintang's long held economic beliefes about National Capitalism have resulted in major apprehension. Chen Cheng has used the hesitancy to push for his own populist measures, including public distributions and rationing of essential goods, price controls and allowing bartering to stabilise the currency. Further radicals have suggested a mass default, working through the crisis by utilising government directed work programs.

Unending Dysfunction

The Nationalist Government Falters
The Chinese nation has been reunited under a Nationalist Government, but if they hope Chinese nationalism alone will be able to hold their new state together they would be mistaken. With foreign powers driven out and rival governments destroyed, there are few external forces keeping the movement together. Meanwhile, the expectations placed on this new government - from the people, from the governors, and from foreign creditors - have begun to outpace the central government's ability to meet them.\n\nThe first major sign of strain is the economic freefall that has occurred following unification. Years of deficit spending, high military expenditures, endemic corruption and inefficiency, devastation of economic enterprises, and continued resistance to taxation have caused the debt to spiral. The takeover by the revolutionary Kuomintang has caused many productive elites and technocrats to flee, while foreign creditors have begun to demand payment immediately. The central government lacks the monetary reserves to stabilise the currency, causing inflation to skyrocket.\n\nThe failing economy has had nation-wide ramifications. Unrest has begun to escalate as the people protest increased taxation in peacetime. The possibility of further unrest has precluded cuts to military spending on the central army. The only solution going forward in [FROM.Capital.GetCapitalVictoryPointName]'s eyes is for [FROM.GetLeader] to centralise power, slashing provincial subsidies and disbanding warlord armies. All of this has created a unique moment of vulnerability, one that other players may seek to exploit.

Return of the Baojia System
The Kuomintang might have a revolutionary heritage, but their shift towards a more conservative, status-quo defending force has been palpable. Many Whampoa officers, including senior Generals have identified themselves with men like Zeng Guofan and Hu Linyi, provincial Governors who worked under the dying Great Qing to fight off the Taiping Rebellion.\n\nAccording to the new party line, Zeng and Hu embodied the military professionalism and discipline of a proper officer, while retaining the scholar-gentlemen background expected of the proper Chinese elite (a military career traditionally is looked down upon in Chinese culture, esteemed commanders are expected to be scholar-bureaucrats first and soldiers second). The reverence of Kuomintang officials for these figures is an ironic, if perhaps understandable, turn for a party that at one point identified with the southern originating Taiping rebellion.\n\nIndeed, in their quest to restore order in a local area and revive party morale while fighting socialist insurgents and warlord rebels, the Kuomintang have officially embraced the principles supposedly set forth by Zeng and Hu over a century ago. Catholicism and the Republic of China
In the 1930s, during the nadir of the Kuomintang's existence, He Yingqin converted to Catholicism. The General had grown up in a changing Guizhou, where the French influenced southwest saw many Catholic missionaries convert prominent local families, including Wang Wenxiang (his wife) and her family. In such a troubling time, he found comfort in the faith (albeit reluctant to share any specific details about his motivations), including its emphasis on familiar concepts such as self-discipline, social justice, and personal cultivation, and continues to practise as President.\n\nBeing head of state as a religious minority is a balancing act for President He. A private, stoic man by nature he is able to carry on with dignity in public though is careful not to appear to show excessive favouritism for a religion many Chinese nationalists view as foreign imposed and foreign controlled.\n\nHe, and many other Chinese Catholics, retains an affinity for Chinese tradition. Fortunately for the nation's most visible practitioner, recent religious and political developments have helped smoothen some of the old divides between the two worlds. The Sacred Congregation for the Propagation of the Faith has declared Chinese customs related to honouring their ancestors not necessarily superstitious in nature and permitted (with certain caveats), thus resolving the so-called \
A man of faith.
A New Premier
Dai Jitao, the revolutionary sage, is retiring. Having spent the last few decades in service of the revolution as Dr. Sun's translator, a Shanghai underground cell leader, a propagandist and writer, a party luminary, and for the past few years as Premier of the Executive Yuan, Dai has elected to close his storied career with a dignified retirement now that the nation is reunified. His relationship with the President, though odd and sometimes troubled, has proven to be a fruitful one - together they have built a new Republic.\n\nThough there is some speculation about how voluntary Dai's retirement is and what he will be doing next, the departing Premier has reassured assembled party members in his retirement party that he will likely remain in the public sphere to help continue to guide the new Republic from the sidelines in its formative days. He has mused spending his final years either in education or in one of the less taxing positions in the government like the Control or Examination Yuans.\n\nHe has not been shy about his opinions on his successor. Dai Jitao has castigated Yang Yongtai as a two-faced sellout to capitalist and reactionary interests, frequently making snippy comments both in public and private about his treacherous dealings. He has all but confirmed that Yang is responsible for his semi-forced retirement, and though there is little he can do to stop him, that has not stopped him from railing against him on the way out.

He has mused spending his final years either in education or in one of the less taxing positions in the government like the Control or Examination Yuans.\n\nDai has been circumspect with regards to his successor, speaking about him respectfully as the occasion demands. He has expressed hope that Chen will be able to find common ground with his protégés in the unrelated Chen brothers, though tensions have unfortunately already surfaced.

Whampoa's Revolution
The National Revolutionary Army was created to be the Army of the Revolution, and the Whampoa Military Academy was established to train the armed cadres of the vanguard party. The men who gathered on those shores, though some may not have known it at the time, were about to do something special. They did not just build a loyal, dedicated liberation force for the people, but also united the hearts and souls of patriotic young men together to build bonds that will last a lifetime.\n\nThe circle of men who met as instructors at Whampoa came from around the country. The future President He Yingqin came from Guizhou, then Headmaster Chiang Kai-shek and leading Political and Organisation department officers like Dai Jitao and Chen Qimei came from Zhejiang, and the Eternal Premier of the Party Sun Yat-sen came from Guangdong. But the utter commitment to rebuilding the nation, encapsulated in the Three Principles of Nationalism, Democracy, and Welfare, is something that transcends provincial boundaries.\n\nThe soldiers with their rifles and the commissars with their words worked in tandem, in a way not seen anywhere else in the world. They fought through death and suffered through defeat and exile, but never stopped until their ordained mission was complete. Hand in hand, the army and the party freed their nation from the warlords, the imperialists, and the forces of corruption that plagued their beloved homes.\n\nThis spirit of revolutionary fraternity has lit a torch for all of China to follow and the soul of Whampoa will go down in Asian history as a beacon of hope for all the oppressed people of the East. The partnership between the Whampoa Clique and the CC Clique is expected to continue into the next age of Chinese history, guarding and realising the lofty promises made and restoring the nation's character from within.
Brothers in arms, comrades for the future.
The General and the Genro
The Kuomintang after the Reorganisation of 1924 burned with revolutionary passion, aiming to overthrow the Beiyang Republic and establish a true republican government which would shepard the masses and the state towards a new, equitable and prosperous future without imperialist vultures.\n\nSadly, the Kuomintang would collapse during the Northern Expedition, with the most faithful National Revolutionaries taking a patriotic retreat to Guizhou province. Under He Yingqin's grandiose leadership, the Kuomintang has not only repudiated the Beiyang warlords, but freed all of China from the clutches of imperialist oppression. However, with the task of National unification having been completed, the process of building a new China has become the new paramount task of the KMT.\n\nGeneralissimo He Yingqin understands that in order to build up a new China that can reach common prosperity for the people, the Party must adapt to the new responsibility. The old days of ultra nationalist revolutionaries and fiery rhetoric can no longer serve our Party's goals in carving the path for China's future, and the party must now reform itself into a respectable organisation.\n\nWith this goal in mind, He Yingqin has allowed western-educated scholars, elder statesmen, and technocrats who all seek a modern China to take part in his government. While He Yingqin trusts Zhang Qun and Yang Yongtai to lead these political independents to chart a democratic and modern future for the nation, the Generalissimo and the Whampoa Clique will ensure that this New China maintains order and stability, and warlords be kept at bay.\n\nIn this, many have compared the new government to that of the Meiji Restoration, where Emperor Meiji surrounded himself with technocrats, modernisers, and intellectuals who helped him build Japan into the first modern nation in Asia. Indeed, Zhang Qun and He Yingqin found much inspiration in the Japanese system due to their years of studying in Japanese academies.\n\nWhile many expect this new democratic China to be dominated by the Kuomintang for decades to come, the people of China are hopeful for a good modern future, where the decades of backwardness and chaos will be a forgotten memory. The era of revolution has passed, and a new age of freedom and modernity has arrived at China's doorstep.
Revolutionary by method, moderate by aims...
Tutelage of Action
For as long as the Kuomintang has persisted as a revolutionary force of the Chinese People's will the Party has always endeavoured to liberate China's lower classes. Naturally such sentiment resulted in quite controversial agreements, and the allure, or infiltration, of socialism within many of the party's membership. Utterly shattered in the late 1920s, the Party practically split itself at the seams. With two rival Central Executive Committees and two rival remnant groups fighting parallel struggles for the National Revolution. Those who bridged the divide were commonly ridiculed by either side, and yet here remains such a fallacy.\n\nStaring out onto the rolling streets and urban maze of [ROOT.Capital.GetCapitalVictoryPointName] from his office, Generalissimo He could only breathe sighs of restrained relief. Consolidating the Future
Marching ever forward on the path of progress for the Chinese nation, Generalissimo Chen has sought he establishment of the Sanmin Zhuyi Youth Corps, or the Three People's Principles Youth League. Intended to indoctrinate a new generation of young men with the Party's philosophy, and rejuvenate the ailing, aging and corrupt Party, the Youth League hopes to produce a new era for the Kuomintang in the future. One that will willingly allow the Old Guard Party membership to pass on the torch to a more vigorous and spartan body. Inspired by the victory parades, jubilees and bugles following the nigh civlisation ending conflict with the Rising Sun, the League has taken on a passionate patriotic spin. With many of the educated youth willingly joining the League, a series of youth halls and guest houses have been established all across the country to promote further enlistment.\n\nHosting summer camps, sports games and other recreational activities, the League has also aided in creating battlefield service and propaganda teams to be utilised by the party in future endeavours. With its influence reaching into even the education system, the League has also taken upon itself another annal of political consolidation. Promoting Chen Cheng's achievements both in the field and in administration, the Youth League has reached a breaking point of expansion.\n\nLimited without outside assistance, some members within the Central Reorganisation Committee are suggesting that the Generalissimo tap into the shattered Central Club's networks within the nation, utilisng the Chen brothers and their extensive ties to civilian bureaucracy to see a rapid expansion of the League. Love Born from Hard Times
Chiang Kai-shek's impact on the trajectory of Chen Cheng's life was powerful, permanent, and brief. Cut short by the former's death at the hands of disgruntled soldiers, or perhaps citizens, during the Northern Expedition, Chen's tenure as the Generalissimo's protégé never truly developed into its true potential. Nonetheless, some semblance of his second mentor's philosophy and actions still remain within the Little Generalissimo's life. The tenets of Whampoa, or perhaps Chen's spartan lifestyle. Most vividly however, is his arranged marriage to one Tan Xiang, daughter of the eponymous Tan Yankai.\n\nBeginning originally with Dr. Sun's attempt to arrange the marriage between Song Meiling and Tan, that attempt ultimately fell short as Tan was forever married to his first wife, even in passing. Instead, Tan opted to become Song's godfather, and Sun himself instead endeavoured to arrange the marriage of Chiang to Song. With Sun's death and all other family members except Song Ailing opposing Chiang's marriage, it would only be saved by Tan's intervention. Following the Northern Expedition, Tan would find himself as a weaker faction within the remnant Guizhou, hoping to strengthen his position, Tan would conspire with Meiling to see Chen Cheng and Tan Xiang marry in 1931.\n\nHaving been previously married, many considered it a mere political move on Chen Cheng's end. And yet no matter how much influence Song, Tan or Chiang had on their marriage, Chen and Tan Xiang have proven themselves to be a very harmonious couple. Despite their wedding being held in Kuomintang Guizhou, and despite all the trials and tribulations the two have endured since then, they have endured in their love for eachother. Taking up a position next to her husband, Tan Xiang has skyrocketed from merely trying her best to provide relief for the wounded during the Second Sino-Japanese War to the First Lady of China. Similarly a devout feminist in her political work, Tan Xiang has become vice-president of the Chinese Nurses Association, Girl Scouts, and various other institutions.

To Undo Millenia of Injustice
Contemporary studies of Chinese history for the last 2000 years show recurring patterns of war and peace. Many sources can be attributed to such periods, at least in their individual contexts, but perhaps the one shared quality, is that of the dynasty and thereby the nation's inability to maintain a proper balance between land and population for any length of time. Shadowing a period of resounding progress and advancement, the population and wealth of the nation would undoubtedly increase with it.\n\nReaching a critical conflict point where land was insufficient, violence would be the immediate answer to the people's qualms. Uprisings, civil conflict, as if it was the scientific application of misery to correct China's balance. Lighting the Flame of China's Youth

And so follows the establishment of the National Military Education Movement.\n\nTo transform the education system, one must first militarise it so that the teachers and students of the future will train and study to be fighters in an ultimate conflict. Schools are to be organised akin to military regiments, with taechers, faculty and school administrators pressed into compulsory military training, and forced into the frugal, spartan and disciplined life of the army.\n\nIn regards towards the students themselves, indoctrination shall start in nursery, with children granted guns and warships as toys, with pictures of battle placedu pon the walls. In adolescence they shall be enrolled into boy scout groups with a strong military backbone, and special emphasis shall be situated upon physical training and group sports competitions. Finally in their teenage years they shall start formal military training.\n\nWithin the curriculum itself, physics, chemistry and engineering shall all be oriented towards their wartime applications. Poison gas, explosives, nothing is off limits. Reviving Chinese Culture
Throughout the tumultuous development of the Chinese nation-state its national culture and character has similarly experienced calamity, reconstruction, and the turning tides of history. And yet that has brought forth nothing but ruin, malaise, corruption, and misfortune upon its people. The Chinese societal outlook of the present era encompasses two extremes on a continuum. Rather than be animated by sentiments of patriotism or nationalism, the Chinese are motivated by individualism, familial ties, and clan connections on one hand, and by utopian ideals of cosmopolitanism decadence on the other. Suffice to say, neither extreme is conductive towards the development of true nationalism.\n\nTraditionalist autocracy of the family and clan elders itself has become institutionalised as it serves the interests of maintaing Feudal rule within the nation. And to their benefit, the Brotherhood of Man, or Datong, from Confucianism has made the weak intellectuals of the nation too attached to such concepts. Even worse, the Chinese have borrowed materialism and individualism from the West. They have all but utterly replaced self-realisation with individual rights and freedoms, with material pleasures being their primary concern.\n\nAnd so the burden falls upon the Party and the Blueshirts to cultivate a new Chinese Culture. For cultural control is the crux of social, political, educational, and indeed all, activity within the nation. It is the very soul of national restoration. It has but a mystical quality that causese everyone unknowingly to be under its control. And it is the task of the Blueshirts to harness this power.
Demolish the decadence of the West!
Focus on establishing a culture of control for the masses.

The New Right Awakens
Once, the Kuomintang was the party of the Chinese Revolution. As the beloved eternal premier desperately tried to secure support for the dying flames of revolution in 1920, it was the Zhejianese elements of the party with Chiang Kai-shek, Dai Jitao, Chen Guofu, and Zhang Renjie who continued the revolutionary work desperately in the form of running a stock enterprise to raise funds for the revolution's cause. Let it be remembered that as the party was infiltrated by counter-revolutionaries and social-imperialist agents of the Third Internationale, that it was Dai and his followers who urged the Kuomintang to return to her true native roots as a Chinese Nationalist Party, a party grounded in the moral ethos of China.\n\nThe country lies devastated from years of internal civil strife, foreign imperialism, and the destruction of the Second Sino-Japanese War but the ambitious so-called \

The Opposition Grows
Despite the eternal premier's instance on a tutelage-led period of revolutionary reform, even since the early days of the Xinhai Revolution before pessimism pervaded the political thought of the Kuomintang; Dai Jitao himself believed that republicanism lied within the rule of law. As the Kuomintang organisation grew and grew and with it more and more degenerative elements entered the party, Dai continued to insist on maintaining the party's nominal separation of powers even if he is able to forsake individual liberties being sacrificed on behalf of public order.\n\nSince the take over of the New Right, the Party has once more found itself in a convoluted mess as reactionary elements have entered the government with a lack of qualified personnel within the former Kuomintang elements within Yunnan. Liberal opportunists, members of the budding opposition within the China Democratic League, and criticism from the Old Right, warlord generals, and the Left continue to voice their concerns and opposition towards President Dai's regime.\n\nTo his close confidents, one of his preliminary steps towards solidifying the new regime will be to purge party factionalism, which Dai sees has an ever growing tree of disunity, watered by corruption and national weakness. With the secure support of the Central Committee, an executive order has been passed forcing party members who had either drifted or distanced themselves from the KMT in 1911, to re-register with strict examinations on their character, party policy, and Sun's theories. New members are henceforth to be placed under similar scrutiny with only provisional membership being granted in the meantime.
The party will be purged of opportunists, bandits, and militarists!

The Bitter Brass
During the Right's long exile in Yunnan, party leaders Dai Jitao and Chen Guofu have always managed to play an amicable role in the complex relationship between the military and the civilian wings of the Kuomintang. The Chinese Revolution after all, is one that was determined by military successes, but in accordance with the principles laid out by Dr. Sun - the time for military rule was to come to an end with a period of political led tutelage. Having long been allies with Whampoa's top brass, the leaders of the New Right are now experiencing a strained relationship over the military.\n\nHe Yingqin's resignation from politics has prompted unrest and anger among military personnel who are finding it hard to accept civilian dominance over military affairs. Dai's ambitious plans for reforms are rumored to cut down on military spending, disband large portions of the military (especially mercenaries), and prepare spending for large economic projects.\n\nFurthermore, there are also those within the Whampoa Clique who are outright uneasy with the Generalissmo's retirement from politics, finding themselves increasingly sidelined in the face of political oppurtunism from those within the CC Clique. In their vacuum, the Civil Engineering Clique and the Blue Shirts Society have seemingly taken over the influence of the military, a cautionary warning to the civilian administration of the KMT to not neglect the interests of the National Revolutionary Army.
The party must tighten its grip on all matters, including the military.
Whampoa has always defended the party, and will continue to do so.
Sometimes it is best to know our place and avoid trouble.

The Land Reform Proposal
As part of the Fundamentals of National Reconstruction from the late Dr. Sun Yat-sen, land reform remains one of the Kuomintang's most populist measures—a platform from which the Party derives its national legitimacy as a true revolutionary party to liberate the oppressed masses towards the glories of national salvation. It's no secret that the land reform issue remains contentious, as both the Left and Right wings of the Party have splintered along ideological lines regarding its implementation.\n\nWith the New Right's hold over the party line secured, the Party now draws its ears to its two main idealogues Dai Jitao and Chen Lifu in regards to land reform. A National Land Reform Department has been created by the Executive Committee of the Reconstruction Commission of the National Government, spearheaded by the politicians Zhang Lisheng and Zhang Daopan, who have pledged to the people that the Party will fulfill the tedious task of national land reform.\n\nBelieving that the country's future lies in its rapid industrialisation, Dai Jitao has opined that the Party should instead focus on investments to the industrialisation of the country's coastal developed areas which would spur economic development in the countryside. His policies, which have been applauded by members of the gentry and the landowning class, insist on maintaining provinical land autonomy rather than government intervention.\n\nAs Secretary General of the Executive Committee of the Reconstruction Commission, Chen Lifu has voiced for the need for extensive land reform under careful government supervision. Unlike Dai's lassiez-faire approach, Chen has argued that the government ought to take the initiative and to undertake the arduous task through the careful lenses of government bureaucracy in order to minimise corruption and to improve government efficacy in domestic affairs.
Dai is right, we must focus on industrialising the country first.
Chen Lifu's proposals for expansion are correct.
New Culture
In the years following the success of the Xinhai Revolution, a vibrant wave of new thoughts, philosophies, and ideas arrived from the nations of the West into China. Philosophers and intellectuals like Chen Duxiu and Hu Shih hailed the New Culture Movement as an escapade from the brutal world of warlordism. Western culture was hailed as a corrective source for Chinese culture's now established passivity and irrationalism. Conservative thinkers like Liang Shuming disagreed, declaring that western philosophies were incompatible with Chinese culture, a view supported by Zhang Junmai.\n\nWhile he initially agreed with the attempts to revolutionise national culture, Dr. Sun Yat-sen ultimately found the movement to be unpatriotic. He proclaimed that the Kuomintang was the keeper of Chinese morality and the nation, and his followers like Dai Jitao soon followed suit. This became the foundation of the New Life Movement, a Neo-Confucian counter-attack. Preaching that a new age was coming, New Life thinkers invoked Confucian ideas of collective life (gongsheng) and the utopian vision of world unity (datong). A revitalised moral community and human performativity would produce this coming epoch of human civilisation.\n\nChen Lifu is a late comer to the discussion, trained to be a flexible propagandist for the party. Accusing everything not grounded in Confucian moral teachings as Syndicalist, his lectures at the Central Political Institute combined western vitalist philosophy, traditional Chinese cosmology drawn from Yijing (the Book of Changes), Daxue (Great Learning), and Zhongyong (Doctrine of the Mean), and the teachings of Dr. Sun and Dai Jitao. While many within the Party see the New Culture Movement as ultimately a necessary component of the Chinese National Revolution, there are certainly those such as the followers of Dai Jitao who believe that the country ought to revert to its Confucian path. A growing number of the young guards within the party's intelligentsia offer that Confucianism and the concept of Vitalism should be incorporated to fuse both Western and Chinese philosophies to create a true hybrid revolutionary culture.
A revolution against old culture is needed!
The country seeks New Life not New Culture.
A hybrid revolutionary culture is what will save China.

A Chinese Hollywood
Inspired by the grandiose pictures held in Paris to commemorate the socialist revolutionary cause or the burgeoning Russian film industry that glamorises the spectacular nature of the SZRS, the domineering figureheads of the CC Clique, Chen Lifu and Guofu have mentioned the Kuomintang's necessity to reach the masses through modern media. The culture and the arts argues the elder Chen, ought to showcase the beauty and brilliance of Chinese civilisation, while also upholding the Party's duty to instruct and guide the masses into the Three Principles of the People.\n\nWhile films and the arts of a political nature have traditionally been dominated by the political left, the dominance of the New Right has led to virtually every leftist filmmaker, actor, or actress being censored under new careful party scrutiny. Under the popular playwright Zhang Daopan, the Chairman of the Chinese Artist Association, a new National Drama School has been created in Nanjing to popularise Chinese opera and classical music as a contrast to the degenerative Western music that flourishes in the Legation Cities. As new film makers such as Zhu Shilin are leading the rebirth of Chinese cinema following the mass censure of left-wing artists, the Chen brothers themselves have organised the Association for Film Education to give annual prizes to the three most outstanding films based on their education content and dedication to national patriotism.
Let's go to the movies!
Decree on Law and the Humanities
As part of National Reconstruction, one of the fundamental goals of the national revolution was to revitalise education in China, to expand education efforts beyond just mere studies of Western modernity, but also to foster a new generation of revolutionary youth. The spirit of educational modernity was one of the first major steps of reform even before the Kuomintang, educators like Cai Yuanpei endeavored to birth a new generation of revolutionary-minded intellectuals dedicated to the revitalisation of China.\n\nPremier Chen Guofu has proposed however, a controversial law to be passed which would reduce funding and close down the law and humanities departments within Chinese universities for the next ten years. During this decade, the government would then set up quotas for the number of graduates needed in agriculture, engineering, and medicine - ultimately arguing that the number of students in the technical fields ought to be greatly expanded over those in the legal fields.\n\nThe move has attracted considerable controversy within the Chinese academic world and even within the CC Clique. Many CC Clique members, such as Zhu Guangqian, have intellectual and educational backgrounds. Deng Xipeng, the first President of the Student Federation of the Republic of China and head of the history department at Sun Yat-sen University, the historian Luo Jialun, and the Chairman of the Chinese Artists Association Zhang Daopan have joined in on this appeal. Zhu Guangqian in particular has even gone as far as to say that the Party ought not to interfere with civil education.\n\nOf course, the irony is that those within the CC Clique do adhere to an idealistic form of Chinese traditional history as well as the legalistic principles that guided the first Qin Empire. And of course, there is the question, that if there are no lawyers to protect and interpret the laws for the citizens; and if there are no historians to protect and teach history to the people - then who really knows the truth in the country?
Chen pushes through - the decree is passed.
Maybe let's put this on the backburner for the time being.
They are right, the party ought not to interfere.
Revolutionary Wisdom and Revolutionary Works
Over the years, many have claimed to have understood the complicated dialectic work of the late Eternal Premier - yet none of them have been able to reach the genius and understanding of the Three Principles of the People than Dai Jitao. Certainly the ideology of the party has been diluted with the debacle of warlordist military officers and Marxist-oriented counter-revolutionaries. But at last, we may have achieved a true understanding of the National Revolution; for it is only under President Dai who may fitfully call himself the Apostle of the Cultural Revolution.\n\nRecently published to be intended as both party doctrine and his memoirs, the President has recently finished his magnum opus: \

Religions and the Task of National Salvation
As a great country with a variety of faiths stretching from not just native Chinese folk religions but also Islam, Christianity, and Buddhism (which held foreign origins but have been adopted in various sects and faiths throughout China), the question of religion has inevitably arisen with the dominance of the New Right. While many of the party's original members including Sun Yat-sen and Chiang Kai-shek were expressed members of the Christian faith, the question of religion has generally been avoided for the sake of national unity.\n\nPresident Dai, a devout Buddhist since an early life-changing conversion, has voiced his support for a nation grounded on virtue, chivalry, and morals. He argues that while religion cannot be accepted uncritically, nor opposed, nor dismissed out of hand, the only course remaining is to work toward reforming it so that it might unify the Chinese people. His personal journey with Buddhism, from his early conversion to his current leadership, connects his beliefs and values with those of the nation, inspiring a new wave of moral leadership.\n\nTo place a greater emphasis on religious observance, the Party has officially commenced Buddhist holidays to be observed with the same rigor as the holidays of October 10th (to commmemorate the Wuchang Uprising), the birth and death date of the eternal premier Dr. Sun Yat-sen, and the death of the 72 martyrs in Guangzhou. School assemblies and public gatherings are to be frequented by monks and religious speakers, with the goal of promoting a morally upright education for the future youth of China.\n\nFurthermore, the Ministry of Monoglian and Tibeaten Affairs have officially promoted the Tibeaten faith of Tibeaten Mahayana Buddhism which is observed by those in Tibet, Mongolia, and Bhutan. While Chinese remains of course the official language and script to be used in public, the Ministry has officially stated that Tibeaten can be used in religious practices and that the study of the tantric texts should be promoted and protected under national law.
Muslim, Buddhist, Christian, we seek a true moral and honest nation.
A Buddhist Nationalism
Since its entry into Chinese popular religions in the Tang Dynasty, the veneration of the Buddha has undergone both renaissance and fall in China. Ever since the anti-Manchu Taiping Rebellion, Buddhism has undergone a steady decline - seen by many Han nationalists as foreign and un-Chinese. Yet the teachings of the Buddha still remain popular amongst Chinese intelligentsia, with some proposing that Buddhism retain its strength and hegemony in cultural circles rather than in the political arena.\n\nFor President Dai who claimed to have been saved by the Buddha during a failed suicide attempt, Buddhism may very well be the key to national salvation in the form of \
Towards eternal nirvana we strive.
A Radiant Future
Since his ascension to the presidency of the Republic of China, Chen Lifu has often spent many long hours in his office, dreaming and scheming of the days to come. At long last, the revolution that the two Chen brothers and their mentor had worked for had finally reached a stage of completion.\n\nAs he muses for the future of China, he pens in his journal - his firm conviction that the new Chinese state will supersede any nation that may have come before it. The new state has both the custodial responsibility of all of its citizens and the technological know-how to create a truly modern yet moral society. By combining the moral virtues of the East and the technological ways of the West, it was indeed possible to engineer and facilitate the revival of the Chinese race and civilisation.\n\nHe smiles as he pens his remaining thoughts, that this new China will be what he calls as the Radiant Future. A China organised from province to province, district to district, village to village in the form of moral communities. The people will share together a common duty to serve the state and their families, held together by the comforts of love and sanctuary - with all moral ailments and vices cast away in favor of a new, righteous, evolved class of individuals who will exemplify the revolutionary consciousness. Together, they shall strive not just for the continuation of the Nationalist Revolution but also towards a world of shared values and purposes...
May the beams of the White Sun shine forevermore.
A Grand Retirement
Rumours that Dai Jitao has been preparing to retire has been swirling around [ROOT.Capital.GetCapitalVictoryPointName] for several months now, something that was finally confirmed a few weeks ago when President Dai submitted his resignation to the Central Executive Committee.\n\nFurther speculation about his successor abounded as the CEC met behind closed doors. The leading candidates were Chen Lifu, Chen Bulei, and some whispered about the possibility of a military candidate. In the end however, the message behind Dai Jitao's choice of successor was unmistakable - his young, fiery protégé Chen Lifu would become President. About a decade younger but already boasting an accomplished resume working with other leading figures such as Chiang Kai-shek, Hu Hanmin, and Sun Fo, Dai's choice indicates his desire to pass the reins onto the next generation, the one that fought during the Northern Expedition rather than in Xinhai. His choice also solidifies the civilian leadership of the party, ending a long rein of military dominance on the right.\n\nIn support of his brother's candidacy, Chen Guofu has stepped down as Premier to avoid the appearance of nepotism. Chen Bulei (no relation), an intellectual close to Dai, is expected to be appointed in his place as a steady hand behind the Executive Yuan. Chen's ascent is expected to herald in an age of more collective leadership rather than the party strong-man style that has existed since Dr. Sun, but it would be unwise to discount the numerous ideas the young man has of his own.\n\nA grand ceremony has been thrown in Dai and Chen's honour, even as the former joked during his toast that he is not going away anytime soon. Indeed, both are expected to retain their positions on the CEC even as Dai intends to mostly retire from public life, musing either spending more time in religious studies or taking up a teaching position at Sun Yat-sen University.
Three Cheers to the Two Chens!
We Will Miss You President Dai!
The Chief Task of Human Evolution
In writing his own theories on philosophy, Chen Lifu has created his very own intellectual orientation, his doctrine of vitalism. To introduce the complicated dialectic, Chen first introduces the concept that the goal of humanity, is to dominate the universe and nature - all things natural fall into a natural hierarchy of spiritual and material qualities.\n\nHe rejects Henri Bergson's claim that choice was crticial to progress, arguing that humanity cannot handle freedom- excessive freedom promotes social conflict, and man ought to focus on efficency. Vitalism is the solution to excess consumption and freedom. Vitalism comes from Chen's own study of traditional Chinese medicine and western philosophy, arguing that vitalist elements make up all matter. These elements are the tiniest, most fundamental things in the universe but they constitute parts of everything, in his own pretension to proposing scientific theory - Chen has redefined the atom and matter in a manner akin to Hans Driesch's theory of entelechy. If that is the case, then the party which is made up of people, carries vitalism - vitalism thus must be strengthened within party members in order to improve natural human efficency.\n\nIt is with this idea, that he has even gone as far as to say that it might be beneficial for those who don't contribute to society to die as they merely just consume resources. To Chen, it is those who are worthy, who carry these compoments of strong vitalism - that are the natural predecessors towards the final evolved form of humanity.
Is this our species' destiny?
Revival of Chinese Medicine
Traditional Chinese medicine (zhongyi) is an alternative medicine practice drawn from traditional medicine in China. The practices of traditional Chinese medicine include health and healing practices, Confucian philosophy, diet, exercise, acupuncture, and various schools of thought. These forms of holistic healing have been long practiced by the Chinese, with some of the earliest works on Eastern therapeutic activities dating back to the Shang Dynasty (14th-11th centuries BCE).\n\nTraditional medicine rejects Western practices like injections, surgery, and pharmaceutical drugs; rather, it relies on concepts invoking circulation channels, one's \

And yet this is hardly a moment of triumph.\n\nAll around the Party and its cadres, one can behold the devastation wrought upon the nation. A New Party for a New Era
Initially founded by Tan Pingshan and Zhang Bojun within Shanghai, the Provisonal Action Committee's original aims were that of rivalling the Kuomintang. For it had become evidently clear following the Northern Expedition's collapse that the Revolutionary Party of Dr. Sun could not bring the people to their salvation, or institute Chinese socialism. For Tan and Zhang, Provisional Action was a new start for a new revolutionary movement, one independent of the machinations of men like Chiang Kai-shek.\n\nYet with the arrival of Deng Yanda and Song Qingling to Shanghai following their own escape from the encroaching Warlord forces, the movement became co-opted by the dynamic duo. Instead of projection from outside the Kuomintang, the Provisional Action Committee would instead steer towards reforming the KMT from within.\n\nNow with President Zhang at the head of the nation, it is ultimately up to him, and Marshal Zhu, as to the character of their rule. For there is no position for a Red Napoleon to coup their movement no longer. Presently there exists two options for the President and Premier. Follow through with our blood oath to the White Sun, Blue Sky and Wholly Red Earth, remaining within the Kuomintang movement and adopting the nomenclature of the Revolutionary Committee of the Chinese Kuomintang.\n\nOr embarking on our own path, forming a new party, one built for the Chinese Peasants and Worker's. Kuomintang Outsiders at the Centre
Despite our triumphant advance, in spite of our resolute promises, and in spite of our convictions, the nation and Party remains apprehensive to adhere to the vows of what they view as nothing more than outsider upstarts that were graced with Lady Luck. Having successfully vanquished the Central Committee and saved the nation from being sent into a damning existence ruled by revolutionary tyranny, the [ROOT.GetRulingParty] has instead found itself thrust into a political blackhole, acting as its centre.\n\nFar from the old political establishment and lacking in the national credentials expected for their current stature, the periphery factions of the Kuomintang have immersed themselves in perhaps their greatest trial yet. For the military campaign was only half the battle, and constituting a political front whilst meeting the ever necessary demands of Party and populace is undoubtedly an uphill battle. One that must be met head on if they are to survive.

Competing Economic Demands
Marching out from Kunming, the provincial Kuomintang has only now fully come to terms with the broad spectrum of ideologies within the nation. Whilst Yunnan was perhaps a microcosm, easily managed, at least comparatively, the full nation finds itself in a fury and uproar. Let alone on the political front, the economic expectations of the new Kuomintang government are widely varied and far reaching.\n\nNecessitating different expectations from [ROOT.GetLeader] and the government, the peasants have begun to demand for immediate land reform, the workers petition for increased rights and protections, and to counter-act this the businessmen and gentry rally to protect their own interests and the stability of the Chinese markets.\n\nEven further, the Party itself is split upon its own economic policy. For much of the orthodox petition for the close following of National Capitalism as prescribed by Dr. Sun, whilst other liberals within the Party insist on a more western style form of economy, aiming for economic growth first and foremost. Whatever the case may be, the antidote to the nation's economic woes must be found soon...

Power vacuum in the Provinces
Due to the circumstances of the Warlord Era, the provincial party could never truly establish much more than limited or ad-hoc administrations within the various Chinese provinces as it all came under [ROOT.GetLeader]'s control. Due to this disparity, the lands that have endured a longer administration under the Kuomintang, such as Yunnan and Guizhou, enjoy a certain level of comparative stability and peace. However far flung provinces or Central Committee strongholds such as Hubei, Jiangsu, Guangdong and Beijing all sport a light level of practical anarchy.\n\nWith China's resumed conflict effectively tearing apart any form of regional political administration, the various military forces that put down arms and surrendered under the guise of protecting their provincial control remain either unwilling, apprehensive, or hostile to listening to a periphery faction of the Party that many view as just another upstart. Or even worse, as Yunnan Provincials and outcasts asserting total dominance upon the nation.

The New Kuomintang's Land Reform
Land reform is one of the most pressing and most divisive issues in contemporary China, with considerable social, economic, and political implications. The Kuomintang's rural agenda is anchored in Sun Yat-sen's Land-To-The-Tiller proposals, which have become an integral part of the party's National Reconstruction Agenda.\n\nIn this more orthodox approach to the land reform, most Kuomintang theoreticians call primarily for a land rent cap to eliminate extortionate rents by landlords, which often are demanded at full value even in bad harvests. These contracts are often undocumented and enforced by gentry-hired militias, and cause many peasants to fall deeper into debt. The party has advocated for greatest government oversight and regulation for rural contracts. There are also two other major legs to the Kuomintang land reform programme.\n\nThe first is streamlining taxation by the government, which has grown increasingly burdensome and cumbersome thanks to overlapping jurisdictions, wartime needs, and blatant corruption. Conservatives have argued that land redistribution is pointless, and would rather focus on promoting economic development that can facilitate industrialisation, suggesting that we cooperate with business and agricultural leaders to encourage infrastructure construction like shared granaries and water control infrastructure. Along with greater military policing of rural areas and funding land banks, this would raise the fortunes of the countryside as a whole.\n\nThe second is compulsory purchases of large estates, targeting in particular absentee landlords. The government is then supposed to sell the land to the tenant farmers that till the land and/or promote cooperatives. Leftists in the party have advised we take it further, with uncompensated nationalisations and mandatory participation in communes.\n\nAs the unifiers of China, we are now bestowed with the authority to begin this large scale national project that may reshape Chinese society forever.
An orthodox approach to the issue is best.
We should be conservative in our reforms.
Our revolution is not complete without reform!
The United Front Work Department
Perhaps one of the most controversial moves that is on the campaign platform of the [ROOT.GetRulingParty], at least in the eyes of the mainstream Party, is that of the matter co-operation and ties with China's innumerous Dangwai, all of which have loosely coalesced around the newly established China Democratic League. Bringing forth some of China's greatest democratic minds such as Huang Yanpei, Zhang Junmai, Zhou Shizhao, Shen Junru, Luo Longji, Li Huang, Xu Qin, Liu Chongyou and numerous others, the party is perhaps the lasting legacy of Liang Qichao's work with the nation. Uniting the Young China Party, the Chinese Constitutionalist Party, the China Vocational Educattion Society, the China National Democratic Construction Association and many other minor democratic parties, they have formalised into a block of likeminded reformers, willing to engage in good faith with the government.\n\nWith President Zhang's daring quest to construct a broad United Front for a brighter future of the Chinese nation, alongside his numerous friendships with many of the CDL's leaders, there has been provisional plans to enter into a United Front not too dissimilar to that of the old United Front between the Kuomintang and the Chinese Syndicalist Party. However such a move has seen a rising simmer of discontent from within [ROOT.GetRulingParty], as the only possiblities for sister parties is that of the CSP, or the CDL.\n\nBoth naturally have their drawbacks, with the CDL distrusted by hardliners of Zhang's own party, they similarly are apprehensive of socialism in practice. On the other hand, the CSP has reached a level of estrangement with President Zhang, following the revolt against the Central Committee. Some within the President's camp however, have even begun to advise against such measures, reasoning that the [ROOT.GetRulingParty] is fully capable of building a democratic socialist future on its own terms.

Healing the PAC Rift
The Provisional Action Committee, as it stands now, was not originally intended to be a faction of the Kuomintang. No, it was intended to be a fresh start. A new party, not shackled by the failures of the Party State in its ambitions to unite the nation. Originally promulgated as the brainchild of Zhang Bojun and Tan Pingshan shortly after the Northern Expedition's collapse, the fate of the prototype PAC would ultimately be sealed by the founder's invitation to Song Qingling and Deng Yanda to the founding proceedings. Ultimately supplanting Zhang and Tan as the centre of the movement, the PAC would become the Provisional Action Committee of the Kuomintang, intent on reforming the party from within.\n\nSwiftly becoming disillusioned by Song and Deng's leadership and suspicious towards the Third Internationale, Tan would soon depart from the mainline PAC, intending to host his own regionalist branch where he would be king. Meanwhile Zhang would remain closely tied to the Deng Yanda line, ultimately following the committee until being sent to Yunnan province.\n\nIn a parallel vein of thought another internal Kuomintang opposition faction to have risen in light of Wang Jingwei's Central Committee is that of the National Revolutionary Army's 10th Army, and its leader Chen Mingshu. An amicable man who has a close friendship with President Zhang, he has been left afloat in the new Chinese political order. Generally opposing class warfare, he takes a far more reformist spin on Chinese socialism. Of course, even within Wang's own camp and in-between there were dissenters. Gu Mengyu and his Reorganisation Comrades Association-Moderates, or Sun Fo and the Reconstruction Faction.\n\nAttempting to bridge the political divide to strengthen the nation, Zhang has sought out these varied groups to ascertain their willingness to collaborate. Privy to the third parties, and more democratic than Tan or Deng and Song, Zhang also maintains the traditionally Marxist character of the PAC, at least compared to the likes of Gu, Chen or Sun. And so ultimately whomever the President brings to his side will at least provide a unique perspect for the Party and Government.
In the name of revolutionary unity, bring forward Tan's Committee.
The Chinese people are in need of an antidote, and Gu's RCA can provide it.
We cannot forget our democratic character, Chen is our man.
The Zhang Family Library
A prolific writer from a young age, President Zhang has always been in some way, shape, or form immersed in the world of literature and writing. A studious and diligent student in his youth, Zhang would travel to Wuchang Normal University in 1916, at a mere 20 years old and in practical poverty, yet would nonetheless be admitted to their English Department. Becoming fast friends with the likes of Yun Daiying, Zhang would participate in Yun's Liqun Book Club. Meeting the famous Xiao Chu'nu through Yun, Zhang would ultimately start his own reading club and student union under their guidance.\n\nBringing his love of literature wherever he went, Zhang would briefly serve as principal of Xuancheng Normal University in Anhui before embarking to Germany in 1922 to study at the University of Berlin. Garnering a degree in philosophy, Zhang would be roommates with Zhu De, and would even consider joining the Chinese Syndicalist Party.\n\nUpon his return to the nation, Zhang began to collect a wide variety of books from all walks of life. Including 277 Ming editions with 2,239 volumes, 53 Qing editions with 323 volumes, and numerous other types of literature. A prolific reader and writer, it is reported that in his downtime from governmental office, Zhang appreciates nothing more than resting within his recently constructed family library, surrounded by his tomes. Numbering nearly 10,000 volumes in total, the Zhang Family Library has become a titanic collection, truly displaying his love for the medium.\n\nEarnest, idealistic, adoring, the library is but a singular testament to Zhang's utter dedication to his craft, an energy he brings to the political field. Perhaps even such a discipline shall translate into Zhang's children, only time will tell.

Democracy for the Peasants and Workers
Ever rushing forward, ever ambitiously swinging the dial towards progress and utopia. That is the mission upon which President Zhang and Premier Zhu have dedicated their lives towards. On the opposite hand, beguiled as a leftist detractor by his compatriots on the Right. And yet in his heart he cannot fault them for their opinions. The [GetYear] Party Election
United once more as Student and Teacher, the vanguard duo of the legendary Li Genyuan and stalwart Zhu Peide managed to successfully weather all storms that crashed against their walls. Navigating opposition from radicals on the left and right, the Kuomintang regime would aid in the ever relentless stride of the National Revolution. And in the Party's darkest moment, Li and Zhu would march forward like their beloved Cai E had done decades before.\n\nDespite their triumphs it has become swiftly apparent that the Li-Zhu alliance is coming to its conclusion. With his ambitions difficult to sate, and having re-entered the spotlight with the backing of his old allies in the well-funded Political Science Clique, Li Genyuan has utilised the clique's full political machine to stake his own claim for party leadership of the Kuomintang.\n\nFor his part Zhu Peide has been rumoured to be in talks with better established factions within the party, dangling a potential Premiership to entice them to his side. Evidently, Zhu aims to push Li from power and install a more amenable candidate. Amongst his suitors are the three major titans of the old right: Sun Fo and his Reconstruction Faction, Zou Lu and Western Hills, and even that of Hu Hanmin and the Elders Clique. Enticed by a return to power, they are also suspicious of Li Genyuan and the PSC with their deviation from party orthodoxy.\n\nUnder immense pressure, Zhu has been forced into calling for an election for Chairmanship of the Party, and with it the Presidency of the Republic. Having lost his main pillar of support, Zhu has been forced to scramble for allies and a major agreement with the Old Right would grant him enough votes to consolidate support. On the flipside, Li has begun to parley with his old close friend Gu Zhongxiu, a traditional North KMT-Beiyang candidate. However, Zhang Qun has made his move pressing the young guard of the PSC to sponsor Yang Yongtai as his Premier.

The National Defence Fortification Plan
Constantly beset by outsider invaders since time immemorial, the Chinese nation has endured tragedy in a cyclical process of war upsetting the dynastic world order. This is readily apparent in the recent besetting of the Second Sino-Japanese War upon the nation. Wreaking havoc, national trauma and turmoil upon the Chinese People, none know such a travesty better than her nation's soldiers.\n\nIntending to prevent future invasions at all costs, Generalissimo Zhu Peide has drafted a plan with his greatest strategic fellows of the Third Army. Entailing a total fortification effort, the Government would finance an expansion of fort and transportation lines in preparation to both repel an invasion and transport military supplies to potential frontlines of a presumed invasion. Split into three lines of national defence, the first would span from Jinan to Baoding. The second line would run from Yanzhou to Anyang, and the Southeast would have the Xicheng line. In addition, the cities of Nanjing, Wuhan, Xuzhou, Kaifeng, Hangzhou, Wusong and other critical urban centres would receive additional urban fortifications.\n\nSecretly selecting personnel from various military and political departments alongside the military academies, with additional guidance from foreign experts, Zhu has begun to survey the terrain and draft further plans to build defensive positions in Beijing-Shanghai-Hangzhou area. Dividing the area into three defence zones, the defence system would be tailor made according to the different terrains of the zones: namely the Nanjing-Shanghai area, the Shanghai-Hangzhou area and the Nanjing area.\n\nHailed as a resounding investment for the future definite safety and protection of the nation by Zhu's allies in the NRA and Government, there have been concerns raised by the Political Science Clique. While they had endorsed heavy industry prioritisation prior to unification, Li Genyuan and his technocratic allies are pushing for a focus on ambitious planning for economic modernisation, rather than militarisation. While they support investments into transportations, especially railroads, they stubbornly refuse further investment into fortifications.
Forever secured from imperialistic incursions!

Rule of the Orthodox Generalissimo
An oddly reflective mist has settled itself within the nation. In stark contrast to the atmosphere of chaos, suffering and bloodshed that had commonly permeated all provinces of China for the last few decades, instead one finds a stern, and cautious, air of what is perhaps considered to be optimism. Reflecting upon his long-spanning and terribly storied career, Generalissimo Zhu Peide is perhaps the epitome of a revolutionary soldier, through and through. Embroiled in the life of a warrior at a young age, Zhu's patriotism — and revolutionary sentiment ushered by Li Genyuan — would see him fight in Xinhai as part of the Double Ninth Uprising. Forming a bond forged by blood with the likes of Zhu De, Fan Shisheng, Jin Handing, Tang Huaiyuan and numerous others from Kunming, Zhu would spill his own blood and risk his own life to transform his nation.\n\nTime and time again he answered the bell of revolution. Be it through the National Protection War, the Constitutional Protection War, the Northern Expedition and every conflict in-between. And so it would once again fall on Zhu's shoulders to rally the National Revolutionary Army in its darkest moments, and continue the Long March as planned out by the late Chiang Kai-shek. Acting as Generalissimo in the Party's most dim hour, Zhu would propel the Party once more to prominence; fulfilling the unification of the nation.\n\nPerhaps even time hasn't slowed him down one bit, as the Generalissimo has whizzed away in this period of national reconstruction attempting to fulfill his duties of statesmanship to their very best. And despite concerns about being upended by the ambitious Li Genyuan, Zhu and his ally [ROOT.GetSecondInCommand] have managed to secure a future accord for the nation. One in which the New Right and Old Right overcome their differences, one where constitutional rule is prioritised — whatever form it takes — and one where the National Revolution is fulfilled to its utmost. The nation can rest well, safeguarded by its most loyal defender.

Resurgence of Kunming's True Hero
Possibly one of the more ironic twists of fate, it was not the revolutionary young guard that ushered in the new era of National Revolution. Nor was it the Kuomintang party core, those loyal to Dr. Sun Yat-sen to the utmost. Instead it was the one of the grandfathers of revolutionary sentiment within the Chinese nation. For it was Li Genyuan; son of the Yunnan gentry, Li Genyuan; father of the Kunming Military Academy, and Li Genyuan; rival to Cai E, that has been thrust to the Presidency of the nation. A founding Tongmenghui member, a revolutionary hero, and a shining statesman. Li's career is evidence that one's hard dedication, grit, and determination can allow anyone to persevere and endure under the most difficult circumstances.\n\nArranging for Cai E to return to Kunming in 1915, Li would participate from the very beginning in leading the charge against Yuan Shikai's maddening despotism. Briefly taking a leading role in the Southern Military Affairs Council and as Governor of Shaanxi, Li would ultimately find himself once again corralling the soldiers of the Yunnan Army. Ultimately scorned by Tang Jiyao and Li Liejun, Li would leave for Beijing to participate in the political scene there. However his influence in the south would remain, albeit short lived. With Yang Yongtai's attempted ousting of Sun Yat-sen from Guangdong alongside his protégé Gu Pinzhen's forsaken rule in Yunnan fizzling out, Li would ultimately retire following Cao Kun's bribery scandal.\n\nYet one can hardly keep an old dog down. Returning to national prominence following the Kuomintang's seizure of Yunnan, Li would immediately set out to ensure that this time his ambitions shall not be fettered. Originally a founding member of the Political Science Society, and European Research Affairs Society, alongside maintaining contact with Northern Political Science Clique members such as Gu Zhongxiu and Huang Fu, Li would find himself at a critical juncture in the life of Political Science. Ousting Zhu Peide in a maverick move of political brilliance, the Old Political Science Clique has managed to finally fulfill their duty ingrained into them as revolutionary soldiers. One thing is for certain, old soldiers never get sick of the wetwork.

Commission on Administrative Efficiency
Endeavouring to properly provide the foundations for a modern nation, the Political Science Clique has pressed forward — much to the chagrin and jeers of their Old Right contemporaries — with the true and thorough modernisation of the organs of government. Focusing primarily on the Executive Yuan and its inefficiencies, Premier Yang Yongtai has begun to draft a proposal plan to streamline the Executive Branch. Resolute in preventing such matters such as the Hu Hanmin Crisis, and the general lackadaisical management of the Executive, Yang's self-promulgated and headed Commission on Administrative Efficiency has advised the government with a 3 step plan to revolutionise and iterate upon the current system.\n\nFirstly, the Executive Yuan's bureaucracy must absolutely improve its current methods for maintaining archives and consequently simplify the procedures for handling important official documents and correspondence. Second, the government must design a new, effective system for classifying and filing public documents, as the committee points out the current structure purports a bureaucratic labyrinth of inefficient malaise. Lastly, the Executive Yuan should abolish superfluous administrative agencies in order to improve efficiency and coordination.\n\nFollowing on, Yang posited that the National Economic Council should be extended to accommodate river conservation, all of the various agencies dealing with agricultural reforms and rural cooperatives should be abolished with their duties relinquished to the Ministry of Industry. Alongside that the Ministry of Communications should be established, and that opium elimination should be shifted from the Opium Prohibition Council to the Military Council. Finally, the Ministry of the Interior should be placed under Kong Xiangxi's supervision, with an economic yearbook produced by the Ministry of Industry annually.\n\nWith Political Science's functional dominance of the government legislature and executive, it is practically a formality for such measures to receive the Presidential greenlight, however the appointment of the controversial Kong Xiangxi and further centralisation of matters in the executive has broadcasted a minor air of disturbance in the political field.
