Triumph of Yunnan Federalism
Kunming is triumphant. The armies of Yunnan, a mighty force during the 1910s before being humbled in the following decade, have returned to their past glory in a decisive campaign across the broken nation. In doing so, they have propagated an ideology also thought defeated in the 1920s: Chinese Federalism.\n\nRallying a band of warlords, regionalists, constitutionalists, democrats, and opportunists, the Federalist Movement fought for a scholar-gentry led republic with a division of power between the traditionally powerful central government and the increasingly autonomous provinces. Dominated by southern leaders but with sympathisers in the north, the movement was able to wrest some nominal autonomy in the 1923 Cao Kun Constitution but nothing as far reaching as they hoped and the concessions they did gain were only sporadically respected.\n\nNow they are in position to implement a system of their own choosing. However, it has come at a great cost. The nation has been devastated by years of war, the military bloated and of dubious loyalty to the civilian government, and the movement itself is heavily divided. [ROOT.GetLeader] and his allies will need to navigate these challenges in the coming months.
A victory for democracy and constitutionalism in China!
The provinces have won their freedom from tyranny!
The Central Army and the Warlords
The challenges of military centralisation in warlord China are hard to overstate. Decades of increased political, economic, and military autonomy to the provinces during the late Qing dynasty, combined with another few decades of on-going civil war in the post-Xinhai era have weakened central authority and allowed various local figures to amass powerful independent armies. The provinces grew hyper-militarised as any show of weakness may result in their neighbours or national governments to invade their territory, draining their treasuries and further weakening their hold as local forces find alternative ways to raise funding.\n\nEnding this crisis is no easy feat. The nation cannot afford to maintain this many divisions in peace-time, and many units will need to be demilitarised. But this requires a degree of trust between the provinces and the central government, and failing that the central government must be powerful enough to enforce demilitarisation. In addition to the political ramifications, every unit disbanded means more young men becoming jobless, which has historically led to spikes in banditry. Past efforts at demilitarisation have triggered provincial rebellions as the central government attempts to keep their best forces intact at the expense of their allied warlords.\n\nYunnan's leaders have wrestled with this question for years, maintaining an oversized military to protect themselves from their rivals but also suffering the consequences from it. When Tang Jiyao was governor he wrote of the need for China to have a strong central government capable of fighting imperialism and arbitrating between the provinces. Zhang Taiyan on the other hand has insisted on a diplomatic solution, allowing the various provinces at least two divisions of their own. The CDL has written extensively on the need for nationalising the armies, yet often dependent on the warlords for protection.\n\nEither way, demobilisation is expected to begin in the near future, with the brunt of it being born on the warlords, leading to tensions.
The central government must nationalise warlord armies.
Demobilisation must be balanced to protect provincial rights.
Economic Tailwinds Ahead
Unification is a chance for economic prosperity as the fighting ends and reconstruction begins, but the short-term outlook is very difficult. As China transitions from a war economy, magistrates and citizens alike are forced to take stock of the losses suffered over the years. Farms, businesses, and infrastructure have been destroyed by fighting or looting. Displaced refugees are slowly returning home to find little left of their old lives, while disbanded soldiers struggle to find jobs. Law and order in some parts of the country remain broken as bandits and insurgents continue to plague certain areas. Disputes over land, water, and business rights as the nation reintegrates have emerged amid the chaos.\n\nOn a larger scale, the end of years of civil war has created difficult challenges for the new government. Debts are coming due and can no longer as easily be put off as the new government attempts to assert its legitimacy. Creditors of defeated governments, sometimes backed by foreign powers, have also attempted to force [ROOT.GetLeader] to honor their bonds. The on-going demobilisation crisis has further reduced government coffers and fears of hyperinflation have grown. Raising taxes will create unrest from the war-weary people and on-going issues with corruption have limited its effectiveness.\n\nOne can only wonder how much wealth was lost over the years not only through direct acts of destruction and foreign exploitation, but also the enterprises that never got off the ground because entrepreneurs were too scared to invest during wartime due to instability. There is no way to recover the lost growth China might have seen if the post-Xinhai situation developed differently, but now is the time for our government to chart a better course for the future.
Raising taxes and paying off our debts will restore faith in the economy.
Defaulting on Beiyang bonds and printing more money will pay our debts.

Federalism Realised?
The unification of China under the Yunnan-led Guangzhou Federalist Government has brought one sad chapter of our nation's story to a close but as a new age dawns new questions emerge. In order to win the various wars of unification, the new central government had to build a large, powerful army and a similarly powerful state apparatus - the traditional two arms of central government tyranny. Doing so has bankrupted the nation and left empty coffers as the country needs to pivot towards peacetime.\n\nAs a result, inflation and political tensions have been inflamed. Resolving this cocktail of social, economic, military, and political issues will require a complicated series of negotiations to demilitarise the nation, implement national reconstruction, and resolve lingering political questions over the role of the provinces and the central government. Many, however, doubt [UPC.GetLeader]'s sincerity and fear that the costs will be borne on the outlying provinces.

The Federalist Electoral Order
A new political order has come to the land, now dominated by the victors of China's wars of unification. The Grand Republican Alliance and the China Democratic League have become the premier political parties in the new republic, battling for control over provincial legislatures across the country. But the greatest prize, and the one that will be hardest fought, will be for control over the Presidency - and with it the valuable patronage and executive positions it controls.\n\nThe Grand Republican Alliance is seen as the older of the two (the technicalities are debatable), tracing its roots to the old Federalist movement of the 1920s with many of their leaders fighting in the original Xinhai Revolution. Though it has a more conservative, establishment support-base with the gentry and merchants, some of its leaders have had more eclectic beliefs. Its emphasis on protecting local self-rule has made it powerful, especially in the south where it was founded, however it also has made the party more dependent on the bickering personalities of its regional leaders.
$YUN.endgame.310.intro$\n\nTang Jiyao, being the long-time President, has consolidated the GRA under him. With Zhang Taiyan's illness worsening, he has announced his departure from politics. Tang represents the more conservative and authoritarian side of the GRA - ruling in the Liangist, traditional mold rather than his Western, liberal peers. His greatest challenge ahead will be to not only win the coming elections (given the circumstances, he is the favourite), but also convince the people of the elections' legitimacy.\n\nWith the CDL poised to challenge him with a surprisingly extensive election campaign of their own, Tang must thread the needle to protect his legacy. With Chen Jiongming similarly taking a step back from national politics due to poor health, he has nominated Ma Yuhang, a former Chen deputy, to be his running mate.

Intrigues of China's Democrats
The China Democratic League may have been a relatively new development, but it is the product of decades of efforts by Chinese reformers to unite various liberal and progressive intellectuals together to promote democratic reform. Liang Qichao, the mentor and prototype for many of the CDL's leading figures, attempted to form an organised political alliance as early as 1913 when the Progressive Party united all non-Tongmenghui factions together in Beiyang. More immediately however, the formation of the United National Construction League in 1929 was the first iteration of what would ultimately become the CDL.\n\nThe CDL, dominated by middle class intellectual and business circles, is as unwieldy as the scholars, bureaucrats, and bankers that dominate its leadership. The left wing of the CDL include the National Salvation Federation and various smaller regional groups often associated with socialist splinters. The liberal wing of the party includes the Crescent Moon Society, the China National Democratic Construction Association, and the China Association for Promoting Democracy. Many Liangists have gathered around the Democratic Socialist Party, which combines a reform socialist economic programme with a nationalistic, somewhat traditionalist social outlook.
$YUN.endgame.311.intro$\n\nThe CDL does benefit from a support base that is less regionally defined than the GRA, with branches in the north, east, and south where China's largest population centers are located. Opposition to Tang Jiyao's rule has coalesced around Luo Longji, who has sought to build on this momentum to win the coming elections. Attempting to shed his reputation as \
$YUN.endgame.311.intro$\n\nCun Shusheng may be the President, but in some ways he's also a dark horse. A regional intellectual leader not particularly affiliated with any of the major parties in the CDL, his national prominence mainly stems from some of his recent writings about village reform and his unexpected leadership of Yunnanese forces to unification. He has sought to broaden his base by naming Luo Longji as his running mate, adding a Jiangxi born but Northern educated celebrity to his ticket. Still, Cun's alliances with various socialist and warlord figures are bound to be scrutinised as he fights to be more than just a lucky politician from China's periphery.
The CDL takes a leftward turn.
The CDL develops around its liberal wing.
The CDL courts the Liangists.
The Other Parties Assemble
The GRA and CDL have effectively conspired to monopolise the post-unification political system, but the other parties of the republic have resolved not to go down without a fight. Gearing up for elections, political agents from parties legal and illegal have organised as they battle to retain their existing niches and try to expand to other constituencies - exploiting weaknesses in the GRA and CDL's ground games.\n\nThe [UPC_GetDCPPartyName_nocolour_long] and the Young China Party both have Liangist roots and cater to a non-liberal political tradition. Liang Qichao, the father of China's reformist tradition, bounced around the political spectrum throughout his life. His guiding principles of constitutionalism, civic nationalism, modernisation, and a balanced approach towards Western Science and Chinese traditions continue to be popular especially in Northern China. With many northerners feeling marginalised by the South's new found dominance under the Federalist system, both parties have gained momentum.\n\nBeiyang politics has traditionally eschewed Western style political parties, especially after the Yuan Shikai-Tongmenghui conflict made parties effectively synonymous with revolutionary sedition. But with the southerners demonstrating its potency, many northerners have begun experimenting with party politics. Still, the stigma remains and nostalgia for the old gentlemanly politics endures. Regional political machines continue to run politicians stubbornly holding onto their independence and catering to local interests.\n\nThe far left, shunned if not alright oppressed by authorities, continue to operate. Always the best at mass mobilisation of voters, particularly the disaffected and impoverished, they remain a force to be reckoned with. The KMT and CSP endure but splinters have emerged. Some embrace the new republic, others have vowed to fight on in vain hope for their revolution.
Without political parties, we will be swallowed up in the new order.
The north remains as stubbornly independent as always.
The Death of Zhang Taiyan
Zhang Taiyan has passed away in his home, with conflicting reports of a severe asthma attack and fever or sinus cancer. What is undisputed however is the outpouring of grief around the nation. Newspapers across China have reported his death and politicians from all over the political spectrum have expressed their condolences.\n\nPresident [UPC.GetLeader] has granted his family's request for a state funeral, though there was some initial disagreement over where he ought to be buried, delaying the process. Zhang's will instructed his children to defend the republic and avoid taking any positions in foreign-installed governments (reflecting his continued fear of foreign imperialism), but had no clear instructions of where he wanted to be buried. Some of his associates however dug up a will he wrote all the way back in 1915 while imprisoned wishing to be buried in Hangzhou next to Liu Ji, the great scholar who aided in the foundation of the Ming Dynasty. Apparently he had even purchased a nearby lot after he was released from Liu's descendants, and it is not too far away from other Chinese heroes like Zhang Huangyan and Yue Fei.\n\nZhang's 1915 will lamented that his contributions will not be remembered as fondly as Liu Ji's due to his support of Yuan Shikai. It would appear that he was wrong. His heroics during the Xinhai Revolution aside, his contributions to Chinese literature and study of classics along with the development of the Federalist republic have fundamentally shaped this new era in China. Still, it will be up to the living to manipulate how he will be remembered in the public's collective memory.
Zhang Taiyan was a great patriot and revolutionary.
Zhang Taiyan is a founding father in our federalist democracy.
Zhang Taiyan's contributions to academia shall not be forgotten.
The State and Instability
The [GetYear] Elections have been inching steadily towards its conclusion but tensions endure. While many places are expected to hold their votes in an orderly manner, conservatives are afraid of radical agitators manipulating the masses while progressives have expressed concern about the integrity of the vote.\n\nThese issues are to be expected in a young democracy. Chinese elections historically have been relatively muted affairs, generally decided by the local gentry and power-holders. To have elections this dynamic has upended decades of entrenched politics, with some incumbents genuinely at risk of being unseated by a new generation. This uncertainty however has created both an atmosphere of hope and one of fear.\n\nThe old Beiyang Republic has always revolved around strongmen and their (usually provincial) allies. The Federalists claim to be different, but many have noted that Yunnanese and other southern figures continue to hold major levers of power in the army and bureaucracy and even non-governmental institutions such as party offices and businesses. Even in the northern and eastern dominated academia and the press, those with ties to Kunming and Guangzhou have seen their careers elevated. With their influence having gone national in scope, the petty differences between Yunnan factions have been rendered irrelevant to most outsiders. Critics decry the emergence of a so-called \
The republic was better when voting was a restrained, modest affair.
Free expression of the people's will is what democracy is about.
Widely Contested Elections
China's democrats have long waited for free and fair national elections and had clung onto hope that the Federalist Movement would be able to provide them. Unfortunately however, as more and more races are called it would appear that they will have to wait for many years more.\n\nWhile many districts did hold legitimate (or reasonably legitimate) votes, many others were marred with numerous irregularities and accusations of fraud. Narrow races in many core provinces were called in favor of conservative candidates and challenges dismissed by unsympathetic election commissions. In many outlying provinces, there have been reports of voter suppression or sometimes even outright vote rigging by Central Army officers. The flagrant biases of most provincial establishments towards the status quo have overwhelmingly favoured one side: the Grand Republican Alliance.\n\nDespite objections from opposition groups, [UPC_GetSTAPartyLeader] was named President and hurriedly sworn in. Attempts to challenge the results in court have begun, but most realise the futility of the situation. The Army has clearly chosen their favourite after months of chaotic campaigning and their commanders are unlikely to budge now. Faith in democratic procedures have fallen by the wayside, and as more and more legislators take their seats in [UPC.Capital.GetCapitalVictoryPointName], it has become clear that there will be no institutional resistance to his rule.\n\nDesperate, CDL leaders have called on their followers to take to the streets to protest the results but it seems they are only delaying the inevitable.
Democracy at work I suppose.
A Presidential Self-Coup
The President of China has almost always held considerable power, far more than what the constitution grants. And when it is not the President it is often another strongman behind the scenes. Maybe the drafters of the Federalist Constitution were hoping the legislature or the provinces or plain democratic norms would be enough to stop the same from happening in their new republic. Or maybe they accepted this reality long before ratification.\n\nEither way, President Tang has been accumulating more and more power, consolidating control over the executive behind himself and his cronies. His agents have begun a systematic campaign of harassment against dissident figures and the opposition have found fewer and fewer legal avenues to oppose his rule. Even some members of his own People's Government Party have found themselves sidelined as Tang moves to consolidate power around himself and a small clique of Yunnanese officials dubbed the Dian Clique. Prime Minister [GetSecondInCommand] himself is said to be in the hot seat. Slowly but surely the democratic safeguards put in place by the constitution have been dismantled.\n\n[UPC_GetCDLPartyLeader] and other CDL leaders have made one last ditch attempt to protest Tang's power grab, circling legislation around the National Assembly calling for significant restrictions to Presidential decrees and a national unity government involving all parties. But it has achieved little traction from other parties, most electing to adopt a wait and see approach if not outright submitting to Tang's rule.\n\nCDL intellectuals have also begun airing their complaints in editorials around the nation, hoping for widespread resistance but finding mostly apathy from the war weary people. Most of the provinces are similarly firmly under Tang's grip, either due to loyalty, fear of repression, or their Governors' disdain for radical action. Government aligned press outlets have accused the CDL of being subversive as the young republic's democracy erodes.
The people chose Tang to lead, not them.
Fall of the CDL Government
The [GetSecondInCommand] government has been teetering for some time, weakened by controversial decisions, a discontented military high command, and poor handling of China's various post-unification crises. Public support for the government has plummeted, banditry is said to be on the rise, and the elites have begun to clamour for stronger leadership to take control. Most damningly however, it seems that [UPC.GetLeader] has lost the faith of the magistrates and military officers that his government depends on to function.\n\nA small riot in the capitol finally gave them the pretext they needed to act. Several student activists in [UPC.Capital.GetCapitalVictoryPointName] were arrested by the police, accused of being part of a seditious socialist cell. It drew considerable condemnation from the student body and after protests turned violent local authorities ordered dozens rounded up including several of their professors - worsening the unrest and feeding into the perception that the CDL harbors socialist agitators in their ranks. Many of the scholars detained are CDL members, who have pleaded with the President to intervene on their behalf.\n\nHe hardly got the chance to. As word of the incident reverberated around the capital, rumors of his intention to order the release of the suspects began to swirl. [UPC_GetSTAPartyLeader] exploited the moment, calling for a vote of no confidence against Prime Minister [GetSecondInCommand]'s cabinet. The motion was carried with astounding speed - many legislators were not even present to vote. Later that night, President [UPC.GetLeader] disappeared, believed to have been arrested himself in a palace coup. The former Prime Minister and many other CDL members have fled, hoping to rally sympathetic allies in the provinces.
We can expect [UPC.GetLeader]'s resignation soon...
Democracy's Martyrs

The Yunnan Army struck hard and struck fast - crippling the budding opposition. Army cadets have organised throngs of loyalists to assault CDL protesters while the police raided the homes of their leaders.\n\nEven more chilling were the assassinations in broad daylight. Li Furen and Wang Ren were shot in Xi'an. Li Gongpu, one of the \

Brave Poets in a Wounded Nation
The Second-Sino Japanese War has wrought untold horrors onto the nation, and especially onto the individuals that compose it. Mothers lost sons, children lost fathers, brothers lost sisters. It was a conflict against complete imperial domination and cultural annihilation, the last stand of all Chinese against those who would seek their demise. Captured with extraordinary use of photography, and even filmed footage, nonetheless the age-old tradition of prose and poetry has captured such brutal events in perfect stasis.\n\nAmongst the Yunnanese-led China Democratic League is a plethora of individuals tutored by those of the Crescent Moon Society in constructing poetry, and one particular individual's popular patriotic platitudes have found a resurgence amongst the nation's people now that he himself has risen to prominence.\n\nYang Ming, an ethnic Bai born in Dali Yunnan in 1919 is particularly young for his age, born in a generation that did not know anything but the Warlord Era. At the age of 9, he was an apprentice at a store in Xiaguan, and then at 16 in 1936 he travelled to Kunming to study at a Sino-German University, and during the Sino-Japanese War had roared into action drafting and producing a plethora of both propaganda and poetry.\n\nIntroduced by Wen Yiduo to the United New Poetry Society, Yang Ming's most popular piece has to be that of \

Revolutionaries Amongst Reformists
Ever since the introduction of Socialism into the dynamic, young, and fluid Chinese political battleground of the Warlord Era, there has been a divide between those that seek class struggle as the method for the proclamation of their socialist ideals, or that of participation in bourgeois democracy to advance people's liberation.\n\nInfamous for their propagation of revolutionary struggle, the most fervent and ferocious, and adamant, advocates of the former within China have to be that of the Left-Kuomintang, and the Chinese Syndicalist Party. Interpreting the Three Principles as a prescription for society's woes, and a manual for the implementation of socialism within China, organisations such as the Provisional Action Committee had long riled up the masses, imploring the masses to pick up arms and depose their oppressors.\n\nPerhaps the greatest mass political organisation espousing the latter is that of the Left-Wing of the China Democratic League. Ranging from the National Salvation Federation to the Democratic Socialist Party, the CDL-Left has, perhaps wittingly, welcomed a large portion of socialist revolutionary defectors. Ex-Kuomintang aficionados, or even home-grown socialists, have joined the China Democratic League in droves as the Kuomintang diminishes in stature, relevance, and is constantly harangued by the state apparatus; largely for wanting to overthrow said state apparatus.\n\nBut this enlarging of the CDL with new, radical, blood has seen a divide within its own leadership emerge. To work with the radicals would undoubtedly damage the movement's perception amongst certain interest groups, and even the opposition. However, not working with the radicals within the CDL would openly admit to further factionalism within the CDL, hurting the influence and strength of the CDL as a whole.
We cannot admonish them for their practice.
Shall we ask them how compromising Sun Yat-sen was?

Travelling to Tengchong, Cun founded the first Overseas Chinese school in Yunnan, Yiqun Middle School, and served as its principal. Subservience to the legislature, to the executive? Like Father, Like Son
As the son of the esteemed Tang Jiyao, Tang Xiaoying's political career is seeminly on the rise with the recent unification of the country. An economics student from Tokyog Imperial University, the younger Tang has grown up in a China much different than that of his father. Yet still the elder Tang attempted to install virtue and morality into his successor. When Xiaoying was younger, his father had taken him out to the slums of Kunming where he saw the lower classes squandered together in filth and poverty. The elder Tang sternly reminded his son to remember to give back to his community and to use his victories for good.\n\nNow with Chinese unification present, the younger Tang has risen through the ranks as a member of the PGP. With Tang seeking to ensure his bloodline continues to have some sway and influence in national politics, his adivsors have proposed that perhaps Tang Xiaoying could seek a career post as a commissioned officer within the Young China Party (YCP) dominated military. Doing so could rein in the YCP's influence in the greater Chinese political environment while ensuring a friendly voice exists within the military ranks.\n\nAlternatively, another proposal seeks to utilise Xiaoying's existing political networks and experience to be involved in the actual political apparatus. In doing so, he would play a pivotal role of mediator and politician while keeping the interests of Tang Jiyao alive well after Tang's death.\n\nAnother idea is for Xiaoying to simply join the growing national bureaucracy of the Dian Clique as a bureaucrat. Using his background in economics, Xiaoying can be a perfect fit for the Transportation Department or the Treasurey, ensuring the cogs of the national machine continue to function smoothly and without corruption.

The Constitutional Research Association

And at the centre of a particular local movement would have to be none other than that of Tang Jiyao's only son, Tang Xiaoying.\n\nFounding the Constitutional Research Society with people such as Zhou Xinmin, Pan Dakui, Jiang Liangfu, and Zeng Zhaolun, the Society focused on the production of reports and seminars discussing the domestic constitutional movement, the situation of constitutionalism in countries abroad, and various calls for the strengthening of democracy and expanding the influence of the Chinese democratic movement.\n\nPerhaps bridging the gap between the People's Government Party and China Democratic League, the CRS' hallmark publication was that of \

Atop the House of Cards
Chinese politics has always been a rather precarious house of cards. Carefully constructed through backroom deals and slick handshakes, even Beiyang's best and most Machiavellian statesmen could easily be sundered by one wrong move, or one wrong tip of the hat. Just ask Duan Qirui, Zhang Zuolin, or Cao Kun how their ambitions played out.\n\nYet nonetheless, this is not the Beiyang Republic. There is no inherent corruption, inherent patronage, cutthroat deals, or decades of political baggage. This is a new, fresh, and rejuvenated republic. Built upon the blood of martyrs, and the ideals of provincial freedoms, and national democracy. But, with a new republic, there remains a large amount of old faces. Those old acolytes of the Zhili, Fengtian, the Chinese Constitutionalists, Political Science, Young China, various other groups continue to persist in the new political battleground amongst the upstarts of the Grand Republican Alliance and China Democratic League.\n\nBut this is certainly no large hurdle for Tang Jiyao's firstborn. Conniving and sincere, in his own way, Xiaoying himself has made great strides in building genuine connections amongst the whole political battleground. From his own cliques within the Grand Republican Alliance, be they People's Government Party, Dian Clique, People's Renewal Society, or the Public Interest Party. Or those from his personal connections with the likes of Luo Longji, Wen Yiduo, or Li Gongpu, bringing him sway with the Crescent Moon Society and National Salvation Federation of the China Democratic League.\n\nOr even perhaps the political independents? The Endeavour Society, Political Science, or even the merchants. And what about the Liangists? The Young China Party, and Chinese Constitutionalists, both groups of which Tang retains close ties to.\n\nWith his rule stabilised, there remains the opportunity for Xiaoying to pull on his ties, to play a balancing role amongst the interest groups of the republic, favouring some assortment of individuals to aid in building a new future for China.

Education and Society
Whilst ostensibly President of the whole Chinese Republic, Cun Shusheng often finds himself away from the capital, returning to his hometown. An educator at heart, he frequently makes a habit of attending the various schools in the region as an educator, and most particularly, in that of Yiqun Middle School. Perhaps better suited to the role of Professor, or Dean, rather than that of a politician, let alone one of the whole nation beyond Yunnan, Cun has nonetheless found no issue in adopting the mold of a true statesman.\n\nImbued with a patriotic fervour, and an understanding that education, not political reform, not revolution, not grand-standing foreign ambitions or the blight of total societial rejuvenation, but education, and education alone is the wellspring of China's issues. If the people cannot read, if they cannot write, how are they to be expected to participate in a civic democracy? To formulate their own ideas, their own concepts. To advance the nation onto new heights?\n\nWhen Cun took the incumbent position as president, the nation's best and brightest could not even compare to the average set of alumni of an American University. But similarly, when Cun took the incumbency of Heshun Central Primary School, the majority of children were outside schooling, and most adults under 30 illiterate. And yet look at Heshun Township now! The majority of children are enrolled in education, and the majority of adults able to read and write.\n\nAnd so it is to this example that Cun aims to rectify China's greatest blight restraining it from true prosperity. To usher in a new era, the people must but merely devote themselves towards the development of the country's economic construction, and its cultural, educational, and scientific undertakings. Working ever diligently, ever relentlessly, Cun assumed the presidency of a wounded and hurt nation. But by the time his term ends, there is no doubt in his mind that he will leave the people a fresh utopia.
From a spark, to a prairie fire, to a flame engulfing the whole nation.
Heirs of the Future
There is much to do, and much to do about the President of the Republic. Born with only scant memories of what came before the Warlord Era, Tang Xiaoying is but one of many of the new generation tasked with stewardship from the old. Whilst his father was perhaps one of the most important individuals in the whole era, what does that make of him? Was his lot in life much better? Did he benefit from it? Perhaps he did, perhaps he didn't.\n\nNonetheless, his father died pursuing the dream of a free, democratic, and federalist Chinese republic. A scholar at heart but a warrior in reality, that is all of which Tang Jiyao had fought for his whole life, and it had cost him such. But his mission didn't die with him, keeping the dream alive are all of his allies, all of his friends, and most importantly, his son. His flesh and blood. And it is to this testament that the nation has been brought together to finalise the dream that Jiyao, and so many others, died fighting for.\n\nKeeping the dream alive, that is what this is for. But that isn't to say that Xiaoying isn't genuine, no. In a world as harsh as our's, with so much lust for wealth, for power, few genuine statesmen exist that scant but many are cast down as nothing but brokers of their own benefit. Yet, Xiaoying carries genuine conviction in spades, a grassroots organiser, an impromptu soldier.\n\nHis father bestowed upon him the torch of a whole era of Xinhai revolutionaries, all those that died, their dreams of a Chinese democracy did not die with them. As long as Xiaoying, or any of those for which the old generation of reformers and revolutionaries impacted, remain alive, then their debt will be repaid. It may not be certain, it may not be guaranteed, it may be the flaunting of idealists even, but we must try.
We are all lights shining in the darkness.
People's Government and Fengjian Federalism
Zhang Taiyan had spent much of his life confronting the fundamental question at the heart of democracy: how to protect the freedom of the individual whilst granting the state sufficient power to provide public services to its citizens. This had long been what tore Taiyan apart, and drove him in his endeavours, and in his philosophy. The Buddhist concept of Emptiness enabled Taiyan to see the creation of the nation-state as merely a pragmatic service.\n\nIn fact, not only the nation-state, but liberalism, was a means to an end, everything served to reach a balance between the freedom of the individual, the authority of the leader, and the power of the state. All Federalism did, in the end, was maximise the accountability of the individual, their leaders, and the state.\n\nTaiyan is gone, but perhaps his greatest follower lives on. In President Tang, the unfettered and unrelentless autonomy, Fengjian, sought by Taiyan had been tempered by the cold, harsh reality. A revolutionary hero, a warrior-scholar, a statesman, and a crafter of a new republic, Tang Jiyao is a man of many achievements. But they are all done in service of the people, and to his country. His Federalism serves a dual purpose in his eyes, that of a statebuilding project, and that of a Buddhist rite.\n\nImproving the lives of the people, pursuing a constitutional, stable, and anti-Militarist republic, Tang has always walked the narrow road. Perhaps seeking the long-term vision, he naturally straddled between autocracy and revolution. A nationalist, but with an abject fear of populism, he had made many enemies, but in the end, what do they have to stand for?\n\nDemocracy has been stewarded unto the masses, in all of its beauty, in all of its ugliness. Setting the example, Tang has not brought unto the masses a new era of Beiyang despotism, but the genuine democratic dream he and Zhang Taiyan had long sought. At the end of the day, there is but one deciding factor in Tang's mind. \

Sins of the Father
It is the unforgivable weight of our genetic bloodlines for which our descendents must bear the cross of their forefathers. Tang Xiaoying understands this greatly; his father, beguiled, admired, everywhere he travels they make comparisons between him and his father. But it is the democratic cause, the Federalist cause, for which his father fought and died for. And it is for this that Xiaoying has been thrust into his position.\n\nNaturally, the long and unsteady road of the Chinese revolution onto the long sought after ideal has not been a straight one. Bounding idealism burst into the chaotic flames of Xinhai, engulfing the Qing Dynasty in the heat of its own apathy, and from the ashes building the Beiyang Republic. Yet even the utterance of such a name invokes feelings of malaise or disgust. And it is a pity. A pity that so many died for a failure. That so many died before being comforted by the conclusion of their dream.\n\nBut there is one fuel that stokes the fire of idealists and revolutionaries all around the world: hope. Hope for a better future, for a better life, not just for them, but their descendants too. Xiaoying may honour his father by bearing his banner, but so too does the lowly peasant, the worker, the politician, the soldier.\n\nXinhai took over a generation to complete, and even then, there is still work to be done. And so, one cannot lament those who gave their lives, or the sins of their past, or of their betters. There is always the next generation, the next era, to bear the torches of the revolution, of democracy, of idealism. And so, as Xiaoying works in the Presidential Palace, as the politician works in the assembly, as the worker in the factory, as the peasant in the field, as the soldier in the trench, all of China works towards a better future. No matter the cost, or the cross to bear.
The past is gone, we have to look forward.
A Republic of the Victors

A skilled scholar is almost as good as a bold strategist.
Finishing the Mission
Finally, after what seemed like decades with sparing interruptions, the nation has once again been united, and seemingly, for good. And again it would appear history has repeated itself, as a warlord clique, eponymously named after its province of origin, has fulfilled such promises of national unification.\n\nConstantly a pivotal player in national politics, Yunnan had seemingly had its true ambitions consistently blunted, either by a contingent of provinces united against them, or buckling under the sheer weight of responsibility due to their posturing.\n\nNonetheless, their present efforts cannot help but harken towards the days of Cai E, Li Liejun, and Tang Jiyao in 1915. And though so unlikely at the time, given Cai's death and the collapse of their movement, it could perhaps be considered the natural course of events that Yunnan would eventually finish its mission started decades ago, affirming itself as the true Master of the Eastern Continent.\n\nYet Yunnan's Thermidor attitudes have not been welcomed too kindly by the established Beiyang National Order, who have seen a group of rowdy upstarts uproot their own entrenched positions, and supplant themselves in their place. Similarly, the democrats and revolutionaries alike find few friends in the incumbent government once more, as whilst a softer hand is at the wheel, the government itself remains cautious and conservative.\n\nStuck between a rock and a hard place, the Yunnan-led government has sought to position itself as bearers of the Five Races, and of the Southern revolutionary-style populism, to little avail, as the people seek real, genuine change. Not a revival of the old order.
We must build a nation from scraps.
Consequences of Economic Mismanagement
Suffice to say, China's economic standing both internally and internationally leaves much to be desired. Years of civil war, followed by a war of national reunification, and then of national survival, has sufficiently depleted both any and all financial reserves nationwide, whilst similarly damaging infrastructure, factories, homes, and other facets of the nation-state.\n\nInvestors have fled, or otherwise are in the process of doing so, with debts accumulated during the Warlord Era or in the wartime conditions finally being cashed in, not least to mention the years of lost economic growth from the collapse of relative peace and stability brought forward by the Zhili's tentative unification bid.\n\nFurthermore, looming uncertainties about future prospects, and lack of optimism in any measurable progress emerging from the new government has coincided with an increase in corruption, property disputes, abuses by the military, and a general anathema to good conduct.\n\nAlbeit by the far the largest economic issue to be addressed is the size of the massive Chinese military at present, as conscription and mobilisation has ballooned the armed forces. Similarly, with a juvenile and inefficient bureaucracy at the helm of the governmental organs, common sources of government revenue such as tax collection has failed to meet expected national standards.
We cannot move forward by glaring at the past.
Strength and Unity will arrive with time.
Infants in the Lion's Den
Perhaps utterly out of their depth, the isolationist minded Dian Clique has been thrust into a position quite unbeknownst to them. Having been willing, at least in restrained manner, to aid Tang Jiyao's ambitions for national unification, few expected that they themselves would be the men within the Capital, delegating and deriving solutions to all myriad of national political issues.\n\nAnd yet, their unification has thrown the established order into chaos; originally divided by North and South, various political conventions were entrenched within the regional politicians from those areas, evident enough in attitudes during the various North-South Peace Talks of the Warlord Era. Even on an individual provincial level, politics and statesmanship was enacted with its own unique provincial qualities.\n\nBut the same actors that occupy positions in the cabinet remained relatively unknown, at least outside their pocket of the Southwest. Thrust into a complicated world where brute strength and coup d'états hardly win men any favours, they are left without any trump cards to push through their policies, instead having to rely on raw political skill; of which they are lacking.\n\nWith experiences navigating provincial politics and its assembly, the sheer scale of their new responsibilities calls upon them to handle not only the national assembly and attitudes, but various forces that they had once crushed. Revolutionaries and radicals, aiming their guns at the President's Chair, democrats harnessing calls for democracy, provincial warlords demanding autonomy, and the old order aiming to protect its established interests.\n\nWith a lack of cohesive vision, a corrupt bureaucracy, and a general state of disorder harming any efforts of true statesmanship, the new Chinese Republic may very well be sowing its own seeds of further civil war, should it waste any more time cutting to the root of the Chinese society's issues.
Let us strike an accord with the intellectuals and scholars.
You can hardly teach old dogs new tricks, let alone lions.
China's Own Sword of Damocles
Placed into a uniquely vulnerable situation, China's suffering of the 20th century has perhaps reached its greatest low yet. All too aware of such a fact, the national military has engaged in various abuses both of their power, and as a cautioning manouevre aimed at the President-Marshal to maintain his loyalties to the army, and assurances of their rights, autonomy, and protections.\n\nOf course, such a situation is untenable and equally as exploitable. With the economy in shambles, politics quickly dissipating into chaos, and the Dian Clique bearings its teeth against its own reflection, the increasing sense of weakness and vulnerability within the new republic has become an open secret.\n\nWithin the countryside resistance continues to form, propagate, and expand as bandits and revolutionaries seem to conduct parallel campaigns of enacting \

A Moment of Weakness
The National Protection Alliance is victorious, having successfully marched from Guangzhou to Beijing and toppled all their rivals before them. They have far exceeded the performance of their precursors, which brought down Yuan Shikai's attempt at crowning himself Emperor but ultimately fell to infighting and failed to destroy Yuan's Beiyang Army in battle.\n\nThe victory of the NPA has catapulted [YUN.GetLeader] to great fame across the nation, allowing him to declare himself President of a reunited Republic. Albeit, unification does not necessarily mean forever peace, especially with the inflated importance of the isolationist and regionalist [YUN.GetLeader]. Unification brings great expectations faster than it provides tools to handle them, and [YUN.Capital.GetCapitalVictoryPointName] is facing a raft of economic, military, and political challenges that leaves them open to a challenger.\n\nWith calls to increase centralisation and subdue the various autonomous warlords, various regional leaders, both major and petty, have scrambled to compose some sort of preparation for a future civil war. Those with ambition seek the presidency, those without it act in defiance of their reduction of military power, by Yunnan of all provinces.
$YUN.endgame.604.d.base$\n\nHaving long sat within his province for decades, Yan Xishan has perhaps been granted his most premier moment to sally forth and claim the mantle of the Beiyang Republic. One of the last Great Northern Warlords to remain standing, many look towards Yan to preserve the integrity of Northern Dominance, and perhaps if the stars align, they very well might see their wish come true.
$YUN.endgame.604.d.base$\n\nFeng Yuxiang is no stranger to revolts against erstwhile allies, and whilst he is in a unique position to both capture Southern populist sentiments and Northern Beiyang fears, some within the Guominjun have suggested relenting. If only to make a far more grand power play from within the system, not outside it.
$YUN.endgame.604.d.base$\n\nWith history working against [ROOT.GetLeader], it is only inevitable that the Central Government become stronger as time passes, and perhaps now, in the heat of the moment, the opportunity to crush our rivals is at its most ripe.

[FROM.GetLeader] Declares \
The unification of China was a joyous moment, but behind closed doors it was also a treacherous one. Victory pushed [YUN.GetLeader] directly into the national spotlight from obscure origins, something that was bound to draw the jealousy, ire, and even confusion of his erstwhile allies. It has also thrust onto him tremendous expectations, which his government has scrambled to meet as they sort through various military, economic, and political challenges.\n\nFearing the inevitable centralisation of the country, [FROM.GetLeader] and his [FROM.GetName] have refused to go from allies to subject. Denouncing the President they had helped install not a year ago; announcing their desire to fight a new war to protect the nation. It would seem like a long shot, but they are striking in a uniquely vulnerable moment for [YUN.Capital.GetCapitalVictoryPointName] - a troubled government already beset by post-unification difficulties.
$YUN.endgame.605.d.intro$\n\nFor Feng Yuxiang, this is but another in a long line of betrayals in his consistent ambitions for higher power and influence. Already a snake, his Guominjun has proved reliably resilient, and with ties across both North and South, it would appear he aims to sweep victory from the clutches of Dian.
$YUN.endgame.605.d.intro$\n\nFor Yan Xishan, this is a reflection of his sheer ambition. The National Protection Cabinet
Long Yun has come a long way in such a short period of time. Once a subordinate officer for an outlying warlord, he became Governor and then a pretender and now suddenly he has become the undisputed President of all of China. But this newfound power comes with weighty responsibilities and he cannot rule alone.\n\nBuilding the government needed to see China's post-war reconstruction starts with appointing a sympathetic Prime Minister to head the government. While both the old Beiyang and Guangzhou governments have been forced into submission, maintaining legitimacy as a regional faction turned national government requires diplomatic tact and inside connections.\n\nThe Li brothers, Long's relatives by his former marriage and essential to helping maintain power. Li Peitian is the elder brother and was Long's chief ambassador to various governments in addition to his various experiences in administration. Though apparently not well liked, he nevertheless was a major negotiator with the Beijing and Guangzhou political establishments and has overseen talks with democratic figures in Yunnan. Elevating Li to the Prime Ministership (and his brother Li Peiyan to a senior role in the Cabinet) would formalise the system as it stands, but would also confirm people's suspicions that Long Yun plans to rule as a Cai E-style autocrat.\n\nThe alternative is to appoint a Beiyang insider, ideally one already under Yunnanese influence. Tang Jiyao had worked to cultivate some as part of his People's Government Party, and another set of brothers stood out. Wang Dong was a protégé of Zhang Taiyan while in Zhejiang, a descendant of the prestigious Wang family. His brother Wang Rongbao was a legislator, ambassador, and constitution drafter for the Beiyang government. Selecting him would at least show some limited deference to the old political system and a limited commitment to constitutional rule.
The Li brothers are our preferred choice.

The Wang brothers are a necessary one.
Xiong Qinglai, the Mathematician
Xiong Qinglai was born in Yunnan in 1893 to a government official. Despite the upswing of anti-foreign sentiment in the province, he began studying English and French while attending the Yunnan Provincial Institute of Higher Learning in 1911. This allowed him to win a provincial government scholarship to study mining in Belgium in 1913 but his studies were interrupted by the outbreak of the Great War.\n\nAs a result he went to Paris and completed his undergraduate studies at the Lycee St. Louis before pursuing advanced education at the University of Grenoble where he studied mathematics, analytic mechanics, physics, and astronomy. In 1920, he was awarded a Master of Science degree but was rushed home when the revolution overtook France.\n\nAfter returning to China, he relocated to Nanjing to establish a Department of Mathematics at the National Southeastern University where he wrote textbooks on mathematical subjects ranging from geometry to differential equations, a pioneer in introducing modern mathematics to China. After a brief stint at Northwestern University in Shaanxi (disrupted by warlord conflicts), he joined the faculty at Tsinghua University where he oversaw the establishment of the Department of Mathematics.\n\nIn the 1930s he returned to Europe where he earned international acclaim for his paper \
A brilliant mind grown right here in Yunnan!
The Family Business
Ever since Long Yun's marriage to Li Peilian in 1915, the Li family has always had Long Yun's ear. Li Peilian was a descendant of Li Canting, a famous doctor, and was known for her artistic talents and beauty. The exact story of how they met has become a fixture of local propaganda and folklore, but what is clear is that she was an extremely close confidant and effective in mobilising women in support of her husband as a \
Long fancies himself as above personal politics.
Long and his family enjoy the fruits of power.
Lu Han's Uprising
A final confrontation between Hu Ruoyu and Lu Han inches ever closer, and in some ways this will be the final showdown between north and south. The two men have been at odds for some time, and fighting the wars of unification only provided a brief respite. The fact that both men are seen as lucky outsiders to national politics has only fueled further uncertainty as they compete for control over the army and bureaucracy.\n\nFar away from home, Hu Ruoyu has tried to get the old Beiyang establishment in his corner. As the new President, he has a greater national profile and is able to count on foreign backing to maintain his rule, but he also shoulders a greater degree of culpability (fairly or not) for the challenges of national reconstruction. Similarly, by trying to ingrain himself as a successor to the old Zhili Clique, he draws on a divisive legacy.\n\nMarshal Lu Han, his rival, has mainly worked to absorb his relative Long Yun's former allies into his camp. He has also tried to cast a wide net, courting former supporters of the Guangzhou government, regionalist sentiment, republican nationalism, and even Kuomintang style populism. At an institutional disadvantage, he decided to strike first, tapping into anger at Hu's alleged dark dealings with foreign powers, corruption, and autocratic leadership to try staging a coup.\n\nTaking advantage of Hu Ruoyu entertaining foreign dignitaries, Lu Han ordered his men to surround key positions in the Capital, hoping to arrest Hu Ruoyu and his chief lieutenants. But Hu, well familiar with Tang Jiyao and Long Yun's downfalls, was wise enough to escape and gathered his own forces for a counterattack. By nightfall...
Lu Han was in control of the capital!
Lu Han had fled the country!

The New Prime Minister
While Hu Ruoyu and Lu Han clashed, working quietly in the background was the newly appointed Prime Minister Zhou Zhongyue. Zhou is a Yunnan elder statesman, an ethnic Bai politician who studied in Waseda University in Japan before returning to China to work as an educator in 1907. He helped to lead protests against the Unequal Treaties signed by the Qing dynasty which granted British and French exploitative mining rights in Yunnan province, and in 1910 after the Pianma incident he was sent by the provincial government to Beijing as part of a delegation protesting foreign interference.\n\nHe was made secretary general of the Governor's Office by Cai E after the Xinhai Revolution and in the coming years served in educational, economic, and political roles in the Beiyang government. But when Cai E rebelled against Yuan Shikai, he stayed behind to cover Cai's escape and later was forced to flee to Japan, thus missing the National Protection War.\n\nAfter Cai E died in 1916, Luo was retained by Tang Jiyao and played senior roles - even briefly serving as Governor of Yunnan. He helped mediate between Tang, Long, and Hu during the various coups and also aided in the establishment of the Yunnan Tongzhi Museum.\n\nHe largely retired in the early 1930s, but amid the chaos in the province he was sent as an emissary to Beijing as a representative. The connections he made there and his relative neutrality made him one of the few worthy candidates worth appointing Prime Minister.
Zhou is an excellent conduit between [ROOT.GetLeader] and the legislature.
He is a reliable, silent servant of state - and more importantly the President.

With many of his close friends and allies having either joined the Kuomintang, or otherwise participated in the Tongmenghui, Long in-essence has been surrounded by agents of revolution throughout his career. A President and His Republic
How would history view the Chinese Republican experiment? A success? A failure? Or something in-between? Having sundered China's millennia of dynastic history, the Xinhai Revolution unleashed a true whirlwind of ideas out into the open. But the whirlwind was all consuming, and with the counter-offensive of Yuan Shikai, China would once again be cast into decades of misery, poverty, and civil war.\n\nYet there alone stood men of defiance: the Dian Clique. But there can be no folly or mistake, their defiance in the face of Yuan's personalist power plays resulted in the fracturing of the nation and the ensuing Warlord Era. But alas that has all come to pass, and once again the Dian Clique has transformed the nation, this time perhaps on the path to unity, not struggle.\n\nA Yi soldier of the Yunnan Army since his early days, Long Yun's career has been nothing if not remarkable. Constantly and consistently distinguishing himself in campaigns in multiple armies, the \
Every King needs his Scholar.
A New Order...
For the first time since Cai E in the 1910s, Yunnan has a voice in the central government. And this time, Yunnan is on top. Few believed he would try it, even fewer believed he could, but Hu Ruoyu was able to unite all of China under him and his provisional government. Little more than a regional footnote in Chinese politics a decade ago, Hu Ruoyu has become the President of the Republic.\n\nHu Ruoyu reflects the modern evolution of Cai E's ideology - with a belief in a powerful, border-line absolute military executive ruling \
...and a continuation of the old.
From Militarist to President
The post-Xinhai environment has rewarded bold, ambitious men - allowing them to rise far above their station with the right amount of determination, military prowess, charisma, ruthlessness and good fortune. Lu Han, an ethnic minority from a distant province, was an unexpected victor but perhaps embodies the shifting fortunes of the era.\n\nHaving fought, befriended, schemed, rabbleroused, and betrayed his way to the top, Lu Han commands a united China as its new President. He remains an enigma to many even now - a childhood friend and later betrayer of his former patron, a populist who has always been connected to the establishment, and a regionalist who managed to unite the nation.\n\nPerhaps the best way to understand Lu Han would be to trace the line of Yunnan leaders before him. Cai E's tenure in Beijing as a wary lieutenant of Yuan Shikai saw him court Liang Qichao and his various parties and emphasise constitutionalism but also support borderline autocratic, militaristic Presidential authority at the expense of parliament and regional autonomy.\n\nCai died shortly after beginning a stalemated war to defend the republic against Yuan Shikai's coronation. Tang Jiyao succeeded him after a brief power struggle and emphasised Federalism, constitutionalism, and even a move towards civilian albeit authoritarian rule. The generation after that retained Cai and Tang's authoritarianism and moved towards an isolationist brand of regionalism but also maintained at least some of the civilian-focused rule Tang developed.\n\nSo what will Lu Han, arguably the fourth leader of the Yunnan Clique, do now that he rules all of China? Nationalism and centralisation seems inevitable, favoritism towards Yunnanese and other southerners as well. Beyond that is a mystery - but idiosyncratic, pragmatic leadership is nothing new in China. There will likely be appeals to \

The Fight for Human Rights
Forever a perennial struggle is that of the fight for human rights. Not just the citizen's rights, the ones granted to them by by the state, but that of intrinsic human rights. Granted to us from the very spark of our existence. It is those rights, the freedom of thought, of organisation, of speech, of occupation, and so many more, those fundamental and inalienable rights upon which serves as the law of nature. They are the basis of the Crescent Moon's struggle, and dare they be the basis of the whole struggle of the whole China Democratic League.\n\nThe Chinese Constitution must be the first in the world that backs up its democratic embellishments with true citizen's democracy. For a free and truly democratic system must place human rights before even the paper they are demarcated upon. If a man so much so has a thought in his mind, there is but the desire to express such a thought. And if there is such one desire, it cannot simply be denied. Sic semper tyrannis; humankind must be allowed to exert itself free and willing, not be cowed into that of what other's desire.

Patrolling the Mountains
With the ousting of the rotten structure of Tang Jiyao's regime from the province, it has become evidently clear to the chiefs of the National Revolutionary Army that there remains holdouts within Yunnan's mountainous terrain. Pockmarked by deep valleys and assortments of titanic stone behemoths, Yunnan is the epitome of the Chinese interior's rugged terrain. We shall deploy NRA soldiers to eject any remaining guerillas from the province.

Once the leading force of Republicanism in the South, the Yunnan Clique finds itself humbled by the injustices of time. Forced to kneel at the foot of the Beijing Government, Governor Tang Jiyao finds his government deadlocked in a struggle against the remnants of the Kuomintang forces in Guizhou. Styling himself the leader of the Federalist Movement, Tang seeks to not only crush the KMT rebels, but carry out unification on his terms. Will the heir of National Protection see the Federalist banner fly across the Middle Kingdom, or shall the dream of the National Revolution be reborn with the flame of vengeance?

§lMaster of the Eastern Continent§!
Long a hotbed of Anti-Qing sentiment, §YYunnan§! resolutely took up arms following the proclamation of the §lXinhai Revolution§! in 1911. Under the leadership of §mLi Genyuan§!, the Kunming Military Academy had become the centrepiece of the revolutionaries' chessboard in the region with its alumni and faculty all having ties to the §lTongmenghui§! in some form. Under the auspices of §mLi Genyuan§!, §PCai E§!, §aLuo Peijin§!, §PLiu Cunhou§!, §mGu Pinzhen§!, §aTang Jiyao§! and others, the §YYunnan§! §lTongmenghui§! launched their own solidarity uprising with §lXinhai§! on October 30th, 1911 (The Ninth Day of the Ninth Month of the Lunar Calendar), dubbed the \

The Stacked House of Cards
§MThe Yunnan Clique§!\nSurrounding the delicate balance struck within §YYunnan§! are the various disparate political factions of the province. With the core and incumbency held by §aTang Jiyao's§! eponymous §aTang Clique§!, they pose to be the current face of provincial politics. Loyal directly to §PCai E's§! successor, §aTang Jiyao§!, the §aTang Clique's§! past is as marred as its leader's. Espousing both authoritarian and progressive policies with the ultimate goal of promoting §YFederalism§!, the §aTang Clique§! endeavours to secure the support of reformist intellectuals whilst still maintaining the loyalty of the gentry and officers. Similarly related is the §cPeople's Government Party (Minzhidang)§!, the successor to §aTang's§! earlier §cDemocratic Party (Minzhudang)§!, representing the Governor's more reformist, democratic and conservative approach inspired by §cLiang Qichao§!, §cZhang Shizhao§!, and §cZhang Taiyan§!.\n\nRunning in tandem in the pursuit of democracy is the noble §lCrescent Moon Society§!, formed as a collection of Anglo-American educated intellectuals who wrote for §lCrescent Moon Magazine§!. Often coming into conflict with left wing writers due to their adoption of a more moderate and modern conservative outlook of Chinese Culture, the §lCrescent Moon Acolytes§! soon began to promote a humanistic form of constitutionalism, with a focus on human rights and education.\n\nIn conjunction there remains the §PHu Ruoyu§! and §gLong Yun§! Cliques, centred around their eponymous figures. Adopting various forms of semi-progressive governmental outlooks, the warlordist cliques truly desire only to rule at the top, strengthen Yunnan, and persist within the province, at least on the surface. Counted among them are various minor interest groups, such as the §mYunnan Chamber of Commerce§!, the §nYoung China Party§!, and others.\n\n§MThe Kuomintang§!\nWithin the §kKuomintang's§! remnant regime that has consolidated itself in §YGuizhou§! remain many factions of disparate ideological interpretations of the Three Principles of the People, and the §aGuofu§!'s will. Beside loyalists of the Old Right, the Guizhou §kKMT§! feature the heartbed of the New Right sect of the party, under its chief ideological theorist §aDai Jitao§!. However the true reigns of power rest with the descendants of §PChiang Kai-shek§!, in the form of the §PWhampoa Clique§! led by §PHe Yingqin§!. Through the promotion of §aDai's§! palingenetic ultranationalism claimed to be sourced from traditional Chinese beliefs, §PHe Yingqin§! seeks to revitalise China in pursuit of the National Revolution.\n\nDue to §aDai's§! efforts, the Zhejiang Society of Revolutionary Comrades, currently headed by the §aChen brothers§!, has aspirations to coalesce into the informal entity of the §aCentral Club Clique§!, technocratics with their own \

National Defence Fortification Plan
To prevent annihilation from forces outside of China's national borders, the nation has embarked on an ambitious national fortification plan aimed at reinforcing central control over the frontier.
Returning to the Spiritual Root
The Northwest frontier of China is but an untapped cornucopia of resources and virtue. It must merely be tapped by the Central Government. Securing both China's borders, and instilling a sense of pride within the spiritual hotspots of the nation, the Kuomintang have begun a programme of careful cultivation within the Northwest territories, ensuring to modernise whilst maintaining the delicate balance within the region.
Constituent of the Chinese Confederation
Federalism at its finest, the central government has imbued within the constituent provinces a variety of sweeping powers. Whilst not as extreme as how Zhang Taiyan would've preferred it, the essence of his ideals still shapes national and provincial policy in the field.
